###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-01T15:48:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B68250D.6DB27FB3@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<b996aa65.0107311108.47a4b218@posting.google.com> <_VG97.13236$ar1.42778@www.newsranger.com> <9k7img$kt3$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <3b67592e_3@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9k7q0n$2cj$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB wrote:

> Good manners don't pay the bills.  If you don't discuss this and let
> the headhunters know what acceptable rates are, they'll keep throwing
> the cheap rates about and signing up people who prefer good manners to
> a living wage.

If they keep signing up people, then they are offering what is acceptable to
those people.   Wages aren't set by what we want to live on, they are set by
whatever the worker and the employer agree upon.  Both parties consider the
alternatives and make the deal which is better than the alternatives.

The most cost effective workers sometimes cost more.   But if the worker would
rather earn less than be without a job, the employer should entertain his offer.
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

```
year.....
>  or that vacation 3 times a year......or the 2nd summer home in the
> hamptons.....damn skippy, u must be doing something right if you think
104,000
> isnt a living wage.  Good for you. didnt know Bill Gates posted under
> SkipppyPB.

Quick calculation: nobody I know works 2080 billable hours per year (except
the workaholics, but they are going to die young anyway and are not a
threat). There's the time spent, for example, marketing (looking for that
next contract) , managing the "business" side of your career (billing and
collecting) , holidays, and much needed vacations.

Laywers use 1500 bilable hours per year as a reference number. I don't find
that a bad benchmark at all.

And frankly, 1500 hours at $50.00 is pretty decent money in my neighborhod.
Better still if the FICA tax is half-paid by a client or agency.

MCM
```

```
I've
> averaged over 2100 for the last 4 years. Now I'm taking 6 months off.

So do the math:

4.0 years x 2100 hours =     8400 hours
.5 years  x 0 hours     =           0 hours
-------                                  ----------------
4.5 years                            8400 hours

Average 1867 hours per year, 24% above the 'reference' figure I used. Equal
to 234 days or 46 weeks, four days.

Joe Sixpack works:
  52 weeks  x 5 days     = 260 days
    National Holidays     = (  10 days)   (decent holiday allowance)
    Vacation                 = (   15 days)  (three weeks, not bad)
                                      ------------------
 net days worked per year  235.    (pretty close to 234)


BTW, taking this time off is a wise decision. There's more to life than
working.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <20010807213223.00873.00001108@mb-mm.aol.com>`

```
On 08 Aug 2001 01:32:23 GMT, yyjohnyy@aol.com (Yy JOHN yy) enlightened
us:

>>If you don't discuss this and let
>>the headhunters know what acceptable rates are, they'll keep throwing
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>SkipppyPB.  

That may look like a good rate but since it is contract work that
means you are responsible for your own health, life and dental
insurance.  Also, you'll probably have to pay your federal, state and
local taxes out of that.  Add to that the fact that you would need to
maintain your own retirement fund without the benefit of an employers
matching funds.  Suddenly that $50 an hour shrinks down to around
$25-30$ or at best $62,400 for a year.  However, the contract is only
for 6 months and $50 an hour is the top rate.  

I stand by my criticism.  However, if you are a contract programmer in
between contracts with nothing else on the line, go for it.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

When I'm feeling down, I like to whistle. 
It makes the neighbor's dog run to the end 
of his chain and gag himself. 

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

```
What no days off?  No holidays?  No Vacation?  Consultants don't get paid for
that time off.  In addition, there's a serious "penalty" - like 15% for being
self employeed.  You see, there's no "employer" portion of SSI or medicare paid
by the employer.  In reality a $50.00 consulting rate, is more like $28-30 an
hour at a "Regular" Job.  In addition there's no employer portion to your
medical insurance etc.  While even this is a "Livable" wage - a single wager
earner at this rate with a family is going to have a tight budget.

In article <20010807213223.00873.00001108@mb-mm.aol.com>, Yy JOHN yy says...
>
>>If you don't discuss this and let
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>SkipppyPB.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T12:38:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<svvc7.648$zS3.20698@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <20010807213223.00873.00001108@mb-mm.aol.com> <Eylc7.3498$NJ6.14216@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message
news:Eylc7.3498$NJ6.14216@www.newsranger.com...
> What no days off?  No holidays?  No Vacation?  Consultants don't get paid
for
> that time off.  In addition, there's a serious "penalty" - like 15% for
being
> self employeed.  You see, there's no "employer" portion of SSI or medicare
paid
> by the employer.

The tax penalty is not 15%.

(For purposes  of discussion, Payroll Tax = FICA = "Social Security Tax" +
"Medicare Tax")

If you are incorporated (which I just did last week, which is why I'm 'tuned
in' to this topic), you pay 15.3% FICA: the employer and employee halves, SS
(12.6%, capped) and  Medicare (2.7%, not capped). (Plus FUTA and SUTA).
(Plus corporate tax on any company income you don't pay yourself as salary
or bonus).

As an employee, you pay half the payroll tax; although I think I can make
the case that were an employer not required to match the 'employee portion'
of the payroll tax, it would be paid to the employee as cash wages, so the
employee is paying it all anyway.

If you are not incorporated, you pay the same FICA (except in this case it's
called "self-employment tax"), but you get back half the SE tax as a credit
on your personal income tax. (There's all kinds of multipliers and
exclusions, so numbers are not exact).

All the other non-cash compensation of a "regular" job (paid holidays, paid
vacations, subsidy for medical insurance, etc.), of course, increase the
"true" annual compensation of a "regular" job; such add-ons are not present
for the self-employed. (When my customers pay me, they rarely add a few
bucks to the check as "contribution towards Mike's vacation." How 'bout
yours?)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T13:52:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DAwc7.3644$ym4.144982@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <20010807213223.00873.00001108@mb-mm.aol.com> <Eylc7.3498$NJ6.14216@www.newsranger.com> <svvc7.648$zS3.20698@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In article <svvc7.648$zS3.20698@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

[snippage]

>As an employee, you pay half the payroll tax; although I think I can make
>the case that were an employer not required to match the 'employee portion'
>of the payroll tax, it would be paid to the employee as cash wages, so the
>employee is paying it all anyway.

Mr Mattias, I think that case runs contrary to most economic sense.  What
reason might be given for assuming that the employer's portion of payroll
tax would be, if no longer required, maintained as an expenditure ('paid
to the employee as cash wages') instead of being taken as profit by the
employer?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T18:25:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iAAc7.1035$h_3.195382@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <20010807213223.00873.00001108@mb-mm.aol.com> <Eylc7.3498$NJ6.14216@www.newsranger.com> <svvc7.648$zS3.20698@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <DAwc7.3644$ym4.144982@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:DAwc7.3644$ym4.144982@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> In article <svvc7.648$zS3.20698@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
> Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >the case that were an employer not required to match the 'employee
portion'
> >of the payroll tax, it would be paid to the employee as cash wages, so
the
> >employee is paying it all anyway.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That case does NOT run contrary to economic sense.

Let's suppose I am an employer with a job for which I can economically
justify spending $100.00 to hire an employee.

If I give the employee a cash wage of $92.89, after I pay the 'employer
portion'  7.65% FICA tax, I've spent my $100.00.

If I don't have to pay the FICA tax, I can give the employee $100, and still
not have broken my budget.

Granted, I'd _rather_ keep 7.65% percent for myself; but if I can afford a
cash wage of $100 instead of  $92.89+tax, I get a better employee for my
$100.

Now, if FICA tax were a fixed dollar rate per time unit and not a
percentage, this argument fails as long as everyone must pay FICA tax.
(Which today is not the case).

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T20:04:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g1Cc7.3697$ym4.145961@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <svvc7.648$zS3.20698@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <DAwc7.3644$ym4.144982@iad-read.news.verio.net> <iAAc7.1035$h_3.195382@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In article <iAAc7.1035$h_3.195382@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
>news:DAwc7.3644$ym4.144982@iad-read.news.verio.net...
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>not have broken my budget.

What you *can* do, Mr Mattias, and what you *will* do might be two
different things.

>
>Granted, I'd _rather_ keep 7.65% percent for myself; but if I can afford a
>cash wage of $100 instead of  $92.89+tax, I get a better employee for my
>$100.

So one gets a better employee for a higher wage?  No, Mr Mattias...
granted that one increases one's chances of doing so but the increase in
wage does not necessitate an increase in quality.

What is paid in wages and associated costs is an expense.  You are saying
that if a portion of this expense is removed the resulting funds will
still wind up as an expense.

It might be valuable to use the theory of labor as commodity to illustrate
this.  With FICA you pay $100 for a unit of the commodity.  FICA is
removed and you suddenly have to pay only $92.35 for the same unit of
commodity.  It makes no economic sense for you to continue to pay $100 for
that which you can obtain for $92.35.

DD
```

```
> >> >As an employee, you pay half the payroll tax; although I think I can
make

portion'

the
> >> >employee is paying it all anyway.
> >>
> >> Mr Mattias, I think that case runs contrary to most economic sense.
What
> >> reason might be given for assuming that the employer's portion of
payroll
> >> tax would be, if no longer required, maintained as an expenditure
('paid
> >> to the employee as cash wages') instead of being taken as profit by the
> >> employer?
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >If I don't have to pay the FICA tax, I can give the employee $100, and
still
> >not have broken my budget.
>
> What you *can* do, Mr Mattias, and what you *will* do might be two
> different things.
>
I would say that if the employer does not have to pay the FICA tax (or any
other type of payroll tax), then someone else would have to pay for it,
unless the service that was provided with the funds raised with said tax
will no longer be provided. If the employee takes over the part of the
employer, it is reasonable to expect those employees to ask those funds be
given to them as a compensation.  Some (probably the majority) will get it,
some will not.  In any case, these are not monies that would end up in the
pocket of the employee, in any case. They do not end up in the pocket of the
contractor, either.

Regards,
Luis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T15:46:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SlTc7.3776$ym4.149095@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <iAAc7.1035$h_3.195382@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <g1Cc7.3697$ym4.145961@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B73DE8B.77FE5DFB@cusys.edu>`

```
In article <3B73DE8B.77FE5DFB@cusys.edu>,
Howard Brazee  <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>NA wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>programmers.

I'm not sure what is meant here by 'real life'... but spending in a way
that is beneficial to programmers is not necessarily the same as
programmers getting more money.  Mr Mattias' words were:

--begin quoted text:

If I give the employee a cash wage of $92.89, after I pay the 'employer
portion'  7.65% FICA tax, I've spent my $100.00.

If I don't have to pay the FICA tax, I can give the employee $100, and still
not have broken my budget.

--end quoted text

... which prompted my reply of 'what can be done and what will be done are
not necessarily the same'.  For example, the monies which no longer ahve
to be used to pay the employer's portion of FICA might be spent, instead,
on a new Project Management software package that prints out
prettily-colored charts... *everyone* knows that this is 'beneficial to
the programmers'... and everyone knows that I am the King of England, as
well.

DD
```

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-08-11T13:20:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Eiad7.3871$ym4.148989@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <3B741488.CC247A0F@cusys.edu> <ZZVc7.3795$ym4.141972@iad-read.news.verio.net> <0Y8d7.52$4M3.12493@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
In article <0Y8d7.52$4M3.12493@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
>news:ZZVc7.3795$ym4.141972@iad-read.news.verio.net...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>should stagnate out of fear of what will be done?

Mr Mattias, might you be able to be so kind as to address what I *have*
written, rather than what you wished I might have written?  I do not
believe that I cam anywhere close to stating that such things are even
vaguely possible conclusions, Mr Mattias, and your bringing them up show a
moderately... interesting bend of argument.  What I pointed out was that
you made an assertion... remember

--begin quoted text

... were an employer not required to match the 'employee portion' of the
payroll tax, it would be paid to the employee as cash wages, so the 
employee is paying it all anyway.

--end quoted text

... using 'would', which I took to be used as expressing a conclusion
based on a contingency or possibility in the subjunctive clause cited
above ('were... would').  If my understanding is incorrect then please be
so kind as to correct it.

If my understanding *is* correct... then when asked how this argument -
which appears to run contrary to most economic sense in that it states a
decrease in expense will not be taken as a profit but, rather, maintained
as an expense in the same class - you and others do not respond in the
same definite manner.  Instead of talking about what 'would' happen, as
above, the language changes to what 'could' happen.

When such happens it is my wont to point out the differences between 'can'
and 'will'... nothing more, nothing less.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T15:37:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l8s6h$6bv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <svvc7.648$zS3.20698@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <DAwc7.3644$ym4.144982@iad-read.news.verio.net> <iAAc7.1035$h_3.195382@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <g1Cc7.3697$ym4.145961@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B73DE8B.77FE5DFB@cusys.edu> <3B747CAB.DD7AFC70@mescoma.com>`

```

On 10-Aug-2001, "David P. Bretz" <DBretz@mescoma.com> wrote:

> I'd love to know the companies you worked for..............in "real life"
> if they get  their labor cheaper they give bigger CEO bonuses!

A large company doesn't micro-manage a small department's budget.   They
give the small department money and that small department spends it.  That
department will try to squeeze in as much as it can from its budget as best
as it can.
```

```
definition of each. It all depends based upon my experience.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T19:20:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PLVd7.270579$Gk.16455568@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <svvc7.648$zS3.20698@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <DAwc7.3644$ym4.144982@iad-read.news.verio.net> <iAAc7.1035$h_3.195382@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <g1Cc7.3697$ym4.145961@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3B73DE8B.77FE5DFB@cusys.edu> <3B747CAB.DD7AFC70@mescoma.com> <9l8s6h$6bv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6rVd7.7719$Ki1.645742@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On 13-Aug-2001, "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> This subject is not that interesting. However, now that the words
> "micro-manage", and "small department" have been used, I'd appreciate a
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> > as it can.


I guess my answer is circular.   "micro-manage" is telling the department
the details on how to spend the money.  It's all relative - is it
micro-managing when the comany requires certain security standards, or
requires laws be followed?  Maybe - but that is necessary and good.

But a CEO doesn't get bigger bonuses by making sure a department doesn't use
some cost savings to keep a contractor for an extra week.  That is a waste
of his time and counter-productive.   He gets bigger bonuses by fostering an
environment where entities working for him do so productively and
efficiently.  This works best when they have freedom to allocate resources
according to their determinations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T01:00:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DK_d7.4081$ym4.161092@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <9l8s6h$6bv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6rVd7.7719$Ki1.645742@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <PLVd7.270579$Gk.16455568@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
In article <PLVd7.270579$Gk.16455568@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>,
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>
>On 13-Aug-2001, "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>requires laws be followed?  Maybe - but that is necessary and good.

Hmmmmm... I recall reading something when I was on contract with Perdue
Farms, the chicken-making company.  Seems that they had this subsidiary
that had a piece of equipment so old that drive-belts were no longer
manufactured for it; they found out that a certain size of children's
bicycle-tire inner-tube worked just fine.

One day they got a call from the Home Office... ol' man Frank Perdue
Senior (who had, supposedly, retired and handed the reins over to Frank
Junior a few years back on his (Senior's) 80-some-odd birthday) was
calling, asking why their expense-sheets were showing this frivolous item
and who the hell was buying their kids inner-tubes on his nickel?!?

But... since '(a) large company doesn't micro-manage a small
department's budget' then I guess Perdue Farms isn't, by definition,
large.

[snippette]

>He gets bigger bonuses by fostering an
>environment where entities working for him do so productively and
>efficiently.  This works best when they have freedom to allocate resources
>according to their determinations.

Mr Brazee, with all due respect... have you ever heard of 'Chainsaw Al' or
'Neutron Jack'?

DD
```

```
I strongly believe that there are companies that are managed the way Mr
Brazee says, even though I have never worked in any of them. I know of
places, though, where managers are paid according to how lean and mean their
departments are. That normally translates in managers trying to squeeze as
much blood, sweat and tears as they can from their workers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T04:43:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h02e7.45155$gj1.4201208@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <9l8s6h$6bv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6rVd7.7719$Ki1.645742@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <PLVd7.270579$Gk.16455568@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <DK_d7.4081$ym4.161092@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9la19u$pqk$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```
It's true, Luis. Things like that happen. Some CEO's should not work at that
level. I've seen that myself. However, Howard in Colorado thinks this is a
general method. Even when there are detail standards, budgets, and monthly
reporting, the results of this depend a whole lot on how much each part of
the company contributes to the bottom line. If IT is unfavorably changing
that number, they will hear about it. Also, should IT need to spend a lot of
money to bale the company out of a problem, the money will flow regardless
of the practice of the day to day operations.

Warren Simmons
```

```
Luis Gomez-Almeida <ar656@freenet.carleton.ca> wrote:
>NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
>news:DK_d7.4081$ym4.161092@iad-read.news.verio.net...
>> In article <PLVd7.270579$Gk.16455568@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>,
>> Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>> >I guess my answer is circular.   "micro-manage" is telling the department
>> >the details on how to spend the money.  It's all relative - is it
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>much blood, sweat and tears as they can from their workers.

Mr Gomez-Almeida, from what I have seen there are more than a few
different kinds of companies and managers.  Mr Brazee has announced that
in the 'real world' things happen a certain way; there have been a few
folks, now, who have pointed out that their experiences are otherwise.
This has caused Mr Brazee to admit to relying on a circular argument.

There's good places, sure, and bad places, and good folks and bad folks...
but when something is labelled as 'real world' which directly contradicts
my experience so strongly I feel a need to shame myself by showing how far
from this 'real world' my experience has been... just call me silly.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T13:28:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lb8vf$9cj$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T14:50:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lbdp8$bko$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <PLVd7.270579$Gk.16455568@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <DK_d7.4081$ym4.161092@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9lb8qc$9b2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Qvae7.4109$ym4.163144@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

On 14-Aug-2001, docdwarf@clark.net (  NA) wrote:

> >> But... since '(a) large company doesn't micro-manage a small
> >> department's budget' then I guess Perdue Farms isn't, by definition,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> quantity... how are you establishing what 'most companies' do?

Since I haven't studied every company in "the real world", obviously, I am
discussing what I have seen.  Your reply sounds like when I say "Lawrence of
Arabia" is a good movie - and someone says "in your opinion".  Of course -
my opinion and observations are what I have!


> >Yep.  They're like the civil war surgeon with the saw, cutting of legs
> >before the gangreen kills the patient.   That patient doesn't end up
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> asked to give back what was given them.

They certainly have changed in the aspect you mention - compensation to
CEO's has risen dramatically.  I suspect neither of us are happy about that.
  But back when owners ran companies, they fought unions much more
personally.  With managers running companies, it isn't nearly as personal,
and there are fewer strikes, for instance.

Quite often, I have seen small companies have a hard time growing until the
original owner retires.  That original owner KNEW he knew best about
everything, and wanted things done his way.  When he left, the new managers
didn't know as much, and allowed subordinates more freedom to do what they
do best - and the companies grow.

Centralized planning doesn't work very well.  Not in governments, nor in any
large organization.   Use the skills and initiative of subordinates.  By
giving power to them, you gain more power for yourself.  (power appears to
be the big motivator)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T16:58:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lbla1$ek6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <9lb8qc$9b2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Qvae7.4109$ym4.163144@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9lbdp8$bko$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dice7.4126$ym4.163206@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

On 14-Aug-2001, docdwarf@clark.net (  NA) wrote:

> What does this have to do with a CEO getting a larger bonus based on
> decreasing labor costs?

What does that have to do with whether or not CEOs micro-manage a department
to make sure that they don't hire someone else when they get free money?

Most of my experience with various companies was as a contractor.   I know
how the departments played with their budgets to get the work they wanted
done.   They didn't have the CEO looking over their shoulders to make sure
that when they hired someone cheaply, that the "savings" went to the CEO's
bonus.   Instead, when cost savings occurred, the budgets did not get
reduced, and they were able to keep workers longer and the departments were
more productive.

That is what I have seen time and again.
```

- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <9lb8qc$9b2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Qvae7.4109$ym4.163144@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9lbdp8$bko$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dice7.4126$ym4.163206@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9lbla1$ek6$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:9lbla1$ek6$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 14-Aug-2001, docdwarf@clark.net (  NA) wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> What does that have to do with whether or not CEOs micro-manage a
department
> to make sure that they don't hire someone else when they get free money?
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> reduced, and they were able to keep workers longer and the departments
were
> more productive.
>
> That is what I have seen time and again.

I am very glad that you have had such a wonderful experience in different
contracts. You see, I too am a contractor, and too have worked in different
projects, and organizations.  Unfortunately, most of my experience has been
in projects that were very big, and did not end up costing less money than
budgeted and finishing ahead of schedule. Thus, upper management was not
satisfied with lower management simply spending the money any way they
wanted as long as the project was done, because... the project was NEVER
done with the money originally budgeted.  Therefore, they were not so kind
to IT managers hiring contractors for $80.hour when they could get similar
help for $35/hour.


Kindest regards,
Luis
```

```
Oh, you are right! On the 2000 form 1040, Line 27 where you put the one-half
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** akent29@home.com (Arthur M. Kent)
- **Date:** 2001-08-11T02:30:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<akent29-1008011924210001@c1314071-a.fedwy1.wa.home.com>`
- **References:** `<E80796C746830F98.109E280B93E86D14.4E2D622B76154718@lp.airnews.net> <20010807213223.00873.00001108@mb-mm.aol.com> <Eylc7.3498$NJ6.14216@www.newsranger.com>`

```
This is not true in some cases.  I have been a W-2 COBOL Contractor for 3
1/2 years, and the consulting company I work for does pay for 6 holidays
(New Years Day, Memorial Day, July 4th, Labor Day, Thanksgiving, and
Christmas), and for every 2000 billable hours, I do get paid for 1 week
vacation.  

When I started with them, I was making "near the lower end" of the hourly
rate for COBOL contractors.  In the intervening 3 1/2 years, I have
received 2 increases in my hourly rate, and now I am making above what is
the maximum hourly rate for COBOL contractors (in the Seattle area).  I
also have Medical Benefits, Direct Deposit, and I participate in a
non-matching 401K program.

In article <Eylc7.3498$NJ6.14216@www.newsranger.com>, Thane Hubbell
<nospam@newsranger.com> wrote:

> What no days off?  No holidays?  No Vacation?  Consultants don't get paid for
> that time off.  In addition, there's a serious "penalty" - like 15% for being
> self employeed.  You see, there's no "employer" portion of SSI or
medicare paid
> by the employer.  In reality a $50.00 consulting rate, is more like $28-30 an
> hour at a "Regular" Job.  In addition there's no employer portion to your
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >That isnt a living wage ?  no wonder I cant afford that new SUV every
year.....
> > or that vacation 3 times a year......or the 2nd summer home in the
> >hamptons.....damn skippy, u must be doing something right if you think
104,000
> >isnt a living wage.  Good for you. didnt know Bill Gates posted under
> >SkipppyPB.
```

```
*------------
Mr Kent,
Thane was talking about INDEPENDENT CONSULTANTS (he specifically
referenced "self-employed").  I note that you call yourself a
"contractor", altho you are, in fact, an employee.  Most Independent
Consultants would not call themselves contractors -- altho contracts
are EXACTLY what they do!  It's not prejudice, nor  meant to put down
employees of consulting companies, it's a matter of public relations:
"Consultant" sounds as if it should get more money than a "mere"
"Contractor". *LOL*
```

```
is

> > also have Medical Benefits, Direct Deposit, and I participate in a
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

paid for
> > > that time off.  In addition, there's a serious "penalty" - like 15%
for being
> > > self employeed.  You see, there's no "employer" portion of SSI or

$28-30 an
> > > hour at a "Regular" Job.  In addition there's no employer portion to
your
> > > medical insurance etc.  While even this is a "Livable" wage - a single
wager
> > > earner at this rate with a family is going to have a tight budget.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Contractor". *LOL*

I call myself a contractor and not a consultant, since very few are
interested in my opinions. Thus, I work under a contract, and do as I am
told.  If they ask, I will gladly give my opinon, based in more than 20
years of experience developing business applications.

Regarding rates, it should be noted that the rates for COBOL programmers,
while able to provide for the necessities of life, by no means will put you
in the upper echelons of the economic structure of society. It is the kind
of money that teachers, police officers, nurses, construction workers and
technicians get paid, not what physicians, lawyers, dentists, or architects
get.

I am not arguing if this is fair or not. But then, it should come as no
surprise that those applying for university studies prefer anything but
computer science.  This job (programmer/analyst) will very likely put food
in your table, but will not make you rich. Not, by a long shot.

Regards,
Luis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T04:08:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010812000816.20092.00003580@ng-ca1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<akent29-1008011924210001@c1314071-a.fedwy1.wa.home.com>`

```
>Subject: Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers
>From: akent29@home.com  (Arthur M. Kent)
>Date: 8/10/01 7:30 PM Pacific Daylight Time


>This is not true in some cases.  I have been a W-2 COBOL Contractor for 3
>1/2 years

I too am a W-2 employee.  My firm insists we call ourselves 'consultants'
instead of 'contractors'.  Ok - whatever :)

I get 10 paid holidays and right now am up to almost 4 weeks vacation.  I'm
also what they call a staff manager which means I have some people working
'under' me that I cover their general personel problems like finding W-4 forms
to fill out, time sheet corrections, evaluations, minor employee/ client
situations, etc.  I get a small stipend for this and a budget to take everyone
to lunch once a quarter. I also know the billing rates and the pay rates of
these people  (not all of them are COBOL people, I have several different
specialties in my group). I'm also aware of the profit margins and expenses of
the company I work for as part of my manager duties.  This puts me in a pretty
good position to have a good idea of rates in the area.

I tried the 1099 route one year.  That was enough for me.  I prefer someone
else doing the bookkeeping :).  While the rate I got as a 1099 was much higher
than what I'm getting now (gross) after taxes, insurance (and this is for
family coverage as I had kids) etc etc I was left with less than I had made the
previous year as a 'direct' employee of a company.  After that I said no
thanks, switched companies and am perfectly happy even if I think I'm a tad
under paid (but doesn't everyone think that)  :).

Taking into account the information I know when I see a posting I determine if
it's W-2 or 1099 and decide if it is worth my while to investigate.  So far I
think I'll stay where I'm at :)

Eileen
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Rate range for COBOL programmers

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** ar656@freenet.carleton.ca (Luis Gomez-Almeida)
- **Date:** 2001-08-02T18:53:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kclkj$lge$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`
- **References:** `<b996aa65.0107311108.47a4b218@posting.google.com> <_VG97.13236$ar1.42778@www.newsranger.com> <9k7img$kt3$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <3b67592e_3@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9k7q0n$2cj$1@freenet9.carleton.ca> <3b693f83_5@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
To talk about rates for COBOL programmers can be misleading, since almost
nowhere strict COBOL programmers are needed.  For starters, in most cases it
is Programmers/Analysts that are wanted.  This makes a lot of sense since
anybody with some experience in business applications will tell us that the
programming part is very rarely the difficult one, but understanding the
application, the data base components, and the business needs.

But, on top of being able to do good analysis, as well as test and
implementation plans, there is also the need for other technical knowledge:
IMS, IDMS, DB2, JCL, VSAM, CICS, are but a few that come to mind. And how
proficient the candidate is required to be in these techniques should add or
subtract to the rate that is offered.

Cost of living in the vicinity has little influence in the rate for the
contractor.  This is so because the candidate will not be moving permanently
to that place, and towns that have a low cost of living are normally as
boring as sucking an unsalted nail, and are (most of the time) far from
airports. If the candidate is young, he/she will want a place where they can
shake their bones after working hours. If he/she has a family, then he will
appreciate being close to an airport, and being able to work a compressed
week, so they can go home frequently.

Those brave enough to post jobs in these newsgroups are advised not only to
post rates, but also the requirements with must have, and nice to have, as
well as years of experience required in each of the important subjects. It
would also help if the recruiter has some idea of what is involved in
developing business applications. If a contract is for less than six months,
then deeper and broader knowledge might be in order, but if the contract is
for longer periods, some of the skills could be learned on the job. If the
resume shows that the candidate has been able to work with different
languages and environments in the past, this might be an indication that
he/she is able to learn fast.

And still have not mentioned a single rate, but I would say that $50/hour
does not prompt me to leave my job and apply. But, this is today. Maybe
tomorrow, if the assignment is in my  town, and/or I  have been without a
contract for more than 45 days, then I might consider that a very good rate.

Regards,
Luis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [job] Cobol Programmer needed-rate range

- **From:** Steve Thompson <sthompson_2@neo.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-01T20:44:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B68699B.600FC988@neo.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<b996aa65.0107311108.47a4b218@posting.google.com> <3B676B3D.F83B66D2@neo.rr.com> <3B67FEC3.E9E564C4@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> Steve Thompson wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> What does it stand for?

Check Processing Control System - It is the back-bone software for a bank
that handles large amounts of paper checks. Generally, when a CPCS person is
wanted, they also need them to know how the interface works with the Check
Sorter(s). This is very specialized software, and the last time I worked on
it, you also had to know something about 3270 protocols, because you did your
own 3270 screen build/writes!

Later,
Steve Thompson
OSP Consulting
330/335-9907 office
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T13:16:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0108061216.17a25661@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com> <T3c97.612$hj4.35379@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <9k444c02bmj@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
Not that I'm one to defend MicroFocus, Merant or whatever. However to
my recollection MF have always had some sort of licence charges since
we started using the COBOL in '83. Obviously if one could claim that
they were misled about run-time licences then there may be a case in
law but...

We recently did an exercise of looking at MF, AcuCobol and Liant/RM
COBOL. They all have licence charges but they all have different
schemes - now there's a surprise! However there is no harm to try a
considerable amount of negotiation and get the rate down, and you may
be successful if you have a significant number, in effect if you can
guaranteed to purchase N licences per year, say.

I would agree that MF appear to ignore the smaller developers in their
scheme of things and this is a point that I frequently make to them
with the appropriate complaints!! We have also wanted to develop some
free or nearly free software which would sell for not more the ï¿½50, if
we actually charged the individual, and this is simply impossible with
a run-time licence model so we are looking at Java for this.

There is little doubt that COBOL is considered by most of the compiler
developers as a heavy weight language for use by big business and thus
can justify a hefty charge for the compiler etc. Whether the Tiny
COBOl or GNU compilers will be useful as a 'personal' development
system is another matter but it may be that this is the direction the
lower volume end of the COBOL development market will have to move. At
the moment we've stayed with MF/NetExpress but that was mainly for
conversion convenience (MF3, 16-bit, to NetEx for 32 bit) without
major conversion problems but I have to admit that our feelings of
loyalty to MF has not increased during the process

Graham
Olympic Computers(UK)
```

```
Why didn't you add Fujitsu, CA-Realia and ICOBOL to your evaluation?  (Just
Curious).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2001-08-07T00:22:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0108062322.e5ca180@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com> <T3c97.612$hj4.35379@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <9k444c02bmj@enews4.newsguy.com> <8a02b0a1.0108061216.17a25661@posting.google.com> <f9Eb7.857$NJ6.3872@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<f9Eb7.857$NJ6.3872@www.newsranger.com>...
> In your evaluation of Liant/Merant/AcuCorp  - which did you find had the most
> reasonable runtime fees for the small developer?
> 

Probably Liant/RM, but it seems that we have what could be called the
"mobile phone tariff situation" (having just seen such a sketch on
BBCTV over the weekend and I suppose that the rest of the world has
the same enjoyable situation). There is the purchase price; sometimes
there is a professional (or some such word, enterprise, e.g.) pack
which is more expensive but "offers more"; then there is the annual
maintenance; then there are the end-use licence charges, sometimes per
user, sometimes banded, always steeped in mystery. Then there is the
scope for saying "but what about us...and our special case...and we're
converting from X...and your charges are more than Y but we like your
product more" and, of course, getting the offer in writing, in a
legally binding sense.

There's no doubt that you could set up a spreadsheet covering the
various pricing schemes but I suspect that haggling still has great
scope; really comparable pricing just doesn't exist as far as I can
see.

> Why didn't you add Fujitsu, CA-Realia and ICOBOL to your evaluation?  (Just
> Curious).
> 
> 

Fair point - mainly because I couldn't find a UK source for these at
the time ('98). I had searched for various COBOLs and found several;
discounted a few of them as being likely to cost too much in
conversion from our existing MF2/3 code, either dialect or facilities
(but I don't have the details of the comparisons any longer).

In the end while we could have used either RM or ACU, and from a point
of view of usability the ACU IDE was quite nice, while RM seemed to
cost less, we were, now what's the technical term - oh - stuffed,
because we had inadvertently used some MF extensions back in the mid
80s. In addition we used some extended ADIS facilities. Neither of
these would have been trivial to deal with and we hadn't anticipated
the scale of the changes. So NetExpress was the path of least cost and
it isn't bad...

However the current work is removing ADIS usage so soon we may be back
in the looking around business.

graham
Olympic Computers (UK)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-07T12:42:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B6FD411.2B9B0882@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com> <T3c97.612$hj4.35379@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <9k444c02bmj@enews4.newsguy.com> <8a02b0a1.0108061216.17a25661@posting.google.com>`

```
graham wrote:
> 
> Not that I'm one to defend MicroFocus, Merant or whatever. However to
> my recollection MF have always had some sort of licence charges since
> we started using the COBOL in '83. 

With the earlier MF CIS Cobol and MF Level II Cobol there was always a
licence requirement for the run-time.

With MF Cobol/2 (up to and including WB 3.4 for DOS, OS/2 and Windows
3.x and WB 4.1 for Windows 32bit) one could distribute programs with a
free static or dynamic run-time as long as one kept to a restricted set
of facilities.  I never found it necessary to go beyond those facilities
as listed in the licence.

There were run-times that did require a run-time licence to be
purchased, these were part of the 'OSX'.

Microsoft Cobol, which was a version of MF Cobol/2, had no run-time
licence fees for any part that could be distributed.

Netexpress did allow free run-time distribution in at least some
versions, some MF salesmen have been known to deny any and/or all of
this as it seems they would prefer developers to send money on a regular
basis regardless of what the actual licence says.
```

#### ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>

```
Another alternative is to purchase (if possible) the NetExpress v3.0 (not v3.1)
of NetExpress.  The v3.0 base RTS is free to ship with any created executables,
per the Merant licensing agreement for that version.  With v3.1, Merant
introduced the new licensing scheme ...

Hope it Helps ...

TL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T11:06:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sAva7.211751$Q9.53207194@news1.elmhst1.il.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com> <ylJ97.203027$Q9.50657864@news1.elmhst1.il.home.com> <3B689A7A.DC60C203@flashmail.com>`

```
Then they are lying!  :-)  My copy of the license agreement (license.txt) file
that shipped with v3.0 tells me (in writing) that I am allowed to ship the base
RTS (not the server components) free of charge!  I am bound to the licensing
agreement for v3.0, as long as I never upgrade to v3.1.  I'm a software vendor
myself, and there is no way that they can legally retro-enact a pricing scheme
like that just because of a version upgrade.

The question is, will they sell you a v3.0 license?  Probably not ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T15:07:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kf0c6$t3u$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com> <ylJ97.203027$Q9.50657864@news1.elmhst1.il.home.com> <3B689A7A.DC60C203@flashmail.com> <sAva7.211751$Q9.53207194@news1.elmhst1.il.home.com>`

```
I cannot tell what is "truth" and what is "legal interpretation" but the
following is the information that I received from one of my MERANT (Micro
Focus) sources,

"As to the question regarding the Net Express Application Server license
charges, technically the charges were part of Net Express v3.0 and v3.1.
The difference that v3.0 only charged them, if I can remember correctly,
when applications were being sold using the product.  In other words, if the
application created were being used within your company, then the
Application Server license were free.  However, if they were being sold,
then the seller was supposed to have paid the Application Server license
fees for those they were distributing.  This was not very clearly spelled
out in the license or elsewhere, so it appears.  With the release of Net
Express v3.1, it was decided to just make all of the Application Server
licenses chargeable.  However, there was  some level of amnesty provided
below certain usage levels (50 users comes to mind, but not having been that
close to Net Express at the time, I haven't committed all of that to
memory).  I believe the amnesty was strictly for those customers upgrading
from Net Express v3.0 to v3.1.  Obviously, Sales could answer that question
better than I, as they likely deal with the issue on a regular basis."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Making stand-alone programs with Net Express to use on other PCs

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-04T13:38:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LVSa7.17127$ar1.62421@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3B64A432.7ADEE510@flashmail.com> <ylJ97.203027$Q9.50657864@news1.elmhst1.il.home.com> <3B689A7A.DC60C203@flashmail.com> <sAva7.211751$Q9.53207194@news1.elmhst1.il.home.com> <9kf0c6$t3u$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
This all matches my recollection - except one thing.

This references "Application server" which is not the same thing as the regular
"runtime" as far as I can see.  Applicaiton Server is a separately purchased
runtime that you run on a server - like a web server running CGI applications.
How does this relate to the regular "runtime"?
```

```
Warren,
  Have you ever read the (Fujitsu / Artur Reimann's) paper on OO COBOL?  I
think it *might* help you to understand what this "paradigm" is all about.
There is a PDF version on the web at:

 http://www.adtools.com/info/whitepaper/oocoby2k.pdf

It is now a LITTLE out of date as far as some of the "details" of what is in
the draft of the next Standard, but I still think it is an EXCELLENT
starting out point for anyone trying to get a hold on what OO COBOL is "all
about".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Writing out packed decimal data on os/390 and importing into as/400

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-01T17:37:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B683E93.6F15C158@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<9f163bd3.0107261251.2184d5c0@posting.google.com> <FMZ87.10686$ar1.32465@www.newsranger.com> <7yV97.13934$ar1.45421@www.newsranger.com>`

```
>
> >In article <9f163bd3.0107261251.2184d5c0@posting.google.com>, Scooter DNeedle
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

In 1979, I worked in a system that had just moved from IBM DOS to IBM OS.  Because
IBM changed the way they stored unsigned numbers, we needed to make sure old data
compared correctly with new data.   I'm not sure of my memory, but we either moved a
signed number to an unsigned and back, or what makes more sense, we moved an
unsigned number to a signed and back.

You might want to carry your sign separately, and even skip passing data as packed
altogether.
```

#### ↳ Re: Writing out packed decimal data on os/390 and importing into as/400

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>
- **Date:** 2001-08-04T23:05:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lc%a7.148$eU4.841@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<9f163bd3.0107261251.2184d5c0@posting.google.com>`

```
I've never worked with AS/400, but mainframes usually have compiler
options to control how picky to be about the packed decimal format, maybe
the 400 has something similar, then you can just change the compiler
option and not have to change the program or the data either one. Be sure
to check for unwanted side effects though.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE CORRESPONDING - Was Re: R: Convert RmCobol 85 to Text File

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-08-02T00:02:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B6897F4.DE32C2FB@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3b53a7e9$1@newsgate.hknet.com> <9j0lmv$7hv$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it> <m4r7ltgmu1rcd0tndapols0pqut2dudavj@4ax.com> <2s_47.22490$Kf3.292704@www.newsranger.com> <3B5482F7.17FC53AF@home.com> <3b555404_1@news2.newsgroups.com> <9j4cd7$cvv$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b561f38_2@news2.newsgroups.com> <3B5EC2A9.279DC7A6@cusys.edu>`

```
MOVE (ADD/SUBTRACT) CORRESPONDING would be a lot safer if there were
either optional compiler diagnostics noting which fields didn't pair up
or optional expansions of the CORRESPONDING to show the individual field
operations.  The other complaint about qualification would not be as
strong if COBOL implemented FILE-NAME.RECORD-NAME.GROUP-NAME.FIELD-NAME
as an alternative to FIELD-NAME OF GROUP-NAME OF RECORD-NAME OF
FILE-NAME. The lack of this alternative has infuriated me for over 30
years.  Lack of a consise and straight forward (to an English speaking
person) means of qualification has lead to not having as many copies of
a record description as need in a single program.  I look forward to at
least getting COPY ... REPLACING LEADING ...  BY ... .

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> figure out what you were trying to communicate.
```

```
Thane,

I believe the whole point about the "dot" reference model (as used in Java
and C) is that there is no longer ANY NEED for qualification.

Reference modification would be handled simply like this...

instead of :  my-field of my-record (1:20)
you would code: my-record.my-field (1:20)

It is simple and clear. But it isn't "English-like" <G>.

Pete.
```

```
James,

you misunderstood my post.

There would be no need for COBOL qualification using the "of" or "in"
qualifiers.

I agree that what I wrote was ambiguous, but you don't honestly think, after
writing this language since 1965, I don't recognise what qualification is,
now do you <G>?

Pete.
```

## Thread: MicroFocus COBOL in Windows Millenium problem — 1 this month / 3 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [2001-07](2001-07.md#t-a1e8ff98dc)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MicroFocus COBOL in Windows Millenium problem

- **From:** Roblec <roblec@rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-02T03:14:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B68C568.3EE32BE3@rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3B658243.A84AAFBD@vienna.com.pl> <3B659332.5D406AD@home.com>`

```
Three ADIS object files (adis.obj adisinit.obj adiskey.obj) must be
linked into the executable, or they may be linked together in a
standalone ADIS.EXE in the current directory or found in the DOS PATH. 
Also the file ADISCTRL must be in the current directory or in the
directory specified in the COBDIR variable.

> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: Error Message — 15 msgs

### Error Message

- **From:** jc_va@hotmail.com (Buck Turgidson)
- **Date:** 2001-08-02T07:53:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com>`

```
I am supporting some COBOL, and am by no means an expert.  I am
getting a compile error on the following code.  Does anyone recognize
it?
 
I'd be grateful.


                       IF PREMIUM-BASE-NO
                           MOVE PREMIUM-BASE-AS-OF-DT OF W-WK
                                   TO  AS-OF-DT OF W-WK
                                   
                           IF BENEFIT-PROGRAM OF CHECK (1:1)  NUMERIC
                           AND BENEFIT-PROGRAM OF CHECK  NOT =  '4XX'
                                                                     
                               IF BENEFIT-PROG-EFFDT OF CHECK        
                                       >  AS-OF-DT OF W-WK           
                                                                     
                                   MOVE BENEFIT-PROG-EFFDT OF CHECK  
                                           TO  AS-OF-DT OF W-WK      
                               END-IF                                
                           END-IF                                    
                                   
                           PERFORM TB000-CHECK-JOB
                           IF USE-JOB-YES

                               PERFORM TJ100-SET-ANNUAL
                               ADD ANNUAL-RT OF W-COVRG
                                       TO  PREMIUM-BASE-SUM OF W-WK
                               SET PREMIUM-BASE-YES  TO  TRUE
                           END-IF
                       END-IF





 4333                     IF BENEFIT-PROGRAM OF CHECK(1:1)  NUMERIC
* 313-S**************************************************             
(   0)**
**    Too many subscripts
  4355         ELSE
* 562-S************                                                   
(   1)**
**    An "ELSE" phrase did not have a matching IF and was discarded.
  4383                     IF BENEFIT-PROGRAM OF CHECK(1:1)  NUMERIC 
* 313-S**************************************************             
(   1)**
**    Too many subscripts
  4408             END-IF
* 564-S******************                                             
(   1)**
**    A scope-delimiter did not have a matching verb and was
discarded.
  4409         END-IF
* 564-S**************                                                 
(   1)**
**    A scope-delimiter did not have a matching verb and was
discarded.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error Message

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-02T16:32:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kbrp9$tg2$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com>`

```
The code looks ok, but I think the Micro Focus compiler might produce this
message when this reference modification symtax is used when the OSVS
language directive is in effect.  The Mainframe OS/VS compiler did not
support reference modification (a feature introduced in ANS 1985 COBOL), so
the error is legitimate when the MF compiler is being used to emulate OS/VS
behaviour.

"Buck Turgidson" <jc_va@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com...
> I am supporting some COBOL, and am by no means an expert.  I am
> getting a compile error on the following code.  Does anyone recognize
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
> discarded.
```

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>

- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Buck,

It's hard to tell without the data description of the CHECK record. Look to see
if your ref modified data item is part of a table where a subscript or index is
required. If so put the ss# immediately before the (1:1) followed by a space. If
you can't tell, send a copy of the CHECK rec.

Regards, Jack.
```

#### ↳ Re: Error Message

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-02T14:23:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108021323.11b9dfb7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com>`

```
jc_va@hotmail.com (Buck Turgidson) wrote in message news:<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com>...
> I am supporting some COBOL, and am by no means an expert.  I am
> getting a compile error on the following code.  Does anyone recognize
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
> discarded.

*----------------------

Your syntax IS correct, as far as the standard goes.  You MAY have an
old compiler, which does not allow reference modification.   You get
the other error messages because the compiler ignores the entire IF
statement containing the "error".  On the other hand, I've seen this
error before where it SEEMED it should not be. Could you show us your
DATA DIVISION entry for CHECK? Start with the 01 level that contains
it.

Stephen J Spiro
```

##### ↳ ↳ RE: Error Message

- **From:** "Dr. Carlitorr" <l_l@NOSPAM.ole.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T06:51:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wRra7.192656$vV5.6510638@telenews.teleline.es>`
- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com> <20010802193049.05130.00001744@nso-cs.aol.com>`

```
The correct one is: BENEFIT-PROGRAM OF CHECK(1:1)
COBOL consider "field of xxxx" as a field name in its whole.
But because of the error "too many subcripts" it seems that the compiler
doesn't recognise reference modification. Redefine the field to have the
first byte separate and change the code. Or move to another field to
separate the first byte.

f5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> escribi� en el mensaje de noticias
20010802193049.05130.00001744@nso-cs.aol.com...

jc_va@hotmail.com
> (Buck Turgidson) writes:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Might the answer be that the relative indexing should be on the actual
field?
>
>        BENEFIT-PROGRAM(1:1) OF CHECK
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: RE: Error Message

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-04T18:44:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XnXa7.34$Qa3.56@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com> <20010802193049.05130.00001744@nso-cs.aol.com> <wRra7.192656$vV5.6510638@telenews.teleline.es>`

```
The mnemonic I use to remember where ref mod should appear in the coding
sequence when using subscripts, indexes, and/or 
qualifiers is:

If the feature was chronologically introduced to the language BEFORE ref mod, it
should appear BEFORE ref mod in the coding
sequence.    

HTH, Jack.
```

```
... NUMERIC test.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services Inc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error Message

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-08-07T02:09:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-WQUBn4wD6XBI@thishost>`
- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com> <20010802193049.05130.00001744@nso-cs.aol.com> <K_Sa7.17130$ar1.62341@www.newsranger.com> <3B6F3EBC.F4F59643@istar.ca>`

```
I thnk the problem is that the reference modification has to follow 
the field name, not the qualifier name.

I use a "if numeric" test on reference modified fields in both IBM 
Visualage COBOL and Merant NetExpress and the test works (I don't know
about other compilers though)

Lorne 

On Tue, 7 Aug 2001 01:06:44, "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
wrote:

> My guess is that you can't use reference modification when doing an IF
> .... NUMERIC test.
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error Message

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T23:04:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9knpd1$hj6$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com> <20010802193049.05130.00001744@nso-cs.aol.com> <K_Sa7.17130$ar1.62341@www.newsranger.com> <3B6F3EBC.F4F59643@istar.ca> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-WQUBn4wD6XBI@thishost>`

```
As stated elsewhere (in this thread), this simply is not correct.

ANY ANSI '85 conforming compiler should accept (and require when in
"conforming mode") that the reference modification occur AFTER the
qualification - not after the "1st" data-name.

As I have said (and as someone else originally pointed out), the Micro Focus
compilers for MANY years have provided "IBM OS/VS COBOL" emulation mode.  As
there are some "syntax" issues with the way that this '74 (not '85) Standard
compiler works with parenthesis and  combined abbreviated conditional
expression, it does NOT allow reference modification (which was an addition
in the '85 Standard) in a conditional expression when the OSVS dialect is
selected.

It does fully support reference modification when NOOSVS is specified or
when any of the (various) '85 Standard "emulation" modes are in effect.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Error Message

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-08-07T07:14:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-IwxBsMvpLUqH@thishost>`
- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com> <20010802193049.05130.00001744@nso-cs.aol.com> <K_Sa7.17130$ar1.62341@www.newsranger.com> <3B6F3EBC.F4F59643@istar.ca> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-WQUBn4wD6XBI@thishost> <9knpd1$hj6$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I have to admit I was in error on the location of the reference 
modification. I did a little program (compiled with IBM VACOBOL ) and 
found that the reference modification MUST follow the qualifier, not 
the data-name. Apoologies for mis-leading anyone.

"The compiler is always right - the programmer is usually mistaken" 
:-)

The numeric test is valid when used on a reference modified field (at 
least the IBM VACOBOL)

Lorne

On Tue, 7 Aug 2001 04:04:14, "William M. Klein" 
<wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> As stated elsewhere (in this thread), this simply is not correct.
> 
…[66 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Compiler options and Reference Modification was Re: Error Message

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T09:52:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B710B88.53424277@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com> <20010802193049.05130.00001744@nso-cs.aol.com> <K_Sa7.17130$ar1.62341@www.newsranger.com> <3B6F3EBC.F4F59643@istar.ca> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-WQUBn4wD6XBI@thishost> <9knpd1$hj6$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Having been bitten by that nasty where reference modification is legal
in MOVE statements but not IF statements if you specify the OSVS option,
I should have thought of the answer.  It was especially hard to find
because the OSVS option is on in one compile environment but off in
another so it took a while for me to check compile options.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, Inc.
```

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T12:32:05+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lb2fj$ro3$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<f98999c8.0108020653.5ee50c5@posting.google.com> <20010802193049.05130.00001744@nso-cs.aol.com> <K_Sa7.17130$ar1.62341@www.newsranger.com> <3B6F3EBC.F4F59643@istar.ca> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-WQUBn4wD6XBI@thishost> <9knpd1$hj6$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I received a private email on this issue, saying that the problem was in
fact that the item in question had an OCCURS clause and therefore required
subscripting/indexing.  Apparently a mix-up between different versions of
the application.
```

```
associated with the positions offered; to do otherwise leads many to
conclude that you are either trolling for resumes or running a blind ad to
determine rates.

tassi, connessa con le posizioni offerte; fare al contrario conduce molti
concludere che trolling per i resumes o il funzionamento una pubblicita
cieca per determinare i tassi.
```
