<a id="t-d51e44707b"></a>
## Thread: Programmer Performance Evaluations — 36 msgs

### Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-02T16:38:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Greetings,

I'm probably opening a can of worms here that I really don't want, but I'll
do it anyway.

I'd like to get some feedback from everyone on what criteria you use to
judge how effective, efficient and work oriented a programmer is.  What do
you judge productivity on?  Is there some qualitative method you use to
compare one programmer to another?

Old straws like number of lines of code don't seem to be effective.
Differences in coding techniques often render that comparison ineffective.
Bug rates also seem to be somewhat arbitrary.  For instance, when code is
being developed, you tend to expect bugs.  Gross targets, like number of
programs written, fail to take into account complexity of the programs
themselves.

I've done some scanning in the trades for possible metrics to use and I
haven't made much progress.  Any input from others "in the trenches" would
be greatly appreciated.

For background, I'm a Systems Manager in a COBOL/CICS/VSAM shop.  We do both
production and maintenance work.  I have 3 teams under me, with a total of
15 programmers at present.

Thanks,

Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T14:31:03+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Paul,

I wouldn't normally respond to a post like this  because, as you said, it is
a can of worms <G>.

However, you were frank and stated your position and I believe you are
sincere.

Here's what works for me....obviously, there are no hard and fast rules and
individuals are the most important thing, but the following generalisations
have been arrived at over a 37 year period, of which at least 15 years have
been managing (and, more importantly, as a manager, working with...) other
people...

1. Forget metrics. Management is more Art than Science.

You are already finding that indicators like lines of code mean diddley...
(I also have severe reservations about Function Points (when used to measure
performance; OK for assessing predicted workload, but even then, don't take
it too seriously. There is NO substitute for experience and
understanding...). Most of the supposed metrics that I have encountered have
a more negative effect on morale and performance than they are worth. There
is little to be gained by measuring the performance of a team which is
already de-motivated by knowing that they are not trusted and will be
subject to scrutiny and bean counting. Besides, they will probably spend
more of their time filling in activity logs than you can really afford...<G>

The ONLY criterion on which I measure performance (my own and other
people's) is: "Was the desired result achieved?"

If it was, was it achieved in the desired timeframe and cost constraints?
Did it "hurt"? (We have all worked on "painful" projects). How could the
"hurt" be alleviated in the future?

2. So, if we are not going to measure anything, how DO we assess
performance?

1. When allocating tasks to people sit down with them 1-on-1 and go through
the job in hand. Ask THEM to tell you how long it will take to deliver. If
their answer is unacceptable, don't erupt and start screaming "incompetence"
<G>, simply ask for a justification. Be open, honest, and fair. If they are
"sandbagging" tell them you will get someone else to do it, (or do it
yourself) and find another task for them.

(This works extremely well... I had a case once where a prima donna
estimated a certain job at 10 days because he wanted to "coast" it and I was
a new Manager who he hadn't met before, so he made the mistake of assuming I
was just like all the rest... I expressed surprise at his estimate. He then
asked me how long I thought it would take. I said: "around 6 days for a
reasonably experienced programmer; a man of your calibre could do it in
4...." He then challenged me to find ANYONE who could do it in 4 days, so I
did it myself. Never had a further problem with that particular individual.
And the others looked at me in a different way also...<G>.  (Of course I
worked 12 hours a day on it, but they didn't know that <G>, and it was worth
it for a few days to establish credibility...)

If they continually "pad" estimates, consider firing them. It is a betrayal
of trust.

If they genuinely will take longer than you think is fair, factor in their
experience and training and cut them some slack. Tell them you are doing
this, but don't do it for more than three months. If they need training, see
that they get it. (Provide it yourself if you have to...a major
responsibility for you as a Manager is to help your team to develop.)

Once estimates are agreed with individuals, do everything you can to ensure
they succeed. Support them and help them. Make sure they get the resources
and tools required to do the job.

Now it is possible to measure performance. If they continually achieve the
targets they have set, and you have agreed with them, EVERYBODY is happy,
and you can argue that your team are performing well.

If they don't, (and, if there are any, it will always be one or two
individuals that don't...), have a little private chat with them expressing
your concern, find out if there are other factors affecting their
performance and again, cut them some slack, but only on the understanding
that their performance improves.

If it doesn't, fire them or move them. If you can't do either, make it clear
to your own Management that there is a problem and you are now carrying dead
wood which will debilitate the whole project and put it at risk.

(In all the years I've been doing this stuff, I had ONE occasion where this
was necessary and, fortunately, I was empowered to fire the persons
concerned. I didn't want to do it and I took far too long over it. My Boss
advised me to do it (but wouldn't force me) and I believed I could "manage"
the situation. He was right and I should've done it much sooner.

The lesson learned is: If you find people with incorrigible attitudes for
their own twisted reasons, get rid of them at the start or after one
straightforward and blunt discussion. It isn't personal. It just debilitates
a team to know that one or more members are covertly hostile and "swinging
the lead", while everybody else is motivated and trying to achieve their own
and the project's goals.)

3. PLAN your projects, but avoid TASK oriented planning. GOAL oriented
planning works much better. So your MS Project plan should state GOALS and
their expected achievement dates, not TASKS and how long they should take.
(Individual programmers will be assigned tasks, as described above, but
these should always be in the context of achieving a certain goal. Stress
the goal, NOT the task...) When planning, assume 6 hours actual work from
each individual per day... If you feel comfortable assessing tasks in hours.

4.Try and avoid using the Waterfall. If your Company implements it as a
Management Policy, do EVERYTHING you can to get that policy changed.  RAD
type Management, based on User involvement, Timeboxing, and iteration
towards agreed prioritised goals, is INFINITELY more successful.

I am presenting on this at CW2001. Try and attend if you can. I am currently
preparing a lot of "give-away" material on this subject that is designed to
help Project Managers. Sorry if this sounds like Evangelism, but I have
tried it both ways...RAD and the Waterfall. There is no comparison. And
COBOL projects CAN be RAD managed with or without so-called "RAD Tools".

The whole idea of metrics is connected back to the Waterfall. You can see
from the discussion above how the overall GOALS are MUCH more important than
the individual tasks. Instead of wasting time filling in metrics forms, get
your teams motivated to achieve the project goals. Then turn them loose and
they will spend their time working towards the goals, rather than worrying
about the performance statistics.

4. Make a point of chatting each day to individuals for a few minutes about
what they are doing, and listen to them. Ask them if they feel they will
achieve the goal they are working to. If not, find out what went wrong and
help them fix it. It is incredible how the whole atmosphere of working
changes on a team that is succeeding. Work actually becomes fun, problems
are a temporary diversion like a crossword puzzle that provides satisfaction
when you solve it.

Promote an atmosphere where it is OK not to know something, provided you ask
about it. Positively reinforce "right" action and attitude.

And take them to lunch when they achieve milestones. <G> (Seriously, it is
very important to show them that you really do value what they are doing. I
have personally paid for team lunches in Companies where it wasn't the
"policy" to do so. The amount spent is well worth it.)

SUMMARISING:

Back to your original request...

> I'd like to get some feedback from everyone on what criteria you use to
> judge how effective, efficient and work oriented a programmer is.

First, "effective and efficient"...
Do they deliver on time and on budget? Are your time constraints realistic?

Remember that overstressed teams are a false economy. Pressure can be
applied for brief periods only, and when it is, make sure that you are the
last to leave at night...If you have to pull an "all nighter" (and I don't
recommend it) do NOT coerce them into it, encourage them. Provide pizza in
the office, or a meal break. Don't let ANYONE work after 1:00am. The fatigue
induced errors cost more than sleeping on it and working a few extra hours
for the next few days. Besides, if you plan it properly and monitor progress
in an informal way, you shouldn't need to pull all nighters...

"work oriented"
(Personally, I don't care whether they are "work oriented" or not...I've
worked with people who were fanatical surfers or skiers and saw "work" as
simply a means to this end... No problem. They deliver in the Workplace,
they get to indulge their passion. And, as a manager, I now have something I
can motivate them with...."I'll pick up your ski pass next weekend, if you
get that thing running by Friday". (Beats paying overtime...<G>)

>What do
> you judge productivity on?

Results.

And how much of an asset to the Team as a whole they are.

If the "best" programmer is an insufferable asshole who treats his/her
colleagues with disdain, work on some attitude adjustment. Until you can
achieve that, keep them fairly isolated. If it looks like a lost cause, get
rid...Good work that causes other people's work to suffer is NOT good
work...

On the other hand, a junior colleague who is eager to learn and enthusiastic
and listens to the more senior people, can be a huge asset, even if they are
not technically as productive (yet) as their peers. I don't like "levels"
within a team and I abhor the "us and them" mentality of "management" and
"workers". Everyone in the team is valuable or else they wouldn't be there.
In brainstorming sessions and walkthroughs, EVERYONE has an equal right to
be heard and their opinions and ideas considered and weighed. It is WHAT's
right, NOT WHO's right...

>Is there some qualitative method you use to
> compare one programmer to another?

Why would you want to? They all (including yourself) have strengths and
weaknesses. That's why we build teams.

If there are budget cuts and someone HAS "to go" (and I am assuming that
this is inevitable and you have fought it tooth and nail <G>), your
judgement call should be based on the saving that must be made,and not on
the individuals in the team.

Obviously, most of the above is simply opinion. But I have tried to address
the issues you raised, within the scope of a very limited post.

It is refreshing to see Managers who are prepared to actually ask these
questions. I wish you every success.

Pete.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T14:43:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B6AB8C7.F4F684E6@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
To me, the hardest thing to evaluate is when different workers have different
levels of success - when the hardest jobs go to the most capable workers!

You have a real mess of a system - even the best programmer has a hard time with
it.  His work is slow, the users see troubles with this system.  Meanwhile,
another system runs nicely with the programer there getting productive work
done.

But if you switch the programmers around, the first project falls apart
completely, and the second project is more efficient, but who cares?

I suppose the problem needs to be addressed before evaluation time.  Make sure
that the programmer with the tough system does some other work which would allow
you a better comparison point.

It's tough when the standards have you use a lot of metrics - and you want to
discount the troubles so that the programmers get rewarded on their contribution
rather than the difficulty of their assignments.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T20:57:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mfEa7.1245$1p1.89818@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com> <3B6AB8C7.F4F684E6@cusys.edu>`

```
My boss once asked me to visit a site to check their status on the part of a
corporate project they were doing, then visit with the manager of the
corporate site working on the project.

First site was done, programs in the vault, metrics all over the place.
Apparently, this staff had always done things that way.

Second site, manager had no idea of the status of anything. Shrugged his
shoulders.

What happened?  I was never told.  However the manager never got a more
responsible job.
I don't believe in the metrics method, but some job tracking must be done to
keep everyone on the same page, and support what ever measures have to be
taken.

Pert methods were used a lot.

Warren Simmons

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>

Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com...
> Paul,
>

is
> a can of worms <G>.
>

    <snip>


>
> >Is there some qualitative method you use to
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Obviously, most of the above is simply opinion. But I have tried to
address
> the issues you raised, within the scope of a very limited post.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree with your statement that, yes, we are all different.  My quest is to
get a feel for others by comparing them to myself.  I have no desire to be
immodest, but I am a good COBOL programmer.  You do something for nigh 20
years, and eventually you get good at it.  Again, not to be immodest, I feel
that I may be significantly better at programming than many others (again,
only in COBOL).  I get worried sometimes when I attempt to apply a
timeframe, that I know I could hit, on someone else who may not be as
technically adept.  Granted, skill levels change (hopefully improving with
time).  Hence the need for comparison.

I'm probably chasing a mirage here, but it's something that has come back to
me a number of times over the last 5 or so years.  I haven't come up with
anything, so I throw myself upon the mercy of those who may have more
experience <G>!

Thanks for your insight.  I've already printed it off and will be studying
your suggestions further.

Paul

ineffective.

is

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > haven't made much progress.  Any input from others "in the trenches"
would

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

```
I can't see the post you made about the "orchestra" which was quoted by some
others in this thread. Apparently the Newsfeed server is not echoing it.
This is a frequent problem with CLC.

Anyway, I'll respond to what I CAN see...<G>

Comments below...

Pete.

"Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:9qAa7.31664$gj1.2988477@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> Thanks Peter for your input.  I find it very helpful.  I'm going to snip
> most of it, but I did have one item I wanted to respond back to you on.
> It's...
>

>
> I agree with your statement that, yes, we are all different.  My quest is
to
> get a feel for others by comparing them to myself.  I have no desire to be
> immodest, but I am a good COBOL programmer.  You do something for nigh 20
> years, and eventually you get good at it.  Again, not to be immodest, I
feel
> that I may be significantly better at programming than many others (again,
> only in COBOL).  I get worried sometimes when I attempt to apply a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Understand totally what you are saying and we all go through this. It is
very tempting to think "Well, I can do this in 2 days, why is he taking 4?".
The fact is that people with less experience than yourself WILL take longer.
That's part of why you are there. To help, encourage, and transfer some of
your own skills to them so they can grow. (the nice thing is that when you
do this, it is good for your own growth also...<G>)

I also understand  (and share) your concern about imposing timeframes (even
realistic ones) on others. That was why I came up with the strategy of
letting them set the timeframe. (Within reason, of course...and subject to
negotiation. I covered this in previous post.) Think about it from your own
point of view. What is the initial reaction when a manager says:"I want this
by tomorrow. Do it now." ?

There is a reflected difference in attitude between the shop where the
Manager says: "I have allocated you 5 days to do this."

And the one where the Manager says: "Let's have a look at this task and see
if we can come up with a realistic estimate, together."

The first relects "Us and Them". The second reflects a Team, where the
Manager is there to co-ordinate the effort and help and support the people
on the shop floor.

Guess which one achieves the best results and has the lesser staff turnover?

I remember  a Manager doing something like this to me once. As the Senior
programmer at the time, he figured it was OK to drop something on me and
demand a result within a timeframe that was simply stupid. I grinned at him
and said: "Well, I hope you find someone who can do it. I can't." He was
outraged, but eventually settled down and we ended up with a tight but
achievable timeframe.

The point is, Paul, that if you create the right team spirit and attitude,
people WON'T sandbag their estimates. They will give you the time it will
honestly take.  In fact, as things improve, you will find individuals trying
to better their own last estimate and, in effect, competing with themselves.
(I know this is true because I've worked this way in the real world and
found it to be so. There is no Academic theory behind it; it just works.
PROVIDED you treat them fairly and honestly and reward extra effort.) As
they succeed, their confidence grows and this leads to more success...and so
on.
That's why, as a Manager, you have a vested interest in making sure they
succeed...<G>

You trust them, but use your own judgement and experience to see how close
they are to your own estimate. I think this is what you mean when you say
"comparing " them to yourself. Nothing is really achieved by comparing
people against each other or yourself. All that happens is that some have
their confidence shaken and others have their ego boosted. Neither of these
results really help your project.


> I'm probably chasing a mirage here, but it's something that has come back
to
> me a number of times over the last 5 or so years.  I haven't come up with
> anything, so I throw myself upon the mercy of those who may have more
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Glad it is of help. I have been fortunate to work with some very enlightened
Managers when I was a Programmer, and some fantastic teams since I have been
a "Manager". I have seen what does and doesn't work.

My experience has been that metrics are, at the very least, of dubious value
and managers who put faith in them are asking for disaster. At the worst,
they are a thoroughly de-motivating and counter-productive imposition on a
project team. There are enough problems in developing and implementing
commercial computer systems without having Big Brother breathing down your
neck at the same time. The only Metric that is worth anything is the Result.

I'm sure you've seen at least one system where programmers fill in the
"status" of various tasks, "Coded" "debugging" "in test" ...all tied back to
the Waterfall. In my naivete I even tried automating this once when I was a
Senior Programmer, tying a status update into Job Submission, counting the
number of compiles and links and tests...honestly thinking that if people
didn't have to do the paperwork they wouldn't object to being measured...(I
was much younger at the time and trying, as always, to do my best...<G> We
dropped it after a few months...)

Got loads of information, but none of it actually helped us achieve the goal
of completing a system. All we could say was that we knew what was going on,
but that  knowledge didn't help us in any way to achieve the end result any
quicker or better.

If one guy takes 10 compiles on average to get something running and another
takes 4, but the one who takes 4 spends days drawing flowcharts and desk
debugging, so they both come out with 4 days at the end, where is the value
in the metric? The only meaningful information is that it takes them 4 days
to get a program clean...the RESULT. (This kind of measurement is even less
use if you are using PCs for development; it doesn't cost any more no matter
how many compiles, links, tests, the guys use...yet we still see mainframe
sites obsessed with this 1970s bullshit...) You could compare the code
produced and decide if one is more maintainable than the other, but that is
a subjective judgement, not something you can measure. Still, as the
Manager, you have the right to make this call and take action if seriously
unmaintainable code is being produced.

I think the reason metrics don't work is because we are dealing with people.
People are notoriously random variables...<G> But when you get them
motivated they can achieve fantastic things...

Regards,

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-04T13:34:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<DRSa7.17122$ar1.62405@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9qAa7.31664$gj1.2988477@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3b6b4a84_7@news.newsgroups.com>`

```
I worked in a shop once with several personalities and styles.

We had one guy who was slower than me, but who you could COUNT on to have bug
free code when it was deployed.  In an "Emergency" situation, where time was
critical and things were burning all around - he was not of much use.  He didn't
handle the urgency well.

Then we had another guy who was GREAT at maintenance.  He could find the right
place to make a change and get it all working very well.  However, he could not
handle anything NEW - from scratch.

Then there's me.  I'm good in a crisis.  I can fix things on fire.  I'm good at
design of new things and I'm a fair maintenance programmer.  My weakness?
Whatever it is, ain't gonna work the first time.  It's gonna have a minor bug or
some small (used to be large! but I'm working on this weakness) problem.  The
upside - I'm getting better and I'm fast.  

Each of these types of programmers have different strenghts and weaknesses.
Different tasks should be assigned to these programmers, and evaluations based
on how well they handle the tasks.
```

Actually Thane, you've described
1) a very smart programmer/analyst (competent programers fix others
    bugs, SMART programers aoid creating them)
2) a straight detailed coder (NOT a programmer analyst)
3) a systems designer working part-time as a programmer

Say what you want (scholars) you can train a dufus to be a 'detailed coder'
System Design is IMHO truly ART.  Its where imagination and experience
meld to solve seemingly complex or nigh on to impossible problems
with Elegant (that means SIMPLE and CLEAR) solutions.

Excellent point about scheduling/planning goals rather than tasks. I'll
have to remember that one.

I agree strongly with the 6 hours/day idea, in fact for a long time I've
felt that to be realistic, scheduling should be planned on 30 hour weeks
rather than 40-60 as many places seem to do. Also perhaps tasks should be
broken down to the point of small tasks being no more than 30 hours in
size. A 30 hour week or task can all too easily expand to 40-50 hours as
it is.
```

```
In article <UZ_a7.172$M3.28505@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>,
Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message> wrote:

[snip]

>I agree strongly with the 6 hours/day idea, in fact for a long time I've
>felt that to be realistic, scheduling should be planned on 30 hour weeks
>rather than 40-60 as many places seem to do.

Leaving aside the fact that most organisations schedule for 40 hrs worth
of *work* per week, thereby completely ignoring things like meetings and
status-reports...

... my memory, admittedly porous, has it that the BLS (the American
Federal Bureau of Labor Statistics)... or maybe it was the DoL (Department
of Labor) advocated adding 25% - or two hours out of an eight-hour day,
ten hours out of a forty-hour week - to any project-estimate for time lost
to FAD - Fatigue, Absenteeism and Delay.

The few times I've had to put together project timelines I've made
sure to include a separate line-entry to account for this... and there's
*always* been someone who has squawked 'This estimate is *ridiculous*...
you can get this job done in four months, not five!  All ya gotta do is
cut out this FAD stuff you have at the bottom...'

DD
```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message

and
> individuals are the most important thing, but the following
generalisations
> have been arrived at over a 37 year period, of which at least 15 years
have
> been managing (and, more importantly, as a manager, working with...) other
> people...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (I also have severe reservations about Function Points (when used to
measure
> performance; OK for assessing predicted workload, but even then, don't
take
> it too seriously. There is NO substitute for experience and

have
> a more negative effect on morale and performance than they are worth.
There
> is little to be gained by measuring the performance of a team which is
> already de-motivated by knowing that they are not trusted and will be
> subject to scrutiny and bean counting. Besides, they will probably spend
> more of their time filling in activity logs than you can really
afford...<G>
>
> The ONLY criterion on which I measure performance (my own and other
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1. When allocating tasks to people sit down with them 1-on-1 and go
through
> the job in hand. Ask THEM to tell you how long it will take to deliver. If
> their answer is unacceptable, don't erupt and start screaming
"incompetence"
> <G>, simply ask for a justification. Be open, honest, and fair. If they
are
> "sandbagging" tell them you will get someone else to do it, (or do it
> yourself) and find another task for them.
>
> (This works extremely well... I had a case once where a prima donna
> estimated a certain job at 10 days because he wanted to "coast" it and I
was
> a new Manager who he hadn't met before, so he made the mistake of assuming
I
> was just like all the rest... I expressed surprise at his estimate. He
then
> asked me how long I thought it would take. I said: "around 6 days for a
> reasonably experienced programmer; a man of your calibre could do it in
> 4...." He then challenged me to find ANYONE who could do it in 4 days, so
I
> did it myself. Never had a further problem with that particular
individual.
> And the others looked at me in a different way also...<G>.  (Of course I
> worked 12 hours a day on it, but they didn't know that <G>, and it was
worth
> it for a few days to establish credibility...)
>
> If they continually "pad" estimates, consider firing them. It is a
betrayal
> of trust.
>
> If they genuinely will take longer than you think is fair, factor in their
> experience and training and cut them some slack. Tell them you are doing
> this, but don't do it for more than three months. If they need training,
see
> that they get it. (Provide it yourself if you have to...a major
> responsibility for you as a Manager is to help your team to develop.)
>
> Once estimates are agreed with individuals, do everything you can to
ensure
> they succeed. Support them and help them. Make sure they get the resources
> and tools required to do the job.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> individuals that don't...), have a little private chat with them
expressing
> your concern, find out if there are other factors affecting their
> performance and again, cut them some slack, but only on the understanding
> that their performance improves.
>
> If it doesn't, fire them or move them. If you can't do either, make it
clear
> to your own Management that there is a problem and you are now carrying
dead
> wood which will debilitate the whole project and put it at risk.
>
> (In all the years I've been doing this stuff, I had ONE occasion where
this
> was necessary and, fortunately, I was empowered to fire the persons
> concerned. I didn't want to do it and I took far too long over it. My Boss
> advised me to do it (but wouldn't force me) and I believed I could
"manage"
> the situation. He was right and I should've done it much sooner.
>
> The lesson learned is: If you find people with incorrigible attitudes for
> their own twisted reasons, get rid of them at the start or after one
> straightforward and blunt discussion. It isn't personal. It just
debilitates
> a team to know that one or more members are covertly hostile and "swinging
> the lead", while everybody else is motivated and trying to achieve their
own
> and the project's goals.)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> each individual per day... If you feel comfortable assessing tasks in

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

currently

to
> help Project Managers. Sorry if this sounds like Evangelism, but I have
> tried it both ways...RAD and the Waterfall. There is no comparison. And
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> from the discussion above how the overall GOALS are MUCH more important
than
> the individual tasks. Instead of wasting time filling in metrics forms,
get
> your teams motivated to achieve the project goals. Then turn them loose
and
> they will spend their time working towards the goals, rather than worrying
> about the performance statistics.
>
> 4. Make a point of chatting each day to individuals for a few minutes
about
> what they are doing, and listen to them. Ask them if they feel they will
> achieve the goal they are working to. If not, find out what went wrong and
> help them fix it. It is incredible how the whole atmosphere of working
> changes on a team that is succeeding. Work actually becomes fun, problems
> are a temporary diversion like a crossword puzzle that provides
satisfaction
> when you solve it.
>
> Promote an atmosphere where it is OK not to know something, provided you
ask
> about it. Positively reinforce "right" action and attitude.
>
> And take them to lunch when they achieve milestones. <G> (Seriously, it is
> very important to show them that you really do value what they are doing.
I
> have personally paid for team lunches in Companies where it wasn't the
> "policy" to do so. The amount spent is well worth it.)

> Do they deliver on time and on budget? Are your time constraints
realistic?

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the office, or a meal break. Don't let ANYONE work after 1:00am. The
fatigue

progress
> in an informal way, you shouldn't need to pull all nighters...

I
> can motivate them with...."I'll pick up your ski pass next weekend, if you
> get that thing running by Friday". (Beats paying overtime...<G>)
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> achieve that, keep them fairly isolated. If it looks like a lost cause,
get
> rid...Good work that causes other people's work to suffer is NOT good
> work...
>
> On the other hand, a junior colleague who is eager to learn and
enthusiastic
> and listens to the more senior people, can be a huge asset, even if they
are
> not technically as productive (yet) as their peers. I don't like "levels"
> within a team and I abhor the "us and them" mentality of "management" and
> "workers". Everyone in the team is valuable or else they wouldn't be
there.
> In brainstorming sessions and walkthroughs, EVERYONE has an equal right to
> be heard and their opinions and ideas considered and weighed. It is WHAT's
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Obviously, most of the above is simply opinion. But I have tried to
address
> the issues you raised, within the scope of a very limited post.
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> > judge how effective, efficient and work oriented a programmer is.  What
do
> > you judge productivity on?  Is there some qualitative method you use to
> > compare one programmer to another?
> >
> > Old straws like number of lines of code don't seem to be effective.
> > Differences in coding techniques often render that comparison
ineffective.
> > Bug rates also seem to be somewhat arbitrary.  For instance, when code
is
> > being developed, you tend to expect bugs.  Gross targets, like number of
> > programs written, fail to take into account complexity of the programs
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > haven't made much progress.  Any input from others "in the trenches"
would
> > be greatly appreciated.
> >
> > For background, I'm a Systems Manager in a COBOL/CICS/VSAM shop.  We do
> both
> > production and maintenance work.  I have 3 teams under me, with a total
of
> > 15 programmers at present.
> >

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-05T15:45:44+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b6cc1b2_5@news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com> <Mh1b7.2555$8J.861418@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```

"James" <mangogrower@telocity.com> wrote in message
news:Mh1b7.2555$8J.861418@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net...
> Peter, are you really in management? <G>

Fair question. (I know you said it tongue in cheek, but I'll answer
anyway...<G>).

The answer is "sometimes".

As a free-lance consultant, I get to provide input to IT decisions and
strategy for various companies. I also get to run seminars on various things
that are dear to my heart (Boolean Algebra and Propositional Calculus for
Programmers, Database design and the Relational Model for Analysts, Project
Management for Project Managers (as you will already have deduced, I am
doing everything I can to get people away form the Waterfall, but RAD can
also be dangerous if it is poorly understood. I usually recommend a pilot
project which will take maybe 6 months and I Manage it myself, ensuring that
the principles are passed on and properly understood and both the business
and the IT involvement is positive and fruitful for all concerned.

Sometimes I work as a contract Programmer (although that is becoming rarer
these days as I get more PM and Consultancy type work.)  On other occasions
I will provide a plan and, if it is approved (they usually are...) then I'll
be given a budget and told to recruit a team and implement it. These are
often the projects which people in the Company won't touch because they are
tehnically or politically too hot...<G>. I love 'em...<G>! The last one I
did was for a major Utility in the English Midlands and it was a case of
getting a team of totally demotivated PL/1 mainframers to work together with
some not too confident VB guys, to utilise both  Client/server and the
Mainframe and produce something that the Company was sadly in need of. It
took nearly 18 months (everyone said it was technically and politically
impossible...) and at the end of it, it was successful but I was
drained...<G> I decided to come back to New Zealand for the Summer and spend
a few months just lying on the beach and re-charging.

I enjoyed it so much I decided to spend the Winter here and work on a couple
of books (one on RAD vs the Waterfall) the other totally IT unrelated...Then
it was Summer again so I stayed and enjoyed that too... Then we had CW2001
coming up in October (it has now been postponed, but will likely happen in
March next year) so I decided to combine presenting there, with returning to
work in Europe. Now I'm starting to see Spring flowers here and thinking
Summer is just round the corner so maybe it will be March before I leave.
Next week I'm off to Melbourne to advise a Software House there on a PC
based ISAM to RDB conversion, and that is the first actual "work" I've
undertaken for over a year...

(In the meantime I've been learning Java, OO COBOL and writing code for a
number of my own projects which had to be pushed to the "back burner"...some
of these are database related tools like DECLGEN  (for PCs) and the latest
conversion aid,  "ISAM2RDB".)

Now, just as I am ready to get back to work, the World goes in to Economic
Decline...<G> Anyway, sometimes what I do is "Management", sometimes it is
"Consultancy", sometimes it is "Programming" sometimes it is "Writing" and
sometimes it is sheer self-indulgence...<G>.

(If anybody reading this needs any of the above services, please contact
me...<G> (Freelances have no shame and will self promote at every
opportunity...<G>))

Pete.



-----= Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

You bring up the idea of replacing the Waterfall system again with a
strictly goals oriented process.  You've mentioned that you are working on a
book yourself, but are there any others you could recommend?  My shop does
the Waterfall thing on work plans, and usually, as far as planning, it's
just guessing.  Switching from Waterfall to Goals sounds like something I'd
really like to consider, and it would also fit well into the programmer
evaluation method we've just been given.

Thanks,

Paul

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>

NA <docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:Vwab7.3211$ym4.121683@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> In article <dP9b7.3016$1p1.272740@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
> Paul Pigott <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would be more than happy to give you some information, if I had anything
worthwhile to provide.  Literally, we had a class on this last Thursday
(8/2).  I'm still in the process of assimilating it.  I say that goals
oriented planning will fit in well with this new methodology because that's
basically what it is.  Everyone being evaluated has a goal given to them.
These goals do NOT have to be programming related, as the same methodology
is used throughout the company.  The manager specifies the goals, utilizing
whatever input is available, and adds new ones throughout the year.  Based
on how well the employee reaches those goals, the evaluation is completed.
I will try to provide more information about this in the weeks and months
ahead, once I've got a better handle on it.

As I say, it sounds intriguing, but it's so different from what I've done
before that I still have some reservations about it, mostly born of
ignorance.

As always, more to come.

Paul
```

Sadly, Paul I really can't recommend any books on the subject. That's why
I've resolved to write one myself.

I'm sending you by private mail a basic paper I wrote on the subject some
time ago. This would be a good "summary" to help you present the case to
your own Management.

I would also recommend that you visit the DSDM website (www.dsdm.org) as
this is a form of RAD which encompasses most of the principles I have
recommended here, including Goal orientation.

I am not a Member of the Organisation but I do endorse the approach.

HTH,

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T02:04:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qcmc7.1745$Ki1.126356@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com> <Mh1b7.2555$8J.861418@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net> <3b6cc1b2_5@news.newsgroups.com> <dP9b7.3016$1p1.272740@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3B6E9717.1DED7E10@cusys.edu>`

```
They aren't mutually exclusive, but, from my perspective, it seems that one
is simpler than the other, at least as we've been doing it.  The Waterfall
process seems to be the way to do it if you want to tightly track where
you're at in a particular project.  But along with that, it requires fairly
constant montoring which takes a great deal of time.

The Goals oriented method, on the other hand, states "This is my
destination".  However the programmer can best get there (within reason) is
the preferred way.  This cuts out a lot of montoring and seems to improve
the lot of management, at least to that degree.  Or it could be seen that it
allows management to be somewhat cavalier in their duties.  Just because
every step in the process isn't being followed closely, doesn't relieve
management's responsibility to stay on top of things.  At best it cuts down
on the administrivia that seems to occupy a larger portion of the day.

Paul

Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:3B6E9717.1DED7E10@cusys.edu...
>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > strictly goals oriented process.  You've mentioned that you are working
on a
> > book yourself, but are there any others you could recommend?  My shop
does
> > the Waterfall thing on work plans, and usually, as far as planning, it's
> > just guessing.  Switching from Waterfall to Goals sounds like something
I'd
> > really like to consider, and it would also fit well into the programmer
> > evaluation method we've just been given.
>
> Why should these be mutually exclusive?
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** ar656@freenet.carleton.ca (Luis Gomez-Almeida)
- **Date:** 2001-08-05T01:52:36-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kimuv$rfc$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`
- **References:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com> <Mh1b7.2555$8J.861418@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
> "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > the job in hand. Ask THEM to tell you how long it will take to deliver.
If
> > their answer is unacceptable, don't erupt and start screaming
> "incompetence"
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > a new Manager who he hadn't met before, so he made the mistake of
assuming
> I
> > was just like all the rest... I expressed surprise at his estimate. He
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > 4...." He then challenged me to find ANYONE who could do it in 4 days,
so
> I
> > did it myself. Never had a further problem with that particular
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Now, if I remember how to do multiplications, 4 * 12 would give 48 hours.
If, as someone was suggesting, that we should estimate days of six hours,
then we are talkling about 8 days of work. Therefore, it was not out of
order to give an estimate of 10 days. It is quite possible that, not knowing
what type of manager Peter was, someone would try to pump up an 8 day work
to 10, since he does not know if the manager will then cut 20% of whatever
he proposed. However, since Peter says that the "prima donna" wanted to
"coast" it, I assume that he is not speculating but talking with some
fundaments. Therefore, it is quite possible that that person was actually
able to do it in 4 days of six hours, since he was really that good, but
with a tendency to taunt and defy management.  I am a bit mistified by the
particulars of this story, where the new manager knows enough about the
programmer to say with confidence that he can do the work in 4 days, but the
programmer knows nothing about the manager and:
1) thinks that he can cheat him, and
2) he is cheated by the manager, and nobody in the shop notices.

I have known cases of people who will only give an honest day of work if
they know that someone is looking over their shoulders. In general, I think
it is better to do without them.

Regards,
Luis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

_(thread depth: 4)_

a
> > > reasonably experienced programmer; a man of your calibre could do it
in
> > > 4...." He then challenged me to find ANYONE who could do it in 4 days,
> so
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > And the others looked at me in a different way also...<G>.  (Of course
I
> > > worked 12 hours a day on it, but they didn't know that <G>, and it was
> > worth
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> then we are talkling about 8 days of work.

The problem I had was that I had to get the background on the problem and
understand  the installation standards, what standard routines were
available, and the "infrastructure" to that particular installation. Someone
who already had this, would not take as long. (I wouldn't have taken so long
if I had had all this at my fingertips...) And I have a feeling it actually
took me 3 days but it was a long time ago and I can't be sure of the exact
figures. I was using an actual experience to illustrate a principle...


> Therefore, it was not out of
> order to give an estimate of 10 days.
It WAS out of order for the person concerned to do so. He was simply trying
it on. He was not used to Managers with technical skill, (the previous ones
had not been very technical and therefore had to take whatever he said. To
be fair, I think that most of the time he gave them reasonable estimates,
but I came along as "the new boy" so he saw an opportunity....After this one
occasion he never did it again and became a very valuable member of the
team...eventually <G>.)

>It is quite possible that, not knowing
> what type of manager Peter was, someone would try to pump up an 8 day work
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> fundaments.

Well, I can't prove it in a Court of Law, but I believe what I said to be
true. I back my judgement in these things and that was my opinion at the
time. (Still is...<G>).

Your point about people padding because they expect the Manager to cut their
estimate is a very valid one. I agree that that does happen. It doesn't on
my projects because they know I won't cut it, and they know I expect them to
be honest, and they also know that if I don't like it, I'll expect an
explanation. But, I will accept their explanation if they make a case and
I'll try and find ways, based on experience, to try and help them achieve
the target we finally agree.

I do NOT expect people to work to schedules that have been imposed upon them
WITHOUT their agreement. (It's simple really...I won't do it myself, so why
would I expect someone else to do it?)  Besides this practice is really
stupid and self defeating when you think about it.  If they know the Manager
will cut their estimate, and the Manager knows they will pad their estimate,
wouldn't it just be easier for all concerned to keep it simple and be honest
and up-front? It implies a lack of trust on both sides and that is fatal. It
introduces "Us and Them" again... I won't tolerate it on my projects and I
wouldn't expect people who work with me to do it, any more than they would
expect me to renege on an agreement or unilaterally impose a deadline on
them.



>Therefore, it is quite possible that that person was actually
> able to do it in 4 days of six hours, since he was really that good, but
> with a tendency to taunt and defy management.  I am a bit mistified by the
> particulars of this story, where the new manager knows enough about the
> programmer to say with confidence that he can do the work in 4 days, but
the
> programmer knows nothing about the manager and:
> 1) thinks that he can cheat him, and
> 2) he is cheated by the manager, and nobody in the shop notices.
>
There's nothing mysterious about it, Luis. At the time this event occurred I
already had 20 years experience as a programmer and knew by looking at
people's past work what their capability was. I made a point of reading a
couple of programs in the source library written by each member of the team,
then backed my judgement and intuition. That's what I'm paid for. If I get
it wrong, I cop it, and accept that responsibility as going with the
territory.

As for his assessment of me, you'd have to ask him...<G> He probably mistook
fairness for weakness. And I certainly look a lot more stupid than I really
am...<G>

> I have known cases of people who will only give an honest day of work if
> they know that someone is looking over their shoulders. In general, I
think
> it is better to do without them.
>
Here we would disagree. I think they get that way because they are totally
de-motivated. This usually stems from a long period of poor or downright
"mis"-management.

Why would anybody take up an interesting career like programming if they
REALLY didn't want to do it?

The atmosphere we should strive to achieve is one where people actually have
fun doing their job and achieve satisfaction and enjoyment from it. I
certainly don't want people on my projects who wake up in the mornng and
think: "Oh shit! I have to go to that awful place again with those miserable
bastards and that prick of a Manager..." (Again, if I won't do that myself,
why would I let anyone else do it<G>?)

In my opinion, the ones who need to be "driven" are the ones who have never
been "led"...

I reckon these people can be salvaged and they can provide worthwhile
contributions to the team effort.  (In fact I know they can because I've
done it...<G>) However the methods for achieving this are way beyond the
scope of this post. You will have to read the book...I'm planning a chapter
on "problem" scenarios like this one...<G>)

Regards,

Pete.

miserable

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> scope of this post. You will have to read the book...I'm planning a
chapter
> on "problem" scenarios like this one...<G>)
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I just want to add that in this regard I am actually quite in agreement with
Peter.  In posting in newsgroups it is not always easy to bring all the
details, and this can lead to misunderstandings.  My statement was not
regarding people in shops were the management is absent, even though there
are many managers :)

I work as a contractor, and having been in different places, I notice that
one of the worst things are indifferent managers, or those that are not
consistent.  In that regard, they are like the worst parents. Several
studies have found that the worst parents are not those who are very strict,
or those who are very liberal, but those who are indifferent and
inconsistent, and for the same behaviour one day will slap the child and the
other day will laugh.  The same could be said of managers. The worst are not
those who will criticize, or those who will not, but those who treat the
same those who do their best and those who just sit at their desks and do
nothing.

Thus, I was talking about the attitude of the programmer. I do not agree
with those who say that the job of a manager should be to motivate me. I am
supposed to be a professional, and the motivation has to come from within.
The job of the manager should be to make sure that I have the necessary
tools to do my job, a decent and comfortable workstation, access to the
libraries, environments and operative systems that I need to work, and to
organize the project in a way that information is shared and available, so I
do not have to go from desk to desk to find out where the JCL to compile
resides.

Now, if besides doing all these the manager is technically sound, is a good
pal, and has good enough conversation, then those are certainly bonus.

Regards,
Luis
```

    --- discussion of padding of schedules, stripping of schedules, and the
various
    --- things that people do when formulating estimates...

One book that I've found very informative in the (non-technical) management
of software projects has been "Death March" by Edward Yourdon.  He speaks of
these different estimating "games" that get played (amongst a number of
other things).  It seems very comprehensive to me, but I was wondering (a)
if you had read it, and (b) what you thought of the strategies it presents?

I realize we are wandering off the original thread topic (Programmer
Performance Evaluations).  It may be time to start a new one.

Paul

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-05T09:40:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xt1b7.2592$8J.867462@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
One thing I would add that most places ignore that the military
stresses:

a regular scheduled (quarterly is minimum) review of how that person is
performing. Even if (or especially if) everything is good, Say it (
preferrably in writing) to reinforce it.  This also allows you to bring up
something small (or maybe several smalls) that really should not wait til
annual review, correct them now with the individual's INPUT.
If you put it in writing, then the second time you can refer to the first
(and ask him/her to bring his/her copy of previous memo to meeting).
Also ask how the employee feels in the position, (overwhelmed,
underworked, just about right, ready for more / harder work, etc)

Done right, this 'nips it in the bud' for most problems, lets the employee
know that his work and work quality and job satisfaction is important to
YOU.


"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3b6a998b_1@goliath.newsgroups.com...
> Paul,
>
> I wouldn't normally respond to a post like this  because, as you said, it
is

>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Here's what works for me....obviously, there are no hard and fast rules
and
> individuals are the most important thing, but the following
generalisations
> have been arrived at over a 37 year period, of which at least 15 years
have
> been managing (and, more importantly, as a manager, working with...) other
> people...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> (I also have severe reservations about Function Points (when used to
measure
> performance; OK for assessing predicted workload, but even then, don't
take
> it too seriously. There is NO substitute for experience and
> understanding...). Most of the supposed metrics that I have encountered
have
> a more negative effect on morale and performance than they are worth.
There
> is little to be gained by measuring the performance of a team which is
> already de-motivated by knowing that they are not trusted and will be
> subject to scrutiny and bean counting. Besides, they will probably spend
> more of their time filling in activity logs than you can really
afford...<G>
>
> The ONLY criterion on which I measure performance (my own and other
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1. When allocating tasks to people sit down with them 1-on-1 and go
through
> the job in hand. Ask THEM to tell you how long it will take to deliver. If
> their answer is unacceptable, don't erupt and start screaming
"incompetence"
> <G>, simply ask for a justification. Be open, honest, and fair. If they
are
> "sandbagging" tell them you will get someone else to do it, (or do it
> yourself) and find another task for them.
>
> (This works extremely well... I had a case once where a prima donna
> estimated a certain job at 10 days because he wanted to "coast" it and I
was
> a new Manager who he hadn't met before, so he made the mistake of assuming
I
> was just like all the rest... I expressed surprise at his estimate. He
then
> asked me how long I thought it would take. I said: "around 6 days for a
> reasonably experienced programmer; a man of your calibre could do it in
> 4...." He then challenged me to find ANYONE who could do it in 4 days, so
I
> did it myself. Never had a further problem with that particular
individual.
> And the others looked at me in a different way also...<G>.  (Of course I
> worked 12 hours a day on it, but they didn't know that <G>, and it was
worth
> it for a few days to establish credibility...)
>
> If they continually "pad" estimates, consider firing them. It is a
betrayal
> of trust.
>
> If they genuinely will take longer than you think is fair, factor in their
> experience and training and cut them some slack. Tell them you are doing
> this, but don't do it for more than three months. If they need training,
see
> that they get it. (Provide it yourself if you have to...a major
> responsibility for you as a Manager is to help your team to develop.)
>
> Once estimates are agreed with individuals, do everything you can to
ensure
> they succeed. Support them and help them. Make sure they get the resources
> and tools required to do the job.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> individuals that don't...), have a little private chat with them
expressing
> your concern, find out if there are other factors affecting their
> performance and again, cut them some slack, but only on the understanding
> that their performance improves.
>
> If it doesn't, fire them or move them. If you can't do either, make it
clear
> to your own Management that there is a problem and you are now carrying
dead
> wood which will debilitate the whole project and put it at risk.
>
> (In all the years I've been doing this stuff, I had ONE occasion where
this
> was necessary and, fortunately, I was empowered to fire the persons
> concerned. I didn't want to do it and I took far too long over it. My Boss
> advised me to do it (but wouldn't force me) and I believed I could
"manage"
> the situation. He was right and I should've done it much sooner.
>
> The lesson learned is: If you find people with incorrigible attitudes for
> their own twisted reasons, get rid of them at the start or after one
> straightforward and blunt discussion. It isn't personal. It just
debilitates
> a team to know that one or more members are covertly hostile and "swinging
> the lead", while everybody else is motivated and trying to achieve their
own
> and the project's goals.)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> each individual per day... If you feel comfortable assessing tasks in
hours.
>
> 4.Try and avoid using the Waterfall. If your Company implements it as a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> I am presenting on this at CW2001. Try and attend if you can. I am
currently
> preparing a lot of "give-away" material on this subject that is designed
to
> help Project Managers. Sorry if this sounds like Evangelism, but I have
> tried it both ways...RAD and the Waterfall. There is no comparison. And
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> from the discussion above how the overall GOALS are MUCH more important
than
> the individual tasks. Instead of wasting time filling in metrics forms,
get
> your teams motivated to achieve the project goals. Then turn them loose
and
> they will spend their time working towards the goals, rather than worrying
> about the performance statistics.
>

about
> what they are doing, and listen to them. Ask them if they feel they will
> achieve the goal they are working to. If not, find out what went wrong and
> help them fix it. It is incredible how the whole atmosphere of working
> changes on a team that is succeeding. Work actually becomes fun, problems
> are a temporary diversion like a crossword puzzle that provides
satisfaction
> when you solve it.
>
> Promote an atmosphere where it is OK not to know something, provided you
ask
> about it. Positively reinforce "right" action and attitude.
>
> And take them to lunch when they achieve milestones. <G> (Seriously, it is
> very important to show them that you really do value what they are doing.
I
> have personally paid for team lunches in Companies where it wasn't the
> "policy" to do so. The amount spent is well worth it.)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do they deliver on time and on budget? Are your time constraints
realistic?
>
> Remember that overstressed teams are a false economy. Pressure can be
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the office, or a meal break. Don't let ANYONE work after 1:00am. The
fatigue
> induced errors cost more than sleeping on it and working a few extra hours
> for the next few days. Besides, if you plan it properly and monitor
progress
> in an informal way, you shouldn't need to pull all nighters...
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> they get to indulge their passion. And, as a manager, I now have something
I

> achieve that, keep them fairly isolated. If it looks like a lost cause,

#### ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** Timothy_S_Coffey@bankone.com (Tim Coffey)
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T10:18:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e32280da.0108030918.4ec568f6@posting.google.com>`

```
"Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message news:<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>...

I'll make the following analogy:

   As the manager of an orchestra of professional musicians, what "metric"
   would you apply to determine how much to pay your primary trumpet player
   (forget for the moment that unions are involved)?
      Would you go by number of measures played correctly (assuming that 
         was measurable)?
      Would you go by rehearsals made/missed?
      Would you go by peer review?

Programming is a lot like playing music (aptitude tests prove a correlation
between musical ability and programming ability).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T18:22:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LZBa7.1222$V94.113376@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Programmer Performance Evaluations

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T14:17:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108031317.4356eb1b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <e32280da.0108030918.4ec568f6@posting.google.com>`

```
Timothy_S_Coffey@bankone.com (Tim Coffey) wrote in message news:<e32280da.0108030918.4ec568f6@posting.google.com>...
> "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message news:<amfa7.25928$LP2.1600864@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>...
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> between musical ability and programming ability).

*-------------------------

Bad analogy, Mr Coffey. 
The conductor DOES go by measures played INcorrectly, and
professional musicians' pay IS based on rehearsals attended as well as
concerts.  If the First Trumpet misses too many rehersals, s/he WILL
be fired.


The equivalent would be:
Does the programmer produce a lot of buggy/undocumented code --- more
than is accounted for by skill level?
Does the programmer miss, or fail to contribute to, team meetings?  Is
the programmer absent a lot, or away from the desk when one might
expect to find him/her there?

These are valid considerations.

Stephen J Spiro
```

## Thread: Passing parms to a db2 program — 12 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Passing parms to a db2 program

- **From:** "Jayashree Karthik" <jayam@odin.pdx.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-03T20:34:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kfqcb$mvu$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu>`

```
I have to pass a date field to a db2 program from the JCL. In my shop (and i
assume in most of the shops) DB2 programs are executed using a proc, to
which the program name and the load lib are passed as parms. In this
scenario how do i pass a parameter to the linkage section of the program. I
have done this before but can't remember now.

Thanks for ur suggestions.
Karthik
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing parms to a db2 program

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T13:03:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B6E95CA.2DDF0007@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<9kfqcb$mvu$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu>`

```


Jayashree Karthik wrote:

> I have to pass a date field to a db2 program from the JCL. In my shop (and i
> assume in most of the shops) DB2 programs are executed using a proc, to
> which the program name and the load lib are passed as parms. In this
> scenario how do i pass a parameter to the linkage section of the program. I
> have done this before but can't remember now.

In our shop, we use parm files for such, and pass a symbolic or two so that  the
proc knows where to find the parm file.  Or you can override the proc DD with an
in-line data field.  We don't pass values in the Linkage division for main
programs unless we're just writing a one-time job such as a conversion job,
standards have us read the parm card.  Standards also say all production jobs
run procs.

You can also override the actual executable line - the JCL that you use to
compile probably does this.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Passing parms to a db2 program

- **From:** "Jayashree Karthik" <jayam@odin.pdx.edu>

- **References:** `<9kfqcb$mvu$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu> <3B6E95CA.2DDF0007@cusys.edu>`

```
This is an onetime not a scheduled production job. I guess if i make the
datevalues a seperate DD it would be more efficient. What i am trying to do
is instead of selecting the entire table for fixing up data i am trying to
retrieve a small portion of it based on date of load and fix it and then go
to the next date range. by having a dataset i can probably go in and change
the dataset and have the job run to fix it. Depending on the JCL or pgm for
the dates would mean changing either of those and wait for implementation
procedures to move them to prod and run it. we have a standard date card in
our shop too. but that gives the current date in all formats. I want to pass
a range of dates.

Thanks for all the ideas. But for knowledge sake i am going to try passing a
parm to DB2 program.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Passing parms to a db2 program

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T17:56:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B6EDA99.9F389EFE@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<9kfqcb$mvu$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu> <3B6E95CA.2DDF0007@cusys.edu> <9km9b9$lot$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu>`

```


Jayashree Karthik wrote:

> This is an onetime not a scheduled production job. I guess if i make the
> datevalues a seperate DD it would be more efficient. What i am trying to do
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> parm to DB2 program.

For a one time job, I don't know why you're using a proc, but either way, it is
pretty easy to have your program read a card.  If you are familiar with passing
parms, that might be a tad quicker, but certainly not if you're not familiar
with it.   If you're using a proc, then you can override the DD name with a DD *
to have your date in the JCL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Passing parms to a db2 program

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T23:30:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3MFb7.958$NJ6.4053@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9kfqcb$mvu$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu> <3B6E95CA.2DDF0007@cusys.edu> <9km9b9$lot$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu>`

```
Hi Jay,

In an earlier post I suggested that you put the parm info in the D/S pointed to
by SYSTIN (it s/b SYSTSIN). I'm assuming you're running a batch job under TSO.
Apparently you misunderstood my point. I'm including sample JCL taken from IBM's
DB2 Pgmmr's Guide:

//jobname JOB USER=MY DB2ID 
//GO EXEC PGM=IKJEFT01,DYNAMNBR=20 
//STEPLIB DD DSN=prefix.SDSNEXIT,DISP=SHR 
//        DD DSN=prefix.SDSNLOAD,DISP=SHR 
 
 
 
//SYSTSPRT DD SYSOUT=A 
//SYSTSIN DD * 
DSN SYSTEM (ssid) 
RUN PROG (SAMPPGM) - 
PLAN (SAMPLAN) - 
LIB (SAMPPROJ.SAMPLIB) - 
PARMS ('/D01 D02 D03') 
END 

Notice that the DSN stmt includes the parm passed to the DB2 pgm. Your pgm
should construct a LINKAGE SECT exactly as you would for a std COBOL pgm
expecting PARM data from the EXEC card in the JCL.

HTH, Jack.   



In article <9km9b9$lot$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu>, Jayashree Karthik says...
>
>This is an onetime not a scheduled production job. I guess if i make the
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Passing parms to a db2 program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T23:49:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kns1l$i7u$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9kfqcb$mvu$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu>`

```
This thread has gone off into a variety of directions.  The "real" answer
(requiring NO source-code programming changes - which was my understanding
of the original question) has to knowing how to work with PROCs.

Step 1 - "look at" the PROC that you are using.  (You can do this by running
a "dummy job" or by looking in a PROC library or a variety of other ways).
What you want to look for is whether the proc does or does not already have
any PARM= statements on the step to which you want to add your
"new/additional" parm.

Step 2a - If the existing PROC/step doesn't pass any parameters, then you
can "send it one" by using a PARM.procstepname= statement on your execution
of the proc.

Step 2b - if the existing PROC/step *does* pass any parameters, then you can
still use the PARM.procstepname= override on your execution JCL, but you
need to be careful about "keeping" any existing parameters by including them
in the override.

For more details, see

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/iea2b600/16.7.3?


NOTE WELL: This "technique" is intended for existing (or even new) programs
that expect to get PARM information in the LINKAGE section of the "main"
program.  This feature is also subject to the CBLOPTS LE run-time option.
This is particularly important to understand if you are using any slashes in
a date field that is passed to your program.

For additional information on that option, see

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea3100/2.1.9?


If you ARE designing (or even modifying) a program's source code, then it
certainly is possible/reasonable to code it so that you do NOT expect such
parms to be passed on the JCL PARM statement.  This note is based on my
understanding that your "GOAL" is to use existing COBOL source code - and an
existing catalogued PROC.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Passing parms to a db2 program

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-07T23:04:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pt_b7.2156$NJ6.9535@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9kfqcb$mvu$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu> <9kns1l$i7u$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

I was under the impression tha DB2 pgms executed in batch via TSO (i.e. EXEC
PGM=IKJEFT01) passed PARM info via the SYSTSIN DD using the DSN command as shown
in my post of 8/6. Has this changed? Please enlighten.

Thanx, Jack.

P.S. I know IBM recently provided a CEE routine that gives the ability to
provide PARM info via the EXEC JCL stmt, but executing TSO in batch has been the
traditional method for years.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Passing parms to a db2 program

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Mark A Framness <farmer@netnet.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T22:03:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B71FD6A.FDEE15FD@netnet.net>`
- **References:** `<9kfqcb$mvu$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu> <9kns1l$i7u$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <Pt_b7.2156$NJ6.9535@www.newsranger.com> <E20c7.97478$Cy.14393594@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
Rufio,

He is trying to kill just a little mouse not elephants.

....JCL As normal.
//INPDTA    DD    *
08-08-2001
/*
... jcl as normal.

Alternatively

//INPDTA    DD DSN=NAME1.NAME2.DATEPRM....

Where NAME1.NAME2.DATEPRM. is a sequential dataset containing the date.

In the program one would set up for a normal file and assign it to INPDTA, then
they would read one record from INPDTA (of course after opening the file and
then close the file after the read is complete) and there it is.

This is completely sufficient even more so because it was stated that this is
for a one time (aka special) job.  If it was to be a daily (or periodic on any
frequency) prod job then there are different ways to go about it and one of
those is indeed to gen JCL programtically and submit it to the intreader (or
whatever that software device is).

Anyway
]\/[ark

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Passing parms to a db2 program

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-07T20:27:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kq4l6$d4r$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9kfqcb$mvu$1@starbug.oit.pdx.edu> <9kns1l$i7u$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <Pt_b7.2156$NJ6.9535@www.newsranger.com>`

```
You are correct that the MAJORITY of "batch" DB2 programs run under TSO, but
it is possible to use the CAF (Call Attach Facility) in batch without TSO.
See (for example)

  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/dsnaph10/6.7?

Having said that, it really depends on what the PROC (from the original
note) actually DID to tell how to "pass a parm" to the specific application
(and how the application program was expecting to get the information).
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2001-08-04T09:47:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_996918433@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

environment.

cleaned up.  If you've moved it to a subordinate EXE, leaks may
be recovered when the entire process ends--that could explain
the difference you're seeing.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Microsoft XML-parser

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T01:09:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YuGc7.4641$NJ6.22008@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<2Ggb7.4062$e%4.119202@news3.oke.nextra.no> <9ktgnm01ql5@enews1.newsguy.com> <BKDc7.829$fT5.13200@news1.oke.nextra.no>`

```
Some COM objects require you to explicitly release the resources.  It appears
you are doing so.  Since this object is almost certainly C++ - there's no
garbage collection to clean up.  I have not looked at this particular com object
in detail -- yet.  It's possible that some resources allocated are reused
instead of being released.  I'm afraid experimentation is the only way to find
out, if it's not documented in the MS documentation.

In article <BKDc7.829$fT5.13200@news1.oke.nextra.no>, Tommy Nilsen says...
>
>I was just wondering if you had any problems when finalizing xml-objects ??
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
