<a id="t-f97a1993b8"></a>
## Thread: SMASHING A FIELD (remove spaces; left justify) — 7 msgs

### SMASHING A FIELD (remove spaces; left justify)

- **From:** dhargette@aol.com (DHargette)
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T02:42:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010805224253.18662.00000673@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
I'm somewhat new to cobol and I'm not sure how to tackle this task.

I've got a 19 character alphanumeric field that will have possible leading
and/or embedded spaces within the field. My user has a need to 'smash' the
field (left-justified) while removing all leading and embedded spaces

Any suggestions for the most direct way to do this?

Thanks,

DavidH
```

#### ↳ Re: SMASHING A FIELD (remove spaces; left justify)

- **From:** lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T02:56:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-dKz7vK0NdWeG@thishost>`
- **References:** `<20010805224253.18662.00000673@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
On Mon, 6 Aug 2001 02:42:53, dhargette@aol.com (DHargette) wrote:

> I'm somewhat new to cobol and I'm not sure how to tackle this task.
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> DavidH

This piece of code requires support of "reference modification" by the
compiler

move zero to out-idx
move space to out-field
perform varying in-idx from 1 by 1 until in-idx > 19

  if in-field(in-idx:1) not = space
    add 1 to out-idx
    move infield(in-idx:1) to out-field(out-idx:1)
  end-if
end-perform
```

#### ↳ Re: SMASHING A FIELD (remove spaces; left justify)

- **From:** "Kent Olsen" <kent@nettally.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-05T22:57:42-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kl12k$55a$1@news.utelfla.com>`
- **References:** `<20010805224253.18662.00000673@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
......
01  SOURCE PIC X OCCURS 19.
01  DESTINATION PIC X OCCURS 19.
01  I1  PIC 9(9) COMP.
01  I2  PIC 9(9) COMP.
....


MOVE 0 TO I2.
MOVE SPACES TO DESTINATION.
PERFORM VARYING I1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL I1 > 19
  IF ( SOURCE (I1) NOT = SPACE)
    ADD 1 TO I2
    MOVE SOURCE (I1) TO DESTINATION (I2)
  END-IF
END-PERFORM.



And as a bonus, I2 is the length of the moved text.

DHargette wrote in message <20010805224253.18662.00000673@mb-mo.aol.com>...
>I'm somewhat new to cobol and I'm not sure how to tackle this task.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>DavidH
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: SMASHING A FIELD (remove spaces; left justify)

- **From:** jraben@cascinc.com (Jeff Raben)
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T13:08:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b6e964b.4654330@news.bullseyetelecom.net>`
- **References:** `<20010805224253.18662.00000673@mb-mo.aol.com> <9kl12k$55a$1@news.utelfla.com>`

```
almost...
01  source-of-all.
    05 source...
01  destination-of-all.
    05 destination....

...MOVE SPACES TO DESTINATION-OF-ALL.

.... clean up the group... move the pieces.

Jeff

"Kent Olsen" <kent@nettally.com> wrote:

>......
>01  SOURCE PIC X OCCURS 19.
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>

and stir with a Runcible spoon...
```

#### ↳ Re: SMASHING A FIELD (remove spaces; left justify)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-05T22:41:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kl3mk$j8u$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010805224253.18662.00000673@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
The COBOL FAQ includes 4 (count them FOUR) different ways to RIGHT justify
an alphanumeric field.  You might want to look at these examples and figure
out how to "reverse" them to left justify a field.  See:

 http://cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm#Sample_right
```

#### ↳ Re: SMASHING A FIELD (remove spaces; left justify)

- **From:** Robaire <robaire.spamoff@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T11:35:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q9etmtgumllpf67vhfdil4h51fgjsppkqa@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<20010805224253.18662.00000673@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
On 06 Aug 2001 02:42:53 GMT, dhargette@aol.com (DHargette) wrote:

>I'm somewhat new to cobol and I'm not sure how to tackle this task.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any suggestions for the most direct way to do this?

I have never heard (or read) the expression "smashing a field".
Could you please give a concise definition of it, and specify if it is
a term that is used only in some regions of the world?

Thanks,

R. Miller ( Robaire )
```

#### ↳ Re: SMASHING A FIELD (remove spaces; left justify)

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T22:19:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5KEb7.107750$li.7848587@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<20010805224253.18662.00000673@mb-mo.aol.com>`

```
Several people have already responded, but to answer your question:
you have to do it a byte at a time. That is, select an input byte,
decide whether the byte belongs in the output field, if so, move the
inspected byte to the appropriate place in the output field.

There is no verb, function, or command native to COBOL that does what
you want, or anything close.


"DHargette" <dhargette@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010805224253.18662.00000673@mb-mo.aol.com...
> I'm somewhat new to cobol and I'm not sure how to tackle this task.
>
> I've got a 19 character alphanumeric field that will have possible
leading
> and/or embedded spaces within the field. My user has a need to
'smash' the
> field (left-justified) while removing all leading and embedded
spaces
>
> Any suggestions for the most direct way to do this?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
CGI calls to the Unix CGI (using STDIO), or to the Windows CGI using direct
calls...

More information is required before help can be offered...

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL CGI on Aix with apache

- **From:** "Jack" <keziah.jones@caramail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T15:15:12+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SIwb7.6668$Yy2.33544@tengri.easynet.fr>`
- **References:** `<y0tb7.5927$Yy2.32585@tengri.easynet.fr> <3b6e928b_9@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> a �crit dans le
message news: 3b6e928b_9@goliath.newsgroups.com...
> Can you give us some more details as to how the CGI interface is
> implemented?
>
On AS/400 our soft use calls those three "procedures" from the system  :

like this :

CALL LINKAGE  IS PROCEDURE "QtmhWrStout"
     USING
                            OUTBUFF
                            OUTBUFFLN
                            QUS-EC
END-CALL.

- QtmhRdStin       - to read the standard
- QtmhWrStout    - to write the HTML
- QtmhGetEnv      - for getting back some informations

> Are you making calls to a COBOL "blind" interface (It doesn't matter
whether
> it is the Windows CGI or the Standard one) or are you making your own
direct
> CGI calls to the Unix CGI (using STDIO), or to the Windows CGI using
direct
> calls...
>
does my first answer is enough ?

> What COBOL are you using?

COBOL Microfocus
```

## Thread: Cobol Covertion to Visual Basic — 2 this month / 9 total

_Thread started [2001-07](2001-07.md#t-34ed54df12)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Coversion to Visual Basic

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T09:03:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B6ECDC8.74F1DE8F@dced.state.ak.us>`
- **References:** `<3b4c2a3c_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com> <1ul1ltopdmvpilitjbh9qld53c27861gih@4ax.com> <3b581085_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com> <Y9V57.235$ZM4.34267@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net> <3b5bef97_3@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com>`

```
As an experienced COBOL programmer I decided a few years ago to enhance my
skillset to include VB. The straight-forward way to learn VB seemed to be to
take some existing COBOL programs & translate them into VB. That way I wouldn't
need to re-develop the logic, etc. Basically, this was the 'verb-for-verb'
translation you would most likely get from a conversion program. (BTW-this
worked as a learning tool. In some respects I would have learned more by taking
a VB class, but after seeing how 'programmers' who've taken VB classes write VB
code I think I was better off avoiding them. No discipline. It appears that the
classes focus on glitz, not on programming.)

Anyway, the result was basically character-based screens, just like you're
complaining about in COBOL. I also tried switching from COBOL indexed (VSAM)
files to VB files & it didn't work at all-performance was much slower. So I
ended up converting the files to a database, then realized that I could just as
easily modify the COBOL programs to use the database as rewrite them into VB, so
the end result was nothing more than a screen 're-design' and that didn't really
improve things any.

The reality is that a conversion to VB requires rewriting the programs to
achieve any benefits, and that requires people.

Lloyd Clarke wrote:

> Mick,
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lloyd
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Coversion to Visual Basic

- **From:** pchq45@hotmail.com (Peter Chong)
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T11:06:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<444b2a14.0108091006.61141852@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3b4c2a3c_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com> <1ul1ltopdmvpilitjbh9qld53c27861gih@4ax.com> <3b581085_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com>`

```
"Lloyd Clarke" <lloydc@ndms.org> wrote in message news:<3b581085_1@mk-nntp-1.news.uk.worldonline.com>...
> G Moore wrote:
> > i think you'll find that the best tool out there is a human being.
> 
One more addition.

Usually, The COBOL program have couple of layers, like presentation
layer (most screen handling), File Layer (File or Data base handling)
Business Logic Layer (Calculation and lots of IF & Evaluate and MOVE
statements) from over ï¿½ Century of experience.

Most case, In Presentation Layer Create a similar look of COBOL screen
to VB GUI designer. Then by the object attached some edit check or
some Business logic from a COBOL program.

In File and Database Layer, Create a similar SQL statement (In my case
use Oracle/TOAD or Access QBE/SQL Server) to generate a SQL code and
copy and paste to VB code or VB file handling code to handling a flat
file.

In Business Layer make class on VB to handle similar COBOL logic to VB
of function call. Make segment like COBOL paragraph. but, no more than
64K of code.  ;-)

Peter Chong
Sr. ERP Analyst, VB/Oracle
```

```
Acucorp will be releasing a Thin Client product which does exact what you
want to do.  The release date for this is October.    The product is
designed so that the application is stored on the UNIX server, where it is
to execute.

On the client side, you would have a small runtime, that calls the program
on the server.   The program is executed on the server, but is displayed on
the client's screen.

Please do not hesitate to contact me if you would like further information.

Thanks
```

<a id="t-bb80ccb87a"></a>
## Thread: MAKE file for COBOL — 4 msgs

### MAKE file for COBOL

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2001-08-06T13:24:05-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0108061224.6ef098a7@posting.google.com>`

```
While we can use MF NetExpress as a reasonable IDE and thus use its
dependency analysis etc. if we want to run the compilations in batch
mode we lose the dependencies and have to do a full re-compile. In
practice this isn;t a major problem because it doesn't take *that*
long, however, wouldn't it be nice if...

I haven't used MAKE etc. for some time but know that it is possible to
set up Make files to call the COBOL compiler subject to the
appropriate dependency rules and this is where the problem lies -
setting up all the dependency rules. I seem to recal that there is a
UNIX utility IMAKE (or similar) that will perform the dependency
analysis but it knows only(?) about C/C++ syntax. Surely there is some
utility that understands COBOL syntax and can create the Make
dependency rules automatically ??

(MF have said that they don't believe the NetEx project dependency
information can be readily accessed outside of the NetExpress IDE)

Any suggestions ?

graham
Olympic Computers
```

#### ↳ Re: MAKE file for COBOL

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>

- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0108061224.6ef098a7@posting.google.com>`

```
Years ago I worked with Realia Cobol and it had a Make file generator that
would look at the Cobol programs and figure out the dependencies of copy,
call, etc. The generated make file could then be run batch. Very nice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MAKE file for COBOL

- **From:** graham@olympic.co.uk (graham)
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T03:41:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a02b0a1.0108080241.24df1a00@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<8a02b0a1.0108061224.6ef098a7@posting.google.com> <slZb7.18662$t41.349936@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <mc%b7.31752$jA2.2212220@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Charles Godwin" <charles@godwin.ca> wrote in message news:<mc%b7.31752$jA2.2212220@news20.bellglobal.com>...
> This is still part of the product although we don't emphasis it. It's part
> of the PAN/LCM configuration Management software.
> 

But you should, you really, really should !!

Interactive IDEs are all very well but real applications need batch
compilations, no, but seriously...

Being able to run impact analyses based on file changes is very
useful; without a usable dependency structure one has to resort to
various search tools and while these do work, because they don't know
the COBOL syntax, they can pick up a lot of noise (matches in comments
etc. or commented out code) and this means there is still a lot of
manual checking necessary. Using a stupid build within an IDE means
that you can recompile all affected modules readily but it may be that
had you known which ones were affected (e.g. some very critical
modules that you didn't want to change) or the full scale of the
changes (e.g. where it may mean a virtually complete system
replacement for existing users rather than a small scale update) you
would have adopted a different approach.

I'm rather surprised there aren't any smart programmable analysers
about; or perhaps its possible with utilities like awk, but I thought
that with COBOL being such a popular language someone must had
produced such a utility decades ago.

graham
Olympic Computers (UK)
```

## Thread: multithreaded Cobol OS/390 ? — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### multithreaded Cobol OS/390 ?

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-07T06:13:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.15d942f610c251d989688@news>`

```
does anyone know if it is possible to do "multithreading" in a Cobol for 
OS/390  in an OS/390 ver 10 environment ???  we do have LE also 
installed 
```

#### ↳ Re: multithreaded Cobol OS/390 ?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-08-07T12:29:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010807082941.19853.00003089@ng-fn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.15d942f610c251d989688@news>`

```
Chuck Haatvedt writes...

>does anyone know if it is possible to do "multithreading" in a Cobol for 
>OS/390  in an OS/390 ver 10 environment ???  we do have LE also 
>installed 

My understanding is that it is not supported (you may have one COBOL thread but
not more than one) - yet; thread safe support is planned for a later release.

That said, I haven't tried it. You can run some experiments and see what
happens. The problem, of course, is it may be one of those situations where
things go wrong with no apparent pattern, so the application starts out being
flaky and cannot get better than that until the compiler generates thread-safe
code.

Hope this helps.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: multithreaded Cobol OS/390 ?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-07T13:09:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kpath$4a3$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<MPG.15d942f610c251d989688@news>`

```
1) Under USS (Unix System Services) this is explicitly dis-allowed, see:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igypg205/3.4?

2) The Workstation product ONLY (VisualAge COBOL) as a compiler option
"THREAD" - (and there are lots of mentions of this in the LRM).  Therefore,
as this doesn't exist for OS/390, I assume (but couldn't find documented)
that it is NOT supported under OS/390 or z/OS (yet?)
```

```
explains how Multitasking with COBOL can be acomplished. I've used it and it
works well.

Rgds,
Beat Gossweiler
DP Solutions AG
Geroldswil, Switzerland
```

```
Beat Gossweiler writes...

>Hi Charles,
>
>Chapter 6.2.7.1 in the Lang Env V1R5 Programming Guide
>
>(http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/CEEU1007/6
.2.7.1)
>explains how Multitasking with COBOL can be acomplished. I've used it and it
>works well.

Hmmm.

I'm looking at the LE Pgm. Gde. books for LE V2R10. Chapter 6 is about creating
and running programs under TSO. Chapter 7 is about creating and running
programs under UNIX System Services (I see that Chapter 3 is about XPLINK; this
is new w/ 2.10, so this woudl push subsequent chapters later); you must be
talking about running under UNIX. Here is a quote from that chapter:

"COBOL cannot run in more than one thread; if a COBOL application is initiated
in a second thread, the COBOL run-time raises a software condition.  COBOL
programs can run in the IPT, or can run in one non-IPT created by a C routine."

So are you just ignoring any raised conditions?

Regards,


 

Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **Date:** 2001-08-12T14:46:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l6mgn$566$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B75466A.2372688B@dp-solutions.ch> <20010812101359.03473.00000781@mb-fp.aol.com>`

```
I think that the question is whether "multi-tasking" is the same thing as
running in multiple THREADS.   I don't view them as identical - but I could
be mistaken.  For info on multi-tasking with COBOL, see:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ceea2100/5.2.7.1.
5?
```

<a id="t-993d25dd77"></a>
## Thread: Recognizing COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Recognizing COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL

- **From:** rick.jones@kaisertwd.com (R Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T09:35:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com>`

```
We have numerous subroutines that are "CALLed" from numerous sets of
reusable code (and the reusable code is automatically "included" into
various programs by Panvalet).  While converting from OS/VS COBOL 2.4
to COBOL for MVS and VM 1.2.2, we discovered some of the called
subroutines needed separate versions for the two environments (I'm not
sure why, may LE).  This caused us to create and maintain two versions
of the each reusable code set that issues these CALLs.

Can you think of a technique the reusable code could employ to
recognize which version of COBOL it is operating in, so a single
version of the reusable code could then use an IF structure to CALL
the appropriate version of a subroutine?

Rick Jones
```

#### ↳ Re: Recognizing COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T16:51:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A6ec7.101120$Cy.14801374@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com>`

```
I worked at American Express during the Cobol-370 conversion,
and there the solution was to set up the default COBOL linklib
to the 370 version, and have a symbolic in the JCl to override to
the COBOL II library, if necessary.

With dynamic linking this worked OK - except, that when testing
you had to be really sure you didn't use the wrong linklib (i.e. a different
linklib from what you would use in production).

"R Jones" <rick.jones@kaisertwd.com> wrote in message
news:643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com...
> We have numerous subroutines that are "CALLed" from numerous sets of
> reusable code (and the reusable code is automatically "included" into
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick Jones
```

#### ↳ Re: Recognizing COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T16:02:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ks9e0$42h$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com>`

```
First and foremost, you really need to find out WHY someone thinks that you
need separate versions of these programs.  The only time that this MIGHT be
true is if you are doing something "illegal" in the OS/VS COBOL version -
that no longer works with the "current" supported environments.  (All OS/VS
COBOL and VS COBOL II run-times are no longer supported by IBM.  OS/VS COBOL
and VS COBOL II compiled programs using ONLY the LE run-time are still
supported)

Having said that, you ask about which environment you are running it.  You
do realize (I hope) that you are NOT asking which COBOL product is being
used, but which RUN-TIME (and/or link-edit) library is being used.  With
this information, it is "relatively" easy to determine if LE is or is not
available during the execution of a program.  All you need to do is make a
DYNAMIC call to *any* LE run-time module.  For example,

 01  Test-Program  Pic X(7)  Value "CEEGMT"
 01  Test-Parms.
    05  LILIAN                  PIC S9(9) BINARY.
    05  SECS                    COMP-2.
    05  FC                         Pic X(12).

    ...

    CALL Test-Program USING LILIAN , SECS , FC
           On Exception
                 **** > do your pre-LE run-time logic
           Not On Exception
                 **** >  do your LE run-time logic
    End-Call

NOTE WELL:  As this is a "dynamic" CALL, you must (of course) have compiled
your OS/VS COBOL (if that is the compiler you used) program as RES, not
NORES - but as the entire concept of "NORES" goes away with the currently
supported IBM COBOL compilers, I hope you are already at - or moving to
this.
```

#### ↳ Re: Recognizing COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL

- **From:** rick.jones@kaisertwd.com (R Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T17:43:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<643dcc2f.0108081643.70a02ff6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com>`

```
I have spent several hours researching why someone here decided separate 
versions are required by COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL.  What I
discovered is our internal documentation is incorrect about why 
two versions are required.  
The real concern appears to be CICS vs. batch.

The problem originates with the CALLed subroutine which turns out
to be in assembler language.  I won't go into all the details, partly
because I don't understand them all, but here is the short version.
It was decided none of the batch programs are to use 31-bit addressing,
but the CICS programs should.  So, two versions of the CALLed assembler
subroutines were created (AMODE 31 & AMODE 24).  Since the reusable code
CALLed one of these subroutines, two versions of the reusable code 
were created (CICS & batch).

So, if I am to return to a single set of reusable code, what I actually 
need is a COBOL code snippet that can recognize whether the current 
program is CICS or batch.

I thank the people that sent emails with solutions for my original
question (is the COBOL code is executing within OS COBOL or MVS COBOL).

Any suggestions about recognizing CICS vs. batch will be appreciated.

rick.jones@kaisertwd.com (R Jones) wrote in message news:<643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com>...
> We have numerous subroutines that are "CALLed" from numerous sets of
> reusable code (and the reusable code is automatically "included" into
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick Jones
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Recognizing COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T00:59:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Uflc7.1418$%o4.148505@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com> <643dcc2f.0108081643.70a02ff6@posting.google.com>`

```
It still may be a good approach to use one linklib for CICS and another
for batch, since CICS has its own separate JCL/procs which shop standards
can make different from batch jobs by one of several concatenated DD's
being either one library or the other.

Of course, if you could convince management to back off their insistence
that batch jobs not use 31, that would be much better.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recognizing COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T05:34:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B7371DC.660107FC@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com> <643dcc2f.0108081643.70a02ff6@posting.google.com> <Uflc7.1418$%o4.148505@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
"Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" wrote:
> 
> It still may be a good approach to use one linklib for CICS and another
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> that batch jobs not use 31, that would be much better.

I have been working on 31-bit conversions quite a bit recently.  My
shop is preparing to upgrade from OS/390 release 2.8 to 2.10 and then
to Z/OS next year.  One of the issues the sysprogs have is that DSA
below the 16-meg line will be shrinking as more space is consumed for
operating system components.  Therefore, some large COBOL programs
that are RMODE(24) will no longer execute under z/OS.  The limit seems
to be about 8 megabytes, and these COBOL programs generally exceed it
by having large tables defined in working-storage.

Another issue the technical services gurus have identified is that old
AMODE(24),RMODE(24) assembler subprograms are not safe to run with LE
(Language Environment) because the savearea chain does not support
31-bit addresses.  I'm no assembler expert, but the thought occurs to
me that this is not a problem unless an LE-enabled COBOL program calls
assembler, which in turn calls a lower level LE-COBOL program.  Twenty
year old assembler programs are being modified to change their
initialization prolog, even when they will remain RMODE(24) due to
dependencies on OS services or control blocks.

Some of the problems I've run into with COBOL 31-bit conversions:

1.  Finding OS/VS COBOL programs that somehow weren't converted during
our Y2K project (there was a mandate that all programs would be
recompiled in VS COBOL II, and we are currently converting from COBOL
for MVS & VM version 1.2.2 to COBOL for OS/390 & VM version 2.4).  By
some lucky chance all COBOL II programs were compiled with RES &
NOCMPR2.  The OS/VS COBOL programs were usually compiled NORES.
2.  Assembler subprograms with missing source (not many of those,
thank god).
3.  Assembler subprograms that need their prolog code changed (some of
them owned by sysprogs).
4.  Assembler subprograms that must remain RMODE(24) due to ACB/DCB
restrictions.
5.  Programs last compiled with VS COBOL II and built as
AMODE(ANY),RMODE(24).  All COBOL programs must be rebuilt as
AMODE(31),RMODE(ANY).  Any exceptions must be documented and
justified.  

Some assembler programs are very tricky to convert to AMODE(31), at
least for me.       

If a batch program has only COBOL components it is pretty easy to
recompile it as AMODE(31),RMODE(ANY), and there is no reason NOT to
fix them.  Pretty soon running RMODE(24) applications will be a "bad
thing", like running 1401 emulation.  
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Recognizing COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T08:58:35-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kwkd7.17574$33.3235819@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com> <643dcc2f.0108081643.70a02ff6@posting.google.com> <Uflc7.1418$%o4.148505@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <3B7371DC.660107FC@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Well, as long as the option All31(OFF) is set, then MVS will automatically
'translate' 31bit to 24bit addresses in calls (provided the call is
DYNAMIC).

Most of those assembler programs can still be converted:

do one of the two:

put the actual I/O below the line and call that from the converted
31bit assembler

have the assembler pgm entered as 31bit, ask for enough below the line
memory to move the ACB/DCB to there AND to copy the code to
execute to below the line.  Then do a BRANCH and CHANGE Address
Mode to go to the 24bit, do the I/O, do the complementary Branch and
change address mode.

"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3B7371DC.660107FC@worldnet.att.net...
> "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" wrote:
> >
> > It still may be a good approach to use one linklib for CICS and another
> > for batch, since CICS has its own separate JCL/procs which shop
standards
> > can make different from batch jobs by one of several concatenated DD's
> > being either one library or the other.
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://arnold.trembley.home.att.net/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Recognizing COBOL for MVS and VM vs. OS/VS COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T20:53:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ksqfa$1vp$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<643dcc2f.0108080835.396d5aca@posting.google.com> <643dcc2f.0108081643.70a02ff6@posting.google.com>`

```
OK - if the issue is being able to call the SAME *assembler" program from
both AMODE(31) and AMODE(24) programs, life is probably even easier (again,
I think you can "forget" CICS vs non-CICS).

If all your programs do a DYNAMIC call (CALL identifier - not CALL
"literal"), then the COBOL run-time will happily do AMODE switching FOR you.

The only "real" issue might be if you are passing parameters to this
sub-program (which in SOME cases would want data "below" the line).  To make
certain that this ALWAYS happens, have every program that passes data to
this subroutine compiled with the DATA(24) option, and everything should
work "like a charm".

Having said that, there ARE real issues about have the same (COBOL at least)
subroutine available in batch and CICS.  These are discussed in the COBOL
programming guide - as I recall.  You might want to start your search at:

 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igypg205/3.1.1.4?
```

## Thread: COBOL dead or alive? — 25 msgs

### COBOL dead or alive?

- **From:** anote_un@hotmail.com (Anote Sangkunakupt)
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T11:20:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com>`

```
Dear all,
   I am a student at Monash University (Australia). I used to code
COBOL on IBM AS/400 about 5 years. I would like to hear some opinion
about the future of COBOL.

Regard,
Anote.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

- **From:** Jon <pb@dazoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T18:09:34-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com>`

```
As long as there are businesses which use computers to do massive
business data processing, there will be some flavor of COBOL.  There is
no other languate out there which can compare with it in that type
environment.  And for every advance in all other compiler  technologies,
there is usually an equivalent improvement made in COBOL.  It's here to
stay.

Anote Sangkunakupt wrote:

> Dear all,
>    I am a student at Monash University (Australia). I used to code
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anote.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T20:26:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l21oe0go6@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <RBwc7.3645$ym4.144940@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9kv7st06qk@enews2.newsguy.com> <6gIc7.3749$ym4.147412@iad-read.news.verio.net> <7ce2d2dc.0108100310.727183d5@posting.google.com>`

```
"Anote Sangkunakupt" <anote_un@hotmail.com> wrote...
>
...and I'm not a
> native English speaker. Because of those, I try to express in
the way
> that I think people can understand me easily. As the
result,the
> wording, the sequence and the whole thing may not be in the
common
> way.

I think your English is fine--you stated your question well, and
made a specific follow-up.  Also you exhibited etiquette that's
often foregone in newsgroup forums.

As to your question, I can provide some speculation for a
restricted viewpoint--Cobol for windows development.  I'm
involved in a pc-based (formerly dos) application written in
Cobol.

Given that there's no expectation nor desire to add support for
platforms other than windows, there's only one reason we've
continued with Cobol--existing code base.  From what I've seen
of the community at large, I suspect this is a powerful, if not
solitary, motivation in many other places.

Although the history of computer languages has seen a lot of
variations, it's not as whimsical as it may seem.  Fortran and
Cobol where on the scene first, and are still in use.  C showed
up and gained a lot of momentum when it was expanded in the 70's
to embrace object-oriented programming.

More languages and variations of existing languages appeared,
but the next significant advance was driven by changes in
operating systems, rather than new ideas in languages.
Windows-style OSs and the event-driven approach they took have
spawned a number of integrated development environments.

IDEs prior to that were a convenience, but with the addition of
such heavy resource requirements, began to qualify as languages
in their own right.  Clipper, VB and now application
environments are become a viable choice.

It's a bit unfair to compare Cobol to C for many reasons, not
the least of which is that C, C++, Java, and C# are considered
to be part of the C family.  By doing so, it's been able to grow
while leaving standardized versions behind to canonize existing
code.  Cobol does not seem to be so aggressively included
towards expansion.

Even though Cobol showed up quite a bit before C, the
specification (and consequently use of) and object-oriented form
has been slow.  If this is a good or bad thing is a point of
dispute, but it nonetheless marks Cobol as sedentary.

I believe this is because two reasonable goals are in direct
conflict with each other:  (1) Refinement of the language, and
(2) support for an existing code base.  There are ways to manage
this, but it won't fly if the community sees no value in the
aforementioned refinement.  This is apparently the case.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T14:37:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com>`

```

"Anote Sangkunakupt"
> Dear Jon,
>    Thank you very much for your responding. Can you tell me which
> features make COBOL still be the best to work with massive business
> data processing? My opinion is businesses are moving to do on the
> web-base.

1. 8 billion lines of existing code, with some programs having over
forty (calendar) years and thousands of man-years of development.
2. More COBOL programmers than any other language.
3. The web is not the answer for everything. Try printing 35,000,000
Social Security checks, 6 million dividend reports, 35 million address
labels and you'll see.

>Do you think COBOL will servive on that? One more thing, now
> Java is a very fashionable language. I know only little about Java
but
> many people say Java is the best now. Do you think that Java will be
> able to replace COBOL? Why?

First, Java is slower than ANY OTHER PROGRAMMING LANGUAGE. Because of
this, Java cannot be used for many mission-critical applications
(stock quotation, airline reservations, etc.).

Java won't replace anything except maybe HTML. MS will not ship its
Java Development Kit with Win XP (take that, Sun), plus a new language
has just appeared: CURL (Java+) which will replace Java as the
trendy-language-of-the-day.

Java allows you to do pretty things. But the difference between Java
and COBOL is the difference between an artist with a paint brush and a
newspaper's printing press.


>     I also would like to here opinions from other as well.
> Thank you.
> Regard,
> Anote
>
```

```
> 1. 8 billion lines of existing code, with some programs having over
> forty (calendar) years and thousands of man-years of development.
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

Thank you for sharing your ideas. I have never considered many of your
pionts before such as speed of Java. I have known that there are about
3 millions of Cobol programmers and only 500,000 java programmers, but
I have never known that the number of cobol programmers more than any
other languages.

Actually, I used to code Cobol before but I haven't coded it for 2
years. I really like it but the information below make me worry. What
do you think about this message?

According to Borck "U.S News and World Report recently published a
list of the top colleges and universities in the United States. Among
them were the likes of MIT and Cal Tech. We polled the top 10
institutions on the list and found that in the classroom Cobol is
mentioned only anecdotally in context of the history of programming,
but not a single school currently offers coursework in Cobol. Not
one." (Borck, 2001)

Borck, J.R.(2001), Cobol programmers, face it: Your favorite code is
out dated and losing support, InfoWorld, 23(2),56.

If there are no new generation of Cobol programmers, it cannot be
servive for long time. However, my university still has Cobol
programming subject.

Anote.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T07:12:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B73DDC7.D0A5AB1C@cusys.edu>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com>`

```
Anote Sangkunakupt wrote:

> If there are no new generation of Cobol programmers, it cannot be
> servive for long time. However, my university still has Cobol
> programming subject.
>
> Anote.

If there are no new generations of Cobol programmers, but there is a large
body of Cobol code then Cobol programmers will be a scarce resources.   As
they get scarcer, they will demand more money, and companies will pay for
the creation of more Cobol programmers.

Sure, they will be more tempted to avoid Cobol the next time they have to do
a big conversion anyway - but that will take some time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T22:06:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%VYc7.242263$2O6.15485039@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com>`

```

"Anote Sangkunakupt" <anote_un@hotmail.com>
>
> According to Borck "U.S News and World Report recently published a
> list of the top colleges and universities in the United States.
Among
> them were the likes of MIT and Cal Tech. We polled the top 10
> institutions on the list and found that in the classroom Cobol is
> mentioned only anecdotally in context of the history of programming,
> but not a single school currently offers coursework in Cobol. Not
> one." (Borck, 2001)

 That's not unusual. The purpose of a university is NOT to teach a
programming language per se. Programming languages can be learned at
community colleges, technical schools, or even from a book ("Teach
Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours").

A university's mission is to teach CONCEPTS. In the case of data
processing, concepts usually can only be taught if the student has
some programming tool available. So the universities tend to teach one
or more programming languages to enable the student to grasp the
concepts. In other words, the university teaching of, say, C++ or
Java, is but a means to an end.

Or, to put it another way: Who, in their right mind, goes to MIT or
CalTech to learn Java? Can one get a BS in C++? An MBA in Java?
Phooey!

Or, alternatively, instead of polling universities, poll Fortune 500
companies. Count their COBOL programmers and Java programmers (if
any). Not definitive, you say? OK, poll government agencies. Poll
university data processing departments.

I polled by community college: They offer, at various campuses, about
50 programming languages, six in COBOL, about thirty for Java. Then I
called their data processing department (you know, the department that
prints the paychecks and keeps track of student's transcripts).
Fourteen programmers; no programs in Java.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** anote_un@hotmail.com (Anote Sangkunakupt)
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T23:54:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
> > According to Borck "U.S News and World Report recently published a
> > list of the top colleges and universities in the United States.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Java, is but a means to an end.

I think it is quite unusual for me. According to the message, it can
imply that the number of new Cobol programmers will be reduced
gradually. That means in the future many companies may suffer with
finding Cobol programmers problem. The salary of the existing Cobol
programmer will be increased. So, if some companies want to get the
new application, they may not select an application, which uses Cobol
because they might feel that it is quite difficult to get supporters.

> Or, alternatively, instead of polling universities, poll Fortune 500
> companies. Count their COBOL programmers and Java programmers (if
> any). Not definitive, you say? OK, poll government agencies. Poll
> university data processing departments.

Absolutely, the number of COBOL programmers overweight Java
programmers. However, if we look on the different angle, we may see
the different thing. If we look at the number of COBOL and Java
programmers in last three years and now in percentage, we may see a
reducing of COBOL programmers and an increasing of Java programmers.
I'm not sure this is correct of not. Correct me if I'm wrong.

When I was working, COBOL is only the language. Now, some of my old
colleagues, who coded COBOL, are unemployees and some changed to code
Java.

Regards,
Anote.
```

```
On 11-Aug-2001, anote_un@hotmail.com (Anote Sangkunakupt) wrote:

> I think it is quite unusual for me. According to the message, it can
> imply that the number of new Cobol programmers will be reduced
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Java.

In this case, the Universities are following industry - giving industry what
it wants.   If industry wants Cobol programmers, we would be getting more
money and they would be training more Cobol programmers.   If industry wants
Java programmers they would be getting more money, and industry would be
training more Java programmers.
```

```
grads and others with the aptitude and sends them to "Mainframe bootcamp"
for training.  It is a shame that real and legitimate business needs are
being sniffed down upon by the Universities.  Certainly research demands
that new languages be developed and explored but how many graduates from
comp sci programs are going to practice computer science?  Face it most comp
grads will be technicians and engineers and COBOL is the language of most of
those engineers and technicians.

managers.

If I recall correctly the AIX people at a former client had a brand spanking
new tool to help with drp/erp and their AIX package program was written in
COBOL.  I do not know where people getting off saying that COBOL is dying.

The programs I supported at this former client were all in COBOL and
supported the needs of the end user much better than a package they brought
in for another line of their business.  Unfortunately they have a management
by magazine manager.

BTW I would prefer to code in C++ ( STANDARD C++ or at least non-MS C++) but
Mark & Lorie need to eat.

anyway
ttyl
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

- **From:** alain.reymond-nospam@ceia.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T20:42:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b7444a9.34258030@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com>`

```
Well, before I began in my company, they where working with Cobol on a
Siemens mainframe. It was in the 70s. Then, they switched to an ICL
under VME. 
In the mid 80's, as I arrived, the company switched to Unix. We have
been working under several Unixes : from SCO to ATT. 
Recently, we began to work under Linux.
During all these years, the programs have been developped, maintained.
New program have been created. But migration has never been a problem.
We took the code, compiled it and the job was made. The files
remainded unchanged.
Having our application running under Linux took the time of
transferring programs and data on the new system and a compilation. No
more.

I think this is the most impressive strength of Cobol : portability
from system to system, during the time. This gives a huge stability to
a (small) company which develops its own application and must rely on
its information system. No time wasted. 

At least, this is our experience. That is why I believe that Cobol
will remain for another couple of years.

Alain

>Dear all,
>   I am a student at Monash University (Australia). I used to code
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Anote.
```

## Thread: DWARF Extensions for COBOL — 1 msgs

### DWARF Extensions for COBOL

- **From:** jeff.lanam@compaq.com (Jeff Lanam)
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T14:24:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.compilers
- **Message-ID:** `<01-08-056@comp.compilers>`

```
Has anyone extended the DWARF debugging format to support COBOL?
It doesn't describe decimal variables, scaled integers, or arrays with
a dynamic occurance count, as far as I can tell.  I'm using the 2.0.0
version of "DWARF Debugging Information Format", dated July 27, 1993.
Is there a later version?  UNIX International is listed as the owner--
they are no more.  I'd like to save myself some work if someone else
has already done the head scratching.  Thanks.

Jeff Lanam	jeff.lanam@compaq.com
NonStop Division
Compaq Computer Corp.
```

<a id="t-974f2c79e4"></a>
## Thread: "Examples" - for the new (and old?) Standard — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### "Examples" - for the new (and old?) Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T15:48:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ks8jv$bc2$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For all of you interested in the topic of code examples for the ANSI/ISO
Standard,  I suggest that you look at the "current" J4 document viewable at:

 http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/01-0540.doc

Having NOT been at the meeting where this was discussed, I don't (exactly)
know what this all means, but YOU might be interested in following up on it
with the J4 chair at:

   doncobol <at> mediaone.net
```

#### ↳ Re: "Examples" - for the new (and old?) Standard

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T15:49:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ksflk$4lj$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9ks8jv$bc2$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Specifically with respect to this extract:

"> Having NOT been at the meeting where this was discussed, I don't
(exactly)
> know what this all means, but YOU might be interested in following up on
it
> with the J4 chair ...

Actually, that's meetings, PLURAL, Bill.  This topic was discussed at some
length at meeting #231 in June, as evidenced in document #01-0530, the final
version of the minutes of that meeting (see agenda entry 16.26, item 16)
available on the same web site, as well as at meeting #232 that ended on
Friday last.

The agenda item that precipitated this lengthy discussion (in both meetings)
was your Public Review Comment #26 (document 01-0215).  I have to admit
surprise that you weren't interested enough in J4's discussions on your
paper to be aware of (much less read and digest) the actions taken by the
committee in response to it (or for that matter to be present at either of
the meetings at which it was discussed).

I think having a web site and an "open source" body of examples of
conformant COBOL programs on a user-accessible repository is a *fantastic*
idea, but I also think managing the repository as well as managing the
editing and verification of the validity and relevance of the examples
themselves is way outside the scope of what the standards committee ought to
be doing or has time and resources to do.    It seems to me this is an
*excellent* opportunity for a user-driven body of conformant examples,
managed entirely outside the purview of the standards committees, perhaps
with some hands-off corporate sponsorship to provide the needed resources.

The formal disposition of document 01-0540 will be reflected in the draft
minutes for meeting #232; those draft minutes will be available (as document
01-0481), probably late Friday afternoon.    In all honesty, I think the J4
chair has enough to do without having to answer a bunch of queries on this
topic, since at least a "semi-official" response will be published in 48
hours or less!

    -Chuck Stevens



"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9ks8jv$bc2$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net...
> For all of you interested in the topic of code examples for the ANSI/ISO

it
> with the J4 chair at:

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: "Examples" - for the new (and old?) Standard

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T16:39:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l1rar$r20$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<9ks8jv$bc2$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The draft minutes for J4 meeting #232 have just been posted as document
01-0481 on the J4 web site; document 01-0540 was discussed at some length
during that meeting under agenda item 17.72.  The disposition of this
document was, according to those draft minutes, "J4 is very much in support
of the idea of COBOL examples being provided on the web, but it is outside
the committe's scope of work to set up or manage such a web site."

    -Chuck Stevens
```

## Thread: Printing im Power Cobol — 3 msgs

### Printing im Power Cobol

- **From:** Carlos Lages <clages@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T21:31:36-03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B71D9E8.2002353@attglobal.net>`

```
Hi , its me again

How can I print  using the DRAFT font  to a printer like Epson FX2180 ,
etc
The reason of the question is because Power Cobol, if I dont select any
fonts
it puts a courier 7.

Using Draft fonts i can get  80% reduction ( 132 cols  in a paper 80
cols)
and its faster.

Courier 7 is slower and I can not get 80%

I really need to use this, any help welcome

Thnks

Carlos lages
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing im Power Cobol

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T01:37:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sPlc7.3516$NJ6.14497@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3B71D9E8.2002353@attglobal.net>`

```
The Epson supports some "Utility" fonts.  You have to find those font names and
use those.  This will get you draft mode -- but it won't get you bidirectional
printing though.


In article <3B71D9E8.2002353@attglobal.net>, Carlos Lages says...
>
>Esta � uma mensagem de v�rias partes em formato MIME.
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Printing im Power Cobol

- **From:** g.friedrich@fiuka.de (Friedrich)
- **Date:** 2001-08-08T23:43:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcfdd616.0108082243.7f248c03@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3B71D9E8.2002353@attglobal.net>`

```
do you have tried "ESC x" with 0=draft 1=NLQ ?
eg."ESC x 0" = draft-modus (in hex: 1B 78 00).
But I think with draft you can't change the width, draft means quality only.
NLQ=near-letter-quality (slow), draft=quick
I use condensed to print more characters on a line (ESC SI) hex:1B 0F)
greetings - Guenther - fiuka
```

- **From:** rovani@rech.com.br

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9kspc1$fl1$1@news.netmar.com>`

```

   The absence of conditional compilation in PowerCobol, in my opinion is a
great deficiency. In my company we have PowerCobol since 1998, but up to now
we could not migrate from NetExpress to Fujitsu due to absence of this
resource. We used the conditional compilation a lot in NetExpress, to
increase the reuse of Copy's in several programs that are a little different
some of the other ones.

   I don't get to understand because Fujitsu still didn't implement the
conditional compilation in Fujitsu COBOL/PowerCobol.

   Another great lack in PowerCobol is the absence of SORT for tables
(occurs).
I apologize for my bad English.

Rovani Marcelo Rech
Novo Hamburgo/RS - Brazil
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Terminology (was: Useless conditional compilation?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T14:52:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l1e6o$lab$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3993D947.CAE2B464@fujitsu-siemens.com> <8n1e5p$euq$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <9kspc1$fl1$1@news.netmar.com>`

```
Both of those features ARE included in the draft of the next ANSI/ISO
Standard.  Have you contacted Fujitsu to see what their plans are for them?
(They have already implemented AS EXTENSIONS many - but not all - the
features from the draft.)

NOTE WELL: "conditional compilation" in the draft Standard is NOT identical
to that in Micro Focus COBOL - but could "easily" be converted from/to it.
```

<a id="t-9af56d7987"></a>
## Thread: COMP-X ? — 1 this month / 11 total

_Thread started [2000-08](2000-08.md#t-9af56d7987)._

#### ↳ Re: COMP-X ?

- **From:** rovani@rech.com.br
- **Date:** 2001-08-09T01:54:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ksqh7$fsv$1@news.netmar.com>`
- **References:** `<1kek5.9420$VO3.154987@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
Hi Lindon,

   Fujitsu doesn't allow the use of the clause COMP-X. You must to alter it to
BINARY, but like Fujitsu doesn't also allow PIC X(xx) BINARY, you should
alter it for the equivalent. In your example, PIC XX COMP-X will be PIC 9(04)
BINARY. To have better compatibility, you should insert directing
BINARY(BYTE) in PowerCobol.

Rovani Marcelo Rech
Novo Hamburgo/RS - Brazil
rovani@rech.com.br


In article <1kek5.9420$VO3.154987@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com>, lbr
<lbr@ntlworld.com> writes:
```

<a id="t-1a296ba859"></a>
## Thread: Update on Fujitsu 6.1 Shell Question — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Update on Fujitsu 6.1 Shell Question

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T04:23:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qlJc7.6796$9e2.1741289609@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```
To all.

A while back I posted a question to the group regarding a strange problem I
was having using the Fujitsu PowerCobol 6.1 "CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST" on a
Windows NT 4.0 server. I never got a chance to respond back to the group
regarding the status of this issue, due to way too much work and so little
posting time.  As it turns out, with many thanks to Thane for his
"Shellexecute" routine that he sent me, the problem seems to be resolved.
But I want to let everyone know that it was NOT a problem with the "file
exist" routine, nor with the Fujitsu code, per se.  Nor did his routine
provide an immediate fix.  However......

I was using the DDE control to execute the shell routine to update our
database and execute a FTP to another server.  When I changed the routine to
use Thane's "Shellexecute" code (which gives a little better diagnostic
return codes), I didn't get much different results but  I discovered
something new about the NT "cmd.exe" shell (read DOS prompt for Windows
95/98 users).  When I ran Thane's code on my Windows 98 development machine,
the routine worked every time, even though it didn't always work with the
DDE routine.  So the "Shellexecute" routine worked more consistently.  But
when I ran it in the NT server, it failed about as much as it did with the
DDE code.  The return code always seemed to contain the same value on every
shell out to the database update routine.  I communicated this back to Thane
and he said to not put too much into the return code and pointed me to an
article on the MSDN site.  After reading it I agreed with him, but it got me
to thinking that something just doesn't seem right here, and after doing
some web research, I found that the NT "cmd.exe" shell actually has the
capability of using buffers and the default is 4.  So just for grins, I
changed the buffer to 1 and the problem seemed to disappear with the
database update portion. Don't know why, but it just seemed to work.

That left the FTP part still not working.  I was using a "freeware" FTP
scripter named FTPBATCH, which always worked fine on my 98 machine (a slower
machine than the NT server) but on the NT server, I noticed that the routine
would take about 45 seconds to go through the FTP login, password,
parameters and the put file routine, and it would never close out the "cmd"
window nor would it complete the FTP.  So I changed the Fujitsu COBOL
program to actually write out a FTP script file, and using Thane's
"Shellexecute" code, actually called the Windows NT FTP executable with
the -s:<script name> command line parameter and it works perfectly every
time.

My many thanks to Thane for all his help in this matter, and hopefully I can
return the favor some day.  Now I know why I'm basically a mainframe guy and
have so much gray hair!!!

Denny
```

- **References:** `<qlJc7.6796$9e2.1741289609@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

<a id="t-36374aad2d"></a>
## Thread: COBOL dead or alive? — 1 msgs

### Re: COBOL dead or alive?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T08:38:18+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B738F6A.CE7FCFE4@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <3B729A69.CB99C3C7@ils-international.com>`

```
> >    Thank you very much for your responding. Can you tell me which
> > features make COBOL still be the best to work with massive business
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > able to replace COBOL? Why?

Yes, Java is a 'fashionable' language, as was Algol, PL/I, Pascal, Ada,
dBase, C, etc. And as is VB, Perl, C++.  The 'Computer Science' types
will move on to the next language when that comes along: C#, Python, or
something new next year.

Businesses cannot change just because 'Computer Science' fashions do,
they have systems that last for decades and cannot afford to discard
them or rewrite them because some students want to play with newer toys.

Cobol has been used for 40 years and can be moved from old system to
current ones, and will move to the next range of computers.  Other
languages change from year to year, which is OK for toys that will be
thrown away, but is useless for businesses.

Java is the language that was seen as the most likely to remain stable
for some time.  At the time it came out C++ was being revised and
changed with its library going through a huge growth, a new VB was
breaking all old programs (which will happen again with next release),
and others were fragmenting into different versions.

Personally I think that Java has passed its peak and the CS types will
move to some new toy such as C# or a derivitive and then this will
fragment.  Meanwhile Cobol will continue while Python will grow as the
internet and scripting language.

As for 'Web-based' I use Cobol for business applications on the web and
Python for scripting.
```

---

- **References:** `<Xns90F97362E8E7BSpender@2.1.1.45> <3B73DC75.F9B0FC64@cusys.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> a tapot� le 10 ao�t 2001 :

> What is COBOL 1?

COBOL i think ( COBOL 72). here, we call it cobol 1.
(cobol 2 for cobol 85 ...)

> I have never seen an equal sign and "TO" together.  

us too ! we sometimes had to convert cobol in cobol 85  programs
and it is the first time we see that. 

>Was this ever working? 

yes, with a old compiler (builder ? in english)

> Why not just go to standard COBOL and see if it understands it,

yes, it works but we didn't sure that it was the same instruction.

> removing the "TO", or even changing the logic to use IF / ELSE / END-IF.
```

#### ↳ Re: a bizarre test in COBOL1 ...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T14:43:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l1dmi$vhu$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns90F97362E8E7BSpender@2.1.1.45>`

```
A) "COBOL 1" is an (incredibly) inaccurate name for IBM's OS/VS COBOL
product.  In that context, "COBOL 2" usually means "VS COBOL II" (a product
that IBM no longer supports).  The current IBM COBOL (mainframe) compiler is
IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM V2R2.

B) It sounds like you haven't used the IBM "migration guide" which is ALWAYS
a good place to start when doing such conversions.  I suggest you look at:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGYMG202/CCONTENTS

C) for this specific issue, look at:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igymg202/4.1.6?

and look for the topic, "Relation conditions" which includes discussion of
this "undocumented extension" and some others, e.g.
    = TO
    > (<) THEN
    > (<) THAN
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

- **From:** cuplan@tampabay.rr.com (Cuplan)
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T18:51:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java
- **Message-ID:** `<3b742894.78321192@news-server>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com> <3B73FD0C.7C799B07@ils-international.com> <3B7413F1.8FFF219E@cusys.edu> <NnVc7.112103$Cy.15891239@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
On Fri, 10 Aug 2001 18:05:33 GMT, "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
wrote:

>I'm curious to get a different perspective on this -
>hence the cross-post

Let me say up-front that I'm not interested in a holy war.  I don't
think COBOL is dead.  I think it has its place, and I think that, in
many cases, its place isn't Java's place.  Given this, I already find
the line of thinking displayed here to be a little misguided.

>"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
>news:3B7413F1.8FFF219E@cusys.edu...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Maybe you're referring to the next question:

Definitely "dozens."  I could rattle off the first dozen without
taxing my brain.

>> >     - Question 2 -  Including Java and Visual Basic, how many computer
>> > languages have tried to bury and replace COBOL in the last forty (40)
>> > years?
>> >     - Answer: Dozens

Unfortunately, the idea of burying an entrenched language on a legacy
system is folly.  I'd be interested in someone finding factual proof
that Java was invented to "bury COBOL."  A language only deserves to
be buried when it is intrinsically flawed.  Otherwise, it will
naturally hold on, and COBOL has done just that.  I don't like COBOL,
but it does get its job done.

>> >     - Question 3 -  Unlike Java and Visual Basic, which programming
>> > language truly runs on more computing platforms than any other?
>> >     - Answer: COBOL

I'm not a COBOL guru, but last I checked, There were multiple
different flavors of COBOL, making this an apples-to-oranges
comparison.  COBOL is kinda like C...there's the veneer of it being
the same everywhere, but there's also the underbelly that everyone
seems to have their different idea of what's a good implementation of
the existing standards.  Given this, a COBOL program based on one
"flavor" must have work done to it to port to another "flavor."
That's the way things go when you compile to native code.  Trying to
say COBOL runs on more platforms than Java is a rediculous comparison
anyway- it compiles to native code!  COBOL is a prettier way of
dealing with native code, much like C is.  By comparison, Java is a
prettier way of dealing with Java bytecodes.  The former members of
this comparison (C and COBOL) are going to be able to target more
systems because all that is necessary is to make a new native code
compiler to hit these systems.  However, C and COBOL aren't "running"
on that target system.  They just make binaries to do that.  This also
means there's no guarantee of portability...hence the folly in
claiming that COBOL runs on any platform.

By comparison, it is semantically true that Java runs on certain
platforms.  Java is a bytecode execution engine.  It is an actual
program written for various platforms.  Java bytecodes have a high
level of universiality because of this.

>> >     - Question 4 -  Which is the only programming language
>> > continuously updated to support the latest in business rules &
>> > technologies for over forty years?
>> >     - Answer: COBOL

I have heard about Visual COBOL and OO-COBOL.  Is there a COBOL
initiative for Web Services?  Handling XML?  Structured exception
handling? I'm actually asking out of curiosity.

>> >     - Question 5 -  Which business oriented programming language has
>> > been selected by Microsoft to support their newest Networks & Internet
>> > platform known as .NET
>> >     - Answer: COBOL. Not Java.

Yep.  That's because they know that businesses would like to bring
COBOL back into the fold.  When you think about the way .NET works,
you can immediately see why COBOL is a better candidate than Java.
COBOL had a mountain of legacy code that could benefit from the
wonderful new .NET CLR libraries, while Java had its own library set
to stand on.

Then there's the political issues between Microsoft and Sun that
almost make the arguments implied in Question 5 a bit dishonest.

>> >     - Question 6 -  Which programming language is the most taught
>> > around the world in schools & universities
>> >     - Answer: COBOL

Interesting.  Is there a source available for this assertion?

>> >     - Question 7 -  Which programming language has more users that
>> > Java
>> >     - Answer: COBOL 3,000,000 (three millions), Java 500,000 (half
>> > million)

What qualifies a "user?"

>> >     - Question 8 -  Which programming language has been recently
>> > introduced not to replace COBOL, but Java!!!
>> >     - Answer: C#

Politics as usual, again.  However, the efficacy of C# as a "Java
killer" remains to be seen.  Then there's also the fact that MS only
fights with things it perceives as a threat.  If it's shepherding
COBOL and trying to kill Java, it's because MS perceives COBOL as a
sheep and Java as a wolf.

Unfortunately, C# is a sick puppy with really bad design principles.
I will be learning it and probably developing in it, but I wish it the
worst at all times.

>> More importantly, since Java became famous (last 3-4 years?) - which
>> language has been the choice for more NEW code than any other language?

I believe the answer here is Java, but I actually don't know.
```

```
best response on this thread.

I'm a COBOL veteran, converting to Java, 'cos I need a job.
But, although it's probably possible to maintain some large*
databases on a network server, who - when it's easier or
better on a mainframe. And I DON'T think trying to use
Java on a mainframe is a good idea. But I also think
anyone wanting to run an n-tier app can benefit from Java.

* - large is perhaps 1Gb+ DB2 or other relational tables,
with 254 partitions etc.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cuplan@tampabay.rr.com (Cuplan)
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T23:19:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java
- **Message-ID:** `<3b746a14.6305968@news-server>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com> <3B73FD0C.7C799B07@ils-international.com> <3B7413F1.8FFF219E@cusys.edu> <NnVc7.112103$Cy.15891239@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3b742894.78321192@news-server> <3B744DC9.836A726F@cusys.edu>`

```
On Fri, 10 Aug 2001 15:10:33 -0600, Howard Brazee
<howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>We all can.   But "dozens" implies something less than 100.

Well, to me, it means 24 or more.

>I don't know if being continuously upgraded is necessarily a good thing nor a
>bad thing.   If it was the appropriate tool back then, the question is - have
>our needs changed?   If so, should we adjust the tool or get a new one?

That's the eternal engineering question.

[I said]
>> Yep.  That's because they know that businesses would like to bring
>> COBOL back into the fold.  When you think about the way .NET works,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>And they don't like Sun controlling Java.

This is true to a certain point, too.  For fun, though, I attempted a
thought experiment to create a system like the .NET CLR - a universal
runtime code library that all languages could compile against.  I
could stuff almost every language into that...except Java.  The
problem is that Java already has a huge runtime library.  How would
you get the Java SDK libraries to fit?  It'd just make the CLR library
huge and unwieldy.  Honestly, there's just a clash of philosophies
here.  I side with Sun, though.

>> >> >     - Question 6 -  Which programming language is the most taught
>> >> > around the world in schools & universities
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>edge countries.

Well, I am a recent grad of the University of Florida.  While I was
there, I took classes that used Java, FORTRAN, COBOL, MATLAB, and MIPS
Assembly Language.  The only language used by multiple *required*
classes was Java.

>> >> >     - Question 7 -  Which programming language has more users that
>> >> > Java
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>I would call a spread sheet a programming language - how many users?

Ah, but a spreadsheet is also an application, and the distinction of
"user" is clear - if you execute the spreadsheet application, you're
using it.  What qualifies a COBOL "user?"  A COBOL programmer?
Someone who uses an native code application compiled from COBOL?  It's
not as clear.

>I bet Java beats out C#.

I'm keeping my money on Sun.  I'll be learning C# just so that I can
have a foot back in the MS camp.  I missed the boat with C++, and my
job options are a tad more limited because of it.

>I have read Java is just catching up this year.   We'll see if it stays that
>way.

Indeed.  It gets my hopes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-11T18:12:40+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.java
- **Message-ID:** `<3B756788.E19137CC@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com> <3B73FD0C.7C799B07@ils-international.com> <3B7413F1.8FFF219E@cusys.edu> <NnVc7.112103$Cy.15891239@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3b742894.78321192@news-server>`

```
Cuplan wrote:
> 
>  Trying to
> say COBOL runs on more platforms than Java is a rediculous comparison
> anyway- it compiles to native code! 

__SOME__ compilers compile to native code, _Some_ others compile to a
portable byte code, _Some_ do either as required by the developer.

AcuCobol compiles to bytecode and run-times are available for at least
130 platforms (may be more) and compiler for one system can compile
programs that will run on any and all run-time platforms.  Compile once,
run anywhere (even over the Internet) has been around much longer than
Java, since the 70s in fact.

MicroFocus will compile to a fully portable bytecode system that has
developed from their original one in the late 70s, or it can compile to
native code which will be portable within classes of platforms (ie same
processor type).

> COBOL is a prettier way of
> dealing with native code, much like C is.  By comparison, Java is a
> prettier way of dealing with Java bytecodes. 

Cobol is a prettier way of dealing with native and/or with bytecode,
depending on the compiler and/or the developer.

>  The former members of
> this comparison (C and COBOL) are going to be able to target more
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> claiming that COBOL runs on any platform.

But then it is folly to claim that 'Cobol compiles to native code' when
you obviously know very little about how various Cobols work.

There are several platforms where Cobol is available and Java is not and
fewer where the reverse is true (there is AFAIK no Cobol for Mac), hence
Cobol _does_ run on more platforms than Java.

As the AcuCobol compiler runs on the same basic run-time (AFAIK) then it
can compile on every platform that the compiled programs can run on.  

> By comparison, it is semantically true that Java runs on certain
> platforms.  Java is a bytecode execution engine.  It is an actual
> program written for various platforms.  Java bytecodes have a high
> level of universiality because of this.

It just copies from and extends the previous examples of portable
bytecoded systems: UCSD Pascal/Fortran/Modula2, MF Cobol, RM Cobol,
AcuCobol.

There is also another language that runs on more platforms than Java and
that is Python.  This is because there is a Jave implementation of
Python that results in Java byte codes thus it runs on all platforms
that Java does _plus_ it runs on, say, DOS where java does not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

```
<riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>__SOME__ compilers compile to native code, _Some_ others compile to a

I stand corrected.

>AcuCobol compiles to bytecode and run-times are available for at least
>130 platforms (may be more) and compiler for one system can compile
>programs that will run on any and all run-time platforms.  Compile once,
>run anywhere (even over the Internet) has been around much longer than
>Java, since the 70s in fact.

I wasn't claiming that Java was the first to that philosophy.  I
honestly didn't know about this, though.  Again, I stand corrected.

>But then it is folly to claim that 'Cobol compiles to native code' when
>you obviously know very little about how various Cobols work.

Indeed it is, and I again stand corrected.

>There are several platforms where Cobol is available and Java is not and
>fewer where the reverse is true (there is AFAIK no Cobol for Mac), hence
>Cobol _does_ run on more platforms than Java.

Again, having never had any experience with COBOL bytecodes, I was
unaware of this.  When I wrote my response, I actually suspected that
my arguments had been taken into consideration and were, in fact,
wrong.  I thank you for pointing it out.

>It just copies from and extends the previous examples of portable
>bytecoded systems: UCSD Pascal/Fortran/Modula2, MF Cobol, RM Cobol,
>AcuCobol.

I never claimes Java to be original in that respect.

>There is also another language that runs on more platforms than Java and
>that is Python.  This is because there is a Jave implementation of
>Python that results in Java byte codes thus it runs on all platforms
>that Java does _plus_ it runs on, say, DOS where java does not.

Yep.  JPython.  I'm familiar with it.  In fact, I'm using it in a
project of mine.  I'm writing a system for loading and executing
various automata, and I wanted to allow Python programmers the
opportunity to write automata for the system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

```
On Sun, 12 Aug 2001 19:07:50 GMT, cuplan@tampabay.rr.com (Cuplan)
wrote:

>On Sun, 12 Aug 2001 13:38:41 GMT, Michael Davis <michael@damaru.com>
>wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>living as a hippie musician. :)

Mr/Ms Cuplan,

This is the second time I've seen you come down hard on C#.

One of the major designers (if not THE designer) of C# is Anders
Hejlsberg, who also designed Turbo Pascal and then lead the team that
developed Delphi for Borland (see interview at O'Reilly's website).
Those are impressive credentials to me and I would tend to give him
the benefit of the doubt in his proven area of expertise .

To what specifically can you point that is so "disgusting" in C# that
one might think that he blew it this time?

R. Miller ( Robaire )
```
