###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** anote_un@hotmail.com (Anote Sangkunakupt)
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T06:42:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108130542.199e2116@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com> <3B73FD0C.7C799B07@ils-international.com> <3B7413F1.8FFF219E@cusys.edu> <NnVc7.112103$Cy.15891239@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KAjd7.58670$jA2.5313318@news20.bellglobal.com> <ybld7.118809$Cy.16777519@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3B7687F3.A370BB3B@damaru.com> <3b76d3b0.164437755@news-server> <q7udntgtpl6mfl6ofo21d4folaip8fngm2@4ax.com>`

```
Dear All,
   Thank you very much for all your comments. At first, I though COBOL
is almost a dead language.  Now, I have known that I'm totally wrong.
COBOL is not a dead language and it will live long becasue of many
reasons such as the ability of itself, the existing COBOL code, the
improvement since 1960s and a lot of profressionals who have rich of
knowledge, experience and skillful like all you. Only one question,
which is still in my mind, that is the problem about young blood COBOL
programmers. Some universities have removed COBOL subject from their
universities and replaced it by some fashionable languages because of
marketing reasons. How many of you and your colleagues are young blood
COBOL programmers? I guess there are a few.
Regards,

Anote.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T18:58:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QrVd7.7721$Ki1.646167@bgtnsc06-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com> <3B73FD0C.7C799B07@ils-international.com> <3B7413F1.8FFF219E@cusys.edu> <NnVc7.112103$Cy.15891239@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KAjd7.58670$jA2.5313318@news20.bellglobal.com> <ybld7.118809$Cy.16777519@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <3B7687F3.A370BB3B@damaru.com> <3b76d3b0.164437755@news-server> <q7udntgtpl6mfl6ofo21d4folaip8fngm2@4ax.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108130542.199e2116@posting.google.com>`

```

Anote Sangkunakupt <anote_un@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:7ce2d2dc.0108130542.199e2116@posting.google.com...
> Dear All,
>    Thank you very much for all your comments. At first, I though COBOL
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Anote.

Anote,

Young blood, I'm not. But before there were books on the subject I thought I
tought about 600 data processing (tab and assembly) people COBOL, and
because of the size of the language then, it took ONE WEEK. Certainly, those
people had already some idea about fields, records, files, and systems.
Because many users of COBOL use inhouse standards for a variety of reasons,
they might still be doing the teaching themselves.

It could be that because you can buy a book including a compiler and learn
it yourself, the higher learning people want to provide some fodder for the
employers who have not as yet begun to understand the investment they are
going to make using the latest and probably the proprietary language of some
vendor. In fact the managers of such companies my not be dry behind the ears
yet. It seems that many are just learning that their data (including their
procedures) are assets also.

Warren Simmons
```

```
On Mon, 13 Aug 2001 11:57:38 -0400, SkippyPB
<swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>On Sun, 12 Aug 2001 17:52:36 -0400, Robaire
><robaire.spamoff@sympatico.ca> enlightened us:
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
>as notable.  

No guarantee, just a very high probability.
When you write proposals for customers, do you include a list of past
accomplishments in order to convince them that your next project
(theirs) will be "as successful"? QED

>In addition, do you think every single person that ever worked with
>Turbo Pascal or Delphi were totally enthralled with it?

Is that your measure of success - 
satisfaction all of the people all of the time?

>>To what specifically can you point that is so "disgusting" in C# that
>>one might think that he blew it this time?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I find RPG disgusting.

Why not be specific - otherwise your comments are useless to us.
If we knew you personally, we might say "Oh really? Aaah, that's too
bad." But we don't, so we don't care (nothing personal intended).

> There are those who are in love with it.
I guess they don't get out much.

>It is a matter of personal taste and has nothing whatsoever to do with
>the skill level of the author.  I believe the same is true for Mr/Ms
>Cuplan and C# (is that really pronounced C Sharp?)
Yes, with a lower-case "s" ;-)

<snipped 19-line signature>

I infer from your comments that the process of designing an integrated
development environment for computer programming is to be evaluated
with the same guidelines as those used to evaluate art - in your case
these guideline seem to be based on "taste".

Vague concepts such as "personal taste", "disgust", "being entralled"
are not of much use when judging the efficiency and efficacy of
software. When you deliver a COBOL application to a client, do you ask
the client to sign-off to the effect that s/he is "enthralled" with
the new accounting system? ( Picture it ... )

Mr/Ms Cuplan made what I consider a vague and disparaging statement
about a product; I would expect Cuplan to stand up and explain the
reasoning behind this statement; and if that reasoning is based on
more vague concepts like "taste", then I'll discount his/her opinions
in the futur.

If not based on facts to back them up, comments are just that -
comments - and I will ignore them, just as the compiler ignores
comments, because they won't be used in the processing of data into
information.

R. Miller ( Robaire )

P.S.
I would love to see DD address these (Cuplan's, SkippyPB's, and mine)
posts.

R.M.
```

<robaire.spamoff@sympatico.ca> enlightened us:

You are totally missing the point of my post.  You were taking Cuplan
to task for disliking a computer language.  You insinuated that this
was a mistake because the author of C# was successful writing other
computer languages.  You insinuated that Cuplan did not have the right
to be critical of C# because of this.  I was trying to point out to
you, unsuccessfully I guess, that a person's like or dislike of a
computer language is similar to a person's like or dislike of a
particular artist.  


>>>To what specifically can you point that is so "disgusting" in C# that
>>>one might think that he blew it this time?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Okay if you insist.  I find RPG disgusting because of it's structure.
I haven't programmed in it since college days, but I really detested
filing out all of those different forms to do different activities.
And I disliked having to remember all of those levels.  The
programming, frankley, was tedious and that's why I found it
disgusting.

>> There are those who are in love with it.
>I guess they don't get out much.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No where did I say anything about designing of anything.  LIke I said,
you totally missed the point.

>Vague concepts such as "personal taste", "disgust", "being entralled"
>are not of much use when judging the efficiency and efficacy of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"Personal taste", "disgust", "enthralled" are not vague concepts but
well known and defined adjectives.

>Mr/Ms Cuplan made what I consider a vague and disparaging statement
>about a product; I would expect Cuplan to stand up and explain the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well maybe Cu plan could be more descriptive but that is no reason for
you to attack him the way you did.  This is, after all, an opinion
board and that was his opinion.  Since no one is getting paid for
this, you got what you paid for.

>If not based on facts to back them up, comments are just that -
>comments - and I will ignore them, just as the compiler ignores
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>R.M.

Well now I know you are new to this board.  Otherwise you'd know two
things:

1)  If it is in Doc's interest, he'll make his opinions known in his
own inimitable way;

2)  It's not a good thing to rouse Doc from his usual comatose state

On Tue, 14 Aug 2001 11:45:01 -0400, SkippyPB
<swiegand@nospam.neo.rr.com> wrote:

>On Mon, 13 Aug 2001 13:45:38 -0400, Robaire
><robaire.spamoff@sympatico.ca> enlightened us:
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
>You are totally missing the point of my post. 

Maybe I did - what was the point of your post?

>You were taking Cuplan
>to task for disliking a computer language.

No I wasn't. The following question appeared in my original post:

  =To what specifically can you point that is so "disgusting" in
    C# that one might think that he blew it this time?=

If I was taking him to task for anything, it was for making an
unsupported emotional qualification of a product that I would not
expect to elicit such highly charged reactions as "disgust" - I have
great respect for the talent of the person who produced it and I think
that asking Cuplan to elaborate on his statement was not out of line.

If someone said "C# has a limited library of functions", I might have
asked that person "limited in what way". No one would have found that
out of line. But to ask someone to explain their public rejection,
based completely emotional grounds, of a product, is unacceptable?

> You insinuated that this
>was a mistake because the author of C# was successful writing other
>computer languages.

I didn't "insinuate" anything - I stated mhy position - I expect C#
has a high probability of being a successful product, and asked Cuplan
if he would explain his statement about C# being "disgusting".
Everything clear, up front, supported - no insinuations.

>You insinuated that Cuplan did not have the right
>to be critical of C# because of this.
Once again, no insinuation. I stated my position, asked that he
clarify his. Please point out, in my post, where I denied Cuplan the
right to be critical of C#.

> I was trying to point out to
>you, unsuccessfully I guess, that a person's like or dislike of a
>computer language is similar to a person's like or dislike of a
>particular artist.  
This is what I understood about your taste :  Something in the line of
"I know what I like, and I don't like that." I have stated my position
with respect to taste.

>>>>To what specifically can you point that is so "disgusting" in C# that
>>>>one might think that he blew it this time?
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>No where did I say anything about designing of anything.

Allow me to quote you here:
  "Just because someone has designed (sic) and implemented 
   successful computer languages in the past does not guarantee 
   future endeavors will be just as notable.  "

Then, you brought in Picasso and Salvador Dali

> LIke I said, you totally missed the point.

>>Vague concepts such as "personal taste", "disgust", "being entralled"
>>are not of much use when judging the efficiency and efficacy of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>well known and defined adjectives.

Just qualifying something with an adjective doesn't mean it's
justified. If I don't spontaneously agree with that qualification, I
think I can ask for clarification - that's what I did. 
>
>>Mr/Ms Cuplan made what I consider a vague and disparaging statement
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>you to attack him the way you did.

According to you I ATTACKED him! 
Is this 

  "To what specifically can you point that is so "disgusting" in C#
   that one might think that he blew it this time?"

really what you consider an attack?

>This is, after all, an opinion
>board and that was his opinion.

In this, a technical newsgroup, I expect discussions in which the
participants, most of who are professionnals, are ready to back up
their claims with evidence if they are contested. If I want unfounded
opinions on anything, there are more appropriate newsgroups for that.
Now that is my opinion, and I have stated the reasoning behind it.

> Since no one is getting paid for
>this, you got what you paid for.
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, now you will know that I am not new to this group - I have been
following it for a number of years ( I recall when DD signed DocDwarf,
not NA  ) and I have probably changed my signature after changing
ISPs. And I will add that Doc's exchanges were, a number of times, the
only reason I would read this group - I just hope he doesn't blush now
- I hate it when he gets gushy.

Regards,

Robaire ( R. Miller )

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T13:17:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l8jv2$2v0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com> <3B73FD0C.7C799B07@ils-international.com> <3B7413F1.8FFF219E@cusys.edu> <NnVc7.112103$Cy.15891239@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KAjd7.58670$jA2.5313318@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

On 11-Aug-2001, "Dr Hackenbush" <hugo.z.hackenbush@sympatico.ca> wrote:

> And if you believe all of that, you will enjoy which computer language ?
>
> COBOL!


Of course, what I really enjoy is being productive in my work environment.
 The old saw about C being a "write only" language doesn't mean that C is
not a fun language to write - but it indicates that it may not be a
productive language to maintain.

Cobol has a long tradition of being maintained.  Some people say that's its
weakness - we have 30 year old programs out there, patched again and again.
 But who is willing to spend the time and money to rewrite when it is
cheaper to modify?

I haven't seen that Java has particular advantages in maintainability.  (For
that matter, I don't see that OO Cobol has these advantages).  Maybe I just
need to be educated - maybe better techniques and tools will come as
existing code ages.

We all know that new projects are more fun than maintenance.   But
maintenance will be required in good times and bad.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T11:23:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108131023.36103497@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com> <3B73FD0C.7C799B07@ils-international.com> <3B7413F1.8FFF219E@cusys.edu> <NnVc7.112103$Cy.15891239@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KAjd7.58670$jA2.5313318@news20.bellglobal.com> <9l8jv2$2v0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote 
> <snip>

> I haven't seen that Java has particular advantages in maintainability.  (For
> that matter, I don't see that OO Cobol has these advantages).  Maybe I just
> need to be educated - maybe better techniques and tools will come as
> existing code ages.
> 
<snip>

"Ravioli code" and "Dumpling Stew" are two terms I have seen for badly
written (usually not COBOL, YET!) OO programs.  The real key to good
maintainability is going to be library management. And that will
include good, cross-referenced, visually readable directories of
re-usable objects.

Style will make a difference as well.  These modules will need
extensive comments, and really good naming conventions.

Stephen J Spiro

PS: The WG4 meeting will be in Las Vegas in November.  Room discounts
are available for delegates, pickets, protestors and demonstrators.
```

A) However, please be aware that ALL WG4 meetings are *CLOSED*.  If you
aren't on an "official" WG4 delegation list (from an appropriate national
Standards body) you are NOT welcome in these meetings.

B) J4 meetings *are* open.  There will be days of J4 meetings and days of
WG4 meetings.  If you ARE interested in attending the J4 portion of the
meetings, then do contact the J4 chair for additional information.

C) These two "co-located" meetings are the week before COMDEX, so if you are
interested in COBOL and plan on attending COMDEX, you might want to look
into attending "some" of the J4 meetings.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL dead or alive?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T17:22:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B795ED1.73D8DEF2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7ce2d2dc.0108081020.53affdb1@posting.google.com> <3B71B89E.B96CB47E@dazoo.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108090454.3d43daa1@posting.google.com> <Bexc7.238002$2O6.15088158@news2.aus1.giganews.com> <7ce2d2dc.0108100444.6089a07e@posting.google.com> <3B73FD0C.7C799B07@ils-international.com> <3B7413F1.8FFF219E@cusys.edu> <NnVc7.112103$Cy.15891239@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <KAjd7.58670$jA2.5313318@news20.bellglobal.com> <9l8jv2$2v0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <4145699b.0108131023.36103497@posting.google.com> <3B78AB99.6ED3997@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```


Peter Lacey wrote:

> Stephen J Spiro wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

I wasn't going to comment on Stephen's original observations because I agree with
him entirely. Similarly your observation is correct - with regard to OO COBOL.

Shortly J4 will be discussing a very important issue for OO COBOL - Standard
classes including collections/dictionaries. To avoid the plethora of duplication
that you refer to, OO COBOL must concentrate on REUSABILITY, i.e. arrive at a
logical hierarchy for Collections so that subclasses can use 'omnibus' methods
from classes higher up in the hierarchical chain. The other factor is they (J4)
must ignore the temptation, "Let's include this, not quite sure what it does, but
it looks like it would be nice to have". There must be a concrete case, with
EXAMPLES,  made for every class used in the standard library.

A further point is that method-names used must be UNAMBIGUOUS. If you and I are
going to use OO, then we shouldn't have to guess at the meaning of methods
described as "isBag", "asBag" - these terse names have been 'inherited' from the
other crypto-OO languages. OK, so critics claim COBOL is verbose - let's use that
to our advantage and make the two preceding method-names clearer - "isItABag",
"ConvertToABag"

Don't sweat it Stephen - I'm preparing a discussion document for J4 in response
to J4/01-0009. <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

---

<a id="t-be72c78235"></a>
## Thread: Perplexing problem on 2 different Alpha OpenVMS platforms — 13 msgs

### Perplexing problem on 2 different Alpha OpenVMS platforms

- **From:** robin.brady@ssb.state.tx.us (Robin Brady)
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T12:11:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<17e7dda1.0108101111.7d5a11e0@posting.google.com>`

```
The following block of code is currently running in a program on an
Alpha OpenVMS v 7.1-1H1 with Digital Cobol v 2.5.

WHO is defined as PIC X(12) value spaces.

    IF WHO = "MS" OR "LG" OR "NC" OR "DD" OR "LY" OR "MG" OR "JR" OR
             "MEH" OR "KK" OR "RB" OR "MIS" OR "PJV" OR "RTS" OR "NJH"
        MOVE "L" TO USER-DIV
    ELSE
    IF WHO = "JN" OR "DF" OR "EW" OR "BL"
        MOVE "S" TO USER-DIV
    ELSE
    IF WHO = "SY" OR "LA"
        MOVE "A" TO USER-DIV
    ELSE
        GO TO 999-END.

When this same program is compiled on an Alpha OpenVMS 7.3 system
running Compaq Cobol v 2.7, there is no compilation or run-time error
but this same block of code does not execute correctly.  Every time it
will take the last ELSE branch which does a STOP RUN.

If I comment out the above IF block and insert the following simple IF
it works fine:

    IF WHO = "SY"
       MOVE "L" TO USER-DIV
    ELSE
       GO TO 999-END.

Also, if I change the PIC format to X(3) it works fine with the more
complex IF block.

Anyone have any ideas?  It's been awhile since I've used Cobol so I
may be missing something that is readily apparent to everyone (but
me).

Robin
```

#### ↳ Re: Perplexing problem on 2 different Alpha OpenVMS platforms

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-10T19:50:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bWWc7.112746$Cy.15939171@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<17e7dda1.0108101111.7d5a11e0@posting.google.com>`

```
If it always takes the last IF, look for a misplaced period. Also
display USER-DIV in every leg of the statement. You might also
play with the unititialised 9 bytes of WHO.

But ultimately, I'd use a definition of just the 3 bytes you want in
the compare, rather than 12. Even if you get it working this tine,
looking at 12 bytes for a 3 byte value leaves a risk that some future
upgrade of COBOL or app code will invalidate this.

    or (WHO-3 = "MEH", or MIS" etc)

```
Robin - I have access to several VMS COBOL compilers.  Can you send me a little
tiny sample program illustrating this?  Just make a tiny program with this code
that breaks and I'll do some testing.

PS - COULD it be that the thing populating the field has changed somehow?  Are
you using Oracle or some other RDB to populate the WHO field?

Given the information you have given us, the length of the field for a literal
compare SHOULD NOT MATTER - if the field is filled with trailing spaces.  If,
however, the field is filled with trailing low-values - the code behaves
correctly.


In article <17e7dda1.0108101111.7d5a11e0@posting.google.com>, Robin Brady
says...
>
>The following block of code is currently running in a program on an
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>Robin

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Perplexing problem on 2 different Alpha OpenVMS platforms

- **From:** Robin <rlb@austin.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-11T04:01:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B74AE0F.CC5D9C76@austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<17e7dda1.0108101111.7d5a11e0@posting.google.com> <vwXc7.5574$NJ6.26727@www.newsranger.com>`

```
You've peaked my curiosity about what is populating this field.  It
is indeed being populated via a host language interface call to an
old database called System 1032.  Nothing in the actual code has
changed, I'm just migrating data and applications to this new
hardware.  I'll investigate further to see how the parameters being
passed back and forth to the 1032 procedure are formatted.  I'll get
to work on a small sample of code to send you.

Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Robin - I have access to several VMS COBOL compilers.  Can you send me a little
…[51 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Robin

if your version of COBOL allows try using-
IF WHO(1:3) = "MS" OR "LG" OR "NC" OR "DD" OR "LY" OR "MG" OR "JR" OR
                        "MEH" OR "KK" OR "RB" OR "MIS" OR "PJV" OR "RTS" OR
"NJH"
       MOVE "L" TO USER-DIV
.............

This then avoids the worry about what is in the last 9 bytes of the field

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Perplexing problem on 2 different Alpha OpenVMS platforms

- **From:** spambait@milmac.com (Douglas Miller)
- **Date:** 2001-08-18T20:15:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lmidm$23l@dispatch.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<17e7dda1.0108101111.7d5a11e0@posting.google.com> <9lkj81$j7l$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
In article <9lkj81$j7l$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk>, "Jason P. Hudson" <jason@44sycamore.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:
>if your version of COBOL allows try using-
>IF WHO(1:3) = "MS" OR "LG" OR "NC" OR "DD" OR "LY" OR "MG" OR "JR" OR
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>This then avoids the worry about what is in the last 9 bytes of the field

One of us is missing something here.

Unless the last nine bytes of the field are never blank, and the third byte 
always is, I don't think that's going to help at all, and for the same reason 
that the original code doesn't work: when comparing two character fields of 
different lengths, Cobol pads the shorter one on the right with spaces to the 
length of the longer one.

Hence, the original statement is equivalent to

IF WHO = "MS          " OR "LG          " OR "NC          " ... etc.

and your suggestion is basically the same thing, with only one trailing space 
instead of ten.

Try this instead:

* Test two-letter combinations
IF (WHO(1:2) = "MS" OR "LG" OR "NC" OR ...  etc.)
* Test three-letter combinations separately
OR (WHO(1:3) = "MEH" OR "MIS" OR "PJV" OR ... etc.)
        MOVE "L" TO USER-DIV
ELSE
        IF WHO(1:2) = "JN" OR "DF" OR "EW" OR "BL"
                MOVE "S" TO USER-DIV
        ELSE
                IF WHO(1:2) = "SY" OR "LA"
                        MOVE "A" TO USER-DIV
                ELSE
                        GO TO 999-END.

>
>Robin Brady <robin.brady@ssb.state.tx.us> wrote in message
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Perplexing problem on 2 different Alpha OpenVMS platforms

- **From:** Robin <rlb@austin.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T02:41:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B7F274B.79D92934@austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<17e7dda1.0108101111.7d5a11e0@posting.google.com> <9lkj81$j7l$1@news6.svr.pol.co.uk> <9lmidm$23l@dispatch.concentric.net>`

```
I wanted to thank everyone who replied to my post and offered their
assistance.  What I ended up doing was changing the format of WHO to
PIC X(3).

Thane Hubbell offered to compile this code on several different
compilers but when I tried to duplicate the problem within a small
"test" program, it ran fine.  I think the problem is that this field
is populated from an RDB (System 1032, tho'I doubt anyone has heard
of it). The code would only fail when I left in the call to the
external PL1032 procedure that passed that string back in an output
parameter.

I thought that was a good explanation although I can't specifically
narrow it down because I'm still wondering why the code works fine
even with just 2 characters in the field.  Won't work with 3
characters in a PIC X(12) but will with 2 characters in a PIC X(3). 
Go figure.

Robin
Austin, TX

"NJH"

IF

just wondering about this.  I learned this lesson using Oracle - where
comparisons would fail because the precompiler rtrims values - but my
comparisons didn't take that into account.  I think the reason that the two byte
values worked is because of how the system might trim.  (It might leave one
trailing space before inserting the low-value characters).  This is one of those
things that makes you go hmmmmm....

#### ↳ Re: ICL COBOL

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T07:51:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B7778E1.837C9705@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<nzZc7.15982$tq.1749039@news6-win.server.ntlworld.com>`

```
dw.norris wrote:
> 
> I am learning ICL Cobol 74.  I have heard there are compilers available
> which would allow me to work from home a little to get practice.  Anyone got
> any links?

ICL wrote many Cobol compilers for many different systems: 1900 series,
System 4, 2903, 2900 VME (C2), ME29 (XEC2), System 10, 1500 series.

They also used MicroFocus CIS Cobol on DRS20, Level II Cobol on PC2 and
Quattro and as Microbuild on DRS300.  MF Cobol/2 found its way onto Unix
based DRS systems too.  

Microsoft Cobol-80 was also used in ICL for some POS systems.

Which particular system were you targetting ?  Which old ICL systems are
still running using 'Cobol 74' ?
```

<a id="t-c09ad30c9e"></a>
## Thread: Beginner Problems Acucobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Beginner Problems Acucobol

- **From:** "Stephan Wiesner" <stephan@stephanwiesner.de>
- **Date:** 2001-08-11T09:53:48+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l2o98$t8d$05$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hi, I'm studying computerscience and have to do a homework in Cobol.
My prof. just threw Acucobol 5.1 at us. No handbook, just some very basic
lessons.

Now I'm stuck.
How can I read what a user wrote into a Entry Field?

Is there a handbook or a userguide or something out there I can use with
Acucobol?

Stephan
```

#### ↳ Re: Beginner Problems Acucobol

- **From:** "Acucorp News" <shaun_gough@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T16:07:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1451C740A890D411B65C00104B95B1C03147BB@ras.acucorp.com>`
- **References:** `<9l2o98$t8d$05$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Dear Stephan.

You can verify the contents of the data entered into an ENTRY-FIELD by
checking the contents of the WORKING-STORAGE item that you defines for the
ENTRY-FIELD.

For example, your program should look like the following:

 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 entry-field-data       PIC X(10).

 SCREEN SECTION.
 01 user-screen.
    03 ENTRY-FIELD  USING entry-field-data, SIZE 10, LINE 5 COLUMN 10.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
 Main Section.
   DISPLAY user-screen.
   ACCEPT user-screen.

You can verify the contents of the ENTRY-FIELD by checking the contents of
the WORKING-STORAGE item entry-field-data in this example.

Thanks
Shaun Gough
ACUCORP Inc.
"Stephan Wiesner" <stephan@stephanwiesner.de> wrote in message
news:9l2o98$t8d$05$1@news.t-online.com...
> Hi, I'm studying computerscience and have to do a homework in Cobol.
> My prof. just threw Acucobol 5.1 at us. No handbook, just some very basic
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

>Thought Z means that the '0' are suppressed. Doesn't make sense if I don't
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

A numeric edited field is not anymore numeric but alphanumeric. Once edited
it can contain blanks, decimal points, commas, plus and minus signs and
currency signs.

Brainbench MVP for COBOL II

different animal.  There's nothing wrong with using ZZZ as the destination for a
MOVE, a GIVING or a COMPUTE.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-11T16:16:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GTcd7.6260$NJ6.27832@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9l2slb$2gb$03$1@news.t-online.com> <9l3g16$257$1@suaar1ac.prod.compuserve.com> <9l3gdr$6mc$00$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hi Stephen,

A few things:

Your hmh cannot be used in a calculation because it it is not a pure numeric
field. Create a numeric field and perform the calc on that field. Move it to the
num ed field when you're ready to display it. 

Your hmh does not contain a  value, nor is one moved to it. You can include a
VALUE clause to the data def or move the value to it.

Numeric edited fields are used for display (or print) purposes, so numeric data
should be moved to them just before display. Any calculations s/b performed on
the numeric version of the field; when display is required, then the numeric
field s/b moved to the numeric edited field first. 

Regards, Jack.


n article <9l3gdr$6mc$00$1@news.t-online.com>, Stephan Wiesner says...
>
>Okay, here it goes.
…[63 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields

- **From:** christer.o.jonsson@home.se (Christer O. Jönsson)
- **Date:** 2001-08-11T12:50:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d46942c6.0108111150.5acdf8f8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9l2slb$2gb$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
"Stephan Wiesner" <stephan@stephanwiesner.de> wrote in message news:<9l2slb$2gb$03$1@news.t-online.com>...
> I read that I can edit numeric fields like this:
> 01 myNumber Pic ZZZ.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Stephan

Hi

Your problem is't the picture ZZZ it's that you try to do calculation
on it, the Z picture is for display/printing and only semi numeric and
can't be used for calculation. You have too have a pic 999 for the
calculation and later move the result into the ZZZ picture.

//Christer O. Jonsson
```

#### ↳ Re: Numeric Edited Fields

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-11T16:45:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l4936$rhq$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9l2slb$2gb$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
A number of replies have talked about the fact that you can't use
numeric-edited fields in computations.  I did want to add one additional bit
of information on this.  It is possible to NOT explicitly move a numeric
edited field to a numeric field to use it in an arithmetic expression *IF*
you use the NumVal intrinsic function (which is probably NOT something that
a new COBOL student would come across).  The following is perfectly valid
(conforming) source code - assuming you have a compiler that supports the
latest ANSI/ISO Standard.

01  NumField-1   Pic S99v99   Value Zero.
01  NumEd-2      Pic  +999,999.99   Value Zero.
01  NumEd-3      Pic   zzz                 Value Zero.
   ...
*  do something to get non-zero values into NumEd-2.

Compute NumEd-3 NumField-1 = (Function NumVal (NumEd-2)  + 123.4) / 100
  On Size Error
         Display "something"
  Not On Size Error
         Display "something else"
 End-Compute

This shows that a numeric-edited field can ALWAYS be the receiving item of a
COMPUTE statement - and that it can also be used as a "sending" field when
it is used in the NumVal intrinsic function.

Having said this, it is OFTEN (possibly usually) appropriate to keep "two"
copies of numeric fields - one edited and one not.  Whether this is
appropriate depends on the application logic needed for your program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T16:19:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a0yd7.1390$4M3.283745@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com> <Xxvd7.3931$ym4.153881@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9l62ue$gh8$05$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
As others have pointed out, limits on table sizes in either the FILE SECTION
or WORKING-STORAGE SECTION are a function of the compiler, not the COBOL
language per se.

Depending on your OS and your compiler, there are 'tricks' you can use to
simulate tables larger than the maximum permitted by the compiler; or system
services which allow you to allocate and access more memory.

You say you are using AccuCobol as a compiler; in what environment are you
running?

And, is this table part of an FD in the FILE SECTION? If so, you may be able
to the file differently - that is, in smaller pieces - and avoid compiler
limitations that way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

was never done before 1985?  COBOL (at least most dialects of COBOL) did
not support tables/arrays greater than 32K until then, you know.

>So, you mean there is no way for me to use more memory?

AccuCOBOL... but again, it might be a Good Thing, *just* for the exercise
of it, to try and figure out how you might do something under the
limitations that existed before you were alive... for longer than you have
been alive.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T07:38:49+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B7775F9.F4338251@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com> <Xxvd7.3931$ym4.153881@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9l62ue$gh8$05$1@news.t-online.com> <Y9yd7.3940$ym4.154366@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
> Stephan Wiesner <stephan@stephanwiesner.de> wrote:
> >I wasn't even born in '68 and now I want big
> >arrays.

Are you using a student or other limited version ?

Large arrays are often _not_ required in Cobol systems.  Many systems
require data to be shared with other users.  Locking data up in an
internal array does not allow it to be shared.  Consider an ISAM file or
adding an alternate key to an existing file to give access to the data
items that you need while allowing others to access the same data.

NA Wrote:
> Many people want something which makes up for shortcomings they have...
> what are you doing that is so new, so unusual, so... unprecedented that it
> was never done before 1985?  COBOL (at least most dialects of COBOL) did
> not support tables/arrays greater than 32K until then, you know.

I think that this says more about the limitations of your experience
than it does about any limitations in actual Cobol.

Cobol 74 was limited to 3 dimensions but had no restriction on any
sizing.  Implementations may have limited the number of _occurances_ to
32768, but a size limit of 32k bytes was certainly not in 'most'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T15:28:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l8rkq$64c$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com> <Xxvd7.3931$ym4.153881@iad-read.news.verio.net> <9l62ue$gh8$05$1@news.t-online.com> <Y9yd7.3940$ym4.154366@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
If your system won't handle large enough tables, consider alternatives.

Probably the best alternative is an indexed file, but multiple tables could
work as well.

I have seen large tables using the property that Cobol doesn't check for
table overflow (have a table followed by a filler), but I strongly
discourage this.   There is no guarantee that the compiler keeps memory
where you think it is.

Describe your table needs.
```

#### ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

<stephan@stephanwiesner.de> wrote:

> Why (or how) can't I use tables with entries that exceed 32K?
> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

What compiler are you using?

I think that limitation is from the compiler. On all the versions of 
Microfocus COBOL I've used (from WB version 3.X to NE 3.1) I have been
able to allocate tables of 500KBytes and more. IBM's VACOBOL has no 
problem with that either.

Is the table allocated in an 01 that is part of an FD record 
definition? Those are limited by the maximum record size imposed by 
the file handler for the compiler.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T18:25:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tSzd7.7145$NJ6.28525@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-FIl3dw1bdVLr@thishost> <9l63u8$8i4$06$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hi Stephen,

I don't know if you realize it, but you've just been DDed.

When we were using mainframe compilers w/a 32K tbl limit, we'd define the tbl
w/enough occurrances to slip under the limit, then immediately follow it w/a
field big enough to contain the rest of the occurrances. e.g.:

01 tbl.
05  tbl-entry occurs 800 times    pic x(40).
05  tbl-pad-1                              pic x(10000). 
05  tbl-pad-2                               etc
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ( NA)
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T23:06:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1_Dd7.3959$ym4.155211@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
In article <tSzd7.7145$NJ6.28525@www.newsranger.com>,
John H Sleight Jr  <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote:
>Hi Stephen,
>
>I don't know if you realize it, but you've just been DDed.

I didn't know that my initials had become a verb!

>
>When we were using mainframe compilers w/a 32K tbl limit, we'd define the tbl
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>05  tbl-pad-2                               etc

I used similar methods in the Oldene Dayse... but I always made sure that
things would line up to the various words *and* that the
overflow-table-areas would be labelled and defined as the original, viz.:

*CHANGING TBL-1 LAYOUT REQUIRES CHANGING ALL OVERFLOW LAYOUTS
 01  TBL-1.
     05  TBL-1-ENTRY OCCURS 3200.
         10  TBL-1-ENTRY-CUSTNO PIC X(05).
         10  TBL-1-ENTRY-ZIPCOD PIX X(05).
*CHANGING TBL-1 LAYOUT REQUIRES CHANGING ALL OVERFLOW LAYOUTS
 01  TBL-1-OFLOW-1.
    05  TBL-1-OFLOW-1-ENTRY OCCURS 3200.
        10  TBL-1-OFLOW-1-CUSTNO PIC X(05).
        10  TBL-1-OFLOW-2-ZIPCOD PIC X(05).
... etc.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T11:03:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xuOd7.30$8E3.13047@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-FIl3dw1bdVLr@thishost> <9l63u8$8i4$06$1@news.t-online.com> <tSzd7.7145$NJ6.28525@www.newsranger.com> <1_Dd7.3959$ym4.155211@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
With the newer IBM mainframe optimizers, you need to make sure you make a
reference to the overflow table "somewhere" in your code or it will be
dropped.

You used to be able to get away with...

01  TABLE-1.
     05 TABLE-ELEMENT   PIC X(2) OCCURS 16000.
01  FILLER                       PIC X(32000).

 .. and refer to TABLE-ELEMENT (21000), which would be stored 10000 bytes
(5000 * 2) into the FILLER area

Can't do that no more.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T14:26:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l99md$l43$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-FIl3dw1bdVLr@thishost> <9l63u8$8i4$06$1@news.t-online.com> <tSzd7.7145$NJ6.28525@www.newsranger.com> <1_Dd7.3959$ym4.155211@iad-read.news.verio.net> <xuOd7.30$8E3.13047@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Even if you DO reference the 2nd table, IBM explicitly does NOT guarantee
that 2 01-levels in Working-Storage will be located at addresses in virtual
storage that follow each other.  This is an INCREDIBLY dangerous technique.
As others have indicated, the size of table (now 16Meg - as I recall) really
should make this technique (two tables handled by the same subscript)
unneeded as well as inadvisable.
```

Sorry Stephan, but I do not think you understood Lorne's question (and
comment) regarding your question.Is that table part of working storage, or
part fo a file definition?  I am pretty sure that I have worked with tables
bigger than 32Kb.  If it were part of a file record, then I would question
the wisdom of defining a file record with a a 30 bytes field occurring 2000
times, instead of having this stored in 2000 records of 30 bytes (plus the
key). Have we already forgotten about normalization?

Regards,
Luis

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T14:39:27+00:00

- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com> <ZpzG4UNLyRNq-pn2-FIl3dw1bdVLr@thishost>`

```
I hit a 32K limit in MVS a couple of years ago - at that time
I just figured it was a limit making records less than 1 track, as
I saw even tape VB records limited to a max 32,760. I don't
recall a compile error indicating that it was a compiler limit.

Oh - if you're in MVS, IMS can't cope with GSAM files that
big. That I'm sure of.

As for the 32K, the first machine I used was in 1979. It was a
Rockwell Aim 65 with 1K.

#### ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T14:40:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l6m5i$893$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
The limits for various parts of COBOL *all* depend on the compiler and
operating system.

IBM's OS/VS COBOL compiler *did* have a 32k limit for tables.  However, that
hasn't been their strategic compiler for a couple of decades now.  There
are, however, a number of compilers that have "options" to emulate it.

One problem that people often (occasionally?) have is to try and use a
"table" where they should be use a file (with separate records) - often an
indexed file.  Or even a SQL database.

In general, when posting to CLC, it helps if you mention the compiler,
release/version, and operating system.  Also, if you are having a
compile-time problem, it helps to show the source code and the actual
message from the compiler.  There MIGHT be a way around your current problem
(without going to another compiler) - but with the information posted so
far, I don't know what it would be.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T07:34:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B778298.FA610191@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com> <9l6m5i$893$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> The limits for various parts of COBOL *all* depend on the compiler and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> are, however, a number of compilers that have "options" to emulate it.

When I last worked with OS/VS COBOL version 2.4, it had a limit of
128K for an 01 record.  We hit that limit with a large table in one
program.  We split the table into two, with some fields in the first
table and the rest of the fields in the second table, so we could use
the same subscript for both tables.

> 
> One problem that people often (occasionally?) have is to try and use a
> "table" where they should be use a file (with separate records) - often an
> indexed file.  Or even a SQL database.

Wasn't there for a long time in MVS a limit of 32K on a file block
size?  Something to do with the maximum buffer size for one EXCP?

> 
> In general, when posting to CLC, it helps if you mention the compiler,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> far, I don't know what it would be.

Always good advice.
```

#### ↳ Re: File record exceeds 32K

- **From:** "Glenn A. Mitchell" <g.mitchell@computer.org>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T02:01:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ihjg7.318943$EF2.40334761@typhoon.nyroc.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<9l5gr9$m0a$05$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
I gave this a try in the latest (5.1?) version of AcuCOBOL on my PC and got
no error messages.

AcuCOBOL does support (at least at some version level) a C-like ALLOC/FREE
functionality which will allow you to allocate a large block of storage,
store and retrieve from it, and release it.  Depending on what you're up to,
that might fit the bill.

Good luck.
```

---

## Thread: PersonalCOBOL help — 9 msgs

### PersonalCOBOL help

- **From:** "Col" <psxzone@blueyonder.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T17:07:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5Jyd7.23735$pv6.2160029@news1.cableinet.net>`

```
Hi. I`m a student who was originally using a Fujitsu compiler to test my
COBOL programs were working correctly. As I have moved on in the course I
really needed to use a MicroFocus version of the compiler due to Fujitsu`s
way of handling Indexed files. So I purchased the student version of
Personal COBOL V3.4 from Merant. My problem is that I can`t seem to find any
way of altering the output to the printer, and my lines of text are spilling
onto the next line making the report very messy. I have a line output width
of over 100 chars which would be very awkward to alter to the standard 80.
I`ve  looked in all the usual places but can`t find anything related. I
really don`t want to have to redesign my report layout. Can anyone help. Am
I`m specific enough for anyone to help?

Thank in advance

Col.

ps. I`m using:
SELECT Print-File ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
not ASSIGN TO "lpt1:".
```

Fujitsu's
> > and MicroFocus' versions differ in the handling of indexed files?

> I took it that he was saying Fujitsu couldn't read/write MF ISAM
files
> (or vv).

Oh. OK, that makes more sense. Actually, I was pretty impressed with
FJ ISAM; for example the ISAM file being WAY smaller than the
sequential file used to build it - due to, I found out, internal file
compression on the part of the FJ file routines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PersonalCOBOL help

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Col" <psxzone@blueyonder.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-08-15T08:14:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Qbqe7.6071$6R6.611652@news1.cableinet.net>`
- **References:** `<5Jyd7.23735$pv6.2160029@news1.cableinet.net> <ssAd7.148623$li.10571168@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3B777659.B7C2E661@Azonic.co.nz> <izYd7.153912$li.11157616@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
I found that FJ did quite a few things different to the `standard` COBOL,
features that are also an advantage, which is OK if you only want to use
programs and data with FJ, but can be a bit of pain if your source has to
work with different compilers. I personally prefer to use the FJ compiler
against the MF compiler but unfortunately I have little choice!

Thanks anyway.

Colonel.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PersonalCOBOL help

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-15T22:21:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pBCe7.14991$VV1.847527@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<5Jyd7.23735$pv6.2160029@news1.cableinet.net> <ssAd7.148623$li.10571168@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3B777659.B7C2E661@Azonic.co.nz> <izYd7.153912$li.11157616@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <Qbqe7.6071$6R6.611652@news1.cableinet.net>`

```

"Col" <psxzone@blueyonder.co.uk> wrote in message
news:Qbqe7.6071$6R6.611652@news1.cableinet.net...
> I found that FJ did quite a few things different to the `standard`
COBOL,
> features that are also an advantage, which is OK if you only want to
use
> programs and data with FJ, but can be a bit of pain if your source
has to
> work with different compilers. I personally prefer to use the FJ
compiler
> against the MF compiler but unfortunately I have little choice!
>
> Thanks anyway.

Woah!

If you're still talking about ISAM files, N.B., no two compilers
handle ISAM files the same way - ISAM files are NOT portable between
compilers (other physical formats may not be portable either).

This is not a failing of Fujitsu, it is a law of the universe.

While the source is portable, the data the compiled program
uses/generates may not be (and probably isn't) portable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: PersonalCOBOL help

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-15T18:25:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lf0fq$46h$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<5Jyd7.23735$pv6.2160029@news1.cableinet.net> <ssAd7.148623$li.10571168@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3B777659.B7C2E661@Azonic.co.nz> <izYd7.153912$li.11157616@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <Qbqe7.6071$6R6.611652@news1.cableinet.net> <pBCe7.14991$VV1.847527@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
I agree with the point that Jerry was making - but I am not certain whether
or not it is "relevant".

If you find things that the Fujitsu compiler does with indexed files that
are not "conforming" to the ANSI/ISO Standard as far as the standard OPEN,
CLOSE, READ, WRITE, etc statements, then you have found a BUG in that
compiler (as they do claim to be Standard conforming).  If this is the case,
I would be interested in the "symptoms" that you find (and particularly if
you have compiled your program with Fujitsu's - or Micro Focus - standard
"flagging" option - to make certain you really ARE using Standard features
and NOT extensions such as split keys or READ PREVIOUS).

If you are talking about having the "object code" produced by one compiler
be able to READ/process the data files created by another compiler, then you
MAY WELL be out of luck (especially for indexed files).  I know that Micro
Focus (used to?) handle some "external" file formats such as C-ISAM and
BTRIEVE.  If Fujitsu also handles either of those "file types" then you
MIGHT be able to share ISAM files across applications created by different
compilers.

NOTE: (IBM) Mainframe programmers - this is a NON-issue for you - where the
"indexed file system" belongs to the Operating System and *not* to the
compiler vendor.  However, in the "workstation" environment, this can turn
into a REAL nightmare.
```

#### ↳ Re: PersonalCOBOL help

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T19:39:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B76DBE2.2EA4C993@home.com>`
- **References:** `<5Jyd7.23735$pv6.2160029@news1.cableinet.net>`

```


Col wrote:

> Hi. I`m a student who was originally using a Fujitsu compiler to test my
> COBOL programs were working correctly. As I have moved on in the course I
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> not ASSIGN TO "lpt1:".

Does Personal COBOL contain PC_PRINT routines - if so write to me privately and
I'll dig out an example using Landscape and Condensed. If Personal COBOL allows
you access to the M/F help site for examples, you could look up there.

Failing that you could contact M/F at Newbury for help. I'm assuming you are
using a laser printer, but PC_PRINT should recognize your ":lpt1", regardless of
whether laser or dot matrix.

PS: use 'assign to :lpti' not 'assign to printer' - it is more specific.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: PersonalCOBOL help

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T08:03:34+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B777BC6.52FFC1AB@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<5Jyd7.23735$pv6.2160029@news1.cableinet.net>`

```
Col wrote:
> Personal COBOL V3.4 from Merant. My problem is that I can`t seem to find any
> way of altering the output to the printer, and my lines of text are spilling
> onto the next line making the report very messy. I have a line output width
> of over 100 chars which would be very awkward to alter to the standard 80.

It seems that you want to have the printer set to 'compressed' or
landscape or similar rather than this being a problem with Cobol.

You could send an initialisation string to the printer, but this depends
entirely on the make and model of the printer and may also be affected
by setting in the compile or run-time.

Find the manual for the printer and work out the codes required.  You
can write hex values into your program using x"1B" for Escape etc.

Check for 'zero insertion' in your Cobol implementation MF).  There is a
run-time option that cause control characters to be preceeded by x"00",
you don't want this.
```

## Thread: Fujitsu V6.1 Install - MDAC Not Current? — 4 msgs

### Fujitsu V6.1 Install - MDAC Not Current?

- **From:** "Lucas, Todd" <Todd.Lucas@C-Cubed.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-12T19:55:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<abBd7.231319$Q9.63652056@news1.elmhst1.il.home.com>`

```
Fello Fujitsu Guru's ...

I am trying to install v6.1 on my home machine, and keep getting "The
MDAC_type.dll system file is out of date - you must run the MS COM update".  I
respond by selecting "Yes", and the MS COM update runs.  I reboot the machine
and hit the v6.1 install again and get the same prompt!  I have also tried
installing the COM update and not rebooting, but keep getting the same prompt.
It does not get to the point that it asks for a license key yet.

I previously removed Fujitsu V5 using the Control Panel Add/Remove programs,
cleaned up all the leftover directories, as well as removed all items in the
Registry that had references to the V5 install.  I'm running WinNT v4SP6.  I
installed the same v6.1 on my machine at work and everything worked great.  The
only difference was that my machine at work did not have the v5 installed on it.

Any ideas on how to get around this?

Thanks - Todd
```

Lucas, Todd wrote:
.
> 
> I am trying to install v6.1 on my home machine, and keep getting "The
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any ideas on how to get around this?

  1) Reformat drive, 
  2) Reinstall operating system, 
  3) Add patches,
  4) Reinstall all software,
  5) Remember that you should have backed up data first.

If this fails to work then:

  1) Buy new machine with XP installed
  2) Use C# for all programming

Actually some later versions of Windows (such as 2000) knows which DLLs
that _it_ wants and it silently puts tham back on rebooting if they have
been changed.  MS may have applied this 'technology' into NT with a
recent patch level.  The basis of this 'feature' is that it makes the OS
more stable (less frequent BSOD) and bugger anyone else's software,
that's their problem (or more specifically the world will be a much
better place when _all_ software comes only from Microsoft and all users
buy every update when it is released).
```

## Thread: Corporate investment in Cobol — 11 msgs

### Corporate investment in Cobol

- **From:** Joshua Gibson <gibsonjb@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T00:27:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B771E8B.E1BF53F5@earthlink.net>`

```
I don't think  we can forget about the amount of money many companies
have invested in Cobol over the years.  This could be a main reason that
they want to stay with this language.
```

#### ↳ Re: Corporate investment in Cobol

- **From:** "warren.simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T02:13:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sJGd7.14225$1p1.1104401@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3B771E8B.E1BF53F5@earthlink.net>`

```
Not only is this a very good reason, but is why Fortran is doing well. The
aircraft industry asked for help to add data base to the FORTRAN resouce,
and CODASYL helped them organize that effort indepentent of the FORTRAN
standards activity.


Warren Simmons
warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net

Joshua Gibson <gibsonjb@earthlink.net> wrote in message
news:3B771E8B.E1BF53F5@earthlink.net...
> I don't think  we can forget about the amount of money many companies
> have invested in Cobol over the years.  This could be a main reason that

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Corporate investment in Cobol

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-15T04:18:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vKme7.46370$gj1.4308245@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3B771E8B.E1BF53F5@earthlink.net> <sJGd7.14225$1p1.1104401@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
After seeing the term "corporate investment in cobol" a few times, it seems
a good idea to extend the point. Most applications in some companies  are
expensed not taken as assets. Should the management become fully aware of
the full investment in IT projects, it might find a new way to book the
efforts of application development and maintenance. In that case, the
"picture of the advisability of using a newer compiler or language" might be
better understood. This might also support more planning, and wiser use of
facilities. Our book keeping for a period  proved that there were
measureable savings on each project. The sum total over time showed a very
large increase in the bottom line. Though an accounting major with some
experience, I was never sure the book keeping was not wallpaper to get the
job done.

I have no doubt that with the use of a standard language there were
substantial savings. How to measure them well seems to
excape us.  I did not have full access to the books.

Warren Simmons

warren.simmons <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:sJGd7.14225$1p1.1104401@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...

> aircraft industry asked for help to add data base to the FORTRAN resouce,

So, no matter how useful a realtional database DB2 is, there will
always be a need for DLI & IMS.

versa? If yes, how?

#### ↳ Re: Linkage Section

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T09:34:56+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dgrentkpei55nbjpmeaj0lh9etea4ragt3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<tnepfa8jcalp99@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Mon, 13 Aug 2001 05:30:18 -0000, <tapas_roy@vsnl.com> wrote:

>I have the following queries in connection to subroutine call in COBOL.
>
>1. Can I pass an array to a subroutine from the main program and vice-
>versa?

Yes, you can.

> If yes, how?

Just like any other parameter:


 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION. 
  PROGRAM-ID. MAINPGM.
 DATA DIVISION.
  WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
   01 My-Table.
      02 My-Row PIC X(10) OCCURS 10 TIMES.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
  Main-Control.
     CALL 'SUBPGM' USING BY REFERENCE My-Table
     GOBACK
     .
 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
  PROGRAM-ID. SUBPGM.
 DATA DIVISION.
  LINKAGE SECTION.
   01 A-Table.
      02 A-Row PIC X(10) OCCURS 10 TIMES.
 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING A-Table.
  Sub-control.
     MOVE SPACES TO A-table
     MOVE 'XXX' TO A-row(3)
     GOBACK
     .
 END PROGRAM SUBPGM.
 END PROGRAM MAINPGM.  
>
>2. How many paramaters could I pass to the subroutine from the main 
>program and vice-versa ?

      
      
      
      
                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)        
                                                            
      What does "Hard Disk Destroyed" mean?
      
        (Another Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)         


-----= Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 16 Different Servers! =-----
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linkage Section

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T14:29:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9l99rm$hug$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<tnepfa8jcalp99@corp.supernews.com> <dgrentkpei55nbjpmeaj0lh9etea4ragt3@4ax.com>`

```
One warning about passing tables (often known as "arrays" in other
programming languages).  If you use the INDEXED BY phrase in your CALLing
and CALLed programs, there is no ANSI/ISO requirement that these "reference"
the same index.  Therefore, do NOT set the index in one program and expect
it to be available in the other.  If you DO need to have references to an
"index value" in both the calling and called program, then create a USAGE
INDEX data item (quite different from an INDEXED BY item) and pass it as
well as the table.
```

#### ↳ Re: Linkage Section

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T22:22:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UqYd7.153832$li.11153976@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<tnepfa8jcalp99@corp.supernews.com>`

```
USUALLY* when you pass a parameter what is actually communicated is
the starting memory address of the parameter. Therefore whether the
item is an array or a variable is irrelevant. Also the variable names
used in the two modules is immaterial. ONLY INTERNAL ADDRESSES ARE
PASSED. For example:

Calling program:
01  MYARRAY.
   02  MYSTUFF OCCURS 10  PIC X(10).
...
   CALL 'MYSUB' USING MYARRAY

Called program:
LINKAGE SECTION.
01  PASSED-THINGY        PIC X(100).
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PASSED-THINGY.

will compile, link, and execute with no problem.

In other words, it makes no difference how a passed item is defined as
far as the compiler/linker is concerned. Of course it makes a world of
difference if you get confused.
The common rule is that the passed parameter be defined exactly the
same in the calling and the called programs.

A common mistake is:

01  MYPARAM  PIC X(10).
CALL 'SUBPROG' USING MYPARAM.

LINKAGE SECTION.
01  MYPARAM  PIC X(1000).
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING MYPARAM.
   MOVE SPACE TO MYPARAM.

Which wipes out 990 bytes of (presumably) good data back in the
calling program, causing all manner of confusing symptoms (such as
"Divide By Zero," "Invalid Function Call," and "Danger, Will
Robinson.")


[*There are ways to pass actual values (instead of addresses), but
don't go down that road yet.]

Question 2: There is no practical limit to the number of parameters
you CAN pass. Anything more than 3 to 6 (depending on the compiler)
take a performance hit. For LOTS of parameters needed by a subprogram,
it is more efficient to collect these disparate parameters into one
group item and pass (the address of) that single group item. For
example:

01 PARAM-LIST.
  02  PARAM-NAME   PIC X(30).
  02  PARAM-DATE  PIC X(8).
  02  PARAM-COLOR  PIC X(4).
  02  PARAM-DOG-BREED  PIC X(45).
  02  PARAM-PRIME-NUMBER  PIC 9(7).
  02  PARAM-QUARTS-IN-A-DECILITER PIC 9(5)V9(5).
  02  PARAM-ATOMIC-WEIGHT  PIC 9(9)V9(9).
...

CALL 'SUBPROG' USING PARAM-LIST.

Remember, how you define the parameters in your program is immaterial
to the passing process (but not irrelevant to your logical process).

<tapas_roy@vsnl.com> wrote in message
news:tnepfa8jcalp99@corp.supernews.com...
> I have the following queries in connection to subroutine call in
COBOL.
>
> 1. Can I pass an array to a subroutine from the main program and
vice-
> versa? If yes, how?
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linkage Section

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T10:30:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u57e7.13$Qe7.5677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<tnepfa8jcalp99@corp.supernews.com> <UqYd7.153832$li.11153976@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:UqYd7.153832$li.11153976@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com...
> USUALLY* when you pass a parameter what is actually communicated is
> the starting memory address of the parameter. Therefore whether the
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robinson.")

Actually, if all that happens is you wipe out 990 bytes of data, that's the
best that can happen here. (or is it the worst?).

The called program does not necessarily have access to the 990 bytes
following the ten bytes allocated in "caller". Trying to access memory you
don't own gets you a S0C2 (mainframe) or GPF (Windows) real quick.

Compilers do not allocate memory for Linkage section items; they expect the
calling routine to allocate and own it.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Linkage Section

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T22:50:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lXhe7.7072$NK1.363319@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<tnepfa8jcalp99@corp.supernews.com> <UqYd7.153832$li.11153976@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <u57e7.13$Qe7.5677@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net
> >
> > 01  MYPARAM  PIC X(10).
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Actually, if all that happens is you wipe out 990 bytes of data,
that's the
> best that can happen here. (or is it the worst?).
>
> The called program does not necessarily have access to the 990 bytes
> following the ten bytes allocated in "caller". Trying to access
memory you
> don't own gets you a S0C2 (mainframe) or GPF (Windows) real quick.

Correct: "Not necessarily." But for small numbers of 'n' [as in PIC
X(n)], almost always. Most of the time there is still Working-Storage
below the passed parameter; following Working-Storage is (usually) the
program code. But you're right; a sufficiently large definition in the
called program will get a memory-boundary error of some sort. Most of
the time, though, you just wipe out essential variables in the calling
program (which are, I was emphasising, are a devil to debug).
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Linkage Section

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-13T18:47:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9stfntopf2j92mhvttbjpsnluht4d5db74@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<tnepfa8jcalp99@corp.supernews.com>`

```
On Mon, 13 Aug 2001 05:30:18 -0000 <tapas_roy@vsnl.com> wrote:

:>I have the following queries in connection to subroutine call in COBOL.

:>1. Can I pass an array to a subroutine from the main program

Yes.

As any other parameter.

:>                                                             and vice-
:>versa? If yes, how?

Only with difficulty, using pointers.

:>2. How many paramaters could I pass to the subroutine from the main 
:>program 

Whatever limit your compiler provides.

:>        and vice-versa ?

None.

Your subroutine can update by reference variables passed by the main program.
```

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

```
Thanks in advance for any help.

Problem: I keep getting the following error message "TKW caused a general
protection fault in module COBLIB.DLW at ..............................."
The program is "Timekeeper" and is written in Cobol.  The error occurs
whenever I try to print, but only from one machine.  The program is housed
on a machine that is also running Win98.  This one has me climbing the wall.
All the machines seem to be configured identically but I must be missing
something somewhere.  The computers that do not host the program have an
icon that is directed to a mapped drive that is hosting the program
(Timekeeper).

If any additional information is required please let me know.

Again Thanks,

Dave H.
```

Use Norton's Ghost to clone an operating machine to the offending
computer. If you still get the error, then it's hardware.

the machine.

## Thread: cobol dat and idx files — 8 msgs

### cobol dat and idx files

- **From:** rurapenty@yahoo.com (Mike Wilson)
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T06:35:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5a04687f.0108140535.26b2d982@posting.google.com>`

```
i have some old dat and idx files that need to be converted to access,
dbase, or text.  i believe they are in ibm cobol format.  how would be
the best way to accomplish this conversion?  i do not have the
original source code for the program that produced these files.
thanks,
mike wilson
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol dat and idx files

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T14:19:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lbbvm$akm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<5a04687f.0108140535.26b2d982@posting.google.com>`

```

On 14-Aug-2001, rurapenty@yahoo.com (Mike Wilson) wrote:

> i have some old dat and idx files that need to be converted to access,
> dbase, or text.  i believe they are in ibm cobol format.  how would be
> the best way to accomplish this conversion?  i do not have the
> original source code for the program that produced these files.
> thanks,

The easiest conversion is between cobol format and text, as they are the
same thing.   You may have some numbers that are binary or packed though.

The toughest part of your job is guessing what the data mean.  You can look
at it and infer that part of it is a name, part of it is a number, part of
it is a billing code, etc.  But you should have some ideas about what data
you want and expect - you aren't converting for the hell of it - there must
be something you're expecting to find and use.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol dat and idx files

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T08:26:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B794316.3633@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<5a04687f.0108140535.26b2d982@posting.google.com> <9lbbvm$akm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> On 14-Aug-2001, rurapenty@yahoo.com (Mike Wilson) wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> be something you're expecting to find and use.

The DAT files will contain the data, the IDX will contain only indexing. Whichever 
system you import into will want to re-index based on its own rules, so you can ignore 
it.

If the DAT contains any binary or packed decimal data you will need to unpack it.  You 
will also have to allow for any conversion which be needed (or may have happened) from 
EBCDIC.  If the file has come from an IBM mainframe system via IND$FILE, Kermit or 
similar, any binaries or packed will have been garbled since they will have been 
converted as if they were text characters.

Even in the latter circumstance, you might find ParseRat (http://www.parserat.com/) of 
assistance. It will be able to handle these files as "generic binary" and export to CSV, 
DBF, XML or several other formats. It can also "ungarble" binaries which have been 
garbled this way.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: cobol dat and idx files

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-15T01:22:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y9ke7.16396$1p1.1306838@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<5a04687f.0108140535.26b2d982@posting.google.com> <9lbbvm$akm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
    If you have a MF compiler, try using the rebuild program to
see what the record length and key(s) start and length is.

    Once you have that you can either use the file loader application
to dump the file to a record sequential file, or write a cobol program
to do so.

    If you have the application (runable), try adding a record with known
values (Ie "AAAAAAAAAA" for first name).

    Don't try to use line sequential untill you know that there are no
binary
or packed fields.

    MF has used several different file formats for indexed files.  Some of
these
formats can be examined directly, some cannot.



Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:9lbbvm$akm$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

look
> at it and infer that part of it is a name, part of it is a number, part of
> it is a billing code, etc.  But you should have some ideas about what data
> you want and expect - you aren't converting for the hell of it - there
must
> be something you're expecting to find and use.
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol dat and idx files

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T11:26:47-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3B796D67.C29C9501@ibm.net>`

```
I believe it is likely that the files are part of an application
programmed using Micro Focus COBOL.  .DAT would be the data and .IDX the
index portion, as another note said.  If you have the Micro Focus
Workbench, you could look at the files as related units.

Without at least record descriptions, though, how do you know what to do
with the data?

One of my colleagues has a similar problem.  In her case, the application
is still running, but no one knows when, how, or by whom it was produced.

Good luck!
========

Did they come from a Unix system?  They could be in C-ISAM format (see
www.informix.com - not sure if C-ISAM info is still there since IBM bought
that part of Informix).

If it is C-ISAM and if I recall correctly, the .dat portion of the file will
contain fixed length records containing the data.  But you will somehow need
to figure out the record length and the field layout inside the records.
That's defined in the program source code (and you don't have that).
