## Thread: multiple occurs depending on clauses in a single working storage variable — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### multiple occurs depending on clauses in a single working storage variable

- **From:** vijay_kg007@yahoo.com (vijay)
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T11:24:48-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a340067e.0108141024.4aa39719@posting.google.com>`

```
i have defined a working storage variable of the type

01 ws-var.
   05 ws-header.
   05 ws-array1 occurs 1 to n times
      depending on sub1.
      10  ws-var2   pic X(10).
      10  ws-array2 occurs 1 to m times
          depending upon sub2.
          15 
 
      10  ws-var3   pic X(2).
    note*** ws-var3 is actually a sort of delimiter*****
now my programs writes this ws-var in a flat file
the program manipulates sub1 and sub2 in such a way that
the max length of rec ws-var does not exceed a fixed limit
say 2000 bytes .
when the record is written a peculiar thing happens
first instance of array is written the ws-var3 occupies the place next
to
ws-var2 thereby overwritting some part of the ws-array2's first
instance
then the second instance of array1 is written ws-var2(next instance)
occupies the place next to ws-var3 therby overwritting the rec
and this goes on and on....

output looks somewhat like this
ws-var2ws-var3ws-var2ws-var3......   and so on(instances of array2
being overwritten each time) 
where it should be something of this type
ws-var2array2ws-var3ws-var2array2ws-var3......

can anyone tell why does the array1 instances get written from
the above positions.....
has this something to do with with depending upon clause
```

#### ↳ Re: multiple occurs depending on clauses in a single working storage variable

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T13:52:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lbs36$97s$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<a340067e.0108141024.4aa39719@posting.google.com>`

```
Having *any* fields following an Occurs Depending On data item - within the
same 01-level - regardless of what SECTION (linkage, Working-Storage, etc)
it is in - is an extension to the ANSI and ISO Standard.  Furthermore, it is
one "extension" that is syntactically allowed in a variety of
implementations but has SIGNIFICANTLY different semantics.
\
Even within the same compiler, it often has different semantics (for example
with Micro
Focus, the ODOSLIDE directive determines how it works).

When you actually have the "object" of the ODO (occurs depending on OBJECT)
in the same 01-level - but after the ODO, then behavior becomes even more
unpredictable (or at least un-portable).

Can you tell comp.lang.cobol which compiler (and operating system) you are
using?  Also check your documentation to see if any compiler
options/directives are described as impacting this behavior.
```

#### ↳ Re: multiple occurs depending on clauses in a single working storage variable

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T19:54:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B79827F.848928C1@home.com>`
- **References:** `<a340067e.0108141024.4aa39719@posting.google.com>`

```


vijay wrote:

> i have defined a working storage variable of the type
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> has this something to do with with depending upon clause

First things first - (a) your 05 ws-header appears to serve no purpose.
(b) your Level 15 doesn't seem to be doing anything either, (c) if your
ws-var3 is and end of record delimiter, shouldn't it be a Level 05 along
with ws-var1.

I make modest use of n-dimensional tables, so I gave it a crack :-

       01 ws-var.
          05 ws-array1 occurs 1 to 50 times
             depending on sub1.
             10  ws-array2 occurs 1 to 100 times
                 depending on sub2.
                 15 ws-var2    pic x(10).

          05 ws-var3   pic X(2).

The compiler NO LIKEE (Net Express) and gave error "OCCURS DEPENDING
subsidiary to OCCURS only allowed with ODOSLIDE". Now if you haven't got
ODOSLIDE, I would suggest you are in trouble ! I'm not clear if this is
what you are trying to achieve, but the following works :-

*>--------------------------------------------------------------------------

$set ODOSLIDE

 Program-id. arraytest.

 Working-storage section.
 01 sub1                     pic 9(03).
 01 sub2                     pic 9(03).
 01 ws-var.
    05 ws-array1 occurs 1 to 50 times
       depending on sub1.
       10  ws-array2 occurs 1 to 100 times
           depending on sub2.
           15 ws-var2    pic x(10).

    05 ws-var3   pic X(2).

 Procedure Division.

  move 10 to sub1
  move 20 to sub2
  initialize ws-var
  move "Apples"        to ws-var2(1,    6)
  move "Bananas"     to ws-var2(2,   17)
  move "Mangoes"    to ws-var2(10, 20)
  move "Pears"         to ws-var2(1,     7)
  move "Grapefruit"  to ws-var2(4,   18)

  display ws-var2(1,    6)
  display ws-var2(1,    7)
  display ws-var2(2,  17)
  display ws-var2(4,  18)
  display ws-var2(10, 20)

  STOP RUN.
*>------------------------------------------------------------------------------

What you have indicated that you are trying to do is 'expand' and
'contract' the table to suit current circumstances. I believe I'm correct
in saying that regardless of your 'elasticity,' memory will be allocated,
(using my example above) for the possibility of a table with 50 x 100
elements. If you haven't got ODOSLIDE - can you arrive at an 'optimum'
number for either ws-array1 or ws-array2 that is 'fixed' instead of
'occurs' - then it should work for you.

Bill - care to comment. Does the new standard introduce any new relevant
rules for OCCURS DEPENDING ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-e9945c3dc6"></a>
## Thread: Nested Programs - Good or Bad? — 85 msgs

### Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

- **From:** "Ken MacKenzie" <ken.mackenzie@bigfoot.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T20:00:52+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`

```
Hi,

I've just started working for a company (5 or 6 weeks) using Cobol on an
OS/390 platform.

I wrote my first program in my usual style - using nested programs.  I had
my code checked (first time in more than 15 years!) and they didn't like the
nested programs because "No-one here has ever done that before"!  And, I was
forced to change them to PERFORMed procedures.

Anyone any views one-way-or-another about their use/efficiency versus
PERFORMs?  I have been asked (by one of their Cobol mentors) to write a
paper justifying their use and I'd like ideas from others.

TIA,

Ken MacKenzie
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T14:26:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`

```
Ken MacKenzie wrote:

>
> I wrote my first program in my usual style - using nested programs.  I had
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> paper justifying their use and I'd like ideas from others.

The problem is that of writing vs. maintaining the programs.  It's easy to write
them that way but the very devil to maintain.  Given that proper documentation
is never prepared, it becomes a marathon of frustration and wasted effort having
to look at another program, then another, then another - just to find that
you're off down the wrong tree.  Then you have to remember where you started and
begin again.  At least if all the modules are within the same program - i.e.,
performed - you don't have to go as far back to start over.

There are definitely cases where programs should be in the form of stand-alone
called modules - but they are all of the utility or frequently-used function
type.  And there are cases where one program becomes too "huge" for varying
reasons.  But IMnot so HO, you have to decide which is more important in the
long run - ease of writing or ease of maintenance.

PL
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T20:17:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B7987D5.F9A89E34@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```


Peter Lacey wrote:

> Ken MacKenzie wrote:
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> PL

Ken,

One thing for sure - if and when they can get their hands on a mainframe OO COBOL -
your current installation is going to hate it <G>. OO is all 'calls' (invokes), not
to a particular program(class) but to an entry-point(method-name) within the class !

It's at least some 5 years since I wrote non-OO so I can sympathize with Peter's
comments about looking through a 'chain of programs' because somebody thought it was
'neat' to modularize into sub-programs. It is valid if the major program just gets
too HUGE - but there's the trade-off - researching what the sub-program does.

As Peter correctly observes, utilities should be stand-alone modules - this concept
is the same as OO classes and a hierarchy to do different things..

My only experience with calling sub-programs was with RM/COBOL '74 - there was a
limit on the number of files that could be opened in one program (you got
file-status 98 'crashed index', if you tried to open the next one - wasn't a crashed
index at all !). This FORCED me into writing a sub-program, closing some of existing
files and opening/closing other files in the sub-program - I hated it, and it really
was a bugger to maintain - no animated debugging tools in those days).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T15:26:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lc1j9$l6i$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
I didn't see the beginning of this thread - but some "thoughts" (no
DEFINITIVE pro or con)

1) The "theory" behind nested programs has to do with "localizing" data to
the procedural code that uses it.  (Similar - but not quite the same as
"encapsulation" with OO).  There is certainly SOME evidence that having data
associated with the procedures that act upon it - is a "good thing"
(especially for maintenance and security).

2) Lots of people who USE nested programs disagree with this, but I am a
STRONG believer in using "COPYBOOKS" (copy members) to define nested
programs.  This "insures" that all "main" programs that use the same nested
"routine" get the same version.  (Of course you need to do a RECOMPILE -
unlike separately compiled programs - to "bring in" the revised version of
the nested program.  But this is (IMHO) easier than trying to keep source
code "in sync")

3) Run-time performance of nested programs versus PERFORMED procedures
versus CALLs to separately compiled programs is TOTALLY dependent upon the
compiler (and run-time).  IBM, for example explicitly recommends nested
programs over separately compiled programs *IF* run-time performance is of
highest concern.  On the other hand, Micro Focus has recommended
"nonestcall" (If I recall correctly) for compile-time (and I think run-time)
performance.

4) It is important that programmers using (AND maintaining) applications
with nested programs are adequately trained.  This DOES include
understanding both EXTERNAL and GLOBAL data definitions - and the
differences between them.  Similarly, you need to learn about COMMON
attribute on the program-id paragraph.

5) Check for 3rd party (and compiler) tools - and their support for nested
programs.  In an IBM mainframe environment (for example) there USED to be
restrictions on DB2, CICS, and debuggers such as Xpediter.  I think that
MOST of these have been removed (by now) - but you should check if "YOUR"
release supports them.

6) Nested programs have been available in ANSI/ISO conforming compilers for
over 15 years now.  If your site is NOT using them, then the chances are
that you aren't using some/many of the OTHER enhancements that have been
available for a decade and a half (and the next Standard is due out
"relatively soon").  You need to SERIOUSLY look at your use of COBOL.  If
you are using it ONLY to maintain existing applications - with a
"diminishing" staff, then you are probably "safe" in sticking to what worked
20 years ago.  If, however, you are doing new development (or even major
enhancements), then I think you need to have "management" look at keeping
your COBOL skills current.

Bottom-Line:
  I know of NOTHING that can be done with nested programs that can't be done
some other way.  HOWEVER, whether nested programs will enhance both your
application's performance AND its maintainability (with adequately trained
staff) is something that should be seriously looked at - and might well
depend on both your programming environment and use of COBOL.

P.S.  If you tell us your "environment" (compiler, operating system, etc) -
we might have some other comments.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

```
"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca
>
> The problem is that of writing vs. maintaining the programs.  It's
easy to write
> them that way but the very devil to maintain.

Disagree. 'Nested' (OO, et al) are FAR easier to maintain (at least MY
nested programs are easier).

>Given that proper documentation
> is never prepared, it becomes a marathon of frustration and wasted
effort having
> to look at another program, then another, then another - just to
find that
> you're off down the wrong tree.

That's a result of poor design: All you should have to do is check the
input and the output of a nested program. The 'nestee' should be a
black box; you don't care how it does whatever it does, only whether
it gives the correct answer. If the subprogram doesn't give the
correct answer, you concentrate your efforts on the called program.

>Then you have to remember where you started and
> begin again.  At least if all the modules are within the same
program - i.e.,
> performed - you don't have to go as far back to start over.
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

- **From:** cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass)
- **Date:** 2001-08-15T12:12:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca>...
> Ken MacKenzie wrote:
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> over.

You misunderstood the question and the concept.

NESTED programs are not the same as SUBPROGRAMS.  You are discussing
the latter, and Ken the former.

With nested programs, you do have subprograms, but they are physically
nested inside the main program, and you only have the one source
module (and therefore one load module).  The advantage of this over
simple performs is the localization of data.  Also, the nested
programs don't require environment divisions, so less physical coding
is needed (as opposed to "unnesting" the subprograms and compiling
them seperately).


Curtis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-15T14:20:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lei6t$a1r$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com>`

```
The question of whether nested programs (not separately compiled programs)
are or are not "more difficult" to maintain than PERFORMed "procedures"
(paragraphs or sections) is DEBATABLE.  If you use "identical" nested
programs in multiple "main" programs, then they do cause difficulty in
maintenance - as you need to "figure out" which main programs use the
routine and how they will be impacted by any changes.  Futhermore, unlike
separately compiled programs (especially those that are "dynamically
called" - in an OS/390 environment), you MUST recompile every program that
that "uses" the same nested program.  (Comments can help - tools such as
SCLM or Endevor can also help - but nothing is "fool proof")

The theory of having "data near procedures" is certainly one that is "touted
as beneficial" by many programming language theorists.  I have no evidence
that it isn't helpful - but I also have no evidence that it IS helpful.
There are a NUMBER of 3rd party products (again in an OS/390 environment)
that can HAPPILY trace data thru PERFORM statements; there are significantly
less (possibly none?) that can do the same thru nested programs.  Therefore
"program/logic understanding" of an UNFAMILIAR program *may* suffer when
using nested programs rather than PERFORMed routines.  On the other hand, if
you "goal" is to have a black box "routine" (handled by such things as COPY
SUPRESSING) then a nested program might well be better than using a
PERFORMed routine.

One thing that I do (still) think is critical - is that you MUST have
adequate training to use nested programs (or any other feature that is "new"
in the last 15 years).  Furthermore, it is important that all your "add on"
tools work with whichever feature you are "introducing" to your environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>

```

On 15-Aug-2001, cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass) wrote:

> You misunderstood the question and the concept.
>
> NESTED programs are not the same as SUBPROGRAMS.  You are discussing
> the latter, and Ken the former.

Yes, so nested programs should be compared with performed paragraphs.


> With nested programs, you do have subprograms, but they are physically
> nested inside the main program, and you only have the one source
> module (and therefore one load module).  The advantage of this over
> simple performs is the localization of data.

Correct

> Also, the nested
> programs don't require environment divisions, so less physical coding
> is needed (as opposed to "unnesting" the subprograms and compiling
> them seperately).

But you just made the point that these aren't subprograms.  Performed
paragraphs also don't need separate environment divisions, nor
identification divisions, nor data divisions.  If this is your criterion,
then nested programs have MORE work, not less.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-08-16T21:32:33+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-663F69.21323316082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20010815195941.17760.00002235@nso-fm.aol.com>`

```
<snip>

Here we go again.

in most other programming languages the concept of nested program is 
known as - wait for it - SUBROUTINES.

Most other programming languages have EXACTLY the same functionality in 
their subroutines as is provided by nested programs in COBOL.

Only in a COBOL forum would you find people arguing about the 
desirability of programming subroutines with local storage. Everyone 
else takes it for granted that this is what a subroutine gets you.

In my opinion the PERFORM statement that allows the execution of named 
paragraphs is an abortion that should be done away with as soon as is 
humanly possible. Only inline PERFORMs should be allowed. And while we 
are at it, can we replace the abysmal PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE|AFTER to 
PERFORM WHILE|UNTIL.

Then the COBOL community will finally get the concept of local variables 
and subroutines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-08-16T15:22:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010816112250.15120.00004082@ng-bj1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<josephk-663F69.21323316082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
No, it's not the same thing. 

In COBOL, nested programs are subroutines where the source is in the same text
file for the callers and the callees. In earlier COBOL, and many other
languages, subroutines are separately compiled and managed. This is why the
whole maintenance issue raises its head.

In PL/I, procedures may be external or internal; if they are internal, they are
like nested programs. But PL/I does not have the analogous construct of
performed paragraphs and sections.

For a traditional COBOL programmer, the need for the nested program concept
seems strange indeed. You have internal routines that you PERFORM (paragraphs
or sections) and you have external subroutines that you CALL.

The PERFORM statement cannot pass parameters, but the CALL can. PERFORMed
routines must be in the same source file; CALL'ed routines (before the advent
of nested programs) must be in a separately compiled source file. Nested
programs are a strange amalgam of the two. 

But it's not a big deal, per se. Good programmers can write good code with our
without nested programs; bad programmers will create headaches whatever they
choose.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

```
news:20010816112250.15120.00004082@ng-bj1.aol.com...
> snip <
> For a traditional COBOL programmer, the need for the nested program
concept
> seems strange indeed. You have internal routines that you PERFORM
(paragraphs
> or sections) and you have external subroutines that you CALL.
>
At the risk of sounding like a cranky old COBOL curmudgeon (...as if there
aren't enough on this list already!), I would categorize nested programs as
a classic example of a 'solution in search of a problem'.

OTOH, nested programs add to the richness of the language, so I'm all for
'em.   Just as long as I don't *have* to use them.
```

```
After coding (OS/390) programs for 14 years, I didn't know you could write
nested programs - I've never needed them.

If you want to improve performance, it's generally easier to save time with
mods to the application design, or the data design, than it is to code
COBOL.
And the older (and more bastardised) the app. is, the more scope for gain
there is in a redesign. The last 2 apps I redesigned saved 80% & 96% of
wall time, respectively. I've never shaved that much by "innovative" COBOL.
```

```
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Using a COPY would certainly let you change code, but I'm thinking of the
change control tools I've used.

Step 1 - check out original module for 6-month upgrade = Vers. 1.
Step 2 - check out copybook for change
Step 3 - check out main module for recompile with new copybook = Vers.2
Step 4 - release Vers.2 with new copybook.
Step 5 - can't release superceded Vers.1.

Is there a controlled development environment that will cope with this
problem?

Using a dynamically linked sub-module will avoid this problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-16T09:54:06-07:00

```
"S Comstock" <scomstock@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010816112250.15120.00004082@ng-bj1.aol.com...

> No, it's not the same thing.  ... In earlier COBOL, and many other
> languages, subroutines are separately compiled and managed.

In earlier COBOLs, some vendors may have provided subroutines (in this
sense) as *implementor-defined extensions* to the applicable standard, but
these features  were neither part of standard COBOL, portable to other
implementations with analogous features, nor universally available.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
> In my opinion the PERFORM statement that allows the execution of named

What makes this syntax abysmal?  (we may agree - but I haven't heard your

> and subroutines.

So the subroutines we use right now are done without getting the concept,
because we don't use them as much as you would like.

So once we "get the concept", how will our programs be easier to maintain?
Or if they are just as easy to maintain, how will they be easier to write?

best - such as faster compiled code?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-16T19:54:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xxVe7.3801$2u.43387@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20010815195941.17760.00002235@nso-fm.aol.com> <josephk-663F69.21323316082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
While not arguing the merits of Nested Programs, I do want to state a contrary
opinion about Perform... 

I use inline performs, but when the preform goes to more than a visible screen
of code, I generally move it all into a paragraph, and perform that paragraph
instead -- giving it a meaningful name.  This way, when looking at the logic of
the program, I can see the function that is being performed, without being
distracted by the petty details (unless I want to be).

PS - I don't like WHILE and UNTIL either.  UNTIL implies a break in the perform
cycle exactly when the condition is true, and WHILE implies a break when the
condition becomes false.  Test Before and After are very clear in their meaning.
Test AFTER always gives you at least one iteraton of the perform.  WHILE and
UNTIL (FOR and WHILE, DO or whatver) vary in behavior by language - assuming the
same behavior in various languages can get you in trouble.


In article <josephk-663F69.21323316082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>, Joseph J Katnic
says...
>
><snip>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>and subroutines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T20:43:12+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-61E4C4.20431117082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20010815195941.17760.00002235@nso-fm.aol.com> <josephk-663F69.21323316082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <xxVe7.3801$2u.43387@www.newsranger.com>`

```
In article <xxVe7.3801$2u.43387@www.newsranger.com>,
 Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote:

> While not arguing the merits of Nested Programs, I do want to state a 
> contrary
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I agree, and in most other languages exactly the same thing happens. 
It's just that their subroutines (Paragraphs) are always localized.

In C you generally write your subroutines before the mainline (but you 
can provide prototypes and then write the subroutines after the 
mainline).

You have a bit more overhead in the coding of a subroutine in COBOL, but 
I don't see that as a disincentive. If I did, I would choose some other 
more concise language like APL or the equally readable C.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T08:07:22-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108170707.61fea541@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <20010815195941.17760.00002235@nso-fm.aol.com> <josephk-663F69.21323316082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <xxVe7.3801$2u.43387@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<xxVe7.3801$2u.43387@www.newsranger.com>...
> While not arguing the merits of Nested Programs, I do want to state a contrary
> opinion about Perform... 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> distracted by the petty details (unless I want to be).
*----------------------

I'll second that, Thane.  In fact, if there is much complexity at all
in the in-line perform, I make it out-of-line (with that all-important
MEANINGFUL name!).

*----------------------

> PS - I don't like WHILE and UNTIL either.  UNTIL implies a break in the perform
> cycle exactly when the condition is true, and WHILE implies a break when the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> same behavior in various languages can get you in trouble.

*---------------------------

There IS NO break in the cycle. The UNTIL or WHILE conditions are
tested BEFORE or AFTER, but not in the middle of the perform.
If you want (and, it looks as if you do not!) to break in the middle
of a PERFORM, you have to use the disgusting new END-PERFORM | CYCLE
instruction. (Incidentally, You can ALWAYS rewrite the code so that
END-PERFORM | CYCLE is unnecessary, by arranging your code so that the
condition will be TESTed BEFORE or AFTER.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
check it out at 
http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/

stephenjspiro at hotmail.com

PS: Next meeting in Gloucester, Massachussetts, USA, if any New
Englanders would like to attend a session or few.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass)
- **Date:** 2001-08-16T09:33:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 15-Aug-2001, cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass) wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes, so nested programs should be compared with performed paragraphs.

Assuming that any comparison is being done.

> > With nested programs, you do have subprograms, but they are physically
> > nested inside the main program, and you only have the one source
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> then nested programs have MORE work, not less.

Well, I was simply clarifying the difference between "subprogram" and
"nested program".  The fact is that a nested program *is* a
subprogram, but a subprogram isn't necessarily a nested program, and
in fact, usually isn't.

If you were to read what I said in the paratheses above, you would
realize that I wasn't comparing nested programs to paragraphs, and I
am in no way claiming that one requires more or less work than the
other.

Frankly, I see nested programs as having dubious value. Sure, they
provide data localization and a way to minimize coupling, but their
overhead is substantial, as you yourself have been saying.  I
shouldn't need an entire ID division and an entire data division in
order to pass arguments into a procedure.


Curtis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass)
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T09:24:47-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<163715e7.0108170824.5a37313@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com> <qv8pntss6nrcc580adi5iqdkabong8f0v6@4ax.com>`

```
Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote in message news:<qv8pntss6nrcc580adi5iqdkabong8f0v6@4ax.com>...
> On 16 Aug 2001 09:33:39 -0700, cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass)
> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Somehow, I can't follow you here ...

Well, I was refering to overhead in terms of source code -- there is
simply more of it that what I would consider necessary.  Why do we
need an entire ID Division *and* entire Data Division just to pass
arguments into a procedure?
Consider C:

void xyz_procedure(char argument1, int argument2) {

 . . .

}

or Pascal:

procedure xyz(argument1:char, argument2:integer);
begin

 . . .

end;

Why couldn't Cobol use a simlar convention with paragraphs, rather
than require an entire nested program?


Curtis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T17:50:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sPcf7.33675$NK1.2335215@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com> <qv8pntss6nrcc580adi5iqdkabong8f0v6@4ax.com> <163715e7.0108170824.5a37313@posting.google.com>`

```

On 17-Aug-2001, cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass) wrote:

> or Pascal:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> than require an entire nested program?


The difference between a performed paragraph and a nested program is the
nested program has local variables.  Would you want to put a variable with a
picture in a paragraph?
e.g.
FIRST PARAGRAPH.
      COMPUTE X = 1.
      PERFORM SECOND-PARAGRAPH.
      DISPLAY 'FIRST PARAGRAPH X="' X '"'.

SECOND-PARAGRAPH.
       DEFINE X PIC 9(5)  VALUE ZERO.
       DISPLAY 'SECOND PARAGRAPH X="' X '"'.
       PERFORM THIRD PARAGRAPH.

THIRD PARAGRAPH.
       DISPLAY 'THIRD PARAGRAPH X="' X '"'.

So let's say we perform FIRST-PARAGRAPH, with a new-fangled DEFINE command
defining a local variable.   What should be displayed?

Remember, we want to make this code easy to maintain.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass)
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T08:32:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<163715e7.0108210732.5cc676be@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com> <qv8pntss6nrcc580adi5iqdkabong8f0v6@4ax.com> <163715e7.0108170824.5a37313@posting.google.com> <sPcf7.33675$NK1.2335215@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<sPcf7.33675$NK1.2335215@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>...
> On 17-Aug-2001, cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass) wrote:
> 
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> Remember, we want to make this code easy to maintain.

There are no easy answers; you are simply explaining Cobol's legacy
baggage, which is a valid concern. Including an optional working
storage section inside a paragraph would be messy, but I would still
prefer it over a mandatory data division inside a whole nested
program.

FIRST PARAGRAPH.

      COMPUTE X = 1.
      PERFORM SECOND-PARAGRAPH.
      DISPLAY 'FIRST PARAGRAPH X="' X '"'.

SECOND-PARAGRAPH.

WORKING-STORAGE LOCAL

    05 X PIC 9(5)  VALUE ZERO.

PROCEDURE LOCAL.

       DISPLAY 'SECOND PARAGRAPH X="' X '"'.
       PERFORM THIRD PARAGRAPH USING X.



THIRD PARAGRAPH.

LINKAGE LOCAL.

    05 X PIC 9(5).

PROCEDURE LOCAL.

       DISPLAY 'THIRD PARAGRAPH X="' X '"'.


Or somesuch.

Not that it will ever happen, of course.


Curtis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T16:14:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8289A1.D49FC4BA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com> <qv8pntss6nrcc580adi5iqdkabong8f0v6@4ax.com> <163715e7.0108170824.5a37313@posting.google.com> <sPcf7.33675$NK1.2335215@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <163715e7.0108210732.5cc676be@posting.google.com>`

```


Curtis Bass wrote:

>
> There are no easy answers; you are simply explaining Cobol's legacy
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

 COBOL 2002 will give you what you want - you can include User Defined Functions, (that's not the same as Intrinsic
Functions),  that have their own Local-storage section (temporary data) - and this works somewhat parallel to the OO method
defintion, which also has a Local-storage Section and can, if necessary, access Global Working-Storage. Check out the FCD at
the J4 site for more specific details.

Sorry - the word "Section" stays <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T08:05:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8359A4.79C776E4@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com> <qv8pntss6nrcc580adi5iqdkabong8f0v6@4ax.com> <163715e7.0108170824.5a37313@posting.google.com> <sPcf7.33675$NK1.2335215@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <163715e7.0108210732.5cc676be@posting.google.com>`

```
Curtis Bass wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote 
> > e.g.
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or somesuch.

You just changed the syntax, but you missed the point (IMHO) that was
being made.  You changed 'Third Paragraph' without answering the
original question which was "Which X is used by Third Paragraph".

Perhaps this illustrates well Howard's point:

> > Remember, we want to make this code easy to maintain.


> storage section inside a paragraph would be messy, but I would still
> prefer it over a mandatory data division inside a whole nested
> program.

What is wrong with a 'whole' nested program ?  Its only a few lines of
code.  It needs a Program-Id, and an 'END PROGRAM' because they may be
nested rather then sequential, it needs DATA DIVISION because there may
be sections: working-storage, linkage.  By adding USING to the PERFORM
and LINKAGE to a paragraph (but failing to add USING), you are more than
half way to nested programs in terms of clutter.

While you may dispute over the syntax, that in fact is quite the least
important part, it is the semantics that is important.  Is the X in
'Second paragraph' dynamic or will it retain values (cf static in C) ? 
IF paragraphs are in a PERFORMed section or PERFORM THRU is a local in
the initial paragraph applied to subsequent paragraphs, if not then how
can this be done ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ USELESS - but "semi-interesting" (was: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T15:30:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lugfk$ka6$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com> <qv8pntss6nrcc580adi5iqdkabong8f0v6@4ax.com> <163715e7.0108170824.5a37313@posting.google.com> <sPcf7.33675$NK1.2335215@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <163715e7.0108210732.5cc676be@posting.google.com> <3B8359A4.79C776E4@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3B8359A4.79C776E4@Azonic.co.nz...
 <snip>
>
> What is wrong with a 'whole' nested program ?  Its only a few lines of
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

In actual fact, with an ANSI/ISO conforming compiler, you only need three
"lines" in a nested program.  You need:

Identification Division - header
Program-ID - header and name of nested program
End Program - "header" (or marker as it will be called in the next Standard)

You do NOT need a data division or EVEN a procedure division.
 -  The data division is optional UNLESS you want to "process some data" in
your nested program (and it can even be omitted if you WANT to process
data - if the data is defined as GLOBAL in the containing program)
 -  The Procedure Division is optional (and an EXIT PROGRAM statement and
paragraph name are assumed) - UNLESS you actually want to "DO SOMETHING" in
your nested program.

As I indicated in the (changed) subject of this note, such a 'nested
program' is pretty USELESS - but it is "conforming".  (Possibly of SOME use
as a "stub" program before developing the "intended" code for an existing
CALL statement.

P.S.  Such a program - without a data or procedure division is "conforming"
in a main program as well.  However, in that case the "assumed Exit Program"
doesn't work as PROBABLY desired;  such a program may, however, be compiled
as a "separately" compiled program that could be called by an "external"
(rather than nested) CALL statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: USELESS - but "semi-interesting" (was: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```
Strictly speaking I suppose we could call you 'stub' program below a 'prototype
program' just like we have prototype methods in that collection document ? Would
serve the same purpose; it's written syntatically correct and can be 'called'
without doing anything as part of a test until you are ready to fill in the
procedural statements.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass)
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T14:12:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<163715e7.0108221312.17f034e2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com> <qv8pntss6nrcc580adi5iqdkabong8f0v6@4ax.com> <163715e7.0108170824.5a37313@posting.google.com> <sPcf7.33675$NK1.2335215@bin3.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <163715e7.0108210732.5cc676be@posting.google.com> <3B8359A4.79C776E4@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3B8359A4.79C776E4@Azonic.co.nz>...
> Curtis Bass wrote:
> > "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote 
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> original question which was "Which X is used by Third Paragraph".

In the original syntax, the situation was ambiguous, because we don't
really know the scope of the local variable in SECOND PARAGRAPH. Was
the local variable passed into THIRD PARAGRAPH, or was THIRD PARAGRAPH
accessing the global variable?  There was no way to know.

That's why I proposed the changed syntax, as an attempt to remove the
amiguity.

> Perhaps this illustrates well Howard's point:
> 
> > > Remember, we want to make this code easy to maintain.

Of course.  Does nesting an entire paragraph inside another achieve
this end? How about a program nested three or four levels deep?

> > storage section inside a paragraph would be messy, but I would still
> > prefer it over a mandatory data division inside a whole nested
> > program.
> 
> What is wrong with a 'whole' nested program ?

I don't recall saying that anything was "wrong" with it.  Go back and
read the thread to see what issues I was addressing, and to see the
overall sequence of the discussion.  I am neither for nor against
nested programs.  I simply see them as having questionable value. 
Sure, they provide data localization, but they are also cumbersome in
terms of code (as compared to modern languages that do localization
more cleanly, such as C or Pascal -- I posted examples elsewhere and
have no desire to repeat them here).  I simply ask whether the
benefits outweigh the costs.

> Its only a few lines of code.

Yeah, things always start out small and innocent, but consider a dozen
programs nested inside a main program, and then consider three or four
or more levels of nesting.

Is it still "only a few lines of code"?

And is it "easy to maintain"?

> It needs a Program-Id, and an 'END PROGRAM' because they may be
> nested rather then sequential, it needs DATA DIVISION because there may
> be sections: working-storage, linkage.  By adding USING to the PERFORM
> and LINKAGE to a paragraph (but failing to add USING), you are more than
> half way to nested programs in terms of clutter.

I am in no way suggesting that the syntax I proposed was ideal. I
realize I am not a language designer; I simply took what was offered
as an example and tried to remove the amiguity.  I did it on the fly.
I did not consult language design textbooks or spend hours thinking
about it.  I simply replied to a USENET posting . . .

> While you may dispute over the syntax, that in fact is quite the least
> important part, it is the semantics that is important.  Is the X in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> can this be done ?

These are valid concerns, like I have been saying. And again, as
originally presented, there is no way to answer your first question
above. It would either have to be arbitrarily defined, or the syntax
modified to accomodate either case.

Bottom line, I personally don't use nested programs because I don't
see them has having enough value for the extra coding effort they
cost.  I would have prefered a C-like approach, but Cobol's legacy
makes that a bit difficult, with its Data Division being so separate
from the Procedure Division.

I would, however, not discourage someone else from using them, should
they see things differently (i.e., the benefits outweighing the
costs), hence I am neither for nor against nested programs.


Curtis
```

```
On 16 Aug 2001 09:33:39 -0700, cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass)
wrote:

>"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
>> On 15-Aug-2001, cmbass_us@yahoo.com (Curtis Bass) wrote:
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>order to pass arguments into a procedure.


I think Micro Focus' PC compiler was headed in this direction.  I
never coded DIVISIONs in nested routines except for the PROCEDURE
division which was mandatory.  All I needed was the PROGRAM-ID to name
the routine.  And I think it was possible to code just what you
needed, like INPUT-OUTPUT without the ENVIRONMENT DIVISION and it's
SECTIONs.  And the word SECTION could be omiited in places too.  I
just wish they would remove the word SECTION from the labels for
STORAGE and LINKAGE.   

 In fact, the MF 3.2 compiler I used would compile and link the
following COBOL source program into an EXE.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.  DISPLAY "Hello world".

And the program worked.  Not bad eh?
```

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T04:35:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com> <3b8229f3.32861858@news.uswest.net> <3B8287F8.ADEA22D0@home.com> <3b8342a7.17838119@news.uswest.net>`

```


freewheelin wrote:

> On Tue, 21 Aug 2001 16:07:30 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> optional yet or do you still have to code LINKAGE SECTION?

As you know M/F allow us to ignore the red tape. When using the appropriate syntax you still have to include
the word "Section". I'm working in OO, so I can avoid a lot of the 'Division' jazz, but here's the start of a
program which is 'conventional' and is used to create OO Bags :-

*>------------------Bag1.cbl----------2001 Aug 20 -------------

*> Note all the 'Division' stuff is missing until we come to
*> Procedure Division

CLASS-CONTROL.
                  Bag                       is class "bag"
                  CharacterArray     is class "chararry"
                  Cobolpicx             is class "picx"
                   .
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

*> 78's are COBOL 2002 'CONSTANTS'

78 TableSize                         value 7.
78 TextLength                       value 20.

01 n                                     pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ws-Bool                          pic 99 comp-5.
etc.....

PROCEDURE DIVISION.

 perform BAG-OF-REFERENCES
 perform BAG-OF-VALUES

 STOP RUN.

BAG-OF-REFERENCES.
etc......

*>-----------------

Linkage is still relevant in both non-OO and OO :-

Linkage section.
01 value1        pic 9(03).
01 value2        pic x(10).
01 value3.
    05 value3a    pic 99.
    05 value3b    pic x.
Procedure Division using value1, value2 returning value3.
...........

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T11:52:09+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m039o$s1v$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <9lejt8$6q9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <163715e7.0108160833.570404c5@posting.google.com> <3b8229f3.32861858@news.uswest.net> <3B8287F8.ADEA22D0@home.com> <3b8342a7.17838119@news.uswest.net> <3B83375F.11A4FCC6@home.com>`

```
Provided you don't want to start your procedural code with a section or
paragraph name, you can in fact omit the PROCEDURE DIVISION header, even
when it's preceded by data definitions.  Also, if the program starts with
working storage, you can actually start with the 01 level number.  Another
example of a minimal program that can be compiled under Net Expresss:

       01 x1 pic x(20) value "Hello world".
       display x1
```

```
"Curtis Bass" <cmbass_us@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com...
> Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:<3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca>...
> > Curtis Bass wrote:
> >
> > >
> > > > The problem is that of writing vs. maintaining the programs.  It's
easy to
> > > > write them that way but the very devil to maintain.  Given that
proper
> > > > documentation is never prepared, it becomes a marathon of
frustration and
> > > > wasted effort having to look at another program, then another, then
> > > > another - just to find that you're off down the wrong tree.  Then
you have
> > > > to remember where you started and> begin again.  At least if all the
> > > > modules are within the same program - i.e., performed - you don't
have to
> > > > go as far back to start over.
> > >
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Lacey

Just try concurrent development, in a controlled development environment
such as LCS, SCLM, Librarian or Changeman.

i.e. consider a program with a main module & a sub module. It's possible
to have someone spend 6 months working to change the main module, and
3 months into the process have someone change the sub module, and
release it a week later, with no impact to the main module.

Try that with nested code, and you've got extra work on your hands, with
version control..

"DASD is cheap" was my bosses favourite cliche, and he also believed
CPU time was cheap - but never try and get extra development time,
just because you are doing concurrent development!


>
> Considering that, with nested subprograms, all modules are in fact
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Curtis
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad? source management system

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T05:31:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.15e66843a2f3b888989689@news>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
In article <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>, 
davecawdell@home.com says...
> 
> "Curtis Bass" <cmbass_us@yahoo.com> wrote in message
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


I implemented a program using 'nested' programs, actually about 110 of 
them nested into a single source module. 

I found the key to maintainability was to have each "nested" program be 
fully encapsulated in a separate Panvalet include member or a separate 
cobol copybook. this was even though the program is "nested" the actual 
source for each is contained in separate unique elements within the 
controlled developement system.... Endevor OS/390 in our case.

ps...  highly modularized IMS update program containing 100+ transaction 
codes. each tran code is encapsulated within a separate module, there 
are "common" modules which are used to do the i/o...

very high volume rates 480 million transactions in 2 ~ 4 hours.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad? source management system

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T05:56:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.15e66e1ec57ab92798968a@news>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <MPG.15e66843a2f3b888989689@news>`

```
In article <MPG.15e66843a2f3b888989689@news>, clastname@nospam.com 
says...
> 
> I implemented a program using 'nested' programs, actually about 110 of 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

ps...   the original code was modularized using dynamic calls. using 
Strobe we saw that about 20 percent of the cpu time was spent in the 
dynamic call process. tried with static calls reduced the overhead to 
about 5 percent...   

static calls to "nested" programs virtually eliminated all the overhead 
of the call...   ie, no standard linkage convention used...  create save 
areas, save registers, restore registers etc...   no environment 
initialization of any type.   just a branch / link followed by setting 
addressability of the "passed" parameters.  

ps...   compile with the "LIST" option and look at the generated code.


our application   480 million transactions  6 ~ 8 calls per transaction.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T20:55:49+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
In article <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>,
 "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com> wrote:

> "Curtis Bass" <cmbass_us@yahoo.com> wrote in message
> news:163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com...
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Actually, there is no need to NEST routines that do not require shared 
variables with the calling routine. I would use nested programs as I 
would use subroutines in other languages. Those subroutines that are 
unique to the mainline stay in the same source file as the mainline. All 
subroutines that may be shared are in seperate source files.

My problem is with PERFORM and more especially PARAGRAPHS. These 
hangovers of a bygone era do not lead to structured style. Especially, 
the lack of local variables within a paragraphs. Nested programs neatly 
provide this function.

I suspect that those of you who object to nested programs will also have 
the same objection to OO methods, which in effect are nested programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T13:59:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lj7te$mba$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```

On 17-Aug-2001, Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote:

> My problem is with PERFORM and more especially PARAGRAPHS. These
> hangovers of a bygone era do not lead to structured style. Especially,
> the lack of local variables within a paragraphs. Nested programs neatly
> provide this function.

So we have different definitions of "structured".   Why is a call of an
external program structured, while a perform of an internal paragraph not?
Local variables can be nice, surely - but are they a necessary part of the
definition of "structured"?



Or the more important question - how does one structure fit business needs
better than the other?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T19:45:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Nvef7.5273$2u.51842@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <9lj7te$mba$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <9lj7te$mba$1@peabody.colorado.edu>, Howard Brazee says...
>
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>better than the other?

I took a class in structured programming logic a few years back and nowhere in
the book was one mention of the concept of called programs.  The concept has
been around for a while, but even sub-programs can get to the point of
rediculous.  I could see the use of a module/program that does some complicated
percentage or tax computation being separate from the main program.  I still
wouldnt like to just use this technique for every single perform.  There is
point in programming that if passed causes the program to border on the
rediculous or insane.

There is nothing wrong with one program calling another.  We use a CICS package
which we have customized over the years that allows programs to call each other.
If you already have the data that is the key to a field like a SSN we just use
it to jump to another program, and possibly back again.  For instance if you
were looking at a student address and wanted to see a list of his classes you
just jump.  Then if you want to see a list of students in the class he is taking
you just jump.  Then if you want to see all the classes the instructor of the
class teaches, you just jump.  All of the jumps are done by entering a line
number of the information as applicable and a code for the screen to move to.
This actually saves the user from entering all the data over and over.

I can't see any real significant advantage to using and endless hierarchy of
called subprograms for a single program.  Normally COBOL doesnt have the
functionality of a truly object oriented language.  Even so, there may be some
compilers that can take advantage of OOP concepts.  I could see it if you were
using classes and polymorphism could determine which class to run for a specific
task based on the object type like is possible in JAVA, but normally COBOL isnt
thought of as being truly OOP.  

It may be possible to conceal the update portion of a program to keep it
separate if most users only have a view-only access.  I have heard of some shops
that like to limit the size of individual programs. I have also heard of some
shops that have programs exceeding 10,000 lines of code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T21:37:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B7D8F27.BC13A0C2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <9lj7te$mba$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Nvef7.5273$2u.51842@www.newsranger.com>`

```


Charles wrote:

>
> I can't see any real significant advantage to using and endless hierarchy of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Java took off about five years ago. ('91 Naughton and Gosling worked on first model
'Oak', then '92 "*7",  followed by 'First Person' through '93/'94, big breakthrough
was Autumn '95, when it featured as a component of  the forthcoming Netscape 2.0,
released in '96).

A viable working OO COBOL has certainly been around since about '95 or earlier, and
it didn't wait around for Java to happen. I've been using the Net Express
predecessor Visual Object COBOL since '95 and now Net Express  - with classes,
collections/dictionaries and GUIs ! It uses all the same buzz words. (Prior to
VISOC, Micro Focus also had another OO product - available in '93 - included in
their product 'The OO Option for Workbench', but I wasn't aware of it).

OO COBOL was first discussed in 1989, and in November '89 there was a symposium in
Scottsdale, Arizona to discuss the topic, which brought the OOCTG (X3J4.1 OO COBOL
Task Group) into existence. A handbook was published by Micro Focus in '93,
summarizing the work of OOCTG. The "X3J4" we now call just plain "J4"..

The only sour note in all this, referencing from the above handbook, ".......First
level approval is expected by 1994, but final ratification is not expected until
1997". COBOL 2002 gives us the first stab at officially recognizing that OO COBOL
exists, but the collections/dictionaries have still to be discussed to determine an
official J4 standard.

There are currently four working OO COBOL compilers, Fjutisu, Hitachi, IBM (Visual
Age) and Micro Focus.

Therefore I disagree with both your statements :-

- "Normally COBOL doesn't have the functionality of a truly object oriented
language."

- "but normally COBOL isn't thought of as being truly OOP", -  by whom ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-18T15:51:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lmkih$607$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <9lj7te$mba$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Nvef7.5273$2u.51842@www.newsranger.com> <3B7D8F27.BC13A0C2@home.com> <4vgf7.5463$2u.53928@www.newsranger.com> <3B7D95CE.B48B4AC0@home.com> <4145699b.0108181217.6b646126@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen's statement is wrong (in one way) and misleading in another.

In actual fact, the IBM compiler has OO support in *3* operating systems,
OS/390 (z/OS), Windows, and AIX (IBM's variation on Unix for the
"workstation").  It does *not* support OO under VM.

HOWEVER, IBM is quite clear that this really is NOT 3 different "compilers"
in the normal sense of the word. The "front-end" is in fact the SAME
"product".  It is true that the "machine" code and run-times are separate
products - but the "compiler" (the part that handles "syntax" and "source
code") is the SAME no matter which environment you do development on - or
plan to run your application on.
```

```
I didn't include either VSE or OS/400 - as they are "slightly" different
products.  I think that the VSE compiler is "sort-of common sourced" with
the OS/390 & VM products - but it certainly is NOT kept "up to date" with
them (which may or may not have to do with the fact that LE itself isn't
kept "current" on VSE).

The COBOL for OS/400 is DEFINTELY a different "product stream".
```

- **Date:** 2001-08-18T18:11:52-07:00

- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <9lj7te$mba$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Nvef7.5273$2u.51842@www.newsranger.com> <3B7D8F27.BC13A0C2@home.com> <4vgf7.5463$2u.53928@www.newsranger.com> <3B7D95CE.B48B4AC0@home.com> <4145699b.0108181217.6b646126@posting.google.com> <9lmkih$607$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<9lmkih$607$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>...
> Stephen's statement is wrong (in one way) and misleading in another.
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote 

> > IBM has TWO OO COBOL Compilers. Visual Age for the PC, and COBOL/390
> > (which is actually part of a family of compilers, and seems to be
> > getting very close to Visual Age, without the GUI stuff).
> >
> > Stephen J Spiro


Ummm, essentially you said what I said in more words, with a little
more detail, after saying it was wrong.  COBOL for AIX is nearly the
same as COBOL/390 ("part of the family of compilers"). And Visual Age
is the same except for the GUI extensions and the associated
"development kit".  The GUI stuff makes the Visual Age product into a
different enough compiler, even if the underlying COBOL is essentially
the same.

The current IBM COBOL manual (Document Number SC26-9046-04) is called 
"COBOL for OS/390ï¿½ & VM" (it includes the COBOL syntax for AIX and
Visual Age).
It includes all of the IBM syntax for Object Orientation... I am
unable to find anything which EXcludes  the OO syntax and
functionality from VM. Perhaps you could point me to the appropriate
reference.

Stephen J Spiro
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-18T20:57:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ln6jq$ksl$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <9lj7te$mba$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Nvef7.5273$2u.51842@www.newsranger.com> <3B7D8F27.BC13A0C2@home.com> <4vgf7.5463$2u.53928@www.newsranger.com> <3B7D95CE.B48B4AC0@home.com> <4145699b.0108181217.6b646126@posting.google.com> <9lmkih$607$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0108181711.3e7fa7ab@posting.google.com>`

```
Once again, check your facts before posting.  Anyone can see the cover page
of the "COBOL Language Reference" at:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGYLR205/COVER

which CLEARLY states,

"COBOL for OS/390� & VM

COBOL Set for AIX�

VisualAge� COBOL

Language Reference

Document Number SC26-9046-04"

It (the language reference manuals) is most definitely *NOT* (as you claim
below) called
  "COBOL for OS/390� & VM"
(that is ONE of the 3 listed "environments" for which the book called "COBOL
Language Reface" applies) as can also be seen if you go to:
 http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/Shelves/igysh211

As for the LACK of VM support for OO, see (for example)
   http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igypg205/3.5.1?

which states (in part),
 "CMS does not support using the following COBOL code ...
  Object-oriented COBOL language extensions (references to objects, methods,
or classes, for example).  ..."

Or any number of other places in the COBOL bookshelf (if you do a "search"
on Object & Orient & VM) for example,
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/igylr205/FRONT_1?

"This book documents extensions for object-oriented COBOL. Object-oriented
COBOL is not supported under VM."

In fact, that is an excellent page to go to, to see that AS FAR AS IBM is
concerned there is "one" IBM COBOL language that covers (as it states on
that page),

"This book presents the syntax of COBOL for OS/390 & VM, COBOL Set for AIX,
and VisualAge COBOL (collectively referred to in this book as IBM COBOL)."

There ARE environmental differences - but the fact that IBM uses the "same"
code for their "front-end" is pretty well known.

I know of NO "GUI support" in any compiler - but do know of such in some
IDE's.  Do you have some evidence to support IBM including "GUI support" in
one of their COMPILERs?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T11:40:37-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108191040.18b15c3f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <9lj7te$mba$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Nvef7.5273$2u.51842@www.newsranger.com> <3B7D8F27.BC13A0C2@home.com> <4vgf7.5463$2u.53928@www.newsranger.com> <3B7D95CE.B48B4AC0@home.com> <4145699b.0108181217.6b646126@posting.google.com> <9lmkih$607$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <4145699b.0108181711.3e7fa7ab@posting.google.com> <9ln6jq$ksl$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3B7F39EC.EF093E78@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote 
<snip>
> Much more pertinent, Bill and Stephen - is anybody actually using IBM OO COBOL
> ?. We know from a message that Bill posted a while back that they, (Big Blue),
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> haven't heard anything since.


I don't know anyone using IBM's OO COBOL (altho somewhere I am sure it
is being done).  Because IBM's OO COBOL is based on a preliminary
draft of the standard, I advise my clients NOT to use the OO features
of COBOL/390 (or any of the other OO COBOL compilers) unless the
Vendor either
1. guarantees an inexpensive migration path to the standard
2. guarantees support for the current syntax for a LONG time.


Unless they have upgraded it lately, Visual Age COBOL runs only on
OS/2, which makes it of limited value.  Has anyone heard differently?

As a historical note, the first 2 releases of IBM's COBOL II were
based on early drafts of the 1985 Standard.  IBM provided the COMPR2
("compatibility with Release 2") option with its later releases, and
support for these features was not dropped until last March.

Stephen J Spiro
Wizard Systems
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T17:44:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fKcf7.17587$ZM2.1555686@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
Now finally that's the gist of it. Use separately compiled subroutines
whenever reasonable, especially when other programs might find them
useful. Use internal nested local routines only for stuff unique to the
program, to emulate a Perform Using, so that it's not as cumbersome as a
regular Perform and it can have its own local variables and accept
parameters. Use performs for the simple internal calls that don't need
parameter passing or local variables.

Generally, only data definitions e.g. record layouts, not executable code,
should be in copybooks. Exceptions might be standardized interfaces, used
macro-style, e.g. with copy replacing, and even then the code in the
copybook should be as little as possible and if it needs to do anything
major then it should call a subroutine. If an internal nested routine
becomes popular enough to tempt anyone to put it in a copybook, or
otherwise to have it called/included by more than one program, it should
be converted into an externally compiled subroutine. If it badly needs to
access global variables, change it to a parameter so it can be a separate
subroutine. Use a copybook to simplify the interface as above if needed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-17T18:15:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Qadf7.22179$Lw3.1245565@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <fKcf7.17587$ZM2.1555686@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```

On 17-Aug-2001, "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC"
<Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>
wrote:

> Now finally that's the gist of it. Use separately compiled subroutines
> whenever reasonable, especially when other programs might find them
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> parameter passing or local variables.

What is so cumbersome about a PERFORM?   Do you use nested programs to
"simplify" a complex PERFORM VARYING?   Nested programs are a complex way of
providing local variables - and the Cobol community hasn't been vocal about
wanting local variables.

> Generally, only data definitions e.g. record layouts, not executable code,
> should be in copybooks. Exceptions might be standardized interfaces, used
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> subroutine. Use a copybook to simplify the interface as above if needed.

We have a lot of programs that do a call for a program abort.   As far as I
can tell, the only effect of this is to disguise where the abort really
occurred.   Another copy member used was created for Y2K conversion.  Now
that copy member could have effectively been a nested program, but it didn't
happen that way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC" <Sending-Email-To-This-Address-Constitutes-A-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP@rmpcp.com?Subject=I-Am-A-Spammer-So-I-Promise-To-Send-One-Thousand-Dollars-To-RMPCP-For-Each-Message>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T01:09:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<exig7.1038$ZN5.170585@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <3B797B6C.6F26F765@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108151112.2d3b3c6f@posting.google.com> <3B7B4D55.D0D6F5BE@mb.sympatico.ca> <163715e7.0108161424.6d734382@posting.google.com> <VmYe7.144601$Cy.20165112@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <josephk-88C39E.20554917082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <fKcf7.17587$ZM2.1555686@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <Qadf7.22179$Lw3.1245565@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
What I most likely meant is that a perform can be very cumbersome when you
really want to pass parameters, e.g. old code that says


move where-I-really-have-it-1 to input-parm
perform paragraph
move output-parm to where-I-really-want-it-1
...
move where-I-really-have-it-2 to input-parm
perform paragraph
move output-parm to where-I-really-want-it-2


would be much simpler as


call "nested-routine" using
  where-I-really-have-it-1, where-I-really-want-it-1
...
call "nested-routine" using
  where-I-really-have-it-2, where-I-really-want-it-2


These could've been done with external programs to call but suppose that
in this case it wasn't felt that that was worth doing, e.g. no other
program would have a use for this routine.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T22:15:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010814181507.00838.00002727@mb-mm.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9lc2ru$kel$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee writes ...

>
>On 14-Aug-2001, "Ken MacKenzie" <ken.mackenzie@bigfoot.com> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>with me though to point to.

In our course, Beyond COBOL II, we discuss nested programs (and in other
courses, too). The main difference in invoking nested programs versus using
performs is that you call a nested program, and you may pass it parameters.
This may make it more general than PERFORMed procedures, since you must hard
code the data item names there.

Nonetheless, few shops use nested programs. They are [relatively] new (only
been around for 15 years, but, hey, who needed training on COBOL II?). And
nested programs are not as clean to maintain as dynamically called external
subroutines.

Cheers,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com

Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

#### ↳ Re: Nested Programs - Good or Bad?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-08-14T21:30:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010814173031.18240.00001988@nso-cl.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`

```
In article <9lbsdq$es4$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>, "Ken MacKenzie"
<ken.mackenzie@bigfoot.com> writes:

>I've just started working for a company (5 or 6 weeks) using Cobol on an
>OS/390 platform.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have found very few instances where I was able to use nested programming 
techniques.   I have on the other hand used sub-program/called program
structures to deal with the localization of variables and reduction of code
ducplication.   The biggest selling point was that by using the called routine 
methodology we coded the procedure only once and if there were any corrections,
the code only had to be changed in one place.  All calling programs
were set up with dynamic calls to access these sub-programs.

In my current environment, I hope to be able to propagate that same 
methodology in an effort to reduce the volume of code duplication.
```
