If a job needs to finish before 7:00 A.M. or it will be killed, and can't
start until after midnight, and takes 7 hours and 1 minute to run, then a
few percentage points of efficiency are a mandatory minimum.

Efficiency needs aren't constant.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alternates to Dynamic Linkage (Re: "nested programs" - terminology)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T19:13:07+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8FD3B3.89410158@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9lk4ni$4o0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <9lk6j3$bbe$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b7e84d4_2@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9mds1h$lef$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b8b986d_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9mguqa$9f6$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <b3638c46.0108281756.248ce6bb@posting.google.com> <3B8DDECE.A2548BD4@Azonic.co.nz> <9mjjpj$msi$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B8E773E.4C284717@Azonic.co.nz> <9mlr20$q41$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <9mm9c5$do9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> If a job needs to finish before 7:00 A.M. or it will be killed, and can't
> start until after midnight, and takes 7 hours and 1 minute to run, then a
> few percentage points of efficiency are a mandatory minimum.

While a single of initial dynamic call _can_ take much longer to
complete than a static linked one, repetitive calls to a loaded routine
are insignificantly more than to a static one, and only use a few CPU
cycles.

Any run that takes several hours is most likely to be:
    * Disk or database limited
    * Variable depending on data volumes
    * Better served by improving other tuning options    

Certainly you could 'prove a point' by adding a CANCEL after each CALL
to show how slow it _could_ be made if you were really struggling to
support your view.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alternates to Dynamic Linkage (Re: "nested programs" - terminology)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T11:46:01+12:00

```
Thanks Richard.

I endorse this 100% and it is a better example than I was able to provide
myself.

The concept I was trying to get across to Chuck was the FLEXIBILITY of true
dynamic calling.

You provided an excellent concrete example of it.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alternates to Dynamic Linkage (Re: "nested programs" - terminology)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** gospodin.spasebaw@tanstaafl.com (freewheelin)
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T15:50:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b8cff7b.4481299@news.uswest.net>`
- **References:** `<9lk4ni$4o0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <9lk6j3$bbe$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b7e84d4_2@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9mds1h$lef$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b8b986d_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9mguqa$9f6$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
On Tue, 28 Aug 2001 13:26:49 -0700, "Chuck Stevens"
<charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
…[44 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Chuck, this is an application system design issue.  Transaction
processors often used this technique.   An application may generate a
large set of transactions and many programs might generate the same
transaction.  Depending on the transaction and its origin, the
destination could be variable, both local and remote.   Rather than
writing each program to deal with each situation we created a general
transaction format in a wrapper for a transaction processor.   The
processor program read from a single queue file and based on tables,
figured out which formatting programs to call.  The idea was to get a
grip on transaction proliferation in applications.  Implementing a new
transaction, removing a transaction or simply changing the scope of
distribution required less work and made things easier when the
business changed its rules.

One such processor I wrote had an initial startup phase where it first
"polled" all the programs loaded in its table and flagged them when it
had trouble and signaled support.  Corporate transaction "04"
generated by the order application might be sent to several other
application sytems while an "04"  from the receiving dock might only
report to the inventory system.  The transaction processor's resource
tables (loaded at startup)  mapped the relationships and they included
PROGRAM-ID names.   So the processor had a single CALL statement.

JF
```

The keyword here is FLEXIBILITY.

Pete.

what

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

an

> >since you know you're going to crash-and-burn if the library you try to
call

be

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

list

something

…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> JF

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alternates to Dynamic Linkage (Re: "nested programs" - terminology)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T11:28:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0108311028.550485dc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lk4ni$4o0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <9lk6j3$bbe$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b7e84d4_2@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9mds1h$lef$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b8b986d_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9mguqa$9f6$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b8cff7b.4481299@news.uswest.net> <3b8d7fb8_9@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
>various people wrote on nested programs, dynamic versus static calls,
etc

Surely the point is to use the appropriate technique for the
circumstances, rather than always one or the other, for example

inline performs - a few lines of code

out-of-line performs - rather more lines of code

nested programs - even more lines of code and for common routines
within a system that can be included via copybooks

static calls to independently compiled subprograms - for routines
common within a system and the names of which are known at compilation
time

dynamic calls - for independently compiled subprograms common within a
system and whose names are not known at compilation time

This is only a very brief summary.  The way in which linkage editing
can be done varies between implementations and can provide a certain
amount of overlap between the last two techniques, as independent load
modules can be linked either statically or dynamically even when
called statically and subprograms can be made reentrant or reusable,
especially when written in other languages.  The use of nested
programs seems beneficial for encapsulating data and procedures from
the remainder of a program while not paying the efficiency overheads
that come from compiling and linking independently compiled programs,
such a use could be recommended where it is known that it is unlikely
to be necessary to frequently recompile the constituent subprograms
while leaving the calling programs unchanged.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "nested programs" - terminology

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T22:03:53+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-3996C0.22035229082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<9lk4ni$4o0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <9lk6j3$bbe$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b7e84d4_2@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9mds1h$lef$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3b8b986d_4@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
In article <3b8b986d_4@goliath.newsgroups.com>,
 "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> Oh, yes it is...<G> (chorus in background..."Oh, NO, it isn't..."...."look
> out behind you!")
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Not to be too rude, but this is a load of "ball pucky".

Look, you have a 128 byte cache - for example. This is broken into 2 x 
64 byte cache lines. Cache line 0 contains the mainline, cache line 1 
will contain the subroutine. Branching from mainline to subroutine and 
back again is simply a matter of switching cache lines. NO memory access 
required - except for data - hopefully in a separate data cache.

Scale this up to Mainframe and current Micro-processor cache sizes and 
you can see that this argument starts to become redundant.

At the end of the day, you can forget about hardware performance issues 
when writing high level languages. If the compiler writer is any good 
they will have at least optimized the code for the target hardware.

With COBOL, you do get a choice of optimizations via different langauge 
elements to perform the same action. I.E. use of indexes versus 
subscripts etc. But please note, SOME compilers will even remove that 
distinction and will optimise away any high level decisions on which 
language element you need to use to get better performance.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Performance of performs and calls was Re: "nested programs" - terminology

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T01:55:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B806DAC.904673D6@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<9lk4ni$4o0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <9lk6j3$bbe$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Based on testing with the COBOL for MVS and VM compiler. Bill's
heirarchy is generally true.  Because of optimizations paragraphs being
PERFORMed may be moved in-line and thus have no more overhead than
in-line PERFORMs.  If the program is properly structured the PERFORM of
a separate paragraph not moved in-line will have less overhead than a
PERFORM in a program not properly structured (overlapping PERFORMs,
PERFORMs with GO TO statements that may go out of range, etc.).  A CALL
of a nested program also theoretically can be moved in-line although I
have not seen it.  The call definitely has less overhead than a dynamic
call both on an intial use and per instance basis.  An earlier posting
to this thread explained why for even the statically linked programs. 
It has to do with how much context switching must be done.

I would be very interested in the performance of nested programs in a
current Micro-Focus environment because their manual says there is a
performance penalty if the compile option allows nested programming even
if it isn't used.

Clark F. Morris, Jr. 

Chuck Stevens wrote:
> 
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>     -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Performance of performs and calls was Re: "nested programs" - terminology

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T10:22:10+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lql7n$jpn$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<9lk4ni$4o0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <9lk6j3$bbe$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <3B806DAC.904673D6@istar.ca>`

```
In current Micro Focus products (Net Express and Server Express), the
compiler option to enable nested programs is no longer required (i.e. the
NESTCALL directive is simply ignored).  Nested programs may be used freely
and there is no performance penalty associated with their use.  Generally a
call to a nested program will be considerably faster than a call to an
external program.

"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote in message
news:3B806DAC.904673D6@istar.ca...
> Based on testing with the COBOL for MVS and VM compiler. Bill's
> heirarchy is generally true.  Because of optimizations paragraphs being
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > Depending on the compiler (and run-time and operating system), in
GENERAL,
> > > performance can be "graded" as follows (from best to worst)
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > To be clear, there is almost CERTAINLY exceptions to this order (based
on
> > > optimization provided by the vendor)...
> >
> > Hmmm.  I still think you might be overstating this as a general rule,
Bill.
> > There is no particular architecture-independent reason why this order
should
> > hold true, nor is any deviation from this order necessarily the result
of
> > some optimization on the part of the compiler implementor.
> >
> > For example, on Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85, a call on a nested program *may*
> > require more resources than a call on an external one of the same name
if it
> > is necessary for the run-time system to determine whether the call is
indeed
> > to a nested program or a separately-compiled one.
> >
> > And once the linkage between the caller and the called program (nested
or
> > otherwise) is established (ordinarily by the first CALL with no
intervening
> > CANCEL) and presuming no further "diagnoses" of program-name scope need
to
> > be performed, any difference in performance among these three mechanisms
> > should be unmeasurable because they all use the same sequence of machine
> > instructions.
> >
> > Unisys MCP/AS distinguishes between "dynamic" determination of CALLing
scope
> > for a "CALL <literal>" statement, which is how I am interpreting
"dynamic
> > binding", and the entire subject of "CALL <dataname>".  Unisys supports
> > "CALL <dataname>" because the '85 standard requires it, but we actively
> > discourage our users from writing applications using the feature; the
> > overhead of constructing the library template, establishing the library
> > linkage, calling the program, and tearing it all back down again on each
and
> > every call is quite high.    If that particular feature is what you mean
by
> > "dynamic binding", then I agree, it's likely to be costly.  But the
> > "binding" necessary for CALL <literal> is sometimes dynamic as well.
> >
> >     -Chuck Stevens
```

Stephan

```

> How do I tell the dammned thing to remove all entries?
> Or, better, a single one (the selected)

#### ↳ Re: How to reset a List-Box

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-08-18T19:43:25+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<XtIqrJANdrf7IwkV@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<9lm0df$uck$00$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Stephan,

What COBOL are you using? If it's Acucobol-GT you can empty a list box 
by using the MODIFY verb to set the RESET-LIST property to 1, as in:

MODIFY MY-LIST RESET-LIST = 1.

To remove a single item you use the ITEM-TO-DELETE property, just set it 
to the number of the item you want to remove (1 is the first, 2 the 
second and so on):

MODIFY MY-LIST ITEM-TO-DELETE = WS-ITEM

or

MODIFY MY-LIST ITEM-TO-DELETE = 3

You can INQUIRE on the SELECTION-INDEX property if you need to know 
which item number is currently selected, or the VALUE property if you 
want to know what text is in the selected item:

INQUIRE MY-LIST SELECTION-INDEX IN WS-SELECTION,
                        VALUE IN WS-ITEM-TEXT.

Of course, if you're *not* using Acucobol-GT, this is all useless to 
you...  ;^)

Best regards

Nigel

In article <9lm0df$uck$00$1@news.t-online.com>, Stephan Wiesner 
<stephan@stephanwiesner.de> writes
>How do I tell the dammned thing to remove all entries?
>Or, better, a single one (the selected)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

```

## Thread: nested programs -- when do you use them? — 25 msgs

### nested programs -- when do you use them?

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-18T18:40:58-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B7F0B1A.58AFCE17@sprynet.com>`

```
I'm all for nested programs, in theory.  But when do you use them?  Do
you use them just for subroutines?  By that I mostly mean routines that
are probably called more than once and require some sort of input?  I
mean, you expect you wouldn't have a program where all of your "perform
whatever-para" are replaced by "call 'whatever-subprogram'" would you. 
I'd like to use them in a reasonable manner, but I'm afraid I'd have a
tendancy to go overboard!

Anyone have a fairly short example of a program with a good use of
nested programs?
```

#### ↳ Re: nested programs -- when do you use them?

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T12:19:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X8Of7.2092$O17.300702@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3B7F0B1A.58AFCE17@sprynet.com>`

```
One of my regular uses is to convert date formats from MMDDYY to YYMMDD. If
you have a program which has a lot of these, it's worth the effort. (Of
course, the program need notbe 'nested', it could be 'stacked'


PROGRAM-ID. MAIN.
...
...
   CALL MDY2YMD USING (any dataname with correct PICTURE)

PROGRAM-ID. MDY2YMD.
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING STORAGE SECTION.
01  YYMMDD  PIC X(06)
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 MMDDYY  PIC X(06).

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING MMDDYY.
   STRING MMMDDYY(5:2) DELIMITED BY SIZE
                MMMDDYY(1:2) DELIMITED BY SIZE
                MMMDDYY(3:2) DELIMITED BY SIZE
     INTO    YYMMDD
     MOVE YYMMDD to MMDDYY
     GOBACK.
END PROGRAM MDY2YMD.
END PROGRAM MAIN.

-
Michael Mattias
Tal Systems Inc.
Racine WI USA
michael.mattias@gte.net

MULTIPLY YYMMDD BY 100.0001 GIVING MMDDYY
and
MULTIPLY MMDDYY BY 10000.01 GIVING YYMMDD.
Those can be modified to include century for Y2K, also.

03  INPUT-DATE-REDEF REDEFINES (whatever current name is)
    05 MM    PIC 99.
    05 DD     PIC 99.
    05 YY     PIC 99.

03  OUTPUT-DATE-REDEF REDEFINES (whatever current name is)
    05 YY     PIC 99.
    05 MM    PIC 99.
    05 DD     PIC 99.

and

MOVE CORRESPONDING INPUT-DATE-REDEF
                                     TO OUTPUT-DATE-REDEF.

No need for a nested program, nice simple supportable code, quick
to write, easy to test and shouldn't cause too many problems in future
COBOL upgrades.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: nested programs -- when do you use them?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T14:20:31+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b8072ce_1@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3B7F0B1A.58AFCE17@sprynet.com> <X8Of7.2092$O17.300702@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <EMRf7.7979$QY3.1024047163@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <NiYf7.2946$O17.429622@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <hVYf7.163182$Cy.22212263@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
I like this, Rufio.

I have been aware of it many years but don't use it.

Guess it is because I tend to avoid "MOVE CORR".

It will still generate three moves of course, but your point about a single
statement and no need for a nested program is very valid.

I think Michael was simply trying to demonstrate a possible use of program
nesting; I felt on reading his code that it hardly justified a
sub-program...

In the case where more is required (as in the example I posted) a
sub-program (or possibly, an out-of-line Perform... if I write it, it would
be a SECTION of code <G>) IS justified.

The topic for the thread is "When do you use them?"

Speaking for myself, I ONLY use them for Methods in OO COBOL (PowerCOBOL
will generate a nested sub-program automatically whenever you write an event
script).

I agree there is a definite place for them, but, as you have shown here, it
is not necessarily for simple tasks like Date reformatting.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: nested programs -- when do you use them?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T12:50:48+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b80600d_2@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3B7F0B1A.58AFCE17@sprynet.com> <X8Of7.2092$O17.300702@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <EMRf7.7979$QY3.1024047163@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Here in New Zealand we keep it simple... gives us more time to go to the
beach...<G>

Why would I use STRING OR MULTIPLY when MOVE can do it?

(Note that Reference Modification in this case is resolved at compile time.
Yes, I know STRING and MULTIPLY are also, but both of them involve more
machine overhead than a simple move, and, Michael, I am surprised at you
still tolerating 2 digit dates...(tsk! tsk!)<G>)

> PROGRAM-ID. MDY2YMD.
> DATA DIVISION.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> PROCEDURE DIVISION USING MMDDYY.
move MMDDYY (5:2) to YYMMDD (1:2)
move MMDDYY (1:2) to YYMMDD (3:2)
move MMDDYY (3:2) to YYMMDD (5:2)
move YYMMDD to MMDDYY
>      GOBACK.
> END PROGRAM MDY2YMD.
> END PROGRAM MAIN.

Although it is less generated code (on the major platforms) than either of
the 2 previous solutions, this leaves me very unsatisfied...<G>

It really should handle the Century (if passed...),so let's make it
polymorphic...

You should be able to pass it EITHER a 6 digit date OR an 8 digit one.

If it is passed a century, it uses it. If it isn't, it calculates it.

But it ALWAYS returns an 8 character YYYYMMDD.

(We will handle dates between 1980 (when most PC BIOSes start...) and 2079
(when most of us will be pushing up daisies and no longer concerned with the
finer points of an Ancient computer programming Language...)

(If you REALLY must have YYMMDD, then use a ref. mod. MOVE to truncate
it...)


So, I would amend it as follows:

> PROGRAM-ID. MDY2YMD.
> DATA DIVISION.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> PROCEDURE DIVISION USING SomeDate.
   if SomeDate (7:2) = space         *> no century passed...
      if SomeDate (5:2) < 81
          move '20' to YYYYMMDD
      else
          move '19' to YYYYMMDD
      end-if
      move SomeDate (5:2) to YYYYMMDD (3:2)
   else
       move SomeDate (5:2) to YYYYMMDD *> use the passed century...

*>routine assumes passed date has been

*>validated elsewhere...
       move SomeDate (7:2) to YYYYMMDD (3:2)
       move SomeDate (3:6) to DateWork     *> Align the remaining fields for
       move DateWork to SomeDate              *> consistency..
   end-if
   move SomeDate (3:2) to YYYYMMDD (5:2)
   move SomeDate (1:2) to YYYYMMDD (7:2)
   move YYYYMMDD to SomeDate
>      GOBACK.
> END PROGRAM MDY2YMD.
> END PROGRAM MAIN.

Ah, now I feel better...<G>

This is still pretty primitive (it should validate what was passed... for
this exercise I have left that out...) but it is certainly worth making a
nested (or Called) Sub-program out of.

Pete.

"Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:EMRf7.7979$QY3.1024047163@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com...
>     You could save yourself some overhead by using:
> MULTIPLY YYMMDD BY 100.0001 GIVING MMDDYY
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: nested programs -- when do you use them?

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T06:18:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B80AB49.1117305C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3B7F0B1A.58AFCE17@sprynet.com> <X8Of7.2092$O17.300702@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <EMRf7.7979$QY3.1024047163@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Terry Heinze wrote:
> 
>     You could save yourself some overhead by using:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Those can be modified to include century for Y2K, also.

This was discussed in some detail in comp.software.year-2000 from
October 14, 1997 thru October 16, 1997.  It was generally thought to
be a bad idea.  Too difficult to understand, and unlikely to be
successfully windowed or expanded by automated conversion tools.

It's also less efficient than the straightforward approach, and the
multiply could generate a S0C7 abend if non-numeric data is present.

My contribution (from 1997) looked like this:

***** begin quote *****

Here is the generated code, using IBM COBOL II Release 4.0 with the
NUMPROC(NOPFD) option:

00872  COMPUTE
   001856  F275 D260 98B5          PACK 608(8,13),2229(6,9)     
   00185C  960F D267               OI   615(13),X'0F'           
   001860  FC73 D260 A395          MP   608(8,13),917(4,10)     
   001866  F353 98BB D263          UNPK 2235(6,9),611(4,13)
   00186C  96F0 98C0               OI   2240(9),X'F0'


000879  MOVE
000880  MOVE
    001894  D203 98BD 98B5          MVC  2237(4,9),2229(9)
000881  MOVE
    00189A  D201 98BB 98B9          MVC  2235(2,9),2233(9)

Somehow, I think there should have been TWO 'PACK' instructions
somewhere to support the compute statement.  I think it is reassuring
that the optimizer combined two MOVE statements of adjacent source and
target fields a single MVC (Move Character) instruction.

So COMPUTE YYMMDD = MMDDYY * 10000.01 generates five assembler
instructions for a total of 26 bytes of code, and the three MOVE
statements generated two assembler instuctions for a total length of
12
bytes of code.  That's certainly not as big a penalty as a call to a
library routine, but the MOVE statements are clearly more efficient in
machine-language terms.

**** end quote *****

I remember being quite pleased that the IBM COBOL II optimizer
converted three MOVE statements into two MVC instructions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: nested programs -- when do you use them?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T02:16:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b811b2b_6@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3B7F0B1A.58AFCE17@sprynet.com> <X8Of7.2092$O17.300702@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <EMRf7.7979$QY3.1024047163@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B80AB49.1117305C@worldnet.att.net>`

```
I totally endorse what you have said here, Arnold, and the object code bears
out my own contribution (which argued that MOVE has to be more efficient).

However, the  OI instruction  immediately after the PACK (which forces a
positive sign)  could be eradicated by making the pictures of YYMMDD and
DDMMYY signed. (The COBOL compiler generates this to force a positive sign
because the picture is unsigned...)

It's another example of how COBOL programmers can "help" to generate better
code by careful data definitions... in the old days we needed to; nowadays
you can argue that it makes so little difference it doesn't really matter...
('OR Immediate' <OI>, along with the other Boolean Operations, are amongst
the fastest executing instructions in the IBM mainframe's repertoire).

I remember once writing an entire program using ONLY 'OR', 'XOR', 'AND' and
'NOT' (the Boolean Operations) on an ICL 1900 in PLAN ('Program Language
1900', the "Assembler" for this machine range), just for a bet... (and to
demonstrate that computers really ONLY 'understand' binary operations when
all is said and done... <G>) This program read input data and actually
displayed processed data, all using ONLY the PLAN mnemonics mentioned...(NO
ACCEPT or DISPLAY or READ or WRITE...or Assembler equivalents) However it
DID modify its own instructions in a way which would make a COBOL ALTER look
like the epitome of good programming practice...<G>

Logical bitwise operations are blazingly fast on most platforms (once you
have the necessary registers loaded...(storage to storage gets a bit
slower...)) and you can do some quite 'interesting' things with them.

They are governed by the rules of Boolean Algebra and Propositional Calculus
(a couple of things very dear to my heart, since I was a very junior
programmer) which enforce an optimized and simple binary logic system, and
underlie the power of computer systems in general.

For those who MIGHT be interested (and I accept that the majority here won't
be...) here's something to whet your appetite....

Consider the case where two data fields must be "swapped" WITHOUT the use of
an intermediate area.

Impossible in COBOL? maybe... but using Boolean Operations, effortless...

field A:  1011   0011
field B:  0010   1100

(arbitrary values, just for fun...)

Using the Boolean Operation 'Exclusive OR' (this means: "one or the other,
but NOT both..."), we can obtain the following:(Compare fields bit against
bit, from right to left...)

1. XOR A:B (Exclusive OR the 2 fields and leave the result in field B...)

field A =  1011  0011
field B=   1001  1111

2. XOR B:A (Exclusive OR the 2 fields and leave the result in field A...)

field A =  0010  1100
field B=   1001  1111

3. XOR A:B (Exclusive OR the 2 fields and leave the result in field B...)

field A =  0010  1100
field B=   1011  0011

The original fields have miraculously swapped values without moving to
intermediate storage...it took 3 (extremely fast) machine instructions...

I leave it to the reader's imagination to think of circumstances where such
a facility could be useful...

Could we emulate this in COBOL?

A better question is: "Why would we want to?" (The 3 Machine instructions
are hardly "self-documenting" and, in COBOL, a bit of extra storage is never
a problem...)

(the answer is that it CAN be simulated in COBOL, but you'll have to write
to me or post here if you want to see how...<G>)

A much better use of this Mathematical Tool is in optimizing complex logic
(you may remember I posted a solution here, which was derived using the Laws
of Boolean Simplification, to Yukon Mama's COBOL "IF" logic maze. To be
fair, the "logic" which Eileen "inherited" was so awful that even such a
tool as this was not able to improve it much...)

The point I'm trying to make (I took the long way round as a bit of
self-indulgence) is that you can argue all you like about optimizing code in
COBOL... it is really irrelevant. If efficiency is your primary goal in
computer programming, don't use COBOL.

Now, before you all start putting on your white hoods and sharpening stakes,
I am NOT advocating that INEFFICIENT programs in COBOL are OK...all I am
saying is that intense arguments about it serve little purpose; efficiency
will NEVER be the reason that people use COBOL. When this design goal is
balanced against others, we can achieve an "acceptable" level of efficiency
with COBOL, and that is good enough for most people. But let's not delude
ourselves into thinking that ANY solution in COBOL can be the MOST efficient
possible....(It CAN be the "most efficient in COBOL" but is it really worth
fighting about?)

Pete.

when you deliver a new application that is 8 times as fast, they are less
upset at
the 2500 hours it took.

If the client asks - wasn't the program you just released supposed to do
that,
You respond by explaining that what you just released, works well as a stop
gap. But the basic appplication architecture is flawed and will have to be
redesigned - but that you can migrate the database from DLI to DB2 for just
another 1200 hours; and that will leverage the application, to migrate it to
the intranet. You fail to mention that if you went to the intranet now, you
could save 800 hours - because you don't yet have the skillset, and you
don't
want the project to go to anyone else.

But hey - that's mainframe consulting for you - I wonder if I should go work
for Arthur Androids/Accenture now?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: nested programs -- when do you use them?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jonathan Guthrie <jguthrie@brokersys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T20:16:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p51vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197>`
- **References:** `<3B7F0B1A.58AFCE17@sprynet.com> <X8Of7.2092$O17.300702@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <EMRf7.7979$QY3.1024047163@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B80AB49.1117305C@worldnet.att.net> <3b811b2b_6@goliath.newsgroups.com> <7Cdg7.325$EW6.121332@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3b811b2b_6@goliath.newsgroups.com...

> I once read somewhere that _all_ programming reduces to but four
> instructions: store, compare, add and jump.

A computer with a single instruction (that one being "decrement and
branch if not zero") is Turing-equivalent and can therefore, in 
principle, do everything any other Turing-equivalent computer (which
is every digital computer I've ever heard of) can do.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: nested programs -- when do you use them?

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T13:12:28+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b8062f4_5@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3B7F0B1A.58AFCE17@sprynet.com> <X8Of7.2092$O17.300702@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Here in New Zealand we keep it simple... gives us more time to go to the
beach...<G>

Why would I use STRING OR MULTIPLY when MOVE can do it?

(Note that Reference Modification in this case is resolved at compile time.
Yes, I know STRING and MULTIPLY are also, but both of them involve more
machine overhead than a simple move, and, Michael, I am surprised at you
still tolerating 2 digit dates...(tsk! tsk!)<G>)

> PROGRAM-ID. MDY2YMD.
> DATA DIVISION.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> PROCEDURE DIVISION USING MMDDYY.
move MMDDYY (5:2) to YYMMDD (1:2)
move MMDDYY (1:2) to YYMMDD (3:2)
move MMDDYY (3:2) to YYMMDD (5:2)
move YYMMDD to MMDDYY
>      GOBACK.
> END PROGRAM MDY2YMD.
> END PROGRAM MAIN.

Although it is less generated code (on the major platforms) than either of
the 2 previous solutions, this leaves me very unsatisfied...<G>

It really should handle the Century (if passed...),so let's make it
polymorphic...

You should be able to pass it EITHER a 6 digit date OR an 8 digit one.

If it is passed a century, it uses it. If it isn't, it calculates it.

But it ALWAYS returns an 8 character YYYYMMDD.

(We will handle dates between 1980 (when most PC BIOSes start...) and 2079
(when most of us will be pushing up daisies and no longer concerned with the
finer points of an Ancient computer programming Language...)

(If you REALLY must have YYMMDD, then use a ref. mod. MOVE to truncate
it...)


So, I would amend it as follows:

> PROGRAM-ID. MDY2YMD.
> DATA DIVISION.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> PROCEDURE DIVISION USING SomeDate.
   if SomeDate (7:2) = space         *> no century passed...
      if SomeDate (5:2) < 81
          move '20' to YYYYMMDD
      else
          move '19' to YYYYMMDD
      end-if
      move SomeDate (5:2) to YYYYMMDD (3:2)
   else
       move SomeDate (5:2) to YYYYMMDD *> use the passed century...

*>routine assumes passed date has been

*>validated elsewhere...
       move SomeDate (7:2) to YYYYMMDD (3:2)
       move SomeDate (3:6) to DateWork     *> Align the remaining fields for
       move DateWork to SomeDate              *> consistency..
   end-if
   move SomeDate (3:2) to YYYYMMDD (5:2)
   move SomeDate (1:2) to YYYYMMDD (7:2)
   move YYYYMMDD to SomeDate
>      GOBACK.
> END PROGRAM MDY2YMD.
> END PROGRAM MAIN.

Ah, now I feel better...<G>

This is still pretty primitive (it should validate what was passed... for
this exercise I have left that out...) but it is certainly worth making a
nested (or Called) Sub-program out of.

Pete.

"Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net> wrote in message
news:EMRf7.7979$QY3.1024047163@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com...
>     You could save yourself some overhead by using:
> MULTIPLY YYMMDD BY 100.0001 GIVING MMDDYY
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<a id="t-37a463d8ca"></a>
## Thread: How to catch File Exceptions — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to catch File Exceptions

- **From:** "Stephan Wiesner" <stephan@stephanwiesner.de>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T10:11:59+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lnsbf$vr8$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
I'm using Acucobol (see, learned something and stat this first now :-)
I want to read some data from a file. That works fine, if the file already
exists and the opening succeeds.
If the file doesn't exist, though, I get an exception and the programm
terminates.
So I need to either catch the exception or check first if the file already
exists.

Stephan
```

#### ↳ Re: How to catch File Exceptions

- **From:** "Paul Pigott" <paul.pigott@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T11:38:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LyNf7.22899$1p1.1776435@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<9lnsbf$vr8$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
In many COBOLs there is a FILE-STATUS entry that can be defined under the
SELECT clause.  The FILE-STATUSs I've seen have always been 2-byte display
fields.  If there are problems with any I/O done to the file, a non-zero
return code is placed in the FILE-STATUS field.  I don't specifically know
about Acucobol, but I would check that possibility first.

Paul

Stephan Wiesner <stephan@stephanwiesner.de> wrote in message
news:9lnsbf$vr8$01$1@news.t-online.com...
> I'm using Acucobol (see, learned something and stat this first now :-)
> I want to read some data from a file. That works fine, if the file already
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to catch File Exceptions

- **From:** "Stephan Wiesner" <stephan@stephanwiesner.de>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T18:57:17+02:00

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to catch File Exceptions

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T14:25:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lp3u5$d2o$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9lnsbf$vr8$01$1@news.t-online.com> <LyNf7.22899$1p1.1776435@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9lor57$epr$05$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
1) Do you really want an "indexed" file to be a ".txt" file?  (Possibly but
unlikely)

2) Is the routine that you do in
    Perform fehler
supposed to be when the file is OK or when it is NOT there?  As you have
coded it right now, it is only performed when you do NOT open the fill
successfully.   You must make certain that you don't try ANY I/O statements
(even CLOSE) after you have received your status other than "00" or "05".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to catch File Exceptions

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T20:38:23+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bnPJZUAvWBg7IwdL@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<9lnsbf$vr8$01$1@news.t-online.com> <LyNf7.22899$1p1.1776435@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <9lor57$epr$05$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hi Stephan,

OK, default behaviour for an Acucobol-GT program is to stop when it hits 
a file error for which no declarative is declared. I'm not going to go 
into declaratives here (it would take a while to explain, and it's 
Sunday night..... You can,  however,  change this default behaviour by 
using the configuration file. Add a line to the file (or create a new 
file) which reads:

ERRORS-OK       1

You can now use this file by using the '-c' runtime flag:

wrun32 -c myconfig myprogram

would run "myprogram" using the configuration file "myconfig". Now your 
program will carry on when it sees a file error, and you can check your 
file status. Note there are other ways to select a config file, this is 
the simplest.

I see that James Gavan has pointed out that you can't open a file
I-O if it doesn't exist. Again you can override this behaviour by adding 
the line:

IO-CREATES 1

to your config file.

Hope this helps!

Cheers

Nigel


In article <9lor57$epr$05$1@news.t-online.com>, Stephan Wiesner 
<stephan@stephanwiesner.de> writes

#### ↳ Re: How to catch File Exceptions

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T18:57:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B800CC8.2E26C309@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lnsbf$vr8$01$1@news.t-online.com>`

```


Stephan Wiesner wrote:

> I'm using Acucobol (see, learned something and stat this first now :-)
> I want to read some data from a file. That works fine, if the file already
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Stephan

Both Fujitsu and Micro Focus have as extensions CBL_FILE routines, one of
which is 'CALL CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXISTS using filename returning result'. Does
AcuCobol have anything equivalent ?

Failing that all you can use, (and it isn't totally satisfactory), is the
COBOL file-status.
Open a file and if the file-status <> "00" - then you know you have a problem.
Either it doesn't exist or it could be a crashed index for an ISAM. Does
AcuCobol have any extensions to the standard file-status code list that will
report 'non existence' ?

Just looked at your reply to Paul :-

Here is how I try to open the (not yet existing) file:

       oeffnenIN.
           Open I-O dateiName
           If NOT dateiFehlercode
              Perform fehler
           End-If

It wont work if it doesn't exist (you can't use OPEN I-O on a non-existent
file) - try this substituting what's necessary in your perform fehler :-

       oeffnenIN.
           Open I-O dateiName
           If NOT dateiFehlercode
                open OUTPUT DateiName

    *> Now you can *only* WRITE (new) records

    *> If you want to be able to WRITE new records and also be able to change
    *> records, (REWRITES), which you have already processed in previous
    *> WRITES, in this run,  then do the following :-

        open OUTPUT DateiName
        close DateiName
        open I-O DateiName

now you can READ PrimeKey, REWRITE and WRITE.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How to catch File Exceptions

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T09:07:06+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B80C52A.FA4A1909@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9lnsbf$vr8$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3B800CC8.2E26C309@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Both Fujitsu and Micro Focus have as extensions CBL_FILE routines, one of
> which is 'CALL CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXISTS using filename returning result'. Does
> AcuCobol have anything equivalent ?

This shouldn't be necessary for a data file.  Open INPUT and check
status to see if it exists and is usable.  If no exist then open output
else open i-o.  However, using select OPTIONAL _should_ avoid the issue
entirely.
 
> Failing that all you can use, (and it isn't totally satisfactory), is the
> COBOL file-status.
> Open a file and if the file-status <> "00" - then you know you have a problem.

You should only check the first byte of the file-status for a successful
result.  The 2nd byte may not be zero when the operation is successful. 
For example: opening i-o a non-existant optional file may return '05' to
indicate that file was successfully created.

> It wont work if it doesn't exist (you can't use OPEN I-O on a non-existent
> file) - 

Yes you can (on many Cobols at least) that is what OPTIONAL is for.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to catch File Exceptions

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T04:47:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8096E3.7DDE12F9@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lnsbf$vr8$01$1@news.t-online.com> <3B800CC8.2E26C309@home.com> <3B80C52A.FA4A1909@Azonic.co.nz>`

```


Richard Plinston wrote:

> James J. Gavan wrote:
> >
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yes you can (on many Cobols at least) that is what OPTIONAL is for.

Mea culpa and thanks for the update. I confess my thinking (and usage) was based on
RM/COBOL, ANSI '74. Just carried that thinking over to M/F ANSI '85 and didn't look
at the OPTIONAL syntax in depth.

Jimmy
```

Stephan Wiesner wrote:
> >
> > > It wont work if it doesn't exist (you can't use OPEN I-O on a
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> doesn't =>>    Open OPTIONAL dateiName

Try using the word OPTIONAL in the correct place:

          select OPTIONAL dateiName
              assign ...


           OPEN I-O dateiName

Of course it doesn't work if put in at some random place.
```

#### ↳ Re: looking to program cobol (help me please)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T19:19:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8011F2.C24901F9@home.com>`

```


Puzzledthing wrote:

> have not touched cobol since i graduated in 1986 from programming school.
> want to very much learn more and start a career in the programming area never
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> puzzledthing@aol.com      once again  thanks   John.

Hmm. 1986 to 2001, and assuming you were about 20, that makes you currently 35 ?
Reality check - bit of a problem making a 'new career' at 35. Not saying it can't
be done, but be aware of it. What has your career been since then - any
association with DP ?

Materials - best starting point, get Thane Hubbell's 'Teach Yourself COBOL in 24
hours', comes with a free version of Fujitsu on CD. Another alternative, if you
can qualify, is the Student version of Micro Focus Net Express - about $US 100.
(Even if you go the Net Express route - still get Thane's book for syntax
concepts).

Small PC firms may/may not be prepared to take on a 'student' once you show some
savvy. Mainframes -  given larger budgets, they may be able to show some
flexibility and 'train' partially qualified students - just don't know. Whichever
route you go, it wouldn't hurt to be able to take along a 'portfolio' of a
smallish application that you have written - say a Menu, Edit file programs,
Print programs, processing programs etc. Then they know you aren't just B/S-ing !

Not critical - but either Fujitsu or Micro Focus, see if you can get a handle on
Object Orientation. As backup to this, learn Java as a complementary language to
COBOL.

Good luck !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: looking to program cobol (help me please)

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<20010819134825.04420.00001495@mb-fh.aol.com> <3B8011F2.C24901F9@home.com> <9lp9d5$3m1$1@freenet9.carleton.ca>`

```

"Luis Gomez-Almeida" <ar656@freenet.carleton.ca> wrote in message
news:9lp9d5$3m1$1@freenet9.carleton.ca...
> Hey, it can be done. I started in Information Systems when I was 30. My
wife
> went back to college to study Computer Programming when she was 42. We
live
> in canada, and we find that this is a lot easier to do in North America
> (Canada, USA) than in Europe, let alone other places of the world where
you
> are un-hirable after 35. Now, this does not mean it will be easy.
>

"Un-hirable after 35?!!" In Europe?!!

I don't think so, Luis...<G>

I have worked as a contractor for 20 years since I turned 35 and am still
doing so...<G>
(And no, I didn't do it by staying with one company...or even one or two
companies.)

You may be right as far as a "career" goes, but there is a "skills market"
out there and if you have something to sell, people will buy it.(Always
provided your attitude is right and you are capable of communicating
effectively...)

The main thing is to change the mindset from "a job for life with sickness
benefit, paid holidays and the corporate pension fund" to "sell what I know
for the maximum benefit for all concerned, take responsibility for my own
pension fund, have fun, and take holidays when I want to and not when the
Company says I can.".

That pound or so of slush inside your head is the best survival tool you
will ever have. Nurture it, stimulate it, keep it sharp, and don't sell it
for less than it is worth.

Works for me...<G>

Pete.

## Thread: Envirnoment variables and fujitsu Cobol — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Envirnoment variables and fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** "David E. Edwards" <dedwards@forje.btinternet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T19:39:49+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lp1v7$ouk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
How can I get to read the environment variabels in windows/DOS from a
Fujitsu Cobol program.

What I am truly looking for is the path that a program is executed from.  I
seem to remember (with my brief sortie into C) that it was available as the
first command line argument.  Is this the case with COBOL and if so how do I
get to read it into my program?

Thanks for any and al comments.

Dave E.
```

#### ↳ Re: Envirnoment variables and fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T23:13:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dKXf7.7325$2u.55768@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp1v7$ouk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
Using special names as described in Chapter 11 of the users guide (V6.1 - it
might be a different chapter in earlier releases -- look for Accept/Display) you
can get the value of different environment varibales.  The execution arguments
won't return the directory it is executed from.  The Current directory can be
obtained using a CBL_ call.

In article <9lp1v7$ouk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>, David E. Edwards says...
>
>How can I get to read the environment variabels in windows/DOS from a
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Envirnoment variables and fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T01:42:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VVZf7.56285$VV1.3772941@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp1v7$ouk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
Easy:

 MOVE 'COMMANDLINE' OF POW-SELF TO COM-LINE.

You can only use this in an EXE, though.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Envirnoment variables and fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** "David E. Edwards" <dedwards@forje.btinternet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T11:07:04+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lqoa0$ikm$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

> Easy:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Tried this.  The syntax I finally found was:
"MOVE POW-COMMANDLINE OF SHEET1 TO COM-LINE"
However this returned a null value.  The only documentation I found said
that I had to define this in the run-time variables box.  This does not seem
to be exactly what I needed.

Thanks
Dave E.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Envirnoment variables and fujitsu Cobol

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T13:05:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CW7g7.59895$VV1.4052358@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp1v7$ouk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <VVZf7.56285$VV1.3772941@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9lqoa0$ikm$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
Yeah, I see you're using V3.

OK, Plan #2

MOVE 255 TO WS-LEN.       [WS-LEN S9(9) COMP-5]
MOVE SPACE TO THE-PATH-NAME.    [THE-PATH-NAME  X(256) VALUE SPACE]
CALL 'GetCurrentDirectoryA' WITH STDCALL USING
        BY VALUE WS-LEN
         BY REFERENCE THE-PATH-NAME

And the very first line in your source must be:

@OPTIONS ALPHAL(WORD)


"David E. Edwards" <dedwards@forje.btinternet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:9lqoa0$ikm$1@plutonium.btinternet.com...

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

said
> that I had to define this in the run-time variables box.  This does
not seem
> to be exactly what I needed.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Envirnoment variables and fujitsu Cobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T07:49:16+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B82046C.3085B2D6@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<9lp1v7$ouk$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <VVZf7.56285$VV1.3772941@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <9lqoa0$ikm$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <CW7g7.59895$VV1.4052358@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Daivid E. Edwards wrote:
> How can I get to read the environment variabels in windows/DOS from a
> Fujitsu Cobol program.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> get to read it into my program?

Jerry P wrote:
> 
> MOVE 255 TO WS-LEN.       [WS-LEN S9(9) COMP-5]
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>          BY REFERENCE THE-PATH-NAME

The 'current' directory is not the same as the one the program was
executed from.  The 'command line' is also a red-herring.  In C the full
path of the executed program is often in argv[0] and the remaining
argv[]'s contain the command parameters.

Jerry P wrote:
> Easy:
> 
>  MOVE 'COMMANDLINE' OF POW-SELF TO COM-LINE.

That, presumably is _Power_Cobol only, given away by the 'POW-SELF'. 
This is unlikely to be of use in a 'Fujitsu Cobol' program.  I would
have expected PowerCobol to be specified it was being used.

Fujitsu version 3 (reading the manual here) does allow SPECIAL-NAMES of
ARGUMENT-NUMBER and ARGUMENT-VALUE.  These can be used in
ACCEPT/DISPLAY, though I don't know what an argument-number of zero will
return.

       WORKING-STORAGE.

       01  Run-Parameter            PIC X(80).
       01  argc                     PIC 9(4).

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

           ACCEPT argc           FROM ARGUMENT-NUMBER
           DISPLAY ZERO          UPON ARGUMENT-NUMBER
           ACCEPT Run-Parameter  FROM ARGUMENT-VALUE

You will need to play with this code to see what, in fact, it does
return.  It may be that the first is number 1 and this is not the
executable.

There is also ENVIRONMENT-NAME and ENVIRONMENT-VALUE, I think that Win95
or so puts the command line used into COMMAND= or similar and this may
be recoverable from there.
```

<a id="t-f943ec26f9"></a>
## Thread: IBM mainframe use of OO COBOL — 69 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM mainframe use of OO COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T15:39:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-on to earlier threads, I thought that I would add this
information concerning the use of OO COBOL in IBM mainframe shops (OS/390
and/or z/OS).

1) My internal sources indicate that IBM *does* know of some (more than
one - less than "lots") of shops using IBM COBOL's OO features.  None of
these shops, however, have gone thru the procedures to be an "official"
reference shop, so which they are is a "secret".

2) Enough shops are using (or considering using) this that IBM was "forced"
to keep the parts of SOMObjects needed for OO COBOL in LE (Language
Environment) when they removed it from being a "full feature" of OS/390 and
z/OS.

3) The SYNTAX on the Workstation (Windows and AIX) for OO COBOL from IBM is
totally compatible with that on OS/390 (and is INCOMPATIBLE with the current
draft of the next ANSI/ISO Standard).  Therefore, it is "highly likely" that
IBM will be needing to provide SOME migration path for existing IBM OO COBOL
source code.  (Whether this is a "source converter" or a "which OO dialect
switch" - is certainly unclear to ME now - but those internal to IBM are
*probably* thinking ahead to this already).

  ***

The issue of OO COBOL on IBM mainframes is related to the "perception"
(incorrect - but fairly common) that OO and GUI's are "inter-related".  Most
"enterprise" size shops that are looking at OO (any language) first (and for
some ONLY) look at it for "user interface code".  As "user interfaces" in
IBM mainframe environments are USUALLY handled by CICS, IMS, ISPF, etc -
this is a "non-issue" for their application development (and maintenance).
If they are moving to a "workstation front-end" for their applications, then
they do look at a GUI *and* OO programming environment.  HOWEVER, as such
programs (by their nature) are done from "scratch" - it is likely that shops
will look at the OO programming languages with the most "press" rather than
where they already have in-house expertise.  (Furthermore, as these UI
portions of applications PRIMARILY run on the workstation, it doesn't
involve any OO on the mainframe.)

Bottom-Line:
  Until it is commonly "perceived" that OO is beneficial to the
NON-user-interface business logic, I don't see OO COBOL catching on (very
much) in IBM mainframe environments. When/If there are *standard*
(off-the-shelf) OO Class Libraries for *BUSINESS* rules, then I think that
they will be "accepted" and brought into use in IBM mainframes - to a much
greater extent than today.
```

#### ↳ Use of OO COBOL

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T09:08:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,
I'm glad you brought this up.  We did an application recently that was based
upon VisualAge COBOL and DB2.  It troubled me that VA COBOL was generating OO
COBOL for the window interface, when there is no adopted COBOL standard that
includes Object Orientation.

Since we were using some IBM assistance to help us over the learning curve, I
asked whether the release of VA COBOL that we were using could be told to
generate "classic" COBOL.  I never got the answer.

I must admit to not having succeeded in using, or becoming used to, OO COBOL.
However, I have tried to play with the code that I've seen in articles, etc.
Often, the code doesn't even compile, because I'm using a different compiler
than the author.  To me, THIS ISN'T COBOL!  The reason COBOL is the most widely
used computer language is precisely because of the standard, that describes not
only the language syntax, but the results of using that syntax.  COBOL is
supposed to accept the same source code on all platforms, and the source is
supposed to perform the same everywhere.

I find the "early adoption" of OO COBOL to be an unfortunate by product of the
slow adoption of new COBOL standards.  People naturally want to try out "what's
now".  However, those who are using the OO COBOL than any vendor offers right
now are taking a BIG risk.

What do others think?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T05:15:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net>`

```

"Colin Campbell" <cmcampb@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net...
> I find the "early adoption" of OO COBOL to be an unfortunate by product of
the
> slow adoption of new COBOL standards.

OO COBOL is part of the new Standards. The lack of this standard has led to
some vendors releasing their own versions of OO. By and large, they are
excellent implementations and we are fortunate that the vendors are more
interested in progressing COBOL than the Standards Authority appears to be.

I don't personally believe that the implementations are so far apart that it
is irreconcilable. The differences are slight. There have ALWAYS been
differences between various vendors' implementations of COBOL.

>People naturally want to try out "what's
> now".  However, those who are using the OO COBOL than any vendor offers
right
> now are taking a BIG risk.
>
The "risk" in getting to grips with OO COBOL right now, is nowhere near as
high as the "risk" in NOT doing so...

Whether we like it or not, the future of computer programming lies in Object
Orientation. If COBOL misses this boat it will be dead in the water within
10 years.

The rise of OO languages like Java and C++ is not just down to fashion. It
is also because the Object Orientation makes them efficient and useful.

OO is the springboard that will give us components and that is where the
future is. (I have written at length elsewhere on this. If you want to see
the arguments go to http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com, take the "Archive"
link, and check out the main article ("Meet the Future") in the April issue
this year. In particular note the last paragraph of this article, where I
offer advice to COBOL programmers as to what to do next.)

You said in your post that you had trouble with understanding OO.

So did I.

OO COBOL is verbose and difficult when you approach it from a COBOL
background. The concepts are obscure and buried in new jargon. However,
there are shortcuts you can take and they are covered in the article
referenced above. Once you have the concepts, it falls into place pretty
quickly.

The game is coming down to using the best tools for the job. One single tool
won't hack it anymore. COBOL is still the best tool for certain jobs and is
likely to be for the foreseeable future.

But it is isolated by its archaic file system, and its lack of "object
awareness" which prevents it playing nicely with other languages.

OO addresses this and puts COBOL on the same footing as the other languages.
Components developed in OO COBOL behave just like components developed in
any other OO Language. The Source Language is irrelevant and should be the
one best suited to the purpose.

If you want to have a future using COBOL for anything other than Batch
Processing, learn OO and encourage others to do so too.

The future belongs to components, and you need OO COBOL to build them.

The "early implementations" which you perceive as "risky", are giving people
a chance to get with the program and improve their skill sets before it is
too late.

Apart from that, there are huge long term benefits in adopting OO, even if
you never write a single component with it.

>>>
> What do others think?
>
<<<
(Bet you're sorry you asked...<G>)

Pete.

> Whether we like it or not, the future of computer programming lies in
> Object
> Orientation. If COBOL misses this boat it will be dead in the water within

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T14:07:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lrn7l$s4p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Well, being a "devil's advocate" - and knowing that I will upset Jimmy and
Pete (at least),

Several people said this same thing about 1994 when there was still hope
that the new COBOL Standard would be out by '97, i.e. that "COBOL would be
dead in 10 years - if there wasn't a STANDARD for OO COBOL by then.  Well we
are now MUCH closer to 2004 than we are to 1994 - and we still don't have a
STANDARD definition of OO COBOL.  (Well, actually, I think that the FCD is
pretty much set in concrete now - but there are ZERO implementations of its
complete OO definition).  Some how, COBOL is still not dead - and the place
where it is STILL most used (mainframes) is NOT clamoring for OO support.

It is my best guess (given the VERY slow adoption of '85 Standard coding
techniques in IBM mainframe COBOL shops - and possibly OTHER mainframe
environments), that COBOL will continue to be used (both for maintenance and
development) in LARGE enterprise (mainframe) environments for years and
years to come.  Furthermore, that MOST of the programs that will be
DEVELOPED (much less maintained) in these environments will be such that
they could ALMOST be compiled by an ANSI '74 compiler (at least a compiler
with a FEW extensions).

Now when it comes to moving out of "entrenched" environments, I totally
agree that not having OO support will harm the "growth" of COBOL.  However,
it is my perception (and of course I could be wrong <G>) that if COBOL did
everything but write itself and pay each programmer $1,000 per line of
code - it still has little or no chance of GAINING "market share" in the
programming environments in which it is not fully entrenched today.  The
"reputation" is just too bad - and the language itself is just too "out of
fashion" for it to gain in totally new environments - regardless of what
features are added or modified.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T19:42:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8168CE.D900003@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9lrn7l$s4p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Well, being a "devil's advocate" - and knowing that I will upset Jimmy and
> Pete (at least),
>

Why not be the devil's advocate - I'm sure they call you all sorts of other
names at J4 <G>. Nope. You haven't upset me - your observations are accurate.
Does OO work - how can you really tell if you haven't access to a compiler.

You can spell out non-OO COBOL very clearly, (just so long as you don't
reference the FCD <G>). That's exactly what Thane has done in his book.

OO - different ball game. Given a mainframer had an OO compiler, might he not
just find it a little intriguing that you can write just ONE program that
accesses any ISAM file, (and that would negate all the sub-programming, nesting
or calling that was in the thread that started this topic).. Similarly, but this
is not parallel to Pete's components, your can write ONE program accessible by
all Dialogs. It's impossible to illustrate either of these concepts in a few
sentences.

All we can hope is that the mainframe vendors on J4 take the topic seriously and
DO produce mainframe OO compilers. Then people can experiment - OO in COBOL will
either fall flat on its face or take off.

I also agree with you on 'market share'. While still healthy in the States -
COBOL tuition elsewhere is diminishing. Those kids we lost to C, C++, Java,
Visual Basic or whatever - we are NEVER going to get them back.

Go chuck another bit of coal on Satan's fire <G>

Jimmy

>
> Several people said this same thing about 1994 when there was still hope
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> > you think?
```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B8168CE.D900003@home.com...
> OO - different ball game. Given a mainframer had an OO compiler, might he
not
> just find it a little intriguing that you can write just ONE program that

nesting
> or calling that was in the thread that started this topic).. Similarly,
but this

accessible by
> all Dialogs. It's impossible to illustrate either of these concepts in a
few
> sentences.

This is the nub of it, Jimmy. "It is impossible to illustrate... in a few
sentences".

There is no glib way to show OO Concepts or actual programming, and
adequately demonstrate the power of this paradigm.

Java gets close, and I still see examples of OO technique in Java which
really blow me away, expressed in 30 lines of code... Try translating it to
OO COBOL and yes, it works and is good, but it is hundreds of lines and not
immediately obvious.

I found a really good Java introductory with simple elegant examples and
decided to translate the whole lot to OO COBOL as a primer for students. I
gave up. It just wasn't the same. (I might try again after I rethink the
whole process.. I think the problem here was not with OO COBOL but with
me...)

Having said that, I think the OO COBOL examples in Will's book are
outstanding and do give some insight into how it works.

But, as in your statement above, you do something really cool with OO COBOL
and realise what power it has. Then you try to explain it to someone with a
straight COBOL background. And you can't. Well, not in a simple succinct
"few sentences".

There is apparently no substitute for "biting the bullet" and learning the
concepts.


> All we can hope is that the mainframe vendors on J4 take the topic
seriously and
> DO produce mainframe OO compilers. Then people can experiment - OO in
COBOL will
> either fall flat on its face or take off.
>
I hope vendors, whether they are on J4 or not, will produce OO
implementations.


> I also agree with you on 'market share'. While still healthy in the
States -
> COBOL tuition elsewhere is diminishing. Those kids we lost to C, C++,
Java,
> Visual Basic or whatever - we are NEVER going to get them back.
>
Be careful about "Never"...I have recently had some enquiries from VB people
who want to learn COBOL <G>.

Pete.

Ah, there you go with the Religion again, Jimmy...<G>

They are not "converts" to COBOL. They are experienced professional
programmers who want to use the best tools for the job (funny, is there an
echo in here...? sure I've heard that somewhere before...<G>)

Hence, expand their skill sets by learning COBOL. They won't DROP VB once
they learn OO COBOL. But the knowledge gained will make it much easier to
interface the two products...

I recommended Thane's book and told them to contact me if they have any
problems.

I believe someone pointed them to my article on components (they all use VB
to build ActiveX controls) and they were surprised that COBOL can be used to
do this.

Pete.

I recently subscribed to Dr Dobbs Journal in hopes of taking the examples they
publish in different languages and adding a COBOL version.  We'll see if I can
find the time.  There are some things that work one way in Java that don't port
well to COBOL (OO) generally.  These have to do with Java's robust
multi-threading support.  Multi-threaded OO COBOL exists (Fujitsu at least) but
I've not messed with it much.  Might this aspect help you in your port?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T16:06:29+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b832ef9_2@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9lrn7l$s4p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3B8168CE.D900003@home.com> <3b81cd71_2@goliath.newsgroups.com> <mTxg7.9431$2u.68709@www.newsranger.com>`

```
Thanks Thane.

The problem is not in the multithreading.

I agree that the Java facilities for multithreading are excellent and the
provision of standard methods and objects like monitor blocks make complex
operations like synchronizing threads a doddle.

I have achieved the same in mainframe COBOL with IMS/DC by using mailboxes
and posting/clearing them for interthread communication.

As I'm not using V6 I haven't tried implementing multi-threaded applications
in OO COBOL, but I would expect the normal synchronization,
re-entrancy/reusability of multithreaded code, and cleanup of terminated
threads to apply. I see COBOL CGI code as being a good candidate for this.
(But then, a Java Servlet has the facilities already built into it...)

Does multithreaded OO COBOL in V6 provide equivalent methods to the Java
"start" "sleep" "yield" "notify" and "join" methods, or is it necessary to
achieve this by subclassing?

Sorry, I've wandered off the topic...<G>

The problem in my port is simply that the "wrapping" required with OO COBOL
renders it larger than with Java. This in turn obscures the elegance of what
it is doing... and that brings us back to Jimmy's original comment regarding
the difficulty of explaining what is happening in a few succinct sentences.

I'll be looking at it again soon.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T19:48:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lrpfu$l3m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9lrn7l$s4p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


On 20-Aug-2001, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> Now when it comes to moving out of "entrenched" environments, I totally
> agree that not having OO support will harm the "growth" of COBOL.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> features are added or modified.

That is my perception too.  There will continue to be some CoBOL programmers
who move to OO on the desktop, but I don't see Java nor C programmers
switching over to OO CoBOL.    Neither of us see new standards actually
changing the share of the market significantly.  The thing that makes CoBOL
out of fashion with techies is the same thing that makes it in fashion with
businesses - it is English like and wordy.

The one open issue is whether there will be any significant amount of OO
CoBOL on mainframe computers in an enterprise environment, replacing
standard business applications.   I haven't seen any evidence that this will
happen.

A niche market that might grow is mainframe web servers using OO CoBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T14:00:28+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b81c0a4_1@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9lrn7l$s4p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9lrn7l$s4p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net...
> Well, being a "devil's advocate" - and knowing that I will upset Jimmy and
> Pete (at least),
>
I am never upset by lively debate or opposing viewpoints. Besides, I really
don't care. As far as I'm concerned the evidence is in and I have stated my
position.

It doesn't matter to me whether people believe me or not.

Can you afford to take the risk that I may be right? I am strongly advising
COBOL programmers to do something about upgrading their skill sets. Even if
my assessment is wrong, (and in some ways I hope it is...) no harm can be
done.

> Several people said this same thing about 1994 when there was still hope
> that the new COBOL Standard would be out by '97, i.e. that "COBOL would be
> dead in 10 years - if there wasn't a STANDARD for OO COBOL by then.

That is NOT what I am saying, and is NOT what I have ever said. It is a
cheap shot to equate what I DID say with the above standard. Seems all you
saw in my post were the words "10 years", Bill.

I do not now, and have never believed that the "life" of COBOL is  totally
dependent on the Standard.

I DO believe that the apparent impossibility to produce a Standard that is
meaningful to anybody, has certainly NOT helped the image or implementation
of COBOL, but this has been overtaken by events and is now irrelevant.

Object Orientation is a completely different matter.

COBOL (in my assessment) CANNOT live in splendid isolation from the rest of
the computing environment. The days when it was "the only game in town" are
over. Modern environments require a multitude of tools and COBOL is just one
of them.  UNLESS it can play with the others on their own ground, it WILL
fade into obscurity. OO COBOL is the only way I can see to achieve this.


> Well we
> are now MUCH closer to 2004 than we are to 1994 - and we still don't have
a
> STANDARD definition of OO COBOL.  (Well, actually, I think that the FCD is
> pretty much set in concrete now - but there are ZERO implementations of
its
> complete OO definition).  Some how, COBOL is still not dead - and the
place
> where it is STILL most used (mainframes) is NOT clamoring for OO support.
>
That's no cause for smugness, Bill. It should be cause for alarm. Yes, the
mainframe sites remain as bastions of "fortress COBOL", resistant to change
and determined to continue as they always have. However, with the technology
which is set to emerge over the next few years they will HAVE to integrate
the mainframe with the rest of the Enterprise, or relegate it to a large
Batch processor.

More and more forward thinking organisations are seeing their mainframes as
fast, reliable, Web Servers. Standard COBOL does not lend itself easily to
this. OO COBOL and component engineering does.

> It is my best guess (given the VERY slow adoption of '85 Standard coding
> techniques in IBM mainframe COBOL shops - and possibly OTHER mainframe
> environments),
(Has it occurred to you that this "slow adoption" could be because training
in COBOL '85 may be perceived as pouring good money after bad...? Where
training budgets are under pressure, people will want the best bang they can
get for their buck. Training keen young people into a 40 year old technology
MAY not be attractive, especially when the demands of the Enterprise are for
Network integration and Web enablement.)

> that COBOL will continue to be used (both for maintenance and
> development) in LARGE enterprise (mainframe) environments for years and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I don't disagree with this. Some sites will keep doing what they have always
done for as long as they can get away with it ("years and years"). However,
market pressures will finally drive the businesses they "serve" to work
"smarter" and the last bastions will gradually crumble. Long before that,
anybody who wants to make computer programming a career will have acquired
the necessary skills and languages to do so. Knowing '85 COBOL will not
carry a heavy price tag in the skills market.

> Now when it comes to moving out of "entrenched" environments, I totally
> agree that not having OO support will harm the "growth" of COBOL.
However,
> it is my perception (and of course I could be wrong <G>) that if COBOL did
> everything but write itself and pay each programmer $1,000 per line of
> code - it still has little or no chance of GAINING "market share" in the
> programming environments in which it is not fully entrenched today.

So we agree OO is necessary for COBOL to progress...<G> And your last
sentence is certainly true if we DON'T do anything to enhance the language
and the skills of the people using it.

> The
> "reputation" is just too bad - and the language itself is just too "out of
> fashion" for it to gain in totally new environments - regardless of what
> features are added or modified.
>
Nope. That is entirely a matter of opinion. What exactly are we talking
about when we say "fashion" in this particular context?

Languages like Java and C++ are not just "fashionable" because that's what
University Lecturers want to teach. They are low cost, efficient, EFFECTIVE
languages when used for the purposes for which they are intended.

So is COBOL (well...maybe not low cost <G>). The OO Extension to COBOL
allows it to provide the same programming advantages the other languages
provide, but in the area where it reigns supreme (data manipulation and
commercial problem solving). But, instead of racing to embrace it, the
attitude is:"Well, OO COBOL is pretty hard to take and I've always done OK
with standard COBOL...why bother?".

I have tried in my posts to this forum to get people to "wake up" and expand
their skill sets before it is too late. Posts like yours, Bill, simply
reinforce the "Hey, it's OK, we're here for years..." attitude which most
existing COBOL programmers WANT to believe. (This is not a criticism; I know
you honestly believe what you wrote, just as I do.)

Like I said at the start, I don't personally care whether they respond or
not. As long as I rang the bell and did my best...

But I can tell you if I was a career COBOL programmer right now, the thought
of spending the next "years and years" writing COBOL '85 on a mainframe,
wading through REXX, JCL, TSO, CICS  and all the ancillary stuff that is
required  before I can even BEGIN to exercise my COBOL skill, being paid the
minimum possible because I haven't got the new technology skills, and
sitting at the bottom of the heap as far as programmers go because I write
COBOL and don't understand Object Orientation and the reuse of encapsulated
common Objects... Watching the "other" programmers dealing with the new
technology, going on the courses, getting all the training, producing
exciting new services and facilities to the User base...

Well, I'd rather drive a truck...

Pete.
