###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T23:25:46+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-4FE8D9.23254621082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <9lrn7l$s4p$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <3b81c0a4_1@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
In article <3b81c0a4_1@goliath.newsgroups.com>,
 "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> But I can tell you if I was a career COBOL programmer right now, the thought
> of spending the next "years and years" writing COBOL '85 on a mainframe,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
First Off: Rexx, JCL, TSO and CICS do the job that they are designed for 
very well. A proper development environment on the mainframe in no way 
differs from the PC. You still have to logon to both, after that you use 
the appropriate tool to write your code and compile it. If the tool is 
good, you even get the compiler messages interleaved with the code that 
is error in your editor. All this is possible on the mainframe. You 
don't even have to know JCL, Rexx or possibly even TSO.

Second: If you think that in the future you will be able to escape OO in 
the mainframe environment then you are wrong. The Current CICS release 
allows transactions written in Java as Enterprise Java Beans. It also 
supports C++. All the CICS api's are available as C++ and Java Classes.

Once the OO COBOL standard is close enough for implementation, I suspect 
that IBM Mainframe COBOL will have it. Then the Classes for CICS will be 
expanded for OO COBOL as well. 

Whilst the pieces are there now, it is difficult to completely program 
an OO server application. In the future this will not be the case. It 
will be simplicity itself to go from the Mainframe Web Server to a CICS 
transaction and back again with all the DATA integrity currently enjoyed 
by CICS only programs now.

Once these goals have been achieved, then OO will take off on the 
Mainframe. Then we can all look forward to Extreme Programming to also 
take off in the mainframe environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T04:36:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b831b97_7@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3b81c7c3_5@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9ltn1k$7m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:9ltn1k$7m9
>
> I'm not real familiar with the current market for non-mainframe CoBOL.
Will
> making it OO gain marketshare?   What languages is it competing with for
> what applications?

The picture that is emerging is that (off the mainframe) languages are NOT
"competing for market share".

What is happening is that strengths of certain languages in certain areas
are being recognised and a philosophy of "the best tools for the job" is
slowly becoming established. (I say slowly because there are, inevitably,
the sites where they will try and do EVERYTHING in Java or C++ or VB,
whether this makes sense or not, just as there are sites where they try and
do EVERYTHING in COBOL...(I call them "One Language Wonders") ).

All of the Languages currently being used are Object Oriented. The new ones
emerging are also based on this paradigm.

Making COBOL Object Oriented won't gain it "Market share", it will simply
enable it to take its place alongside the other languages as a speaker of
the "Lingua Franca" of Client/Server development, which is OO. The market
for COBOL already exists and there are many tasks for which COBOL IS "the
best tool for the job". However, in the absence of an OO Version, it is
tempting for people to use a less appropriate tool because they have to do
SOMETHING...

I already have Client Server applications where VB, C++ and OO COBOL all
co-exist peacefully on the same GUI form. Each component written in the
language best suited to it, and the whole lot completely transparent to the
User. (This stuff has been running in a live environment for over 6 months
now without a single failure by any component).

This violates totally the old COBOL dictum of "One Language for ease of
maintenance". The difference is that these components don't NEED to be
maintained, any more than you would maintain an individual brick in your
house. (You might replace it; you wouldn't try and maintain it).

I am currently working to bring Java and HTML into this same scenario so
that my existing applications can be Web Enabled easily and quickly. Fujitsu
Version 6 (Enterprise Edition) supports COBOL calling Java, but it is via a
limited interface and you cannot call COBOL from Java. As I am not using V6
for reasons which I won't go into here (but you could try reading some of
the posts on this subject in CLC), AND I don't have the Enterprise Edition,
AND I want to be able to call both ways, I am building my own Classes to
enable calling Java  (and, hopefully, vice versa) from Fujitsu Version 5,
Professional Edition. This may be achieved by using JNI or CORBA, I'm not
sure yet as I am still looking at it. It will CERTAINLY require OO COBOL or
PowerCOBOL (which is innately OO).

The point is that there is no point in using a hammer for every kind of job.
Sometimes a chisel is more appropriate.

All of the arguments involved in maintaining Source code or building a
Component Repository are covered in the previously referenced article. I
cannot go into them here. It is a significant attitude adjustment,
especially after a lifetime of working on COBOL Source, to find that within
the foreseeable future this will very probably be replaced because it is
simply not cost justifiable to do it any more, and it is becoming
unnecessary. Off the shelf components, developed in Languages which are
ideally suited for the purpose for which the component is intended, and
selling for the cost of a few programmer hours, will make the maintenance of
large IT development teams unjustifiable.

The keyword here is "reuse". And that is provided automatically by OO.

IT development will be "outsourced" just like the company car pool
maintenance. An Insurance Company (for example) doesn't have a workshop for
the Company car fleet (that is not the kind of Business they are in), why
should they have a workshop for their IT systems? Both facilities (cars and
IT) are an essential part of the Business, but it is (or will be soon)
cheaper to outsource both of them than to set it up in house.

Pete.
(PS there are currently over 100,000 components available, excluding Java
Beans, and the number is rising steadily. A market is developing for
extracting Legacy code into reusable Business Components which can then be
sold, as well as used in house... If this trend continues, even "Fortress
COBOL" may well succumb to it...)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T11:52:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i86h7.286$We3.19671@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3b831b97_7@goliath.newsgroups.com> <20010822234545.18387.00000579@mb-fl.aol.com>`

```
FRLSFLY <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010822234545.18387.00000579@mb-fl.aol.com...
>
> >IT development will be "outsourced" just like the company car pool
> >maintenance. An Insurance Company (for example) doesn't have a workshop
for
> >the Company car fleet (that is not the kind of Business they are in), why
> >should they have a workshop for their IT systems? Both facilities (cars
and
> >IT) are an essential part of the Business, but it is (or will be soon)
> >cheaper to outsource both of them than to set it up in house.
>
> Bad analogy.  For most companies computers are becoming a critical part of
> their product delivery.  Outsourcing their IT would be like UPS
outsourcing
> package delivery.

You should read up on the Microsoft .NET initiative. .NET's existence is
premised on software becoming a service, accessed via some kind of
connection (Internet being the hands-down popular favorite) rather than
something installed on equipment located at the user site.

While not exactly "outsourced" as we use that term today, it's not far off.

While I am not sold on Microsoft having the concept right  - IMO, they are
probably five-ten years early, and the diverse needs of
commercial/industrial users almost automatically preclude use for other than
mass-market (consumer) products and services - I cannot dismiss any
organization with that kind of money, the willingness to spend it, the
desire "make it happen," and the ego to persist.

So far, Microsoft has invested VERY heavily in .NET, providing training,
seminars, development tools, etc for developers at no charge to those
developers. I've just started seeing some advertising for  "for a fee"
training on .NET, but I can't rightly recall if that was Microsoft-sponsored
or VAR-sponsored.


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T00:51:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b84fb59_1@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3b831b97_7@goliath.newsgroups.com> <20010822234545.18387.00000579@mb-fl.aol.com>`

```
I believe that Market forces will drive Enterprises to re-assess the cost of
IT.

When they do, it will become more and more apparent that outsourcing and
component use are the two major routes to reducing IT cost.

(And I'm NOT the only one who thinks so...<G>

"We estimate that by 2003, 70 percent of new applications will be deployed
as a combination of pre-assembled and newly created components integrated to
form complex business systems."

- Gartner )

It would be a good thing for programmers to prepare for Component use by
learning OO now. This way the choice of outsourcing can be shown to be
unnecessary, because the in-house facility has its act together and is
capable of producing and maintaining cost effective systems. Maybe even
selling some components derived from existing code...

If you still doubt what I'm saying, run a Google search on the words
"Software Components"... the same search two years ago returned around 4,000
hits...

Or go and look at http://www.flashline.com and see what major industries and
Enterprises are doing and saying about Component Technology

The key to this technology is Object Orientation.

Of course, you could also just sit back smugly and say "Hey, I've seen all
this before...it won't change anything..."

It's your career.

Quick comments on your post below...

Pete.

"FRLSFLY" <frlsfly@aol.com> wrote in message
news:20010822234545.18387.00000579@mb-fl.aol.com...
>
> You're too late. Large IT development teams were eliminated by CASE tools.
Oh
> wait, no they were eliminated by "Black Box Component re-use".  Or were
they
> eliminated by "Fourth Generation Languages"? I forget...
>

I agree that none of the above replaced "programming as we know it". And,
yes, there have been many promises which were not kept.

Object Orientation is different for reasons I have examined in depth
elsewhere, and cannot re-iterate here.

Believe whatever you like, but if your career is going to be in computer
programming, OO will be part of it.

(No, I am NOT a salesman and I have no vested interest in this other than
alerting COBOL programmers who may not have picked it up yet, that the World
is changing...)

> >The keyword here is "reuse". And that is >provided automatically by OO.
>
> Are you in Sales?  Ain't nothing automatic about it.
>
Properly applied OO guarantees re-usability through encapsulation. Taking
the extra step to wrap your Classes as Components makes this guarantee even
more watertight.

>And from what I've seen
> it would be faster to code a couple of COBOL paragraphs than to call a
> ub-classed component.  Executes faster too.
>

Then you either didn't look very carefully or you haven't seen much...

> >IT development will be "outsourced" just like the company car pool
> >maintenance. An Insurance Company (for example) doesn't have a workshop
for
> >the Company car fleet (that is not the kind of Business they are in), why
> >should they have a workshop for their IT systems? Both facilities (cars
and
> >IT) are an essential part of the Business, but it is (or will be soon)
> >cheaper to outsource both of them than to set it up in house.
>
> Bad analogy.  For most companies computers are becoming a critical part of
> their product delivery.
Yes, this is true of the computers themselves and the systems, but many
companies are outsourcing both software and hardware. How many Companies now
using SAP for their product delivery, once had a large IT department writing
tailored code? Why did they buy SAP? or Siebel? or any of a host of other
package solutions? Why is this trend continuing? Wake up...smell the coffee.
Their IT department failed to deliver what was wanted in a timely and cost
effective manner. As costs go up, alternatives (even poor ones like those
mentioned) become more attractive.

>Outsourcing their IT would be like UPS outsourcing
> package delivery.
Oddly enough that is exactly what UPS do... all around the World they use
sub-contractors as well as their own staff. But that is irrelevant to the IT
point and therefore a poor counter analogy...<G>

> >(PS there are currently over 100,000 components available, excluding Java
> >Beans, and the number is rising steadily. A market is developing for
> >extracting Legacy code into reusable Business Components which can then
be
> >sold, as well as used in house..
>
> And some of them actually work. Sort of.
>
I haven't tried all 100,000 so I can't speak with the same lack of
confidence you exhibit. <G>

ALL of the ones I have tried (there are some 20 odd) have worked exactly as
specified and I have NEVER had a problem with any of them. I can invoke
their Methods and access their properties and events exactly as I can with
Components I build myself, and they all integrate together seamlessly,
through the common COM or CORBA interface, despite the current requirement
for different RTS.

> > If this trend continues, even "Fortress
> >COBOL" may well succumb to it...)
>
> Oh it doesn't matter, all the COBOL code converted over to ADA years ago.
oh no
> wait, that was PL1, wasn't it?
>
<G> No Comment.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T03:01:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B85C446.FE8B9955@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b831b97_7@goliath.newsgroups.com> <20010822234545.18387.00000579@mb-fl.aol.com> <3b84fb59_1@goliath.newsgroups.com> <3B85B9CB.E2F38A88@istar.ca>`

```


"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:

> While I agree with much of what many of you have been saying, at least
> to the extent that the logic is good, I am skeptical about Gartner's
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> find what you want and know what it does?

Valid comment about the documentation Clark. Long time since I was near a
mainframe in a systems capacity, but I know the documentation can be the pits. The
ultimate source has to be the working program.

Last message I replied to Howard you may have noted I roughly sketched what a
Class and a particular method does. I don't know how the eventual model will shake
out in a large installation, but it represents a kind of starting point.

As regards the OO I use, mostly, the description of methods per class are taken
from "*>.comment statements....", the M/F developer put at the heading of his
method-id. How informative it is depends on the 'writer'. Best if it is
scrutinized by a technical writer who speaks 'in English' and does a translation
if necessary. Coupled with this type of documentation, it could well be, that in a
large installation, new arrivals have an introductory walk through of the
installation's own standard Utility classes. As an example, they may have come up
with their own particular way for file handling. Possibly about an hour with
slides, diagrams would get the overall concept to the newcomer - subsequently he
then views the list of classes and methods before he starts writing a line of OO
code.

Same principle would apply if the installation developed, say, a set of
forecasting utility classes. They take the financial or inventory team through a
brief session about the overall concepts in the same pattern.

What is important is that (and I'm talking Net Express), is that you don't finish
up with a method as follows :-

    method-id. "toKatanga".

What the hell is that ? I only know "it takes two to tanga" <G>

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Jonathan Guthrie <jguthrie@brokersys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T20:47:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
 
> On 20-Aug-2001, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
> wrote:

>> Whether we like it or not, the future of computer programming lies in
>> Object
>> Orientation. If COBOL misses this boat it will be dead in the water within
>> 10 years.
 
> Question to all:
> Is Mr. Dashwood correct in his assessment?  Is he exaggerating?   What do
> you think?

Object Orientation hasn't proven the panacea to programming that it
was supposed to.  It can be genuinely difficult to apply objects to
real-world applications in a fashion that is maintainable and which
has decent performance.  Given that and the general poor explanation
of WHY object orientation is supposed to be better and I am 
skeptical of any efforts to "save" any computer program language by
adding object-oriented features to it.  (I once asked a job
interviewee why it was better to design programs by OO principles
and his answer was because OO is so much harder than plain
procedural programming that you had to think more about the
program.  If you need to think more about what you're doing, then
I certainly believe you should do so without having to be forced.)

I look on the object-oriented features of COBOL like adding complex
number types to ISO Modula-2 in order to lure away FORTRAN
programmers.  That didn't work because it didn't touch on the real
reasons why programmers code in FORTRAN.  OO COBOL won't save the
language (assuming it needs saving) unless it touches on the real
reasons why programmers program in COBOL.  Find what COBOL does and
try to make that better.  If that involves object-orientation, then
go for it, but the need has to come before the feature set or, if
the language IS dying, it's dead already.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T04:03:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B832FBF.B901D8C2@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197>`

```


Jonathan Guthrie wrote:

> Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> was supposed to.

Now I could accept the above statement if you actually had said "Because I know
having used this and this........". If yours is just an observation then it just
isn't valid.
But even within the 'other' OO languages, I'd take a bet you can't compare apples
to pears. They (language designers) each tried to better Simula/Smalltalk and
introduce features to fit a specific perception of need, say Gosling with Java
and Soustrup (?????) with C and C++.

>  It can be genuinely difficult to apply objects to
> real-world applications in a fashion that is maintainable and which
> has decent performance.

Again is that from hard core experience, or just hearsay. Coupled to that, which
language, which type of application. As regards performance - my application
ZINGS ! A very brief example - and this is ALL written in COBOL. I have an input
program that uses two dialogs, roughly a total of 325 Windows controls of which a
10 x 24 table of entry fields I have to build dynamically. Agreed that's a fair
number, so don't expect it to splash immediately on the screen like my other
dialogs do. Nevertheless, it is only a slight pause, and if you were thinking of
going to get a cup of coffee while it does its thing - forget it ! It's
displaying at you ! And I'm willing to bet it outperforms dedicated tools like
Visual Basic.

> Given that and the general poor explanation
> of WHY object orientation is supposed to be better and I am
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

So based on a very uninformed and naive reply - OO is the pits !

>
> I look on the object-oriented features of COBOL like adding complex
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> the language IS dying, it's dead already.

Nobody is trying to lure anybody away and it is over a decade since a trio of
dedicated COBOLers advanced the idea of OO to the Standards Committee. There are
at least four J4 members looking in on this NewsGroup. Bill has said his piece,
correctly, '....that while he accepts OO may be an extremely useful feature for
COBOL, it isn't going to get off the ground easily so far as mainframes are
concedrned.....". Don't cloud the issue - a very BIG reason is BUCK$$$$$ for a
new mainframe compiler. Let's see if we can get the other committee guys to
comment. For them - accepting that the emphasis for COBOL 2002 is non-OO COBOL,
geared to the 90% mainframe users, what are your own personal (not company
'official') views on whether or not OO COBOL is a good or bad thing.

"Find what COBOL does and make it better". That's it exactly ! We ALL know what
it does as a result of COBOL 85 and there are additions to COBOL 2002 that will
extend its capabilities. Where does OO fit in ? It ENHANCES an already solid
base. A reminder - 90% of my OO code is existing non-OO COBOL syntax !

Perhaps OO's significance in the COBOL world is REUSABILITY, (and that translates
to MAINTAINABILITY). Go figure, the fewer 'routines' you are writing as a result
of using canned, proven methods, (your own, plus third party),  in OO COBOL, must
in the long term make the whole Maintenance function easier. What is also
difficult to put into a few words, is that you gain greater flexibility in making
changes to an already proven OO application. "Don't like this method...", either
change it or write an entirely new method which contains some additional logic;
best of both worlds Method-a and Method-b can sit alongside each other.(Method-a
could be referenced by tried and true applications because it is known to work,
and that method-B is available for trying on something new. Satisfy yourself
method-B is 'the ticket' - now change your source to go huntin' for Method-b
rather than Method-a

I sincerely believe the above, and don't really give a damn whether or not you
buy into the idea. So you're in management - hope my reply is a little more
informative than that interviewee !. (Just try prying your mind open a teensy
weensy bit to the possibility, certainly within COBOL, that OO might have great
merit).

Apologies if my reply seems a little 'terse' but, like those born-agains, I kinda
seen the light - and believe in it.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T11:19:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108231019.81ca0bf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3B832FBF.B901D8C2@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote 
<snip>
>
> Nobody is trying to lure anybody away and it is over a decade since a trio of
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> comment. 
<snip>

Ok, Jimmy, this is the only place in your post where you missed the
mark.  Most IBM mainframe shops (with which I am familiar -- and that
is a LOT!) have owned (leased?) the COBOL/370 and COBOL/390 compilers
for years, altho hardly anything is yet in production using them.  
The big shops get things like this fairly promptly, if for no other
reason than that the Systems Programmers can set up the environment
and get used to it.  And when IBM drops support (it dropped support
for COBOL II in March), the clients really do not have much choice.

Cost is not a consideration for most MVS shops.  VSE may be different,
but the products (including compilers) are usually cheaper, anyway.


I think the biggest reason for slow introduction of OO will be that
the managers do not understand it.  And don't want to educate their
staffs.

I recently did a contract at one of the biggest stockbrokers in the
world.  I found MANY senior programmers who like INITIALIZE, felt that
EVALUATE and scope deliniters are a waste of time because they are
just fancy ways of coding stuff that doesn't need fancy coding, and
who felt that reference modification is a dangerous practise which
should be avoided.  They never HEARD of nested programs, nor EXTERNAL
data.
I tried to sell an advanced COBOL course "for experienced programmers"
to the division manager.  He had no interest whatsoever.

Stephen J Spiro
President
Wizard Systems
```

OK - I wasn't intending to voice my opinion - but if I gotta, I gotta.

I work in a pure MVS, DB2, DLI, COBOL world. I design data capture
applications
and databases. I don't NEED OOP. I dedicate my time to developing quality
application designs and data models, and the code takes care of its'self.

Any time a new coding process comes up, everyone has to learn the it. This
takes
time and money. Production support suffers, and there are fewer staff who
can do the job.
So I feel that in my current world, the prolems and challenges haven't
changed, so I
feel COBOL does what I need it to do, and I don't want it to change.

BUT, OO in distributed applications is another matter. I support OO in JAVA
(I'm  a SCJ2P).
Just don't try to turn the dog into a cat. (i.e. don't try to make COBOL
something it's not).


>
> No you don't have to buy into OO. Given there was sufficient literature on
OO
> COBOL, and let's presuppose you could get your hands on an OO compiler,
then at
> least give it a looksee, just like you might Java or C++. Your judgment
call,
> regardless of whether considered right or wrong by others, is at least
based on
> fact (experience) rather than just hearsay. You've made your point and you
> aren't sitting there 'piggy in the middle' <G>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > We completed coversion to 370 in 1998 (from OS/VS & VS II). Just in time
to
> > start on Y2K. The OS/VS compiler went away last year. The VS II is still
> > around,
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Rufio,

I read your post with interest.

I would agree with you that in the environment you describe, you probably
don't need OO.

The question I would ask myself, if I were you is:"How long is this
environment likely to continue?"

At the moment it may seem like it is secure, but there are forces at work
which will change the way Enterprises use IT.

I'm not sure what SCJ2P means, but if it means you have Java knowledge then
I would say you have all bases covered.


Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T20:19:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B85662D.B9EB04FC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3B832FBF.B901D8C2@home.com> <4145699b.0108231019.81ca0bf@posting.google.com>`

```


Stephen J Spiro wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote
> <snip>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Stephen,

If what you say is true it is reassuring to know that money isn't the stumbling block.
Sadly, it seems it is HUMANS. I can accept skeptics like Howard, seriously questioning
before he jumps, (but as yet he hasn't got anything mainframe wise to 'jump' to). Then
there is the other group of people here, who in the past have rejected OO out of hand
- possibly some, (maybe none ?) involvement with other OO languages - and they try and
compare apples to pears. "I can do the same thing in non-OO COBOL if I do
this..........:. They *might* think they can, but they are wide of the mark as to OO's
potential. Because COBOL is a disciplined language and business centric, it invites a
different approach to OO, and as I wrote, OO-ing is strongly supported by all the
non-OO syntax..Where I say 'different' you can even substitute 'better'.

Sad thing is your Neanderthals who reject EVALUATE and ENDs etc. - even if IBM got
'generous' and gave them a free machine and OO compiler - these buggers are never
going to make the switch ! I'm not suggesting they MUST switch, but at least give the
methodology a try to see if it works advantageously for them.

One problem is lack of books. There are about half a dozen, with the two - Will Price
and Arranga/Coyle that actually have coded examples. The best that can be said of the
remainder is that they have prototype examples. Eventually a book has to come out that
covers :-

 - OO COBOL basics - classing and methods; definition of all the "funny" words
 - COBOL file handling - how one class program with 30 instances can represent 30
        different files
 - Relationship of OO to Databases, SQL, ESQL
- the Internet stuff , HTML, XML, (big blank in my noddle on this one !)
- GUI-ing and components - dear to Pete's heart
- Collections/Dictionaries which *really* do help to tie all the above together

I shouldn't really have been surprised. I know from thirty years ago that the majority
of computer people just do not accept change - constant use of, 'If it aint broke,
don't fix it", with absolutely no vision..

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ "Upgrading" (was: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T15:34:22-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m3pi4$1eo$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3B832FBF.B901D8C2@home.com> <4145699b.0108231019.81ca0bf@posting.google.com> <3B85662D.B9EB04FC@home.com>`

```
(response to comments "below")

1) I agree with Stephen that "cost" of software itself is RARELY the major
stumbling block to "upgrades" in an IBM mainframe environment.  It usually
is SOME consideration (If you don't know about IBM's policy about the
difference in costs between upgrading between releases and versions - you
have a "real treat" ahead for you).  Actually, there is OFTEN a bigger
consideration in LARGE shops when you "add" a CPU or upgrade the "group"
that your main CPU is in - because the price of everything that you already
own MAY go up.

2) My perception of LARGE IBM mainframe shops is that there is always,
ALWAYS a "backlog" of maintenance and "minor enhancements" that need to be
done on existing applications.  This means that there is *never* time to
re-do (fix from scratch) or do totally new development - places where it is
easiest to use new tools, paradigms, and programming approaches.  Although
it is POSSIBLE to add a few "scope-terminators" or EVALUATE into an existing
application, "switching it" to OO (or even nested programming) is simply
"out of the question - as it can't be cost justified (in terms of
resources - training, money, time, etc).

3) I know of several shops that did "pilot" projects with various
application generators, development techniques, testing tools, and other
"new" ways of solving maintenance and enhancement backlogs - during the '80s
and '90s.  Even when such pilot projects went well (which often they did
NOT), moving the "new" approach into existing application groups was often
exceedingly difficult and equally often unsuccessful.

4) "growth in skills and approaches" in such shops is NOT totally hopeless.
Things like Y2K or IBM dropping support for older hardware/software does
SOMETIMES cause shops to "come up to date" in how they do things.
Furthermore, I have often seen that one (or a few) "experienced" programmers
who are well respected can introduce a "new" technique into the way that
they work - and that others will then follow (gradually - VERY GRADUALLY).

  ***

Bottom-Line (IMHO) - IBM mainframe shops are NOT deterred by the cost of
new/different software (well not as much as PC users are).  They *ARE*
however, severely impacted by the "cost" of actually implementing new
techniques and tools so that their (large) programming staffs actually use
them and expand their use into both new and existing applications.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Upgrading" (was: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T21:04:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m3r32$o8u$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3B832FBF.B901D8C2@home.com> <4145699b.0108231019.81ca0bf@posting.google.com> <3B85662D.B9EB04FC@home.com> <9m3pi4$1eo$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 23-Aug-2001, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> Bottom-Line (IMHO) - IBM mainframe shops are NOT deterred by the cost of
> new/different software (well not as much as PC users are).  They *ARE*
> however, severely impacted by the "cost" of actually implementing new
> techniques and tools so that their (large) programming staffs actually use
> them and expand their use into both new and existing applications.


The best evidence of this can be found by looking at what happened with Y2K.
  In just about every case, a tremendous amount of money was spent to make
very minor changes to code - and switching to newer compilers which the
mainframe shops already owned.  The costs were not in coding, they were in
inventorying the problem, co�rdinating the changes, and testing.

And in opportunity costs, as vital projects were put on hold.  They usually
weren't included in the figures.

And when things were finished, the code looked pretty much as it did before,
the processes looked as they did before, but there were fewer lost programs
for a while.   And they retired the ANSI-68 and ANSI-72 compilers, and my
current shop will do the same thing with the CoBOL II compiler as soon as
they solve a problem in migrating some programs that use ports and
connectivity.   (Connectivity and common components appear to be the biggest
challenges outside of user acceptance testing).

There's no way the existing programs will be converted.   They may very well
be replaced, as companies buy applications that have been written in OO
CoBOL.  Or whatever language the vendor supplies - that won't be a huge
concern to management.

So it won't be the techies who will need to be sold on OO.   Therefore,
technical advantages and disadvantages won't be important in the sales job.
If the vendors find that OO is a good sales buzz word, they will switch.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Upgrading" (was: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T05:00:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B85E039.CCE2CC0C@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3B832FBF.B901D8C2@home.com> <4145699b.0108231019.81ca0bf@posting.google.com> <3B85662D.B9EB04FC@home.com> <9m3pi4$1eo$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> (response to comments "below")
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
>

OK Bill understand what you are saying. But as I read you there is something
fundamentally wrong. It merely reaffirms my belief that 'Big is NOT beautiful'.
Want proof of that, just look out our current commercial areas, particularly our
own game, where the high tech firms are taking a nose dive - they, because of
their 'bigness' and we must sell more, are offering more than consumers can
absorb. Now they are seeing retribution.

I certainly accept that you can't take the BIG BANG and switch existing
troublesome free working systems over to OO just for the hell of it - there is
absolutely no cost justification. But as I indicated in another message, big
shops could experiment with utilities like file handling to see if OO gives them
a pay-off.
Doesn't have to be file-handling, but that which really turns them on as an area
for investigation - perhaps those applications that maintenance wise have become
a pure pain in the ass..

For my info, you've indicated previously, that some of these big mainframe shops
have programming personnel running into several hundreds. It's now some 26 years
since I was in management, (I LOVE it doing my own thing - it fails or succeeds
on my own abilities).

I reflect back to the role of an EDP unit (OK IT department) then, and I
honestly can't think things have changed dramatically, albeit specialties have
been given modern fancy names. Back then it looked like the following :-

                                    Director of Finance (invariably !)

                               Manager of Information Systems (or any other
fancy title)

                 Data Processing Manager        Organization & Methods Manager
                                                                (deals with
'Manual' systems/techniques)

    Operations                    Systems &
(run/schedule                     Programming Mngr
computer)
Key punching       Systems        Programming            Maintenance/
                            Analysts        Manager                  Quality
Control

                        Senr. Analyst    Programming teams
                        per project        allocated to a Project

Back then DB was very new; had it been used add a further leg to the chart from
the Systems/Programming Manager with DB Administrator.

Now use all the fancy words/titles you want now, but I contend it breaks down to
represent the fairly concise organization above.

Consider what the above organization did.  By American standards we had very
limited resources. Started with five analysts and programmers respectively and
when I left in '75, ignoring the managerial positions above, we had roughly 10
analysts and 20 programmers - this for a department store group.

(a)Computerwise we started with an ICL 1900 and incremented up to an ICL 1904
(?) when I left, plus of course increased disk storage.

(b)Firstly we had to maintain the existing accounting (A/P) written by ICL as
the software house. The accountants early on, added bits and pieces to it all
over the place, turning it into a merchandising statistical system - an absolute
monster to maintain. (They had the attitude it was THEIR computer).

(c) I got involved with an inventory counting system - of all things, knitting
yarns as a trial area. Right application, wrong technology, wrong place at the
wrong time. I helped to 'kill' my own application. Meanwhile I was researching
OCR/OMR - we finished up buying an ICL OMR (Universal Document Reader) because
our then DPM was the ex-salesman who sold us the computer. Then we juggled
around for an application to use it ! We eventually used it for Payroll (using
ICL package) and Food A/P (told us which products we were buying).

(d) We were involved with Kimball Tags (part of Litton Industries along with
Sweda cash registers). The 'tags' were two-part tickets used on ladies fashion
merchandise.
Bloody useless system which the merchandisers insisted they needed. Told them
what was sold and in stock, but the 'units' never related to our financial
figures. We had three different goes at this application - at merchandiser
insistence !

(e) We took a look see at ICL's PROSPER - a mainframe Excel/Access,  if you like

(f) Chief Programmer always had his nose sniffing  looking for new ways. His
recommendation, all newcomers had learned COBOL, let's use it as our standard
language. We also mounted an experiment, took a dozen local kids from a tech
college, and used a bright young contractor to introduce them to Structured
COBOL. Lots and lots of comment statements in the style he showed them, but
after a month/six weeks  we had them producing elegant working programs (the
easy ones of course, initially).

(g) VAT loomed as a new entity ('72 - 74) - a revamp of anything associated with
the General Ledger - plus I used it as a sledgehammer to kill the old accounting
system - boy did we breathe a sigh of relief when we got something that we
controlled, had been written and proven by us.

(h) Accounts Receivable - two parts. We had our own store cards, (Visa,
Mastercard were comparatively new). Second, a Hire Purchase system (installment
payments on say fridges, freezers, furniture etc.). This utilized the OMR
document reader - produced coupon books for customer monthly payments.

(h) Ordering - we had an ideal model to work from.- Turitz AB in Sweden
(somewhat like Woolco or Walmart), and an ICL user. They had successfully
introduced and incrementally sophisticated their system since '63. Local stores
record stock, say what they want to order (which eventually became the computer
forecasting 95% of their requirements accurately), submit centrally, produce an
order per store and a grand summary - hit the vendor with the orders plus send
him a Giro cheque paying then and now ! God help him if he banked the cheque and
didn't deliver.

We saw an 'Anglicized' version of this as a key to success for the group. We had
a hell of a time convincing merchandisers and accountants - it was necessary to
bring their thought processes together to demonstrate where they inter linked.
Got real close when we arranged a visit to Turitz along with our MD. He
chickened out at the last minute because some other crisis came up. Never did
get this one off the ground, although I'm told it did eventually happen some
years after I left. Too late - they were bought out by men's' tailor chain
Montagu Burton. Now Debenhams is an independent department store group again.

The following four happened just prior to VAT :-

(i) Key to disk then archived to a mag tape for input to the mainframe. Replaced
our paper tape input. (Never did use punch cards in my career). Forgotten
manufacturer but it was US.

(j) Introduced a PDP 11 to do A/P input. To hell with the accountants griping
about batches of invoices getting missed from key-punching. No problemo, handed
it over to them. It dovetailed into the fact that VAT regulations demanded your
Modulus 97 check-digited ID number had to appear on every invoice. So just keyed
in vendor ID from their invoices (11 digits if my memory serves me well)..

(k) Point of sale experimental. Toss-up might be Sweda (Litton from Sweden) or
Singer, (yep! the Sewing Machine people). ICL guys breathing down our necks to
keep up with what we were doing - big bucks involved if we went this route.When
I left we had two experimental Singer POS in Debenham & Freebody, (now just
another Debenhams branch) in Oxford Street, 'our flagship' and one other in
Marshall & Snelgrove in London. The only thing I'm aware of since I left -
Singer in the States went belly up on the computer division and gave their
autocratic CEO the heave-ho. Guess who bought the Singer POS - ICL !

(l) Disaster, disaster. We bought a family supermarket group in SE London area.
We should've let them get swallowed up by Jack Cohen of Tesco, the other
contender. We paid the family members about 6 million pounds and let the silly
sods still run the organization. This involved me in a considerable amount of
time traveling from the SW up to East London, with overnight stays, on a weekly
basis, primarily PR work associated with VAT.

Now the above isn't listed here as Jimmy's resume, but to indicate that with a
comparatively small team we covered a lot of ground over an eight year period.
People had their day to day functions, but also took on extraneous activities
researching, and jointly arriving at conclusions.Like any EDP department we were
constantly pushing the ball up the hill, but took time out to look around to see
what was going on in the rest of the world.
.
Given your Hollywood 'cast of thousands' that appear in N. American IT shops -
you seriously telling me you can't devote a few key people to research what's
new and coming up ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T05:10:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B85E1B2.81508AA@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3B832FBF.B901D8C2@home.com> <4145699b.0108231019.81ca0bf@posting.google.com>`

```
Stephen J Spiro wrote:
> (snip)
> I think the biggest reason for slow introduction of OO will be that
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> to the division manager.  He had no interest whatsoever.

I second this.  Even 19 months after Y2K I am still converting
programs from OS/VS COBOL to COBOL for MVS & VM (which is being
replaced by COBOL for OS/390 & VM next month).  Many mainframe shops
are still fighting to implement structured programming in their new
COBOL programs.  I still support a few programs that were originally
written in 1976.  Most of these programs will never be rewritten or
restructured.  They'll be replaced when they are no longer
cost-effective to run in their current form.  If you can't get the
programmers (or managers) to move from spaghetti to structured, how
are you going to get them to move to OO?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T00:07:19+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b83a089_8@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197>`

```
Your response really made me smile, Jonathan <G>.

So I've taken the time to respond to it...

see below.


"Jonathan Guthrie" <jguthrie@brokersys.com> wrote in message
news:103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197...
> Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> Orientation. If COBOL misses this boat it will be dead in the water
within
> >> 10 years.
>
> > Question to all:
> > Is Mr. Dashwood correct in his assessment?  Is he exaggerating?   What
do
> > you think?
>
> Object Orientation hasn't proven the panacea to programming that it
> was supposed to.

Who says? Who said it was supposed to be a "panacea"? Does "programming"
NEED a panacea?
There are "good" programs and there are "poor" programs. It is independent
of the Language or Methodology used. Your perception of "good" and "poor"
will depend on where you stand.

To a Manager, "good" programs are ones which are quick to deliver, function
correctly and can be easily maintained, so they cost as little as possible.
A programmer may have a different definition, or at least, may add further
criteria to those stated.

If I was to translate your statement above as follows:

"I heard from some other Managers that their OO pilots were marginal and ,in
some cases worse than what we do now. Everyone I talk to says the concepts
are difficult. I looked at it briefly and found a bunch of jargon which
frightens me, so we don't need it on my watch... Besides, what we do now is
not so bad."

Would that be a fair assessment of what led to your statement?

I submit that your assertion is unsupported by real evidence and really
addresses something that is irrelevant.

It doesn't matter whether OO is a "panacea" or not.

Many of us believe that it is a "better way" of developing computer
software. Some of us have been doing that for a VERY long time and seen fads
come and go.

Object Orientation is NOT a fad.

It is comparable to the breakthrough that occurred when we first started
structuring and modularising code instead of writing spaghetti and
monolithic programs. (There IS a place for large monolithic programs but if
all you do on your site is Batch COBOL you are excused the rest of this
discourse...<G> There is NEVER a place for spaghetti...well, not since
memory expanded beyond 8K...<G>)

> It can be genuinely difficult to apply objects to
> real-world applications in a fashion that is maintainable and which
> has decent performance.

Why is it that throughout the history of commercial computer programming,
whenever a new concept comes along and we don't want to implement it, we
always start screaming about "performance"?

I saw it (in fact, to my shame, I was part of it...<G>) when we went from
Assembler to high level languages. When we went from real to virtual
storage, when we started decomposing functions into modules, and now, nearly
40 years on, it is being applied to OO...

I'm not going to respond to this point because it really has no basis in
fact (OO programs are at least as efficient as any other kind; show me a
structured system, written in COBOL (or any high level language) which you
claim is "efficient" and I'll guarantee to improve its "efficiency"...<G> (I
might rewrite parts of it in Assembler <G>). Certainly, a badly designed OO
system will be less efficient than a well designed non-OO system, but that
proves nothing. The point is that waving the old "efficiency" red herring is
an insult to our intelligence so let's not pursue that one...

Your other point about maintainability is much fairer and certainly more
realistic.

One of the main reasons we use COBOL is because its inherent readability
makes it easier to maintain, right?

But would you take someone off the street, give them a COBOL manual, and
expect to get good quality, maintainable code from them within a week or so?

Bad Management decision. So you saved on training, but it will cost you a
lot of money fixing what they did, always assuming they don't simply throw
down the Manual and run screaming from the office after a few days...(If you
gave them the 2002 Standards manual, this would never happen... but you'd
have to go and wake them every 40 minutes, and there would be very little
actual code produced...<G>)

Well, the same arguments apply to OO. If you are serious about using it you
must TRAIN people to use it PROPERLY. If you do this you will find that
there are Methodologies and techniques which DO allow you to "apply objects
to real-world applications in a fashion that is maintainable" and it ISN'T
difficult.

> Given that and the general poor explanation
> of WHY object orientation is supposed to be better

I'm NOT "giving that", but for the sake of this exercise, let's continue...

The "general poor explanation of WHY OO is supposed to be better" is the
best (only?) point you made so far.

I make part of my living out of writing and consider myself a reasonably
good communicator (except that I can't seem to get through to Howard lately
<G> (my fault, not yours, Howard <G>)), but I confess myself at a loss when
it comes to explaining WHY OO is better. It is VERY difficult to do so in a
few succinct sentences and without introducing jargon.

Here, I'll have a go now...

"OO is a logical extension of already proven concepts in computer
programming. It works by incrementally increasing the functionality of
modules as required, but without losing the existing functionality, and at
the same time allowing the functionality to be reused in many different
places, but with only ONE copy to maintain. (That covers "inheritance"
"overriding" and "interfacing" sort of...). Object Oriented programs are
functionally isolated and share data by passing it between modules. When a
failure occurs it is easy to isolate the problem and fix it.
("encapsulation"). Modules can be grouped together as types and recognise
each other's parameters. (a very poor description of "class" and one form of
"polymorphism")".

Anyone who has not been been exposed to OO concepts will have difficulty
with the above and I would be the first to admit that it does not exactly
trip off the tongue... Even worse, it does not begin to convey the POWER of
this programming paradigm.

So, I concede your point. Only because I have failed dismally to refute it
on paper. In my heart I KNOW that OO is better, but I can't explain why in
the necessary time and space frame here.


> and I am
> skeptical of any efforts to "save" any computer program language by
> adding object-oriented features to it.

To some extent I agree with what you are saying here. It is true that OO has
been added to COBOL as an "afterthought" (It always makes me think of Rolf
Harris and his "Jake the Peg" (a three-legged man) sketch..."I'm Jake the
peg...deedle deedle deedle dum, with my extra leg deedle deedle deedle
dum..."))

Unlike the true OO languages where OO was inherent in the design from day
one, we have to expect a bit of roughness round the edges when it comes to
OO COBOL. Nevertheless, the implementations I have seen (and I currently use
Fujitsu V5) are excellent, and the developers deserve full credit for
providing them.

OO is not being added to COBOL in an attempt to save it. Any more than you
add a steering wheel to a car to save it from the wrecker. It is being added
to make it roadworthy in the 21st Century. It is ESSENTIAL that COBOL
support OO if it is to play any meaningful part in the commercial computer
programming of this century.

>(I once asked a job
> interviewee why it was better to design programs by OO principles
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Now I don't really believe you are influenced by what a job applicant who is
under pressure, and may have had limited exposure to OO, said.

Supposing he had said: "Well, using OO you can model processes in the Real
World, cut your maintenance, re-use your code, and detect and fix errors in
no time flat. On top of that, it disciplines your approach and allows you to
get it wrong before you get it right."  Would such a statement have changed
your mind about OO? I don't think so, so it is hardly fair to quote this in
support of your own prejudiced viewpoint.

> I look on the object-oriented features of COBOL like adding complex
> number types to ISO Modula-2 in order to lure away FORTRAN
> programmers.  That didn't work because it didn't touch on the real
> reasons why programmers code in FORTRAN.
Having programmed in both Modula-2 and FORTRAN, I didn't realise there was a
contest. Complex numbers have always been part of Fortran as far as I know
(obtainable by a function call). I stress that my experience in this area is
limited and it was a long time ago. Your point about the real reason people
use a language is well taken.


> OO COBOL won't save the
> language (assuming it needs saving) unless it touches on the real
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Using Object Orientation DOES enhance what COBOL does best (the manipulation
of data and the programming of commercial applications) because it allows
incremental changes to systems with no ill-effects and opens COBOL to share
the commercially available Objects which we see everyday on the desktop. Try
interfacing to MS Word or ACCESS or EXCEL WITHOUT using OO... or obtaining a
reference to one of these objects (it comes down to the same thing).

But, most importantly, as I have observed here before, OO gives us access to
Components (ActiveX, CORBA) and whether we do this from a Mainframe across
the LAN/IntraNet or from a Client, Server, or Peer, components are the Keys
to the Kingdom...

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T13:45:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m0cva$93q$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3b83a089_8@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
Some of the problems in selling OO to management have been in the selling
method.

For instance, an OO advocate might say that the major advantage of OO is
that it reuses components.   So the manager checks in to real shops to find
out what components get reused.  Typically, he will find this is much less
than advertised, and most of the reused components are simple GUI components
- which aren't of much interest to the mainframe shop.  (It is cheaper to
buy a GUI front end to work with the existing system).

Of course, the big thing is that converting a large enterprise shop to
ANYTHING is very expensive.   So the manager needs to come up with actual
numbers from shops that have made this conversion in the real world.   And
the numbers don't exist.


And the change is bigger than a programming change.  Who pays for and owns
an object?   What do you do if one of your customers wants a slight change,
and you trace down the change to a common object?   The easiest thing to do
would be to copy that object and design it so that one customer did the
changed version.   But this gets around a big advantage of OO.   Certainly
getting all users to help in the type of testing enterprises require is very
costly.   Do we only make components of non-critical code?    Different
methodologies need to be developed for accounting, design, unit testing,
change control, systems testing, user acceptance testing, etc.  That's
expensive and politically risky.

Of course, a shop could have OO programs without an OO environment.   Much
less risky - can do everything the way they are being done now.   But
where's the payoff?  I have seen this with Java programs designed for a web
screen.   This is using an OO language to create non OO applications.

Certainly having an OO shop will force companies to do things they should be
doing anyway.  Companies need better ways of keeping track of their code
now.   With OO, this need becomes vital.   But this isn't a selling point.

The largest OO projects seem to be for programs such as the latest version
of Windows.   I suppose they must have detailed tools to keep track of
components. (But even as stable as it is now, it is possible for one bad
object to make the whole system crash - decision makers fear one bad
component will make their system crash as well) .
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T12:06:30+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b84489e_3@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3b83a089_8@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9m0cva$93q$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Some quick observations below, Howard.


"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:9m0cva$93q$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> Some of the problems in selling OO to management have been in the selling
> method.
>
Whether we "sell" OO to Management or not (and I do NOT advocate doing this)
I believe it is eventually inevitable that it will affect development for
both mainframes and other systems. It is not a question of "IF" but rather
of "WHEN".

> For instance, an OO advocate might say that the major advantage of OO is
> that it reuses components.   So the manager checks in to real shops to
find
> out what components get reused.  Typically, he will find this is much less
> than advertised, and most of the reused components are simple GUI
components
> - which aren't of much interest to the mainframe shop.  (It is cheaper to
> buy a GUI front end to work with the existing system).
>
There is certainly a perception at the moment that OO is only of use for
GUI. This will change as more Legacy code gets mined into components and
these components are tied to databases. The use of existing factoring tools
(and some which are still currently on the drawing board) will make what was
previously deemed "impossible", at first "feasible", then "viable", and
finally "essential".

Existing code libraries will be re-factored and reused. They are too
valuable to throw away and the only thing stopping it at the moment is the
perceived difficulty in actually extracting the Business rules from code
which has been over-maintained over 20 odd years. Market forces will drive
this and OO will provide the means of doing it.


> Of course, the big thing is that converting a large enterprise shop to
> ANYTHING is very expensive.   So the manager needs to come up with actual
> numbers from shops that have made this conversion in the real world.   And
> the numbers don't exist.
>
That is arguable, at the moment. Maybe true right now, but what about in 5 -
10 years?
>
> And the change is bigger than a programming change.  Who pays for and owns
> an object?

You do.

>What do you do if one of your customers wants a slight change,
> and you trace down the change to a common object?

It doesn't work like that. This is much too deep to go into here. Please
read the referenced article. The answer to this lies in design
"granularity". You are asking "when is a brick not a brick?"...

> The easiest thing to do
> would be to copy that object and design it so that one customer did the
> changed version.

Nope. The easiest thing is to phone up the supplier and ask for an
enhancement.  He will give you a date and cost. The cost will typically be
several programmer hours, a few hundred bucks. Alternatively, you would
replace the existing component with one containing all the existing
functionality plus the enhancement you requested. If your supplier is not
keen to enhance what you have (and this is extremely unlikely...my
experience has been that they jump at the chance and are always glad to get
feedback which can improve their product) then you source it from another
supplier.

> But this gets around a big advantage of OO.   Certainly
> getting all users to help in the type of testing enterprises require is
very
> costly.   Do we only make components of non-critical code?    Different
> methodologies need to be developed for accounting, design, unit testing,
> change control, systems testing, user acceptance testing, etc.  That's
> expensive and politically risky.
>
If the existing mechanisms to achieve these functions are OK, then you don't
need new ones. You are testing an end result. How it was (or is) put
together is irrelevant, provided it functions as the users require. Even as
I write this, systems are being developed with "critical code" provided by
third party components. It is very viable.

> Of course, a shop could have OO programs without an OO environment.   Much
> less risky - can do everything the way they are being done now.   But
> where's the payoff?  I have seen this with Java programs designed for a
web
> screen.   This is using an OO language to create non OO applications.
>
> Certainly having an OO shop will force companies to do things they should
be
> doing anyway.  Companies need better ways of keeping track of their code
> now.   With OO, this need becomes vital.   But this isn't a selling point.
>
No comment on the above.


> The largest OO projects seem to be for programs such as the latest version
> of Windows.   I suppose they must have detailed tools to keep track of
> components. (But even as stable as it is now, it is possible for one bad
> object to make the whole system crash - decision makers fear one bad
> component will make their system crash as well) .

One bad module will make the system crash now. You lost me here...

Bottom line:

I believe that Market forces will drive Enterprises to re-assess the cost of
IT.

(And I'm NOT the only one who thinks so...<G>

"We estimate that by 2003, 70 percent of new applications will be deployed
as a combination of pre-assembled and newly created components integrated to
form complex business systems."

- Gartner )

When they do, it will become more and more apparent that outsourcing and
component use are the two major routes to reducing IT cost.

I know of a certain Insurance Company who were spending $20,000,000 a year
on IT. In good times this was not a problem. They had a bad year with
floods ravaging the territory they covered. Payouts were very high;cash flow
was becoming a problem. Then they noticed that it was costing $20,000,000
for IT. The MD (CEO for you North Americans) asked:"Why are we spending this
amount when we could buy the service for less? This is NOT the business we
are in."  Within a month the entire IT systems department was closed, the
equipment sold, the existing software picked up and leased back by a joint
arrangement with two major computer vendors and the IT bill slashed to
around $7,000,000 a year. (I heard that later they moved to a package
supplied by their new IT outsource and the bill went down to around
$5,000,000 a year.)

There is no excuse for continuing to build one-off software in-house when
components can provide the same facility. OK, maybe it isn't there yet, but
it is coming.

It would be a good thing for programmers to prepare for Component use by
learning OO now. This way the choice of outsourcing can be shown to be
unnecessary, because the in-house facility has its act together and is
capable of producing and maintaining cost effective systems. Maybe even
selling some components derived from existing code...

If you still doubt what I'm saying, run a Google search on the words
"Software Components"... the same search two years ago returned around 4,000
hits...

Or go and look at http://www.flashline.com and see what major industries and
Enterprises are doing and saying about Component Technology

The key to this technology is Object Orientation. Ignore it at your peril.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T14:51:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m356g$70h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3b83a089_8@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9m0cva$93q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3b84489e_3@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```

On 22-Aug-2001, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
wrote:

> Existing code libraries will be re-factored and reused. They are too
> valuable to throw away and the only thing stopping it at the moment is the
> perceived difficulty in actually extracting the Business rules from code
> which has been over-maintained over 20 odd years. Market forces will drive
> this and OO will provide the means of doing it.

Not quite.  Also stopping it is the fact that very few managers are willing
to put their head on the chopping block until they can see real live
examples of it working in comparable environments.  Nobody wants to be
first.   (And they know that it will cost more and take more time than
estimated - projects of that size always do).


> > And the change is bigger than a programming change.  Who pays for and
> > owns an object?
>
> You do.

I mean, which of my internal customers pays for and owns the object. 
Accounting rules need to be modified to fit.


> > The easiest thing to do
> > would be to copy that object and design it so that one customer did the
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> supplier.

So we don't write objects, but only buy them?   I don't want to buy business
rule objects.  (GUI objects are fine though).


> > But this gets around a big advantage of OO.   Certainly
> > getting all users to help in the type of testing enterprises require is
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> together is irrelevant, provided it functions as the users require.

Existing mechanisms are not adequate for changing widely used components. 
If each accounting site needs to test a program because of an object was
changed for a sales site, someone needs to pay charge that sales site for
the cost involved.  Right now this is small enough to include it as overhead
- but it appears that this should be a much bigger expense in an OO shop.

> Even  as I write this, systems are being developed with "critical code"
> provided by
> third party components. It is very viable.

I would expect so.  But they won't do so for mainframe environments until
they see a market there.


> > The largest OO projects seem to be for programs such as the latest
> > version
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> One bad module will make the system crash now. You lost me here...

This is the perception of Windows, and Windows is the only large OO system
most of these managers are familiar with.


> It would be a good thing for programmers to prepare for Component use by
> learning OO now. This way the choice of outsourcing can be shown to be
> unnecessary, because the in-house facility has its act together and is
> capable of producing and maintaining cost effective systems. Maybe even
> selling some components derived from existing code...

Of course.  I spent a lot of money to get my masters in OO because I want to
be prepared.   But that is not the only future I am preparing for, as I
don't know which future I will be working in.


> If you still doubt what I'm saying, run a Google search on the words
> "Software Components"... the same search two years ago returned around
> 4,000 hits...

Of course.   The topic is interesting, and has a wide applicability at the
PC level.


> Or go and look at http://www.flashline.com and see what major industries
> and
> Enterprises are doing and saying about Component Technology

Interesting - but I don't see this advertisement showing me where any large
company has converted their business logic to components. (I am not counting
out-sourcing nor buying ERP software as components)


> The key to this technology is Object Orientation. Ignore it at your peril.

The key is good business decisions.  That may be one.  There are others. 
Ignore any of them at your peril.   Make assumptions and costly conversions
at your peril.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T12:28:22+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b859e84_3@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3b83a089_8@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9m0cva$93q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3b84489e_3@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9m356g$70h$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Good response, Howard.

I appreciate that you followed the suggested links, understand my position,
while you don't agree with it, and are unlikely to be persuaded until you
see hard evidence of mainframe sites actually re-factoring code and using
third party components.

I believe this will happen in the next few years but I certainly can't prove
it and I could be wrong.

I have nothing more to say. <G>

Pete.
