###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Jonathan Guthrie <jguthrie@brokersys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T14:30:44-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4911m9.jhf.ln@216.39.196.197>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3b83a089_8@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> "Jonathan Guthrie" <jguthrie@brokersys.com> wrote in message
> news:103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197...
>> Howard Brazee <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote:

>> > On 20-Aug-2001, "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
>> > wrote:

>> >> Whether we like it or not, the future of computer programming lies in
>> >> Object
>> >> Orientation. If COBOL misses this boat it will be dead in the water
> within
>> >> 10 years.

>> > Question to all:
>> > Is Mr. Dashwood correct in his assessment?  Is he exaggerating?   What
> do
>> > you think?

>> Object Orientation hasn't proven the panacea to programming that it
>> was supposed to.
 
> Who says? Who said it was supposed to be a "panacea"? Does "programming"
> NEED a panacea?

Who says?  Well, I do.  The sheer volume of really crappy code written
in so-called object-oriented programming languages argues that simply
claiming that you're using "object orientation" isn't going to make a
project a success.  Nobody in this exchange (although I've seen it 
asserted elsewhere) has said it was supposed to be a panacea.
Mentioning that was intended to be a pedagogical technique to introduce
my real point.  I'll explain more about that, below.

As to whether or not programming needs a panacea, well, the search for
a way to make all programmers more productive is something many people
believe is worth spending a lot of time to look for so I think I can
safely say that it would be awful nice to find one.

> To a Manager, "good" programs are ones which are quick to deliver, function
> correctly and can be easily maintained, so they cost as little as possible.
> A programmer may have a different definition, or at least, may add further
> criteria to those stated.
 
> If I was to translate your statement above as follows:
 
> "I heard from some other Managers that their OO pilots were marginal and ,in
> some cases worse than what we do now. Everyone I talk to says the concepts
> are difficult. I looked at it briefly and found a bunch of jargon which
> frightens me, so we don't need it on my watch... Besides, what we do now is
> not so bad."

Except I'm not now nor have I ever been a software manager.  I'm a 
programmer with 10 years experience with object-oriented programming.
This isn't what I've heard, it's what I've experienced and what I've
seen firsthand.

Look, I don't yet do COBOL, but I like learning new languages and so I
dropped by to check this out to see what I could glean from the 
newsgroup before moving ahead.  However, I've been programming in all
those PC languages that have already made the conversion to OO
programming starting since before I was in high school, lo these many
years ago.  (I've got a 20 year reunion coming up in a year or so.
Yeah, I know I'm just a baby: the first programming I ever did for
other people was in FORTRAN-II on an IBM 1130  that was manufactured 
he same year I was.)

Object-oriented programming has come to dominate the desktop because
the sorts of things you have to do to drive a modern GUI-based
desktop map well to the sorts of problems object-orientation is
supposed to solve.  Since many people define their applications'
functionalites as how they look on the screen, this drives the interest
in (and the level of hype for) object-oriented programming languages,
even resulting in the "bolting on" of "object-oriented" features" to
logical (Prolog, for example) or functional (like ML) programming
languages, which it's really not suited for, as those families of
languages have different means of accomplishing what object-orientation
accomplishes in imperative programming languages.

> It doesn't matter whether OO is a "panacea" or not.
 
> Many of us believe that it is a "better way" of developing computer
> software. Some of us have been doing that for a VERY long time and seen fads
> come and go.
 
> Object Orientation is NOT a fad.

How is object-orientation not being a fad (which I never claimed)
related to it not being a panacea?  Merriam-Webster online (www.m-w.com)
says that the definition of panacea is "a remedy for all ills or
difficulties".  Nowhere in my original post do I claim that it is not
the right solution for SOME tasks.  In fact, as I list above, there
are some tasks for which it is a particularly good choice.  The
question that I have is whether or not object-orientation is a
good thing to do for COBOL.  If it WAS a panacea, object-oriented
COBOL would (by the definition given above) be a good thing.  Since it
is not, it may be a good thing.  Then again, it may not.

You are right when you say that it matters not whether or not OO is
a panacea except to introduce that question, which would be 
irrelevant otherwise.

> It is comparable to the breakthrough that occurred when we first started
> structuring and modularising code instead of writing spaghetti and
> monolithic programs.

Yes, but not comparable to the breakthrough when higher-level languages
first started to be used.  (Cite Fred Brooks' "There is no Silver
Bullet" and "Silver Bullet Refired".)  Again, I'm not refuting your
assertion that Object-Oriented COBOL is a good thing, only pointing
out a limit to your description.

>> It can be genuinely difficult to apply objects to
>> real-world applications in a fashion that is maintainable and which
>> has decent performance.
 
> Why is it that throughout the history of commercial computer programming,
> whenever a new concept comes along and we don't want to implement it, we
> always start screaming about "performance"?

Because advances in computing are usually about performance on some
fundamental level.  Tasks that are too slow using old techniques drive
the search for new techniques and the history of computing is mostly
about a search for more and more efficient (in terms of CPU or total
run time) techniques.  There are exceptions, because CPU efficiency
is not the only important metric, but most research is done trying to
find ways to make what the processor does more efficient.  You mention
coding in assembly langauge, later.  A LOT of the effort in compiler
design and construction over the last 40 years has been focused on
trying to get the efficiency of assembly out of a higher-level language.

Another example would be multitasking.  Multitasking was originally 
invented to make more efficient use of computing resources.

All this focus on CPU efficiency means that the first cry you're 
going to hear about any new technique from someone who doesn't want
to change is that it is inefficient.  In any case, I'm not saying that
OO programming is necessarily CPU-inefficient, just that it can be
inefficient.

> I'm not going to respond to this point because it really has no basis in
> fact (OO programs are at least as efficient as any other kind; show me a
> structured system, written in COBOL (or any high level language) which you
> claim is "efficient" and I'll guarantee to improve its "efficiency"...<G>

That would be difficult for me to do.  I don't know how to program in
COBOL.  However, I've written tens of thousands (and, in some of these
languages, hundreds of thousands) of lines of code in Java, C++,
Objective-C, and Modula-3. which are all object-oriented languages.
I came into this conversation as not even a COBOL tyro, but as someone
who knows a very great deal about object-oriented programming.

When I was writing the lines I wrote above (about slow, large, and
unmaintainable) I was thinking specifically of the Microsoft Foundation
Class Library, which is how most people do MS-Windows programming in
C++, the only language I've done it in.  It qualified as all those
things in 1995 although I expect that it's been improved since then.
(It was also a pain in the posterior to program.  The other class
libraries I've used since then were organized much better.)

I guess the point I was trying to make was that if all the bright
people at Microsoft couldn't make the first versions of MFC fast and
so forth, it must not be intuitively obvious to even the most
casual observer.  Yes, it is quite possible to do a good job of
object-oriented design and implementation, but object hell is
slower than snot because of all the operations that object-orientation
hides from you.  Stepping through an MFC-based program with a
debugger was most instructive as one line of my program called copy
constructor after copy constructor to pass the same string to its
final destination.

Note that those hidden operations are what is supposed to make
object-oriented programmers more productive in object-oriented
tasks than pure procedural programming, so it's not something you
can simply do away with.  The only real way of making ANY programmer
more productive is to hide irrelevant details from him.

> might rewrite parts of it in Assembler <G>). Certainly, a badly designed OO
> system will be less efficient than a well designed non-OO system, but that
> proves nothing. The point is that waving the old "efficiency" red herring is
> an insult to our intelligence so let's not pursue that one...

Why is it an insult to your intelligence?  My point is that, in my
experience, there are an awful lot more poorly-designed object-oriented
systems than well designed object-oriented systems.  (Actually, you
can remove the "object-oriented" from the previous sentence and it
doesn't change its truth value.)  Some of the leading object-oriented
or object-based systems are poorly designed.

> I make part of my living out of writing and consider myself a reasonably
> good communicator (except that I can't seem to get through to Howard lately
> <G> (my fault, not yours, Howard <G>)), but I confess myself at a loss when
> it comes to explaining WHY OO is better. It is VERY difficult to do so in a
> few succinct sentences and without introducing jargon.

> Here, I'll have a go now...
 
> "OO is a logical extension of already proven concepts in computer
> programming. It works by incrementally increasing the functionality of
> modules as required, but without losing the existing functionality, and at
> the same time allowing the functionality to be reused in many different
> places, but with only ONE copy to maintain.

Don't focus on what object orientation DOES, focus on what it IS.
Here, I'll show you:

Structured programming is based on the idea that a program is
composed of data structures which are manipulated by control
structures.  (Or control structures which manipulate data
structures.  It works both ways.)  In the structured view, you have a
good program when the two are in harmony.  Object-oriented programming
takes that a step further and views data structures and control
structures to be different aspects of the same thing.  What this does
is allow similar objects to function in similar ways, often sharing
the same code.  In fact, one of the most powerful reasons for using
object-orientation is that it sometimes allows you to change the
underlying functionality totally without modifying the top-level or
"driver" code at all.  Under the right conditions, this allows the
programmer to focus on the essential details and ignore the rest.

> OO is not being added to COBOL in an attempt to save it. Any more than you
> add a steering wheel to a car to save it from the wrecker. It is being added
> to make it roadworthy in the 21st Century. It is ESSENTIAL that COBOL
> support OO if it is to play any meaningful part in the commercial computer
> programming of this century.

From the point of view that I have, the first and last sentence in this
paragraph contradict each other.  Computer program languages "die out"
by becoming largely irrelevant.  To me, "saving the language" means
allowing it to play a meaningful part in the commercial computer
programming of this century.

>>(I once asked a job
>> interviewee why it was better to design programs by OO principles
>> and his answer was because OO is so much harder than plain
>> procedural programming that you had to think more about the
>> program.

> Now I don't really believe you are influenced by what a job applicant who is
> under pressure, and may have had limited exposure to OO, said.
 
> Supposing he had said: "Well, using OO you can model processes in the Real
> World, cut your maintenance, re-use your code, and detect and fix errors in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> support of your own prejudiced viewpoint.

Suppose I were to tell you that the job application was for a job
programming Windows applications in C++ with MFC, and that was a job
that I had been doing for some months at that point.  Would that change
your idea of my background?  The applicant in question claimed
extensive OO experience in a variety of OO languages, and I was trying
to determine whether or not he'd actually thought about what he was
saying when he said that "OO" was better or if he was just spouting the
standard party line.

I wasn't looking for any particular answer, just some indication that
he knew what he was talking about.  Heck, even saying that he'd heard
from his professors that it was better would have been better than
the answer that I got.  (The lack of answer on this question wasn't a
killer, but some of his answers to other questions indicated that he
wasn't being entirely truthful about all the experiences listed on his
CV.  That's why he didn't get the job.)

If you had given me the answer you gave above, I would be asking you
WHY OO will cut the maintenance allow more extensive code re-use and 
llow for greater error detection.  Simply saying those things doesn't
show me that you've done any more than memorize the list of what's
supposed to be better about OO programming.

Besides, I don't believe the last:  Languages as diverse as Ada and
Common Lisp all are supposed to be designed to "catch programmer
errors".  If form follows function, it is a given that radically
different forms with the same stated function mean that it is likely
that at least one of them fails to accomplish that function.  Since
every language, OO or not, seems to claim an increased rate of errors
caught by the system rather than by human intervention, I conclude that
it is unlikely that ANY of the claims are true.

Code reuse is a different matter and has more to do with writing
documentation than designing code.  My experience agrees with Brooks
that it takes about as much work to turn a collection of functions
into a reusable library as it took to write the functions in the
first place.  That argues against reuse without a conscious effort.
 
>> OO COBOL won't save the
>> language (assuming it needs saving) unless it touches on the real
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> the language IS dying, it's dead already.

> Using Object Orientation DOES enhance what COBOL does best (the manipulation
> of data and the programming of commercial applications) because it allows
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> reference to one of these objects (it comes down to the same thing).

You know that one paragraph I wrote was the core of my post.  You could
have saved us both a whole bunch of typing if you'd just have responded
to that.  Of course, I had no idea you were going to respond so
negatively when I suggested that object-orientation wasn't an automatic
slam dunk for all programming tasks or I wouldn't have put that part of
my post in.  As near as I can tell, you drew the conclusion that I
disagree with the idea that object-orientation works well with what
COBOL is good for and that this conclusion is based on the idea that I
am well versed in COBOL and not in OO programming.  The fact of the
matter is I don't have any opinions one way or another about what COBOL
is good for.  (That is, indeed, why I started reading this newsgroup.)
I'm sorry that I led you to believe that my background was different
than it actually is.  Please be assured that it never occured to me
that you wouldn't automatically know who I was and where I was coming
from even though that seems silly in retrospect.

Usually, when I with Access, I'm using it as a report generator that
pulls data from a SQL database server.  That's because what the system
I most commonly work on is a process-control system and my focus is on
the automated gathering of data from the distributed sites.  That
really doesn't touch on the sorts of things you're talking about.

> But, most importantly, as I have observed here before, OO gives us access to
> Components (ActiveX, CORBA) and whether we do this from a Mainframe across
> the LAN/IntraNet or from a Client, Server, or Peer, components are the Keys
> to the Kingdom...

Umm, I've not done ActiveX, but I did SOM (which is based on CORBA) 
before I stopped doing OS/2, and it didn't require that the
implementation language be object-oriented.  It is an object framework,
to be sure, but its interface was purely procedural.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T10:47:53+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b8437c3_9@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9lrhdm$hbn$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <103vl9.g6d.ln@216.39.196.197> <3b83a089_8@goliath.newsgroups.com> <4911m9.jhf.ln@216.39.196.197>`

```
A very good and fair response, Jonathan.

Thanks for that.

I am sorry that exposure to MFC appears to have blighted your view of OO
(but I agree with your observations regarding this Class Library).

I guess those of us who use it and find it productive will continue to do so
and those who don't won't.

With the background you have you should have no trouble understanding OO
COBOL, although you will become frustrated by the verbosity after using C++.

This verbosity is one of COBOL's great strengths and, while it is tiresome
to a programmer sometimes, it pays for itself when it comes to maintenance.

Many of the arguments I would like to put to you are covered in an article
which you can read at:

http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com

Press the "Archive" button and select the April  2001 issue from the list.
The lead article is called "Meet the Future" and covers WHY OO is important.

We can say what we like here, but the fact remains that the future of
programming is heavily intertwined with Object Orientation. Sooner or later
it will touch everyone involved in program development.

Pete.

…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Using Object Orientation DOES enhance what COBOL does best (the
manipulation
> > of data and the programming of commercial applications) because it
allows
> > incremental changes to systems with no ill-effects and opens COBOL to
share
> > the commercially available Objects which we see everyday on the desktop.
Try

obtaining a
> > reference to one of these objects (it comes down to the same thing).
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

access to
> > Components (ActiveX, CORBA) and whether we do this from a Mainframe
across
> > the LAN/IntraNet or from a Client, Server, or Peer, components are the
Keys
> > to the Kingdom...
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T23:07:09+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-B16F96.23070821082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
In article <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com>,
 "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote:

<snip>
> The "risk" in getting to grips with OO COBOL right now, is nowhere near as
> high as the "risk" in NOT doing so...
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
I would like to agree with Peter on this.

As an example, check out Extreme Programming. This is a technique that 
optimizes the development cycle so that the cost of making a change at 
any point in a project is the same. That is to say, unlike waterfall 
methodologies, Extreme Programming does not suffer from exponential 
costs the more you delay a change into the lifetime of a project.

This flattening of the costs of development is, in my opinion, the 
greatest break through in the theory and practice of software 
construction that has spawned forth from academia.

OO is not the grail, Waterfall was not the grail, RAD was not the grail. 
All these failed because ultimately they do not address the exponential 
cost of making a change once the majority of the programming has been 
done.

How does all of this relate to OO COBOL?
Well Kent Beck and associates designed/evolved Extreme Programming from 
their constant use of OO over the years. This means that the principles 
work best in an OO environment. That is not to say that you could adapt 
them to a non-OO environment, however, there will be no one to help you 
do that. In the OO environment this technique will eventually pay back 
big.

All Mainframe shops and now "downsized" shops (read PC!) will be faced 
with the same problem. New software projects are extremely complicated 
and the cost of developing them is probably the same for both 
environments. How do you approach the old mainframe projects that failed 
during the 70's 80's and 90's but still need to be done? Simply moving 
the problem from Mainframes to PC's does not appear to be solving these 
problems.

In my opinion, once we get a new set of managers, and/or current 
managers that are willing to risk Extreme Programming, Mainframe and PC 
shops will all head down that path. It's very responsive to user needs 
and it keeps the costs of the project down. These are very attractive to 
management - it's just that they are not currently aware of the practice.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

- **From:** psychoticusrex@hotmail.com (Psychoticus Rex)
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T08:20:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1341c7e4.0108210720.7011fa07@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
I'm not such an avid CoBOL programmer but I do use it on regular but
not continuous basis.

I've personally found that using the "right language for the right
job" as a safe tactic in avoiding such little "debates".

Right now I use:
Server Side Java Script or ASP (Depending on the host platform) to
develop web applications.  Thier both capable of presenting pleasing
GUIs at a minimum of effort.
The objects I wrap these GUI's over tend to be top heavy, high
processing systems drivers and need non traditional mainframe
environments to execute.
The major benifit of OO CoBOL I see is the elimination of all those
retched intermediate steps.  I could thus directly wrap a Server Side
CoBOL program and through those Wrapped OO Cobol programs I could use
Other CoBOL programs (batch or otherwise) or assembly or APL or what
have you, to do the dirty work.  The only over head would be the http
server and the CoBOL (interpreter / parser / compiler - depending on
how dynamic you want to make your execution environ.)

With OO Cobol the posibility of having Cobol Scripts would make that
bulky and horednously unmanageable Java database language a thing of
the past.

If I had my way, I'd use SQL for query, OO CoBOL for maintenance and
actions on databases, visual languages for GUIs, and NASM for
functions.  To each their own.

The harmonizing of development environments on a back end where mixing
isn't necessesary (I exclude asm from this since I've never seen a
respectible system which couldn't get along with at least one specific
asm function to make the proceedings more efficient.) could be a
definite boon to the CoBOL industry, where VA Gen and their ilk are
currently holding sway, such things could eliminate scores of training
schedules.  And avoiding unnecessary mixings makes debugging and
errors faster to detect.  The necessary mixing at environmental
transitions makes each language specialized, thus more efficient
(theoretically), and more powerfull to its developer.

This is just a rant I suppose since I didn't come here to debate  a
course of action just put in my two cents worth of a point of view. :)
 I just thought it would be nice to have one language for this, one
language for that, and the intermediate zones would be handled by some
common object interface dependant on the hardware/os running the
system.  Thus everything would port, your user interface would run on
this server (Java), your queries would illicit the same responses from
this server (SQL), your database utilities and management software
would work over here, and the only changes needing to be made would be
in the business logic alone.... a pipe dream I suppose, would make
"re-usability" a moot point though.

G'day!



"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3b814544_4@goliath.newsgroups.com>...
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T19:06:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B81607E.6AE36E6F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net>`

```


Colin Campbell wrote:

> Bill,
> I'm glad you brought this up.  We did an application recently that was based
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> What do others think?

I wont comment on your references to IBM implementations, but my starting point is
your, "COBOL is supposed to accept the same source code on all platforms, and the
source is supposed to perform the same everywhere. ".

Couldn't agree more, and on that issue be nice if I could take IBM mainframe, PC
Fujitsu and Micro Focus source and could read a damned file from one to another.
Sure we have common OPEN, READ, REWRITE, WRITE etc. which all return the same
Standard-specified results - but we can't 'converse' because the internal
mechanics, (data structures, indexing etc.) are 'implementor defined'. Yes we can
read them - if we put them in DB tables.

Now when it comes to only using the 'written' standard we are currently talking
about COBOL '85, plus the '97 additions for Intrinsic Functions. Remembering the
'OO bit' started back in '89 and it will be 2002 before it is 'official' - where
would that put us ? OO-wise we would be in the 'COBOL Dark Ages" until about 2005
when developers dipped their feet into the waters to give it a try. As a
generalization I think it is accurate to say that vendor extensions (suck it and
see), have and always will be, in place before a feature is considered and becomes
part of the Standard.

As a small example, Micro Focus introduced a Level 78 :-

    78 ThisIsANumber                value 12.
    78 ThisIsATextItem               value "Hello World".

Apparently the idea appealed to J4, but they renamed these to CONSTANTS for COBOL
2002. (For 'Constant' read 'Concrete' - you can't change the value). Whether you
call it a Level 78 or a Constant - believe me, this is an extremely useful feature
and amongst other things, allows me to do the following :-

    01 myText    pic x(ThisIsANumber).

May look daft but extremely useful when part of a copyfile !

Back to OO :-

As regards writing the code once and then it is portable between compilers - to be
blunt - that is NOT in a compiler vendor's best interests is it ? Fortunately some
have noted the situation and WG4 representatives, (that's the ISO bit of COBOL
Standards) have hoisted a red warning flag about portability as applied to OO
collections.

>I find the "early adoption" of OO COBOL to be an unfortunate by product of the
>slow adoption of new COBOL standards.  People naturally want to try out "what's
>now".  However, those who are using the OO COBOL than any vendor offers >right now
are taking a BIG risk.

"Slow adoption of new COBOL standards' - no disagreement from me - 1985 to 2002,
speaks for itself. I don't think one can refer to it as 'an early adoption' -
'early extension', if you like.  Not knowing the 'history' I'm willing to bet
compiler vendors were the original motivators, because they had their user-base
asking them about nifty features appearing in Windows and other OO  languages.

"People naturally want to try out "what's new". Certainly an element of truth in
that.
But that's not the case in my situation. I was happily working in DOS mode with the
M/F Screen Section. Knowing that 'monster' Windows 3.1 (?) was lurking in the
background, I was emulating menu-bars, pop-up menus and list boxes - albeit a bit
primitive, but they worked. Then my end-user started searching the 'Net. Cut a long
story short, we discovered a beta version of VISOC (now revamped as Net Express -
plus they jacked up the price !). I had absolutely no idea what the 'OO jazz' was
about, didn't even know the meaning of the words. The reason for switching - this
new product offered 'Windowing' - GUIs.

Had I been aware and searched around the 'Net I could have used any non-OO COBOL
and the Flexus product SP2 to give me Windowing/GUIs - would never have touched OO.
Do I regret the move - No. I wouldn't switched back to non-OO. Whether others will
see the light, remains to be be seen. (And it isn't truly going to take off in
COBOL until there are working compilers available for mainframes). Bill is right to
question whether OO COBOL will ever be popularly adopted - chicken and egg, no
compiler, no users.

>  However, those who are using the OO COBOL than any vendor offers right
> now are taking a BIG risk.

I may be naive but I don't think there is the risk you suggest. Firstly the
Standard itself. J4 have been bending over backwards with new introductions or
changes to COBOL 2002 to ensure they are *backwards compatible* to COBOL '85. (I
don't know details - but '85 has ambiguities which can be read in different ways).
When it comes to vendors - same approach. They are in business to make a buck. That
example Level 78 I showed above. Even when COBOL 2002 introduces CONSTANTS be
assured Micro Focus will still allow me (for backwards compatibility) to use a
Level 78 - only snag with that is that you have lost portability to another
compiler.

As regards OO, a little more tricky. Micro Focus went to bat on a very early
version of the proposed Standard. Fujitsu and Hitachi picked up the baton but used
later (more definitive) versions of the standard. Similarly, as Thane mentioned,
Siemens are producing an OO compiler - and at this point in time Siemens is
probably 'closest' to what the Standard will be, (excluding OO
collections/dictionaries).

The 'guts' of OO is well defined, what classes and methods can do. In terms of
portable code the 'heading' of a Micro Focus source program looks different from
Fujitsu - the latter incorporate the later defined REPOSITORY Section. (Think in
terms of all those #include files that C or C++ source code has). So this will
present a problem, but with all the money already poured into development, be
assured each vendor will make it fit for backwards compatibility with existing
developer code.

An area which may present problems is collections/dictionaries. Again we have three
different versions, Fujitsu, Hitachi and Micro Focus, all supposedly based on
Smalltalk - hardly surprising, it has been around for 30 years and it works !
This may need some compiler vendor 'revamping' based on the final conclusions of
J4. But even that is not an insurmountable problem - given one vendor currently has
a method named "IwantToDoThis" and J4 decides there should be a method called
"ThisIsREALLYWhatIWantToDo" performing the same function - basically all our
intrepid vendor has to do for the interim :-

    *>---------------------------
    method-id. "IwantToDoThis"
    *>-------------------------
    Linkage section.
    01 ThisIsIn                            pic 9(03).
    01 ThisIsOut                         object reference.
    Procedure Division using ThisIsIn returning ThisIsOut.

        invoke self "ThisIsREALLYWhatIWantToDo"
            using ThisIsIn returning ThisIsOut

     *> and delete the code that appeared below for the original method

    End Method "IwantToDoThis".
    *>------------------------------

Presumably at some future date, (2, 3 years ?) the vendor drops all reference to
"IwantToDoThis" and  substitutes "ThisIsREALLYWhatIWantToDo".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T19:58:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0_dg7.45069$Lw3.2654892@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3B81607E.6AE36E6F@home.com>`

```
How many shops had some ANSI 68 CoBOL programs until Y2K got funding to
convert them?

I strongly suspect there are some large shops which had all of their
programs using a current compiler before the Y2K conversions - but this is
only a suspicion, they were outside of my experience as a contractor.

It is far more common to see the following code (and have programmers
understand it):

MOVE "GARBAGE" TO MY-FIELD
      PERFORM MY-PARAGRAPH
           UNTIL NEW-FIELD = MY-FIELD

than
PERFORM MY-PARAGRAPH WITH TEST AFTER
       UNTIL NEW-FIELD = MY-FIELD

Enterprise shops move slowly.   We get a new CoBOL standard - how long will
it take before half of its features become commonly used in most mainframe
shops?  (Don't even count OO features here - I don't know if they will EVER
be used in mainframe shops).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T15:52:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lrtc8$6a9$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3B813613.1F67F62B@ibm.net> <3B81607E.6AE36E6F@home.com> <0_dg7.45069$Lw3.2654892@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
When it comes to IBM mainframe shops, one (aka I) wonder of the '68 Standard
and IBM "old" extensions will EVER die (especially as IBM now DOCUMENTS that
they support such object code - with the LE run-time library).

I really wonder how many programs still have TRANSFORM and/or READY TRACE
statements in them?  (interestingly enough, Micro Focus accepts both of
these AS EXTENSIONS - even if running in ANS85 "mode".  One of my favorite
examples of "odd dialect combinations" when I worked for MF was showing a
TRANSFORM statement nested within an EVALUATE statement <G>)

As for that "yucky" 01-level COPY syntax that works with LANGLVL(1) - I
shudder to think how many CICS programs STILL use it.  (From what I hear,
worse under VSE than OS/390 - but certainly STILL present there.)
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM mainframe use of OO COBOL

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T17:04:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ECRg7.10788$2u.77189@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>, William M. Klein says...
>
There are a lot of smaller companies which for one reason or another have
elected to use an infromation system comprised of basically just flat files.
Mostly these organizations just can not afford to invest in a database, or thnk
they can not.  Often they are using a system built on legacy code and can not
afford the expense of redesigning their entire Information System to a newer OO
System.  For a smaller company it would take a Huge investment in time and money
to change to OO.  The cost has to be offset with some tangible benefit.  

Don't get me wrong, I just took a course in Java, and I understand some of the
benefits and uses of OO programming and have seen what a simple Queery is
capable of doing with a database.  However, I am not the one that would be
paying for such a change.  Many companies just do not want to fund such an
enterprise.  They think that there is no reason to fix something that works.  If
you already have hundreds of programs that do the job, why change?  After a
while we started writing programs more flexible and multipurpose using copybooks
etc.  In the past they considered buying an application package, but the cost
was in excess of one million, and it would have caused a loss of functionality
that we absolutely have to have.  We may end up spending that much money
eventually later on down the road, but to do so management has to feel the need
to pay that bill.

We are developing some internet applications but plan on running COBOL in batch
and just using CGI to send the info to the web page.  We have some programs that
do this now through an IP Server, but they don't hit the live data.  We just
build some files twice a day to use, so we are not hitting live data.  I think
this is kind of a flawed approach, that will end up being scrubbed if we do go
to a Database System, but we don't have any real IT leadership.

To compensate we also have an application called Decision Analyzer that lets us
run different reports on multiple files, and produce fully funtional
spreadsheets and databases, that can be downloaded.  I often use the analyzer
instead of writing programs.

>As a follow-on to earlier threads, I thought that I would add this
>information concerning the use of OO COBOL in IBM mainframe shops (OS/390
…[48 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframe use of OO COBOL

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T18:26:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m0tet$ghi$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <ECRg7.10788$2u.77189@www.newsranger.com>`

```
I think that the way mainframes move to OO won't by encapsulating data with
the code - it will be by encapsulating business rules with the data.

The decision enterprises will make will be to move to a database that
happens to support OO well.  They will then use the tools that work with the
database.

Which leads me to suspect that the way to get OO CoBOL into the mainframe,
is to get OO CoBOL integrated with the database.  Provide Oracle and Sybase
and whomever (PeopleSoft and JD Edwards), with the features THEY want, and
make it easy for them to include the right kind of objects, & interfaces and
make it easy for them to add your compiler to what they make available to
their customers.

Maybe compilers makers shouldn't listen to us as #1, but instead listen to
the databae companies, as our managers will listen to them as well.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframe use of OO COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

Charles wrote:

> In article <9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>, William M. Klein says...
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Charles,

I understand and sympathize with what you have written. There is no way either big
mainframers or modest sized companies are going to take a BIG BANG approach and
switch everything over to OO - the cost would be astronomical. Given that mainframe
compilers with OO do become available, :-

(a) First hump, get over the initial cost of the new compiler - a hard selling job
for EDP folks, particularly if the organization is in any way cash strapped.

 (b) Take a small and new application to try it on.

Even at the current time, given the Three Tier System approach :-

    - Business Logic - e.g. Edit Customer File
    - UI(User Interface) -screens if you are using them
    - DBI(DataBase Interface) - either COBOL files or a DB

it is still possible to gradually move into something more effective, even without
OO.
Take the DBI - yes I do it in a particular way in OO COBOL but it can be paralleled
in non-OO. Approx. a year ago there were two contributors who said they were doing
this.

Very briefly, and I don't want to get into whether it is a sub-program, nested,
called or whatever (currently being hotly debated here <G>), but the following is
achievable both OO and non-OO. Bear with me, I'm so far removed from conventional
style programming because I code solely in OO classes, but essentially you need to
do the following to mimic what I do in OO ::-

(a) To have a 'File Program'' as a TSR, i.e. 'memory resident' while in use - in
other words you have got to avoid EXIT PROGRAM, (which will CLOSE the file before
you really want it to be closed), as a result of a call
(b) Have access to the file-status for this file
(c) Pass it the full pathname of the file you are using
(d) And to mimic OO methods use an entry-point for each file VERB
(e) your file record format is a copyfile used in both Business Logic and the DBI
'File Program'.

Unfortunately it would take too long to spell out the detail here.

Given that you resolve (a) through (e) you have a working 'File Program'. Firstly of
course you test the following against a dummy program. Gently taking ONE file at a
time, you now search for those programs that use this file. Hopefully your source is
decently structured and you have specific PARAGRAPHS that you PERFORM for file
accesses. Don't delete the paragraphs but remove the READS/WRITES/REWRITES etc.
substituting 'Call Programfile 'entrypoint' using AAAA returning BBBB (see I don't
even know the syntax). 'Entry Point' is a separate piece of code for each COBOL file
VERB. Using OO syntax it would look as follows :-

*>----------------------
method-id. "readRecord".
*>----------------------
Linkage seciton.
01 lnk-InPrimeKey                pic x(06).
01 lnk-values.
copy "results.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
copy "customerRec.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==lnk==.
Procedure Division using lnk-InPrimeKey returning lnk-values..

    move lnk-InPrimeKey to Customer-PrimeKey
    Read CustomerFile
        key is Customer-PrimeKey
            invalid key
                 set RecNotFound to true ( in lnk-result copyfile)
            not invalid key
                move Customer-record (in Global W/S as a copyfile)
                to  lnk-Cusrecord
    End-Read

You can even use the same technique to access SEPARATE Table Programs which
are the only ones containing SQL code/logic. I mention this because your comment
above seemed to imply SQL was tied into OO - it is just as legit in non-OO.
For SQL, the above method  becomes, (abbreviated) :-

    SELECT DISTINCT WHERE CusPrimeKey = :host-CusPrimeKey etc...

and with the addition of a copy file for host variables, you can still use the
copyfiles above to return to your Business Logic.


The above models would dove-tail into OO, without major change,  if you ever
switched from non-OO.

> Don't get me wrong, I just took a course in Java, and I understand some of the
> benefits and uses of OO programming and have seen what a simple Queery is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> etc.

A-M-E-N to copybooks ! - see my use above.

>  In the past they considered buying an application package, but the cost
> was in excess of one million, and it would have caused a loss of functionality
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Leadership. If you have people that are gong to pee their pants when asked to make a
decision, or don't think towards the future, you are on a hiding to nothing. One of
the most delightful men I ever worked for didn't use two words in the English
language - 'Yes' or 'No' - he was as frustrating as hell to work for ! (You'll
appreciate computer ones and noughts would be an absolute mystery for him <G>).

>
> To compensate we also have an application called Decision Analyzer that lets us
> run different reports on multiple files, and produce fully funtional
> spreadsheets and databases, that can be downloaded.  I often use the analyzer
> instead of writing programs.

Even given OO it would still make sense to use your Decision Analyzer - you don't
have to re-invent the wheel for reporting.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframe use of OO COBOL

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T21:11:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m173e$nkg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <ECRg7.10788$2u.77189@www.newsranger.com> <3B84196E.B5771987@home.com>`

```

On 22-Aug-2001, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

> Given that you resolve (a) through (e) you have a working 'File Program'.
> Firstly of
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL file VERB. Using OO syntax it would look as follows :-

Interesting.  The most frustrating environment I have worked in is where I/O
was done through called programs.  Sometimes these were layers deep.

I think I have shared one problem - with a called program that accessed a
KSDS file, and passed back a status of "7" if the VSAM status code was not
"00".   Well, a VSAM status code of "97" is a successful return code for an
open.   We never did fix the called program to accept this code, because
sooo many programs used it, that the testing would have been more costly
than the problem.   (I still have no idea how OO users get the users to sign
off on a change, which affects hundreds of complex programs).

I/O is simple.   Moving I/O out of a program obfuscates problems.  The same
environment called a program to do an abort.  Of course, we get a nice dump
OF THE CALLED PROGRAM!!!!.   More obfuscation.


It could be that we are simply using the dark side of OO, and missing the
good side.   But you seem to be advocating just what we paid for.  I hope
someday to see the advantage of living with these headaches.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM mainframe use of OO COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T04:00:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B84807E.81FB9541@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9lp87k$ag8$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <ECRg7.10788$2u.77189@www.newsranger.com> <3B84196E.B5771987@home.com> <9m173e$nkg$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> We never did fix the called program to accept this code, because
> sooo many programs used it, that the testing would have been more costly
> than the problem.   (I still have no idea how OO users get the users to sign
> off on a change, which affects hundreds of complex programs).
>

Ooh you're a hard nut to crack - but you're an OK guy <G>. Rather than pose the
question in the abstract, perhaps you should provide me with a coded problem,
then ask , "So how would you handle this one smart ass ?". Can't anticipate I
would have an answer, but might be worth a try if you can think of something
concrete.

Remember that nightmarish problem Eileen threw at us testing all those gawdawful
codes relating to customers ? For starters, let's assume that particular piece
of logic was required by TEN programs and not just  the one she was referring
to.

One solution might be  -Hey ! I'm not advocating you do it this way, just an
example :-
*>-------------------------
Method-id. "SeeIfTheyQualify".
*>----------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-AccountCode                        pic x(02).
Linkage section.
01 Lnk-CustomerNumber        pic x(12).
01 Lnk-DiscountRate             pic (02)v9(01).
Procedure Division using Lnk-CustomerNumber
                              returning lnk-DiscountRate.

move zeroes to Lnk-DiscountRate
invoke os-CustomerFile "getAccountCode" using lnk-CustomerNumber
                                                                   returning
ls-AccountCode

Evaluate true
    when ls-AccountCode = spaces continue
    when ls-AccountCode = "BR" or "CR" or "FH" etc..
..                move 20 to lnk-DiscountRate
    when ls-AccountCode = "AB" or "DR" or "GH" etc....
                 move 15 to lnk-DiscountRate
    when ls-AccountCode = "XY" or "LK" or "PF" etc.....
                move etc...
    when etc.......
End-evaluate

End Method  "SeeIfTheyQualify".
*>------------------------------

It's a bit crappy, but you can see what it is doing. Test that against one
program and it works for the nine others. The worst that can go wrong is some
idiot will forget to either supply the linkage input or output, and that
(certainly in Net Express), shows up as invalid (Unassigned) linkage - the very
first time you test.

So your argument would be that we have to test all 10 programs - perfectly true
when you initially write them, and you are going to do a comprehensive test, not
just this one method from a Class out there in space.

We all know that marketing/sales people are notorious for wanting to change the
rules on us, and they want the change not now, but yesterday ! However, they are
selling product and they will use every damned lever they can get their hands on
to increase sales. Whether we like it or not, their need is genuine.

Our marketing guys do a deal with Bozo Investments - we are going to give them
.21 percent. Two possibilities, they might be the only ones with the AccountCode
"PF", so we could move that to another line. Probably might make sense if we
introduce a new category - let's call it "ZX". We edit their CustomerRecord from
whatever to reflect an AccountCode = "ZX" - that's a file change not a program
change. Our change to the method is as follows :-

*>-------------------------
Method-id. "SeeIfTheyQualify".
*>----------------------------
Linkage section.
01 Lnk-CustomerNumber        pic x(12).
01 LnkDiscountRate             pic (02)v9(01).
Procedure Division using Lnk-CustomerNumber
                              returning lnk-DiscountRate.

move zeroes to Lnk-DiscountRate
invoke os-CustomerFile "getAccountCode" using lnk-CustomerNumber
                                                                   returning
ls-AccountCode

Evaluate true
    when ls-AccountCode = spaces continue
    when ls-AccountCode = "ZX"
                   move 21 to lnk-DiscountRate
    when ls-AccountCode = "BR" or "CR" or "FH" etc..
..                etc.......
End-evaluate

End Method  "SeeIfTheyQualify".
*>------------------------------

Now we have got to test - but I contend, only ONE of our ten programs. If it
works for the one, logic deems it must work for the other nine. Perhaps part of
the problem of trying to see the wood for the tress is to think of methods as
being discrete bits of code. If you are envisioning turning a method into an
essay with three pages of code, then you are missing the point of methods.
(Certainly if your method DID contain three pages of code - watch out - you
probably are headed for trouble !).

There's nothing innovative about the above method, it just performs one simple
function, (note that I haven't suggested inclusion of the actual discount
calculation - that gets done in another method). You can easily replicate the
above with a PERFORM PARAGRAPH in non-OO. One option would be to code the
paragraph as a copyfile or maybe you do a call to a 'sub/nested/etc.....'
Certainly for your ten non-OO programs it would seem to me it is an absolute
MUST to test all ten. Unless you can throw a spanner/wrench in the works - I
just don't see the above as a problem in OO.

 You may recall a long while back I advocated for medium to large installations,
a DBA/OO Administrator who would sanction in-house libraries of classes, (i.e.,
generally used utilities as opposed to every single program/class written), and
a method such as the above would appear in a list :-
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------
Class-name : XYZ - Performs various checks on different flags in CustomerRecords

List of methods :-
--------------------------------------
Method-name : SeeIfTheyQualify
Description :    Checks AccountCode in CustomerRecord to see if this
                         customer qualifies for a special discount. Returns the
discount
                         percentage if valid; if invalid returns zero discount..

Parameters    :    using lnk-CustomerNumber pic x(12)
                          returning lnk-DiscountRate  pic 9(02)v9(01).
----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

>
> I/O is simple.   Moving I/O out of a program obfuscates problems.  The same
> environment called a program to do an abort.  Of course, we get a nice dump
> OF THE CALLED PROGRAM!!!!.   More obfuscation.
>

Yes I/O is simple - but I recall how many times I groaned when I remembered that
I now had to include File X, go back and include the FD copyfiles etc. Then of
course I set up the paragraphs to Open/Read/Write/Close etc. Apart from typos
this also represents a glorious opportunity to cock things up ! And don't forget
all the testing on the paragraphs relating to file handling.

Get that I/O into a separate program/class - prove it works - and first time you
have a problem,  "No senor. It is not my FileClass/Program - it's your bloody
program's business logic. If you are telling me you got crap from my FileClass -
GIGO !"

Remember, Pete makes exactly the same arguments in favour of components.

>
> It could be that we are simply using the dark side of OO, and missing the
> good side.   But you seem to be advocating just what we paid for.  I hope
> someday to see the advantage of living with these headaches.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```

On 22-Aug-2001, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

> Now we have got to test - but I contend, only ONE of our ten programs. If
> it
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> probably are headed for trouble !).

It may be that our testing standards would move this way.  But I can say now
that it will be fought, and that as soon as someone messes up and gets a bug
into production without having all customers sign off, management will
demand that all users do acceptance testing.

Admittedly, this currently is the hardest part of programming - getting a
user to acceptance test something to make sure it works for him exactly the
way it worked before.   Especially if what is being tested has lots of
options and twists.  (And persuading the user to come up with different
tests than what we created for our unit and system testing).

Maybe we do this type of testing because we can.  Certainly we can't and
don't do exaustive testing when we make a database change and have to
recompile 100 programs and dialogs that use that changed record.

A lot of this is persuading management how much user acceptance testing is
adequate.
```

### PowerCobol(Fujitsu) file question.

- **From:** "David E. Edwards" <dedwards@forje.btinternet.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T23:22:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lpf0o$iu8$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
In PowerCobol, I want to be able to check to see if a file exists.  If it
doesn't, then I want to create it at the same time, I want to send a message
to the user.

Cannot find any way to be able to check file status on an open - tried
FILE-STATUS in the select and also the use of DECLARATIVES (on this one I
got a message saying DECLARATIVES were not supported).

Checking for the existence of a file is important for the app I'm writing,
but I cannot find anything in the docs. that can guide me here.

Hopefully, there is a way to do this.  Can someone help.

Thanks
Dave E.
```

#### ↳ Re: PowerCobol(Fujitsu) file question.

- **From:** Thane Hubbell <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-19T23:16:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SMXf7.7329$2u.55788@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<9lpf0o$iu8$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
Actually this is easy to do using standard ANSI 85 COBOL.  Simply use OPTIONAL
in the assign.  When the file is opened for I/O if it doesn't exist and the file
is created the file status of the open will be "05", and the file will be
created.

In article <9lpf0o$iu8$1@uranium.btinternet.com>, David E. Edwards says...
>
>In PowerCobol, I want to be able to check to see if a file exists.  If it
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: PowerCobol(Fujitsu) file question.

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T01:36:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GQZf7.56241$VV1.3768676@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<9lpf0o$iu8$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
There are at least two ways that come to mind:

TECHNIQUE #1 - OPEN INPUT and check the file status values. If the
file does not exist, you'll get an appropriate file-status value
You'll ALSO get run time errors. To avoid the run time errors, you'll
need a DECLARATIVES section. In spite of what the compiler says,
DECLARATIVES do work. Here's how we do it:

  A. We use a copy book, viz:
     PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     COPY 'C:\CB\DECLAR.COB'.
     ...
     OPEN INPUT MYFILE.
     IF MYFILE-FS NOT = '00'
        OPEN OUTPUT MYFILE.

The DECLAR.COB above MUST be accessed via a COPY command. Do not use
#INCLUDE.

   B. 'DECLAR.COB' looks like:
       DECLARATIVES.
       DECLAR-INPUT SECTION.
           USE AFTER ERROR PROCEDURE ON INPUT CONTINUE.
       DECLAR-OUTPUT SECTION.
           USE AFTER ERROR PROCEDURE ON OUTPUT CONTINUE.
       DECLAR-IO SECTION.
           USE AFTER ERROR PROCEDURE ON I-O CONTINUE.
       DECLAR-EXTEND SECTION.
           USE AFTER ERROR PROCEDURE ON EXTEND CONTINUE.
       END DECLARATIVES.
       PROCEDUR SECTION.

Notice the code in the DECLARATIVES section does absolutely nothing.
We just need the presence of the section to avoid the run time errors.


TECHNIQUE #2 - Use

   CALL 'CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST2' USING
     filename
     filedetails
     RETURNING status-code

This method has the advantage of providing file size, create date, and
create time. "Filename" is the external name of the file (... ASSIGN
TO filename) and is delimited by low-values. DO NOT use the
corresponding CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST as it fails on imbedded spaces in
the path.




"David E. Edwards" <dedwards@forje.btinternet.co.uk> wrote in message
news:9lpf0o$iu8$1@uranium.btinternet.com...

If it
> doesn't, then I want to create it at the same time, I want to send a
message
> to the user.

> Cannot find any way to be able to check file status on an open -
tried
> FILE-STATUS in the select and also the use of DECLARATIVES (on this
one I
> got a message saying DECLARATIVES were not supported).
>
> Checking for the existence of a file is important for the app I'm
writing,
> but I cannot find anything in the docs. that can guide me here.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

- **From:** Carlos Lages <clages@attglobal.net>

- **Message-ID:** `<3B80F068.9DE3ED59@attglobal.net>`

"David E. Edwards"


use this, its works

begin.
    Open I-o  file
    if   wsg-status  = "35"
         open  output  file
         go    begin
    end-if
    if wsg-status  not = "00"
       send a Warning
    end-if

ps.  wsg-status  is pic x(02).

Carlos Lages

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%l_f7.56598$VV1.3789890@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

"TOKYO (Reuters) - Japanese giant chipmaker Fujitsu Ltd. will slash
its worldwide workforce by 15,000, or nearly 10 percent, and close
some overseas plants in response to the high-tech slump, a newspaper
said on Sunday."

But it's not all bad:

"Fujitsu plans to scale down its hardware operations and concentrate
on the software and services divisions with high growth potential to
improve profitability, they said."

http://dailynews.yahoo.com/htx/nm/20010819/bs/fujitsu_jobs_dc_3.html

### Closing Fujitsu character based apps

- **From:** "Alister Munro" <alister@specsoft.freeserve.co.uk>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lq5d1$ot2$1@newsg2.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
Taken from the Fujitsu Cobol softcopy docs...

    'When you click on the Close button of a command prompt
    window, the COBOL application is closed by force, and cannot
    recover system resources or perform "close" processing on open
    files.'

    'When you click on the Close button of the system console
    window, the COBOL application is closed by force, and cannot
    recover system resources or perform "close" processing on open files.'

Does anyone have a solution for capturing the 'close' command
so that it may be handled by code. At worst I would find it preferable
to disable the close button.

Regards

Alister

## Thread: Using Micro Focus Object Cobol for Unix — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Using Micro Focus Object Cobol for Unix

- **From:** adamec.wolfgang@utanet.at (WolfgangA)
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T05:14:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e63a504c.0108200414.6dda7fab@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello, can anyone help me with the following problem:
I've installed Micro Focus Object Cobol - 30 days test version - for
Unix 4.1.30 on a machine. After configuration I started the kde,
opened a text console and typed "tbox" and it worked!! But when I 
didn't use the graphical mode, just typed "tbox" at login I got the
error message "/temp/rts32: error while loading shared libraries:
liboops.so: cannot load shared object file: No such file or
directory".
Does anyone know a solution for this?
Thank you
Wolfgang
```

#### ↳ Re: Using Micro Focus Object Cobol for Unix

- **From:** "Wiggy" <simon.tobias@MERANT.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-20T17:12:17+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9lrdro$g3r$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<e63a504c.0108200414.6dda7fab@posting.google.com>`

```

"WolfgangA" <adamec.wolfgang@utanet.at> wrote in message
news:e63a504c.0108200414.6dda7fab@posting.google.com...
> Hello, can anyone help me with the following problem:
> I've installed Micro Focus Object Cobol - 30 days test version - for
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Does anyone know a solution for this?

Hi Wolfgang. It sounds like your environment may not be set up
correctly. The setting of LD_LIBRARY_PATH needs to include
$COBDIR/coblib for Object COBOL V4.x.

Simon.
```

<a id="t-7e08aac848"></a>
## Thread: Cobol testing methodologies — 11 this month / 14 total

### Cobol testing methodologies

- **From:** chims1@aol.com (ChimS1)
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T01:01:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010820210150.13041.00000679@mb-dd.aol.com>`

```
In many mainframe shops that I've been working for
in the last 20 years, I have found that there exist
various ways to do testing on Cobol programs.
Different people use different theories to sort out
inconsistency that may not apply to a certain
business situation. And those who do the testing
may not be the ones who will make the move of
program changes to the final target of the production
environment. Moreover, testing methodologies are
very different from the administrative techniques of
program migration. They tend to be more iterative and
time consuming while change management is simply
straightforward and standardized.

Many Cobol testing can involve several systems in
order to make certain that a particular modification
will make a right business impact as it is being
assumed before hand. Within each system, there
may be many Cobol sources that need to be written
or modified in order to apply the new business
requirements. Therefore, implementation of new
business models would dictate different approach
to the testing strategies.

Unit testing is almost straightfoward and self-evident.
At this level of exercise, newly applied logics are being
executed to see how the new concept will produce in
terms of outputs and overall business ramifications.
Once newly coded logical routines have been validated
within a certain Cobol program, the next level of testing
should be submitted and explored.

System testing expands the testing discipline of the
involved Cobol object module beyond its own immediate
impact of the program and onto other business systems
and the overall performance of the new inserted codes.
This kind of testing may require actual production inputs
in order to verify the robustness of the change and the
inserted codes. It may also require to have those load
modules moved, copied, or re-linked to a different
environment in order to insure the completeness of the
testing methodology. This kind of creative response to
the challenge of a good testing situation will also allow
the testers to introduce various business parameters as
conditions to act as testing criteria.

A lot of system testing can also be done in conjunction
with the user. By comparing test results from different
approaches of the testing situation, one can have a
better idea on how changes to a particular group of
Cobol programs can have impact or will react and
behave under real conditions of the business realities.

Cobol semantics can closely mimic the business reasoning
of the human mind. Yet, it has the power and precision as
much as other programming languages that are currently
available. By varying the testing methodologies, from one
testing environment into another, and by comparing the
testing results from various setting of the different locale
of the testing methodologies, we can rest assured that our 
applied changes to the different Cobol programs within
a certain business system have met the requirements of
the new business conditions.


Chim Sotuon
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol testing methodologies

- **From:** Charles <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T19:11:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xnyg7.9469$2u.69440@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<20010820210150.13041.00000679@mb-dd.aol.com>`

```
In article <20010820210150.13041.00000679@mb-dd.aol.com>, ChimS1 says...
>
In rule-based software design, the object is to make a list or several sub lists
of the business rules a program is designed to accomplish and then figure every
possible occurrance of the  rulle.  Using this method one could forseeabley come
up with a set of data to test every known possiblity. This could be very time
consuming. 

Ideally it would be great if you had enough stroage for a test area to back up
all the files to where you can test the program with real data.  Sometimes it is
hard to tell what will happen by some small change.  I run programs that
eventually produce Financial Aid Checks for a College, and the slightest change
can affect some student not receiving their Pell Check.  Every year someone at
the state and federal level decides to change the format of the data we download
from the Feds, and every year we have to process the file in a different manner
to get the information we need, and send them back the information they need.

The problem is sometimes you have to run against live data to test something.
We use plenty of tape backups, even when the job runs right.  We also use test
and production runs of some of the programs, so we can see if the program will
working before we start updating files.  We run the program in test mode, back
up the files, and then run in update mode.  There is always the threat of some
corrupt data lurking out there to get you.  This will not always work, so
sometimes we just back up the files and run the programs live.

>in the last 20 years, I have found that there exist

> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> consuming.

Well this is one topic you could go on about ad nauseum. There are many techniques
that you can employ but one important feature, given a reasonable sized group of
programmers - like 'Mission Impossible' have a self-destruct feature. When you are
complacent that it is ready to release, hand the application over to some really
mean minded people - their task, to try and do everything possible to enter data
that is totally incorrect, read files and print reports from things that don't exist
etc. etc.
They need direct access to your database so that they can screw-up existing records
and watch your beautiful application go phhffffut !.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

Dear James,

Testing is a discipline as much as a business situation. There exists a lot of
challenge in reconciling our codes to the business requirements and human
expectation. And so are the rewards. Just like the marketing of any product,
human satisfaction should be part of our testing strategy. Start small and
increase the level of complexity of the testing methodologies. Good luck!

Regards,
Chim

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol testing methodologies

- **From:** manojb@mailcity.com (Manoj)
- **Date:** 2001-08-26T13:52:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ec0f6848.0108261252.364bea17@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<Xnyg7.9469$2u.69440@www.newsranger.com> <20010825095318.21506.00004401@mb-mr.aol.com>`

```
I am a system tester for an application in a COBOL/DB2 environment.  I
do not generally get to see the actual changes that have been made to
the COBOL programs.  I usually have a requirements document to work
from, form which the developers are supposed to prepare their design
and eventually, the code.
    I normally use actual production data for regression tests and to
test actual programs.  I am not concerned with the actual program
logic, but only with the system inputs and outputs (in effect, a black
box testing method) and sometimes with the system performance.  But
sometimes when an application change may involve data that is not yet
in production.  The required data would be available in production
only when the application change goes in.  In these cases, I would
have to manufacture test data.  Not a fool-proof operation, I must
say.  Usually trial and error.
    As in post #3 above I have experienced a certain level of
animosity (including a public harangue once) from developers when
defects are found in the system functionality.  I have now learned to
accept this as part of my job.

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```


Manoj wrote:

>     As in post #3 above I have experienced a certain level of
> animosity (including a public harangue once) from developers when
> defects are found in the system functionality.  I have now learned to
> accept this as part of my job.

I'm not surprised. But your particular job is EXTREMELY IMPORTANT to the overall DP function.
Fortunately, you realize it and anticipate the brickbats. Depends upon your management structure,
but if they (coders) are continuously whining at you, seems an opportunity for the Chief
Programmer (or whatever title is used), on one of those monthly meetings, to spell out the
importance of your department's function.

Ironically the tables get turned - if Operations find an ambiguity in run instructions the Ops
Manager is going to complain bitterly to the Chief Programmer. This is one where the programming
team has to eat humble pie and go along with the Ops Manager's comments and resolve the problem.

See if we can get her to comment. Eileen, does your outfit have a 'Test Team' and how effective is
it ? Seems to me, your 3 a.m. ventures into live run support could be reduced if the 'Test Team'
is an effective element of your organization.

Jimmy
```

A testing method story, old, but perhaps interesting.

We had (have) internal auditors who were continually looking for methods to
check the application to the rules.

During a strike when plants were closed, they created a fictitious plant,
and proceeded to use it to verify each change to the system involved.
(Payroll).  There had been an error in incentive calculation over a period
of years that ran up a large bill to fix.

Warren

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol testing methodologies

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T09:45:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mlg4c$9to$1@allday.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<Xnyg7.9469$2u.69440@www.newsranger.com> <20010825095318.21506.00004401@mb-mr.aol.com>`

```
Chim,
Sounds like you could use a regression testing tool.
One I have worked with is the Progeni product called 3Rs (www.progeni.com).

Using a fixed test database you supply, this system captures screen and
report output and moves it to a PC in a special format.
You make your changes.
You capture the output again.
3Rs compairs the two versions and lets you know what changed.  It's pretty
smart about this - for example, if the change you are making is to increase
the size of the Account number field, which moves all the lines on a report
over 2 characters, 3Rs won't tell you that every line on the report is
wrong.
However - to use an example from another post here - it would tell you if an
entire Pell check were missing or the wrong amount.

One of the neat things about 3Rs is that it works across platforms.  It was
initially designed to test platform migrations.  Those captured output files
are stored in a common format, no matter what platform they were produced on
(CICS, Unisys, Microfocus on Unix or Windows, etc.) and then they are moved
and examined on a PC.  So if you capture a system under CICS, migrate to
UNIX, then capture the new system using the same test data - 3Rs will test
the migration.
As you can imagine, the tool received lots of use during Y2K work since it
can detect and not falsely report inteneded changes like date field
expansion while catching uninteneded errors.

If you want more info on 3Rs contact rtk@nospam.progeni.com (remove the
nospam. of course).

#### ↳ Re: Cobol testing methodologies

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T11:42:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108291042.6e498623@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<20010820210150.13041.00000679@mb-dd.aol.com>`

```
In my experience, one of the most effective "testing" techniques is
rarely used.  This is the "Structured Walkthru".  Because it takes 3 -
5 programmers a couple of hours or more, most managers insist there is
not enough time for this "luxury".  In my experience, and the
experience of everyone I know who uses them, they save MUCH MORE
physical testing time than they use.
In one shop, I was responsible for the key control program for the
entire system.  The COBOL was pretty straightforward, but the CICS
used unusual system services.  This manager not only thought a
Structured Walkthru was a good idea, he suggested opening it to
whoever wanted to attend, so they could see how it was done.  Over 20
people attended, I got some excellent feedback, and the  program was
stable when installed.  The only problems encountered in physical
testing involved interactions with the operating system and CICS
system parameters which could not have been detected in the walkthru.

Structured Walkthrus also promote an environment of "Egoless
Programming", in which the QA people are seen as part of the team, not
"outside troublemakers".
IT IS EXTREMELY IMPORTANT that structured walkthrus be "error
detection processes".  I was very unhappily surprised on one
assignment when I was called in by the manager and told that the
structured walkthru had indicated a large number of problems -- HE
used structured walkthru's as  performance reviews!

Stephen J Spiro
President, Wizard Systems

a COBOL program and vice versa?

TIA

Max

#### ↳ Re: COBOL calling JAVA classes

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>

```
You didn't mention what compiler you use.

If it is Net Express, take a look at the "Distributed Computing" on-line
book and you will see a number of ways. One not included is by using JNI
(Java Native Interface - more details for that in www.javasoft.com) but it
is the most tricky to implement.

You will need Net Express 3.1 with the latest updates from Micro Focus's
site.

regards

Panos

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL calling JAVA classes

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T10:48:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ltvv2$f4r$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B820B51.BD5AC036@generali.it> <9ltbv8$s4l$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <3B824238.8D5A9F4F@generali.it>`

```
as indicated in other notes, I don't think you will have any problems with
Server Express and Java.

HOWEVER,  I believe that IBM has indicated that ILC (Inter Language
Communication) between COBOL and JAVA is in their "future plans" - not
available yet.

Furthermore, I hope you are aware that the COBOL OO syntax in the two
compilers (Micro Focus/MERANT vs IBM) are NOT identical - and neither
"quite" matches what is in the draft ISO Standard.
```

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
