- **Message-ID:** `<9ltlia$6kn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3B820B51.BD5AC036@generali.it> <9ltbv8$s4l$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`

```

On 21-Aug-2001, "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr> wrote:

> You didn't mention what compiler you use.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> is the most tricky to implement.

I would think that in today's world the #1 requirement of an OO CoBOL
implementation on the PC was to easily interface with Java classes and
beans.

Or am I missing the point?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL calling JAVA classes

- **From:** "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T16:44:25+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ltoiu$4cq$1@usenet.otenet.gr>`
- **References:** `<3B820B51.BD5AC036@generali.it> <9ltbv8$s4l$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <9ltlia$6kn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Not at all.

Net Express (and Server Express 2.0.10 and newer) also include much easier
ways to access Java from Cobol (and vv) described in their products'
documentation. I mentioned JNI (which is a Java standard - not OO Cobol)
because it is not included in the documentation and sun itself states that
it is an api to be used by experienced programmers!

regards

panos

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:9ltlia$6kn$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 21-Aug-2001, "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > (Java Native Interface - more details for that in www.javasoft.com) but
it
> > is the most tricky to implement.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or am I missing the point?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL calling JAVA classes

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T16:41:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B829003.D4D3C1B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B820B51.BD5AC036@generali.it> <9ltbv8$s4l$1@usenet.otenet.gr> <9ltlia$6kn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```


Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 21-Aug-2001, "Panos Zotos" <pzotos@syntax.gr> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or am I missing the point?

Really depends on what you want to do. Certainly there is justification for a
tie-in to Java. But reflect, PC-wise there are many stand alone applications
that don't need to get over sophisticated. (Dare I mention him - Tony Dilworth
and his POS, plus others with POS systems that contribute here). There are still
a vast number of small businesses that need 'automation' and the
owner/proprietor just doesn't have the time to become a technical whiz kid; nor
may he be interested in going nuts surfing the Net.

They want stand alone applications that do the basics for them, bookkeeping, run
a cash register and financial statements. They couldn't give a damn about JIT
inventory or forecasting and would be overwhelmed by the boxes of stationery
consumed in such reports.

Yes OO can be very effective for them as well. And just as Joe pointed out, tie
it into SQL.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL calling JAVA classes

- **From:** bsulliv@legacyj.com (Brian Sullivan)
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T20:27:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f8041505.0108221927.1de377f5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3B820B51.BD5AC036@generali.it>`

```
Max <petteno@generali.it> wrote in message news:<3B820B51.BD5AC036@generali.it>...
> Hi listers,
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Max

Hello,

I'm biased (working for the company), but I do have to note that in
PERCobol from LegacyJ, all Cobol programs and classes are
automatically Java classes, with direct and easy integration between
the two. Just instantiate and invoke in either direction.  PERCobol
also directly supports servlets.

We include these samples of servlets, calling Cobol from Java, and
calling Java from Cobol (either INVOKE or CALL) with the product. 
(See directories under samples 'callcobol', 'calljava' and 'getset'
for simple access, 'oo' for more complex examples, and 'servlet' for
servlets.)  Our documentation is online for more info.

http://www.legacyj.com

Regards,
Brian Sullivan
```

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T11:58:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_998395046@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

<a id="t-b9f234c948"></a>
## Thread: erp evaluation — 2 msgs

### erp evaluation

- **From:** Daniel Braunstein <dbraunstein@mediaone.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T01:45:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B830FCA.AD350E1B@mediaone.net>`

```
I am currently evaluating erp systems for my company. Two in particular
stand out as a good fit for our organization of 30 employees. These are
Made2Manage and Syspro Impact. Syspro, as many of you may know is written in
Cobol, while Made2Manage is largely in C++ and FoxPro. Both will do the job,
both look good, both use MS SQL Server as backbone, both are approximately
the same cost.

While Made2Manage may be customized with VB / VBA, Syspro is cusomized with
Cobol using Merant's NetWare.

Would anyone like to share their thoughts on the merits of either, given
this (extremely) limited glimpse.

Thanks,

Danny.
```

#### ↳ Re: erp evaluation

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T11:10:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZqMg7.276$tY6.12034@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3B830FCA.AD350E1B@mediaone.net>`

```
The *only* considerations vis-a-vis the source language(s) of an ERP system
are:

1. Are there differences in one-time/annual license fees for run-time
support / required compilers or interpreters for selecting erp system brand
"A" written in language "A" versus system brand "B" written in language "B?"

2. Will there exist people to maintain the software?

For item one, you proably already have the numbers; they are probably buried
in a vendor proposal, but it's not worth the effort to break them out.
(Because you don't purchase the run-time support without purchasing the
basic system).

For item two, you ask around.

In your case, there are many qualified people available for C++, FoxPro and
COBOL. If the maintenance skills you need must come from existing staff,
that complicates this a bit; but in the abstract your two systems are, in my
view anyway, dead-nuts even on 'maintainability.'

Don't get hung up on 'source language' except for the above.

MCM


Daniel Braunstein <dbraunstein@mediaone.net> wrote in message
news:3B830FCA.AD350E1B@mediaone.net...
> I am currently evaluating erp systems for my company. Two in particular
> stand out as a good fit for our organization of 30 employees. These are

in

job,

with

>

## Thread: Fujitsu printing to HP laserjet — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu printing to HP laserjet

- **From:** J M Pittman <jmpittmanii@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-21T21:15:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8331E0.2FF9EDD0@mindspring.com>`

```
Our organization is converting from Unisys mainframe COBOL to a canned
package running on a server.  Needless to say the reports available are
never just what is wanted, so, as a test I have attempted to convert one
of our old report applications to the free Fujitsu cobol compiler.  My
problem comes in attempting to print on a HP4050, specifically in
adjusting the vertical line spacing.  On a line printer you can start at
the top of a page and either print at 6 or 8 LPI (or use channels with a
tape).  I found a routine at the Fujitsu site for converting the printer
to landscape that involved writing a control record.  There wasn't much
explanation of the elements in the record.  Can someone explain this
control record? Or post an example of how to program HP line spacing in
the free Fujitsu COBOL version.  I would appreciate any assistance as
trial and error is quite a trial.
```

- **From:** Jason Adams <jsa@kona.homeip.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<to99dl2b24jj7c@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8331E0.2FF9EDD0@mindspring.com>`

```
J M Pittman wrote:

> Our organization is converting from Unisys mainframe COBOL to a canned
> package running on a server.  Needless to say the reports available are
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

With hpcl control characters at the head of the report you can
set the spacing to anything you want, in 72th inch incriments
I believe.=, or maby its 144ths of an inch, i forget.

Peek into the hpcl language specs (escape sequences).
Channel with a tape??   Egads man, you go back some.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: fujitsu cobol

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T21:24:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8qVg7.63583$Lw3.3582557@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<FYKg7.342$5_3.24559@news1.cableinet.net> <VbPg7.77328$VV1.5580996@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <LFQg7.5023$5_3.268345@news1.cableinet.net>`

```
Well, there may not be as many jobs as one might think. You've heard
of "Write-once, Run-anywhere?"  Well, COBOL is "Write-once,
Run-forever," so there's not as much maintenance as with languages of
lesser light.

COBOL is used, in the main, by really big enterprises (the government,
Royal Dutch Shell, American Express, the Mafia, etc.) and these
organizations tend to grow their own programmers.

One technique is to infiltrate one of these organizations under the
guise of being a mail-boy, or Vice President of Human Relations and
work you way up to COBOL programmer.

"Allen, Susan A" wrote:

> Carlos Lages wrote:
> >
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> You could create a newsgroup in Portuguese and help each other.

Good idea of Susan's. Create a group where you can converse in the lingua
franca. If you get stuck, one of you that is good at English (and there are
several of you), can jump in here, ask the question and feed it back to your
compatriots in Portuguese.

Just a suggestion, and I don't want to get into the Pope splitting up S.
America in the 1500s between Spain and Portugal, but are Spanish and
Portuguese similar enough that you could use one NewsGroup for both ?

Be warned - don't try and use Babelfish to translate technical stuff - as
somebody from Brazil once wrote, when I was trying to help poor Jose in
Portugal, with Micro Focus PC_PRINT, "Oh my God. It isn't Portuguese and it
isn't English !" <G>

Be assured us 'Anglos' will help out where we can.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

What I have found so far about this (and this is my personal opinion)

Portuguese guys.
They will understand spanish perfectly.
They will even be able to write some bad spanish if required.

Spanish guye (from spain, not all spanish speaking).
Will not understand Portuguese and will not write it (mainly because
they don't even wanna try it). (they are like french/english guys, why
learn another language if the others will learn ours!!!).
If they try they can read Portuguese

Brazilian guys.
Will understand Brazilian (off course).
Will understand Portuguese but will be unable to write it.
Will most probably be able to read/write spanish.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com (Frederico Fonseca)

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message news:<9m5k6q$m$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On 23-Aug-2001, Frederico Fonseca <frederico_fonseca@eudoramail.com> wrote:
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> watch a Spanish language movie.
Brazilian is mainly Portuguese but with some gramatical changes, and a
lot of new words (and this is the main difference), different spelling
in many words (activo (PT) versus ativo (BR)), and a very distinct way
of pronuncing some words.

As I said the Portuguese will be able to understand Brazilian easly,
specially because we are used to have LOADS of Brazilian soaps series
in Portugal.
As for the technical side there are some major problems as there are
several things that we name differently, (E.G Wildcards are called
"asteriscos" (PT) and "curingas" (BR) and there are others like this.)

I would say it is more different than U.S. and Scottish English.


Frederico
```

<a id="t-c0f25a828e"></a>
## Thread: Cobol/DB2-problem processing COUNT(*)-results — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Cobol/DB2-problem processing COUNT(*)-results

- **From:** kroessr@wien.spardat.at (Robert Kroess)
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T09:55:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b858197.9479500@news.sd.spardat.at>`

```
Hi all,

I'm running a Cobol-programm trying to process the result of the
following cursor:

SELECT CUSTNO, COUNT(*)
FROM PURCHASE
GROUP BY CUSTNO
FOR FETCH ONLY                 

I'm fetching each result line into the following host-variables:
FETCH <cursorname> INTO
 :RESULT-CUSTNO , 
 :RESULT-COUNT

When I'm running the program, I get the following error:
DSNT408I SQLCODE = -303, ERROR:  A VALUE CANNOT BE ASSIGNED TO OUTPUT
         HOST VARIABLE NUMBER 002 BECAUSE THE DATA TYPES ARE NOT     
         COMPARABLE                                                  
DSNT418I SQLSTATE   = 42806 SQLSTATE RETURN CODE                     

I've tried different definitions of RESULT-COUNT (e.g. S9(8) COMP,
S9(16) COMP, also COMP-3). It's definetely NO overflow (the maximum
count is 800.000) what causes the error.
What format must RESULT-COUNT do have to avoid this error?
Do anyone have some hints for me?

Thank you in advance,
Robert

mailto:kroessr@spardat.at
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol/DB2-problem processing COUNT(*)-results

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T22:25:34+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b8387b1_8@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3b858197.9479500@news.sd.spardat.at>`

```
Robert,

Your query is flawed.

You need to qualify the SELECT with either HAVING (as you are using GROUP
BY) or WHERE, if you drop the GROUP BY.

At the moment the count will be all rows on the PURCHASE table, and it will
be this EVERY TIME you FETCH the cursor!

If you REALLY want an unqualified scan of the PURCHASE Table I would use the
following:

EXEC SQL
      SELECT COUNT(*)
            INTO  :RESULT-COUNT
         FROM  PURCHASE
END-EXEC
EXEC SQL
      DECLARE cur1 CURSOR FOR
         SELECT  CUSTNO
             FROM   PURCHASE
    ORDER BY   CUSTNO
END-EXEC
EXEC SQL
      FETCH cur1
          INTO :RESULT-CUSTNO
END-EXEC

Pete.

"Robert Kroess" <kroessr@wien.spardat.at> wrote in message
news:3b858197.9479500@news.sd.spardat.at...
> Hi all,
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
> mailto:kroessr@spardat.at

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol/DB2-problem processing COUNT(*)-results

- **From:** Bill <wfsent@optonline.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T11:35:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B83990B.29EC2893@optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3b858197.9479500@news.sd.spardat.at> <3b8387b1_8@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
The original SQL looks fine to me.

GROUP BY is needed because he wants to see how many purchase orders exist for
each customer #. There's no need to use 'HAVING', he wants the results for the
entire table.

Bill

"Peter E. C. Dashwood" wrote:

> Robert,
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol/DB2-problem processing COUNT(*)-results

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T00:26:05+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b83a3ce_2@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3b858197.9479500@news.sd.spardat.at> <3b8387b1_8@goliath.newsgroups.com> <3B83990B.29EC2893@optonline.net>`

```
Yes, you're right, Bill.

I looked at it too quickly. And my solution would need to include DISTINCT
also.

However, I think he will get the same count every time he fetches, or does
DB2 count the subset from each fetch when GROUP BY is used?

It is years since I used DB2, but that's no excuse for carelessness <G>

Pete.

into

        05  suba                        pic s9(9) comp.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol/DB2-problem processing COUNT(*)-results

- **From:** kroessr@wien.spardat.at (Robert Kroess)
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T07:12:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b84abf0.710621@news.sd.spardat.at>`
- **References:** `<3b858197.9479500@news.sd.spardat.at> <3B8399B0.2DD3E07F@optonline.net>`

```
Hi all again,
thank you very much for your ideas and hint concerning this topic.
In the meanwhile, I figured out, what my real problem was.

I was also fetching an A08-field from DB2, which I read into the
following structure:

01 FIELD-A.
   05 FIELD-A-1  PIC X(4).
   05 FIELD-A-2  PIC X(4).

When DB2 was talking about "HOST VARIABLE 002" it wasn't meaning my
counter field; it meant the second X(4)-field. I replaced the
structure with an A08-field and the error vanished.

I wasn't aware that seperating character fields by using such
structures isn't allowed in DB2.

Robert


On Wed, 22 Aug 2001 11:38:25 GMT, Bill <wfsent@optonline.net> wrote:

>Hi Robert,
>    Looks fine to me. In one of my old programs I do a select count(*)
…[45 more quoted lines elided]…
>> mailto:kroessr@spardat.at

This problem is probably because a library is missing.

This is my test program to call the WIN32 API routine

       PROGRAM-ID. TESTAPI.

                         BY REFERENCE  isec

Linkcob testapi

My questions are how do I link my object? What library do I need?

<a id="t-13c70f3346"></a>
## Thread: How do I make a COBOL program wait? — 20 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How do I make a COBOL program wait?

- **From:** manojb@mailcity.com (Manoj)
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T18:28:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com>`

```
I am in a situation where my program needs to wait for say, 5 seconds
after it processes an input transaction and before it proceeds to
process the next input transaction.  The program runs 24x7, so
performing a complicated arithmetic expression a zillion times (which
I presume consumes CPU resources) is not appreciated.  Is there a way
to make the program wait with minimum expenditure of CPU resources for
this specified period of time?  One suggestion I recieved was to write
an assembler routine and call it from the COBOL program.
The environment is OS/390.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

- **From:** "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T02:09:31+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<vBZg7.6796$qc.756711@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com>`

```

"Manoj" <manojb@mailcity.com> wrote in message
news:ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com...
> I am in a situation where my program needs to wait for say, 5 seconds
> after it processes an input transaction and before it proceeds to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> The environment is OS/390.

Check the 370 FUNCTION of date/time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-22T23:43:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m21rl$ebu$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <vBZg7.6796$qc.756711@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
As far as I know, there is no intrinsic function or LE callable service to
cause a program to "wait".  However, there is a COBOL "callable" program
(available since OS/VS COBOL - if not earlier) that will do this.

If you do a CALL to "ILBOWAT0"  (first of  those is the letter "O" then the
number "0")

Pass it a full word binary field - Pic S9(8) Binary - with the number of
seconds to wait.

You should make certain that the COBOL program that is calling this
subroutine is compiled with DATA(24).  If you have a LARGE COBOL program
that you don't want to use DATA(24) with, then make an "intermediate"
program that does the actual call to ILBOWAT0.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T05:43:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B84978F.10DFB15A@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <vBZg7.6796$qc.756711@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <9m21rl$ebu$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> As far as I know, there is no intrinsic function or LE callable service to
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> program that does the actual call to ILBOWAT0.

In my shop there's an assembler subprogram that executes either as an
OS/390 jobstep or as a called subprogram.  It accepts a zoned-decimal
parameter containing the time to wait in hours, minutes, and seconds. 
I believe it is proprietary, but the last time I looked at the source
code, I believe it simply called an MVS system macro.  I can't
remember if the macro is named STIMER or WAIT.

In a CICS environment you can easily use the EXEC CICS DELAY command.

You might try inquiring on bit.listserv.ibm-main (or searching their
archives), or possibly comp.lang.asm370.

It's a fairly common problem, and it's been solved numerous times
before.

Good luck!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James" <mangogrower@telocity.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T09:55:49-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<auih7.5220$64.2841844@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <vBZg7.6796$qc.756711@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <9m21rl$ebu$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3B84978F.10DFB15A@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Or if the program needs to be above the line, make the call 'DYNAMIC'.  The
MVS will translate
the address and perform data-linkage fixup.

It is STIMER, here's a working SUB.  You have to pass the number as
described in the comments.
It could be made to run from JCL easily.  (Just have to check the first 2
bytes = if X'00' then if  next two bytes
are binary number, it was 'exec PGM' called.  (easier than checking what the
return address is).  Only difference
is to skip over 4 bytes if EXEC PGM vs CALL.

*          DATA SET DGWAITIM   AT LEVEL 001 AS OF 08/27/98
00000100
***********************************************************************
00000200
*                                                                     *
00000300
*  MODULE NAME = DGWAITIM                                             *
00000400
*                                                                     *
00000500
*  DESCRIPTIVE NAME = COBOL WAIT SUBROUTINE.                          *
00000600
*    CONVERTED TO ALSO RUN AS A MAIN ROUTINE                          *
00000703
*                                                                     *
00000803
*  FUNCTION =         THIS PROGRAM RECEIVES A BINARY FULLWORD         *
00000903
*                     SPECIFYNG AN INTERVAL AND ISSUES A STIMER WAIT  *
00001003
*                                                                     *
00001103
*  INPUT     : A BINARY FULLWORD CONTAINING A TIME INTERVAL TO WAIT   *
00001203
*              SPECIFIED IN HUNDRETHS OF SECONDS.                     *
00001303
*                                                                     *
00001403
*  OUTPUT    : NONE.                                                  *
00001503
*                                                                     *
00001803
*                                                                     *
00002203
***********************************************************************
00002303
         PRINT NOGEN
00002403
DGWAITIM CSECT
00002700
DGWAITIM AMODE ANY
00002802
DGWAITIM RMODE ANY
00002902
         USING *,R12               R12 <-- BASE REGISTER
00003001
         B     12(R15)             JUMP OVER
00003100
         DC    CL8'DGWAITIM'       PROGRAM ID
00003200
         SAVE  (14,12)             SAVE REGISTER CONTENTS
00003300
         LR    R12,R15             ESTABLISH ADDRESSABILITY
00003400
         ST    R13,SAVEAREA+4      LINK NEW SAVE AREA TO PREVIOUS
00003500
         LA    R7,SAVEAREA         R7 <-- ADDR(SAVEAREA)
00003600
         ST    R7,8(R13)           LINK PREVIOUS SAVE AREA TO NEW
00003700
         LR    R13,R7              R13 <-- ADDR(SAVEAREA)
00003800
         L     R1,0(R1)            R1  <-- ADDR(PARM)
00003900
CALLED   STIMER WAIT,BINTVL=(R1)   ISSUE STIMER WAIT
00004703
         L     R13,SAVEAREA+4      R13 <-- ADDR(PREVIOUS SAVEAREA)
00004800
         RETURN (14,12),,RC=0      END OF PROGRAM
00004900
SAVEAREA DS    18F                 SAVE AREA FOR THIS PROGRAM
00005000
         LTORG
00005100
         YREGS                     SETUP REGISTER USAGE
00005200
         ORG   ,
00006000
         END
00010000

to
> > cause a program to "wait".  However, there is a COBOL "callable" program
> > (available since OS/VS COBOL - if not earlier) that will do this.
> >
> > If you do a CALL to "ILBOWAT0"  (first of  those is the letter "O" then
the
> > number "0")
> >
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T22:16:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m4h1c$b5u$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <vBZg7.6796$qc.756711@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <9m21rl$ebu$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3B84978F.10DFB15A@worldnet.att.net> <auih7.5220$64.2841844@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
Please, PLEASE do not do a dynamic CALL passing a 31-bit "piece" of storage
to ILBOWAT0.  It is simply NOT designed to get such - no matter HOW you call
it.   ILBOWAT0 (and many other "older" programs) really, REALLY expect to
only be passed data "below" the 16M line.  This is the reason that the
DATA(24) compiler option exists.

OBVIOUSLY, even if you compile with DATA(31) you MIGHT get storage below the
line, so a "single test" won't prove anything.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T04:32:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B85D8E3.BB3FC25D@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <vBZg7.6796$qc.756711@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <9m21rl$ebu$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3B84978F.10DFB15A@worldnet.att.net> <auih7.5220$64.2841844@newsrump.sjc.telocity.net>`

```
Thanks for the sample program.  I managed to peek at the routine where
I work, and it uses STIMER with the DINTVL option.  It expects a
parameter containing hours, minutes, and seconds to wait.  The program
edits the input data for validity (whether passed as PARM= in a
jobstep, or as the single parameter in a CALL statement).  The
original program was written before MVS/XA, so a clone was built as
AMODE(31),RMODE(24) which is dynamically callable from an
AMODE(31),RMODE(ANY) COBOL main program.

…[131 more quoted lines elided]…

- **Date:** 2001-08-23T18:52:04+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<ohch7.12362$2u.87794@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Man,

Just curious, why do you need to wait?

Jack


In article <ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com>, Manoj says...
>
>I am in a situation where my program needs to wait for say, 5 seconds
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>The environment is OS/390.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T04:42:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B85DB13.9074773C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <ohch7.12362$2u.87794@www.newsranger.com>`

```
John H Sleight Jr wrote:
> 
> Hi Man,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jack

I can't speak for Manoj, but I know how we use our program.

For example, a batch job (something like MacKinney batch CEMT) is used
to submit a CEMT SET FILE(fctname) CLO command into a CICS region.  Or
perhaps you want to switch the CICS dump data sets, or start an online
transaction.  In any event, you want the batch job to wait a bit
before starting a later step that might depend on the outcome of the
CICS action.  So you put a step in your batch job that waits 30
seconds, which gives CICS time to complete your request.

I support a COBOL program that runs as an "infinite loop" task.  It
constantly reads an ESDS VSAM file in a CICS region, captures
interesting data, and submits it to a different CICS region.  If we
reach end-of-file on the input, there's no sense in wasting CPU cycles
(or channel activity) by opening, reading, and closing the file again
immediately.  So the COBOL program waits 5 seconds before looking for
new data.

On the other hand, some of this kind of stuff might be better handled
by posting and waiting on ECB's.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T05:17:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B85E435.29FD4BC9@home.com>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <ohch7.12362$2u.87794@www.newsranger.com> <3B85DB13.9074773C@worldnet.att.net>`

```

> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

are we familiar with ESDS VSAM , although obviously the latter is a
mainframe file-handling system - care to spell out in simple English, "Why
you need to wait" <G>

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T01:20:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m4rp1$sa6$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <ohch7.12362$2u.87794@www.newsranger.com> <3B85DB13.9074773C@worldnet.att.net> <3B85E435.29FD4BC9@home.com>`

```
I won't try and explain VSAM, ESDS, CEMT, etc (as I think that was a
semi-rhetorical question).  The answer to WHY you might want a "run forever"
program to sometimes "wait" (stop taking CPU time), the answer is VAGUELY
similar to "time slices" in a PC environment. (The following is HIGHLY
simplistic and all MVS, OS/390, and z/OS gurus can have as much a laugh as
they want.)

On a large IBM mainframe it is likely that you have 10's of programs (or
more?) "running simultaneously".  This is done by the CPU swapping various
programs in and out. There are a VARIETY of ways that MVS (and later)
operating systems have for "prioritizing" shops that are "virtually" running
at the same time.  The oldest simply prioritizes which program STARTS when.
Online applications (often IMS or CICS "transaction processing" regions)
keep going forever and do their own prioritization of jobs (and other
resources).  Relatively new (actually not that new) IBM introduced "WLM" -
which can do a lot of "prioritizing" of shops based on whatever "criteria"
you feed it.

HOWEVER, it is still possible to have a job "looping" thru infinite
processing (almost a "message driven paradigm" <G>).   They may even have
"ownership" of some resources (besides the CPU) that other programs will
want.

The BOTTOM-LINE is that sometimes (less and less as time passes and as new
"prioritization" options becomes available) it is important for an
application to say -
    Please, CPU, don't waste any of your "power" on me - but go and do your
thing with other programs and then come back to me "in a little while".
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

- **From:** manojb@mailcity.com (Manoj)
- **Date:** 2001-08-25T15:54:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ec0f6848.0108251454.434c1e10@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <ohch7.12362$2u.87794@www.newsranger.com>`

```
John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<ohch7.12362$2u.87794@www.newsranger.com>...
> Hi Man,
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> >The environment is OS/390.

The program pulls rows from a DB2 table.  The rows come into the DB2
table from an IMS transaction at the rate of around one row per 5
seconds.  So after I pull one row from the DB2 table, I want to wait
for 5 seconds before I check again.  I do not want to keep checking
continously and hit an empty table, wasting CPU time.  BTW, I found a
callable wait macro called SYSWAIT to which you pass the wait time in
HHMMSSmmmm format.

Thanks for all the replies.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How do I make a COBOL program wait?

- **From:** John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T21:54:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fkzi7.269$T4.3529@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<ec0f6848.0108221728.31af3fe9@posting.google.com> <ohch7.12362$2u.87794@www.newsranger.com> <ec0f6848.0108251454.434c1e10@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Man,

If your pgm is a CICS pgm (maybe it s/b since the IMS data provider sounds like
it is), you could have the IMS xaction create a TDQ rec for each DB2 row it
creates. The TDQ would have a trigger threshold of 1 (or any other quant that
makes sense), then your pgm can be STARTed. Of course your code would be
modified so that your pgm would START itself upon exit to propagate the endless
loop. Talk to your tech guys to get the pgm into the CICS automatic startup
library (I forgot its name).

The advantage of this approach is that the pgm executes only when there is data
in the TDQ. Also when your pgm reads the rec, it's immediately deleted. You may
want to rethink the 1 rec threshold to reduce the scheduling overhead.

Regards, Jack 


ticle <ec0f6848.0108251454.434c1e10@posting.google.com>, Manoj says...
>
>John H Sleight Jr <nospam@newsranger.com> wrote in message news:<ohch7.12362$2u.87794@www.newsranger.com>...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks for all the replies.
```

```
Hi Man,

If your pgm is a CICS pgm (maybe it s/b since the IMS data provider sounds like
it is), you could have the IMS xaction create a TDQ rec for each DB2 row it
creates. The TDQ would have a trigger threshold of 1 (or any other quant that
makes sense), then your pgm can be STARTed. Of course your code would be
modified so that your pgm would START itself upon exit to propagate the endless
loop. Talk to your tech guys to get the pgm into the CICS automatic startup
library (I forgot its name).

The advantage of this approach is that the pgm executes only when there is data
in the TDQ. Also when your pgm reads the rec, it's immediately deleted. You may
want to rethink the 1 rec threshold to reduce the scheduling overhead.

Regards, Jack

If it is a batch pgm, I assume you're running at the lowest dispatching priority
that makes sense.

The subject sounds like one of those "How do you make a Venitian blind" jokes.
An answer here might be submit it with a non-existant job class. :)

Jack

#### ↳ Re: OO Cobol Books for the Mainframe

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T00:11:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m23bv$2kh$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01c12b73$87102460$800b163f@chottel>`

```
A few suggestions:

Although explicitly NOT for z/OS or S/390 - still (probably) pretty useful,
  (from - http://publib-b.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/searchsite.cgi?query=cobol

IBM VisualAge for COBOL for OS/2 Object-Oriented Programming, SG24-4606-00

IBM VisualAge for COBOL for OS/2 Primer, SG24-4605-00

Also if you go to:
  http://publib.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr/BOOKS/IGYPG205/CCONTENTS

(hard copy info is:
 COBOL for OS/390 & VM
Programming Guide
SC26-9049-05)

Look for the section called:
 "4.0 Developing object-oriented programs"
```

## Thread: Program errors — 3 msgs

### Program errors

- **From:** pa@schaubroeck.be (Peter Alluyn)
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T09:08:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b84c532.11660138@news.eunet.be>`

```
Hello,

A cobol program on a unix machine gives me the following error message


--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Date : 23/08/2001  Time  : 10:17:46
Execution error : file './name of the program
error code: 114, pc=320, call=15, seg=0
114     Attempt to access item beyond bounds of memory (Signal11) 
key to continue...

Can I retrieve the line of the cobol program on wich the error occured
with these information (pc=320, call=15, seg=0). ?????


Thanks Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: Program errors

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T12:22:01+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m2ped$a5h$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<3b84c532.11660138@news.eunet.be>`

```
The error looks as though this is a Micro Focus compiler - probably the
easiest approach is to use the Animator to debug through the program.  If
this error arises during an animation session execution should stop with the
offending line highlighted.

"Peter Alluyn" <pa@schaubroeck.be> wrote in message
news:3b84c532.11660138@news.eunet.be...
> Hello,
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> --------------------------------------------------------------------------
------
> Date : 23/08/2001  Time  : 10:17:46
> Execution error : file './name of the program
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: Program errors

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T17:08:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B853951.DE95F338@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b84c532.11660138@news.eunet.be>`

```


Peter Alluyn wrote:

> Hello,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks Peter

As Robert said it looks suspiciously like you are using a Micro Focus compiler. As
suggested  use the Animator. A likely cause is a screw-up on your Linkage section
using and returning parameters - all sorts of combinations, so these are just some
:-

    - call xyz using One(pic x(12), Two(pic 9(03)) returning Three(Three being a
        Level 01, 05 pic x(06) and 05 pic 9(02))
    - OR call xyz returning Three
    xyz - Linkage Section
             01  One
             01 Three
            Procedure Division using One, Two returning Three

OR

            Procedure Division using Two returning Three

I could go on an on, but likely a combination of the above having 'extras', leaving
out some, not returning or returning and not correct info at 05 Levels.

Some of the above the compiler catches like Procedure header Linkage is checked
against Linkage section  - but it isn't aware of what you are doing between
programs.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

<a id="t-3ddb604b19"></a>
## Thread: COBOL Conversion from Indexed files to Relational Database — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### COBOL Conversion from Indexed files to Relational Database

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T01:17:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b859762_9@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
I have just completed an exercise assisting a Software House to convert
their very successful package from ISAM to Relational Database.

Tools were developed to assist in this conversion. Procedures were developed
and much experience was gained. The conversion went very successfully in far
less time than any of us could have hoped for.
(60 ISAM files were converted to a 3NF RDB with appropriate indexes,
referential constraints, and the removal of repeating groups and key
dependencies) and the newly created tables were loaded with existing live
data within 2.5 elapsed days (using 3 people and tools for both the
conversion and the loading which I wrote especially for this job). This has
allowed the existing code to be carefully examined and converted according
to a successful strategy we devised.

Many lessons were learned during this exercise which took 7 working days in
all.

I am happy to make both the tools and the strategy available to anyone
considering such an exercise. If you want to try it for credibility, send me
the source COBOL SELECT and the FD for up to three of your ISAM files (no
data required) and I'll return within 24 hours an Access Database with the
appropriate matching structure. OCCURS and REDEFINES in the COBOL source are
handled, along with COBOL data structure (levels), none of which is directly
"translatable" to RDB.

This offer is not indefinite and may be curtailed if there is heavy demand.

While I would not pretend that this is an ideal way to develop Relational
Databases, it is a VERY good way to convert existing Indexed structures.

Please draw on our experience if you are considering this kind of
conversion.

The process applies across PC and Mainframe platforms.

Pete.

## Thread: IBM - and "business components" — 3 msgs

### IBM - and "business components"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-23T22:26:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m4hiq$t72$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
While searching for something else, I came across a couple of INTERESTING
(at least to me) web pages on IBM's "solutions" in the area of "business
components".  These seem as if they MIGHT be of interest and/or relevance to
some of the discussions we have been having in CLC recently.  See:

http://www-4.ibm.com/software/webservers/components/componentsrelease.html

    and

http://www-4.ibm.com/software/ad/sanfrancisco/

a "few" caveats and/or (negative) observations:

A) The "language" that these components seem to ASSUME is Java

B) The operating systems for "San Francisco" are:
    AIX, HP-UX, OS/400, Solaris and Windows NT
which shows an OBVIOUS omission of OS/390 (or z/OS)

C) I have *zero* knowledge of any "real world use" of these components
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM - and "business components"

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T06:08:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8C8723.8FE662E7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9m4hiq$t72$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> While searching for something else, I came across a couple of INTERESTING
> (at least to me) web pages on IBM's "solutions" in the area of "business
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

One comment I'll make and perhaps others can make observations. I downloaded
Absolute which is a decision maker, forget how many files and gifs etc but =
820,117 bytes. Can't even look at it until I install Java - and I think it's
telling me I need Netscape V 6.0.

Now in the overall scheme of things maybe 820,117 bytes is  no big deal given
our forever increasing disk storage. But if you accumulate a whole series of
components I wonder how many times you are going to come across duplicated
features.  Indications are from Gartner that this is going to EXPLODE - so for
each component you use/design/purchase you have n-components X ???? bytes.

Nobody is controlling this - when somebody latches on that there should be a
committee (Uggh !) - it might be just too late to put things to right ????? I'm
guessing at this - as an example the various vendors produced DB tools; after
the fact they probably came together and that's when we got the ANSI/ISO SQL 92.
While partially 'tools' these components are 'additional software' units.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM - and "business components"

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T11:33:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b8d7c19_2@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<9m4hiq$t72$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net> <3B8C8723.8FE662E7@home.com>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B8C8723.8FE662E7@home.com...
>
> One comment I'll make and perhaps others can make observations. I
downloaded
> Absolute which is a decision maker, forget how many files and gifs etc but
=
> 820,117 bytes. Can't even look at it until I install Java - and I think
it's
> telling me I need Netscape V 6.0.
>
Are you saying that this is a component?

If so, what interface is it using? (COM, CORBA, what?)

> Now in the overall scheme of things maybe 820,117 bytes is  no big deal
given
> our forever increasing disk storage. But if you accumulate a whole series
of
> components I wonder how many times you are going to come across duplicated
> features.  Indications are from Gartner that this is going to EXPLODE - so
for
> each component you use/design/purchase you have n-components X ???? bytes.
>
Gartner are not the only ones who believe this will explode... I have been
watching it do so for the past two years...

Please understand that while CLC is a fun and interesting place to be (for
the most part), it is a "backwater" in terms of what is happening in the
mainstream computing World. The majority of people here are more interested
in "fortress COBOL" than in emerging technology and, as a result, are often
unaware of trends in the industry which are tunnelling under the fortress...

Yes, if you use components, then the total space occupied by them will be
equal to the space occupied by each one, multiplied by the number of
components you have....(Duuuuh!) So what? The same formula applies to any
code you have.

>
> Nobody is controlling this -

On the contrary, it is being extremely well controlled in organisations
which are utilizing this technology. There are superb software packages
available for exactly that purpose and they give complete statistics on
where any given component is used throughout the Enterprise, as well as a
complete "component inventory". Your statement indicates that you haven't
done your homework on this Jimmy.

If you mean there is no equivalent to the ANSI Alphabet soup that has done
so much to constipate COBOL, you are wrong on that too.

The only part of component technology which NEEDS standardizing is the
interface. ActiveX is MS proprietary (an extension to their COM) but is OPEN
in the sense that it can be downloaded, and this is the standard. ANYONE can
implement this interface WITHOUT requiring permission from MS or paying a
licence fee.

The Component Object Group provides the same facility for CORBA.

So the "control" you refer to comes down to the individual organization
implementing the technology.

Many Universities are now teaching Component Based Design.

Please visit http://www.flashline.com

Spend some time and browse the site. You may be surprised, not only at what
is available (in terms of component inventory control software) but at the
reactions and comments from Organizations picking up this technology.

COBOL will be dragged kicking and screaming into this scenario  only after
it embraces OO.

(As discussed elsewhere here, COBOL is being dragged kicking and screaming
into OO and there is still fierce resistance to this methodology from
"fortress COBOL"... so what hope for COBOL based components?

I am the ONLY person I know of who is writing them and using them with other
components. When I talk to other component authors they are amazed that I am
using COBOL. Yet it is ideal for some types of component. (BTW, if anyone
reading this is also using COBOL to produce ActiveX or COM components,
PLEASE drop me a line...it's lonely out here on the frontier...<G>))

 I believe it will probably be too late by then for COBOL to make any
significant contribution even though I still believe COBOL is the best tool
for many kinds of job.

The resistance to change and the attitude of COBOL programmers, coupled with
the kind of mentality that believes it is OK to take 17 years to produce a
standard, is sounding the death knell for COBOL as anything other than a
Batch processing language, or a mainframe CICS support language. It seems it
will never make the leap into Client/Server (the COBOL User base for
Client/Server is less than 7% of the total COBOL User base).

This is a tragedy as far as I'm concerned. I feel like someone who is
clinging to his Betamax while the World goes VHS... <G>

The Vendors have provided excellent implementations of COBOL for
Client/Server but even enterprises using COBOL as their mainframe language
have refused to pick them up. And when you look at the posts here, it isn't
hard to see why...

> when somebody latches on that there should be a
> committee (Uggh !) - it might be just too late to put things
> right ?????
First off, there won't be a committee, and there shouldn't be, other than
the existing ones which are serving the Component User community in a very
satisfactory and timely way.

Secondly, there is nothing to "put right". All the component developers I am
in touch with are perfectly happy with the support being provided in their
language of choice (Usually VB or Delphi, sometimes C++) and both Borland
and Microsoft seem quite happy with the way the Interface standards are
being administered.

As far as Fujitsu PowerCOBOL  (V5) goes, support for component development
is outstanding and I use it all the time.

None of us have any problems getting our components to play nicely together.
(I had a few problems with this in the early days but none currently).

> I'm
> guessing at this - as an example the various vendors produced DB tools;
after
> the fact they probably came together and that's when we got the ANSI/ISO
SQL 92.
> While partially 'tools' these components are 'additional software' units.
>
Bad analogy. SQL was released in 1983. The need for a "Standard" was
recognised and the standard was attained in 1992 as you note. There was no
need for a standard until the late eighties because there was resistance to
RDB technology (particularly from an entrenched IMS base on the mainframes)
and the only credible implementation of it was DB2. By the late eighties
this changed and other "Relational Database Systems" became available on
other platforms. This has led to the proliferation we see today (and it is
still expanding))

SQL NEEDED a standard for it to work across platforms.

Components only need a STANDARD interface. They have one, and new releases
of it appear in response to user feedback and vendor enhancement driven by
market forces. It works the way COBOL SHOULD work if it wasn't bogged down
in it's own bureaucracy and resistance to change.

I realise that what I've posted above is not what most people here would
want to hear, but you asked for observations and these are mine. From the
heart.

Pete.

<a id="t-6ee1db6be4"></a>
## Thread: Options when moving COBOL from UNIX to WINDOWS — 7 msgs

### Options when moving COBOL from UNIX to WINDOWS

- **From:** david@quantumcat.demon.co.uk (David Latimer)
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T08:35:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a9026f4e.0108240735.24e9b078@posting.google.com>`

```
I work as 1 of 2 COBOL developers in a very small IT dept!

We currently use MicroFocus COBOL V4.0 on an ICL UNIX box
running UnixWare 2.1.3 (A bit behind the times?).

Within a year or two we will probably move from the UNIX
box to a WINDOWS server.

This is mainly because we won't need to pay for UNIX 
hardware/software support (limited inhouse knowledge) but
also because we would like to introduce some mouse control
to the existing screens (e.g. clicking into fields and on
buttons).

After that we could go full GUI & interact with other
WINDOWS programs like Excell/Word/Outlook.

What might be the best options in terms of compilers/products
and conversion methodologies?

I ought to mention that:
a) I'm not sure if we are 100% ANSI-85 compliant.
b) All our screen handling code (using ACCEPT AND DISPLAY 
with screens defined in the SCREEN SECTION) is in the same
programs as the business logic. (Yes, i know you're not
supposed to now!)
c) We have SYSTEM calls within some programs.

Sorry if i've rambled on a bit but thanks in advance,
David
```

#### ↳ Re: Options when moving COBOL from UNIX to WINDOWS

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T17:20:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b868996.17499361@news.epix.net>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0108240735.24e9b078@posting.google.com>`

```
David:

Our COBOL Tools are completely COBOL compiler independent.

We have assisted a number of companies in conversion from
DISPLAY/ACCEPT to our COBOL sp2 (COBOL GUI Tool) calls.

Our software also supports the UNIX environment, so we understand the
environment from which you are coming.

We have solutions for your Windows Print Manager needs and also
provide multiple Internet enabling solutions, including both thin
client/server solutions as well as web browser client/server solutions

Please contact me at rtwolfe@flexus.com for more information.

Thanks.


david@quantumcat.demon.co.uk (David Latimer) wrote:

>I work as 1 of 2 COBOL developers in a very small IT dept!
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>David


Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Options when moving COBOL from UNIX to WINDOWS

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T18:11:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8699A9.B8E6DA0B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0108240735.24e9b078@posting.google.com>`

```


David Latimer wrote:

> I work as 1 of 2 COBOL developers in a very small IT dept!
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> David

I can't address Unix directly because I haven't used it.

1. If you are comfortable/satisfied with Micro Focus products then it
makes sense to 'upgrade' within the family - unless your searching
reveals cheaper prices, something closer to what you are looking for
etc.

2. I'm assuming you are hell bent on going Windows. If you wanted to
stick with Screen Section does M/F have a sample program for using the
Mouse with Unix ? (in DOS it was ADMOUSE.cbl - (AD = their
Accept/Display or Adis module).

3. Windowing - several options, (I'm reasonably certain the Unix version
parallels the PC Net Express - but check with M/F for specifics). M/F
two options - Dialog System, (successor/extension to their Panels
product) or you could use Windows GUIs using OO classes - don't touch
the latter with a barge pole if you aren't already into windowing/OO.
You can communicate with  Java - but you have to learn the language.
Other alternatives are Flexus SP2 or Norton Screen I/O - you write
conventional COBOL programs and they do the Windowing for you.

4. How long for you to get into Windowing ?. Make a guesstimate, then
double or triple the time - if you haven't been anywhere near Windows
before.

5. Each of the various Windowing options will let you get into
Excel/Word/Outlook.

6. Conversion methodologies. Your Screens are integrated with business
logic. What's new, we all did it that way ! Given there is a reasonable
time slice between what you are doing now and getting into Windowing,
give this one a lot of thought and extract your 'Screens' into separate
programs called by the business logic. Will make it much easier to
convert to Windowing.

7. Given that you feel 'compelled' to get into Windowing - not
suggesting that you should - but maybe you should give OO COBOL a look
see. You could do the Windowing first, and possibly take a look at OO
later - but there's bound to be some extra work, i.e. you will view the
design of your non-OO programs in a particular way, then getting to OO
you will,..... "Oh I wish we had realized, we should have written the
programs this way. Would've made it easier to switch to OO...".

I'm not making a hard case for OO - just mentioning it, so you are
aware, and arrive at your own judgment.

Good luck

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Options when moving COBOL from UNIX to WINDOWS

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-25T09:11:33+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B875DB5.3045943D@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0108240735.24e9b078@posting.google.com> <3B8699A9.B8E6DA0B@home.com>`

```
> David Latimer wrote:
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > hardware/software support (limited inhouse knowledge) but

Moving to a Windows server means that you won't have to pay for _Unix_
hardware/software support but will need to pay someone to do Windows
hardware/software support, and will especially need to pay for licences
and upgrades - it is expected that MS licences will be expiring ones
with a life of 3 years.  

Perhaps you are paying ICL maintenance contracts.  Have you thought
about moving to Linux on bog standard PCs ?  Perhaps Caldera 'Open Unix'
which is Unixware with Linux personality.  In theory it can run all
UnixWare and Linux apps.

> > also because we would like to introduce some mouse control
> > to the existing screens (e.g. clicking into fields and on
> > buttons).

I have systems in MF Cobol that run on DOS, Windows, Unixware and Linux
from one set of source code.  I detect the OS and take some different
paths depending on which it is running on.

It is possible to use the mouse with MF ACCEPT/DISPLAY where a mouse is
available but generally with the sort of systems that are used by Cobol
a mouse gets in the way more that helping.  You can enable the mouse
using a call to adis and then it can be detected as if the user pushed a
function key (ie you fall off the accept).  The XY position can be
detected so that you can treat specific areas of the screen as if they
were buttons.

I have a mixture of older ADIS programs and newer SP2 where some
functions require to use an older program.  It is possible within a
system to switch between SP2 and ADIS so that the original system can
continue to be used while new functions are built or programs are
changed to GUI/SP2.

> > After that we could go full GUI & interact with other
> > WINDOWS programs like Excell/Word/Outlook.

Or you could use StarOffice/KOffice etc under Linux.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Options when moving COBOL from UNIX to WINDOWS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T19:04:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8BEC14.DF4CD03A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0108240735.24e9b078@posting.google.com> <3B8699A9.B8E6DA0B@home.com> <3B875DB5.3045943D@Azonic.co.nz> <a9026f4e.0108280316.63380861@posting.google.com>`

```


David Latimer wrote:

> > You can enable the mouse
> > using a call to adis and then it can be detected as if the user pushed a
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I'll  let Richard answer the rest, but specifically the above. In Windows I use the ENTER key to
terminate data put into an entry-field (that's EXACTLY the way the user had it in DOS and he sees no
reason to switch to that bloody TAB key - ergonomically it's the pits ! He does 90% plus of his
input using the Numpad). It presents a  problem for me because dear old Windows *assumes* he's hit
the OK PushButton. So I have to detect for the ENTER key and is it terminating an entryfield or is
he truly pushing the OK Button. (In OO I check if the CurrentObject is the DefaultPushbutton or
otherwise).

Remember what you want to do with F11 or F12 is no different to pressing F1 for Help in any package
you personally use, written for Windows.

Micro Focus provides "keys.cpy" for Keystrokes :-
-------------------------------------------------------------
*>> Virtual Key Codes. Cab be used when querying the character
*>> pressed on keyboard events

*>> VK_0 thru VK_9 are the same as ASCII '0' thru '9' (0x30 - 0x39)
*>> VK_A thru VK_Z are the same as ASCII 'A' thru 'Z' (0x41 - 0x5A)

78  OVK-BACK           value h"08".
78  OVK-TAB            value h"09".
78  OVK-CLEAR          value h"0c".
78  OVK-RETURN         value h"0d".
etc.....
78  OVK-HELP           value h"2f".
78  OVK-NUMPAD0        value h"60".
etc....

and the ones you are interested in :

78  OVK-F1             value h"70".
78  OVK-F2             value h"71".
78  OVK-F3             value h"72".
78  OVK-F4             value h"73".
78  OVK-F5             value h"74".
78  OVK-F6             value h"75".
78  OVK-F7             value h"76".
78  OVK-F8             value h"77".
78  OVK-F9             value h"78".
78  OVK-F10            value h"79".
78  OVK-F11            value h"7a".
78  OVK-F12            value h"7b".
etc....
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

So you can get a feel for how I use it testing the ENTER key :-

*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "begin".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
*> Here I'm telling it to check for all keystrokes

Linkage section.
01 lnk-Caller                        object reference.
Procedure Division using lnk-Caller.
  set os-Caller to lnk-Caller
  invoke self "wantAllKeys"
End Method "begin".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "checkCharacterCode".   *> PRIVATE
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
*> as the result of a 'keying event' this jumps  into action

*> checks for backspace etc.....

Local-storage section.
copy "keys.cpy".
01 ls-code                              pic x(4) comp-5.
Linkage section.
01 lnk-event                            object reference.
Procedure Division using lnk-event.

 invoke lnk-event "getCharacterCode" returning ls-code

 Evaluate true
    when  ls-code = OVK-RETURN or OVK-TAB
          invoke self "returnEntryObject"
 End-evaluate

End Method "checkCharacterCode".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "returnEntryObject".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

*>. This only 'exectues' if it is an entryfield :-

Local-storage section.
01 ls-object                     object reference.
01 ls-string                     object reference.
01 ls-MethodName                 pic x(30).

01 ls-ReturnValues.
   05 ls-field                      pic x(4) comp-5.
   05 ls-length                     pic x(4) comp-5.
   05 ls-text.
      10 occurs 1 to TextLength
         depending on ls-length     pic x(01).

Procedure Division.

perform varying ls-field from 1 by 1
                until ls-field > MaxEntryFields

    invoke os-Collection(2) "at"
           using ls-field returning ls-object

    if  os-CurrentObject = ls-object
        invoke os-CurrentObject "getText" returning ls-string
        invoke ls-string "trimblanks" returning ls-string
        invoke os-CurrentObject "Getlength" returning ls-length
        invoke ls-string "getValueWithSize"
             using ls-Length returning ls-text
        invoke ls-string "finalize" returning ls-string

        invoke os-Collection(3) "at"
             using ls-field returning ls-MethodName

        invoke os-Caller ls-MethodName
               using ls-ReturnValues
         EXIT METHOD
    End-if
End-perform

End Method "returnEntryObject".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

> Is the XY position character based (80 * 25) or by pixel?

Not the same thing at all. ADIS used with DOS is testing for Line/Column. I suppose you could test
for a specific pixel location given x and y but generally you are talking about Windows controls,
regardless of their size. If you were to substitute a set of Pushbuttons at the foot of your screen
(Dialog) that had captions for F1, F11 or F12 etc., then the Windows  is looking for you clicking
anywhere within the controls x, y, w(idth), h(eight), positioning.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Options when moving COBOL from UNIX to WINDOWS

- **From:** alain.reymond-nospam@ceia.com (Alain Reymond)
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T08:05:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b89f8cd.522120@news.skynet.be>`
- **References:** `<a9026f4e.0108240735.24e9b078@posting.google.com>`

```
We had exactly the same problem on (astonishing!) the same platform.

We studied the problem. Here are some arguments that lead to our
conclusion:

1- You do not want to pay for a Unix hardware/software support. I can
understand. Moving from a Unix to a Windows box will make you pay for
a Windows support which is not cheap for a good NT server and all it's
licences.

2- Porting a program from Unix to Windows/GUI is not just transferring
it to the new system and recompile it. You have to re-think your
entire application to profit from the benefits of the GUI essentially
based on processing a flow of events generated by the user. This is a
very long and expensive work.

3- If you have been running under Unix for many years, you must
certainly have developed some expertise. Why lose that capital?

4- Adding GUI facilities in an application is legitimate. But does the
entire application require that functionality? An idea is to clearly
identify which portions require GUI interactions and find an
appropriate solution: exportation/importation of data or ODBC (Parkway
for example), etc... if your Unix server is integrated in a network.

If you look at the price of a Linux box (Dell for example), you can
find a very good system, ready to run, for a few euros (or dollars),
with Redhat installed. You can integrate it in a network with Windows
stations and access the system through a good telnet program
(TinyTerm, Anzio or many others). You can have drives mounted using
NFS or samba, share printers.

Considering all that points - and others -, we moved under Linux last
December. We bought a MF Cobol licence. It took us half a day to
migrate from our ICL/Unixware server to the Linux one: ftp and
compile. We had just a few changes for the spooling facilities.

Until now, we haven't got any problem with that solution. Every case
is particular. In our case, we are satisfied with our choices.

Hope a real life experience can be interesting.

Alain

>I work as 1 of 2 COBOL developers in a very small IT dept!
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>David
```

<a id="t-d29caebe8d"></a>
## Thread: Have *YOU* communicated with the "new" Micro Focus - about run-time fees? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Have *YOU* communicated with the "new" Micro Focus - about run-time fees?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-24T13:58:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9m689n$p8n$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(Posted to comp.lang.cobol - CC's to various MF people)

It seems to me that NOW is the time for all the "small site" and
"individual" vendors of COBOL applications to communicate with the *new*
Micro Focus concerning the issues around "run-time license fees".

To see that such communication *does* work, I suggest that you look at
www.google.com via the thread at:

http://groups.google.com/groups?q=g:thl2299475642d&hl=en&safe=off&selm=8ibga
0%24f7d%241%40nnrp1.deja.com

(You may need to "paste" it to get that full "URL" - or alternatively go to
 http://groups.google.com/advanced_group_search?hl=en&oe=ISO-8859-1&safe=off

and search comp.lang.cobol for
  Hill NetExpress 3.1 Runtime Fees

It seems to me that Tony Hill (now "head" of the new Micro Focus) was
responsive to "customer input" - as reflected in this newsgroup when he said
(among other things),

"On behalf of MERANT, I would like to thank this group for their comments on
the Net Express run-time licensing policy.

Following a thorough review of your comments, and extensive discussion
within MERANT,  I have discovered that, although we have had a policy of
charging run-time license fees for Net Express for some time, we have not
communicated this at all well to our customers in North
America. Accordingly, I am pleased to inform you that, effective
immediately, we plan to implement a North American run-time amnesty program
for Net Express 3.1 targeted at small development organisations.
  ...
Finally, thanks again for your interest in MERANT. Please feel free to
e-mail me directly on tony.hill@merant.com if you have any urgent comments."

Given his previous comments (when still a part of MERANT) it seems to me
(and of course I could be wrong <G>) that NOW is the time to try and
convince the NEW Micro Focus to take a NEW look at the entire area of
"run-time license fees".

It seems to me that given enough input from current, past, and prospective
"customers" for whom this SINGLE issue may determine whether or not they use
any MF product to produce applications intended for "distribution", that he
(they? MF?) might be able to come up with a GOOD business case for modifying
their entire approach and NOT just going with a "one time" or "one
continent" amnesty program.

I don't think that you can/should expect an INSTANT change in direction (as
the new company is "finding its way" back to meeting COBOL customer needs).
However, I do think that if enough sites can explain

 - Why they have converted from Micro Focus to Fujitsu (or IBM or AcuCorp)
 - Why they are dropping COBOL in favor of other "workstation" programming
languages
 - Why they have NOT upgraded to NetExpress V3.1
 - Why they purchased a competitor's product
 - etc, etc

then, this MIGHT provide MF with the "business case" to change their
"business plan's" reliance on run-time licenses from "small ISV and
developers".

As with many other topics, it is by belief that "user input" DOES make a
difference. Therefore, if you don't ASSUME that someone else is taking care
of this - or that your input won't really make a difference, it is my hope
that the "new" Micro Focus may "re-enter" the small developer COBOL market
as a serious "player".

 --
Bill Klein
 wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
```

All source code was stored on cards.

Fred

Steve.Barney@hartfordlife.com (Steve Barney) wrote:

>> Access the Flexus COBOL Page at www.flexus.com
>> 
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>--Steve

Steve:

We have people accessing and downloading from two different FTP sites
we operate every day.  One of our FTP sites (the one you are trying to
access) is an anonymous FTP site....this means that anyone can log in
and download files.  The other is a secure FTP site and requires a
User ID and Password.

You might want to check with your network administration people.  More
and more companies are beefing up their firewall security to prevent
users from accessing ftp sites from their network.

The gentleman who requested the printer spacing charts was able to
download the Excell spreadsheet file with no problem on his first
attempt.  I suspect that your network has some sort of security block.

If you would like for me to send the file via a private e-mail
attachment, I would be happy to do so.  Send a private e-mail request
to rtwolfe@flexus.com and it will be on its way.

Sorry that you are experiencing trouble.





Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: How is the status of cobol now on Pc ? — 4 msgs

### How is the status of cobol now on Pc ?

- **From:** "Max Bernaert" <edp@verbeke.be>
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T11:00:30+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<H3oi7.5065$35.496716@iguano.antw.online.be>`

```
Hello cobol fans,

Two years ago I stopped with cobol on pc because I was waiting a too long
time to get a powerful cobol compiler for windows.

I went to Delphi, but the language I always preferred is Cobol. I am still
working with cobol on mainframe. I never got the execution speed of cobol
with Delphi. (Delphi with database is about 5 times slower than cobol).

I have some questions about Fujitsu cobol compiler 6.1 :

a) Is it already possible to work with data aware components ?
b) Can you commit or rollback transactions ?
c) Is it easy to access relational databases ?

Thanks for your time to answer.

Max Bernaert.

Salutations from Belgium.
```

Certainly.

>b) Can you commit or rollback transactions ?

Depends on the database and ODBC drivers - so I'll make this a qualfied Yes.

>c) Is it easy to access relational databases ?

Again, depends on the database and your desired access.  If it's SQL via ODBC -
the answer is YES.  

>
>Thanks for your time to answer.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: How is the status of cobol now on Pc ?

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T12:28:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<p7Mi7.12124$5v5.1409986707@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<H3oi7.5065$35.496716@iguano.antw.online.be>`

```
In reference to Fujitsu COBOL 6.1, I'd say it's quite powerful, especially
if you decide to purchase the professional version which includes
PowerCobol.

PowerCobol allows you to "drag and drop" database controls (such as the one
we use for MS Access) on to your form, add some lines of code, and update
the database without ever opening MS Access per se.  Once you get the hang
of how the database field names are set up, it's really quite slick.

Also, we have applications that map network drives to our Unisys host
(mainframe), and pull data from the host without using any file transfer or
FTP software.

All in all, in my opinion, it's quite a powerful product and allows those of
us who have been working with COBOL a number of years to stay in familiar
territory.  Qualified by the fact that there is a bunch of new stuff to
learn regarding Windows Methods, Controls, Properties, etc.  And that's not
all bad!!!

Denny
```

<a id="t-d619da21e3"></a>
## Thread: The demand for CoBOL programmers... — 15 msgs

### The demand for CoBOL programmers...

- **From:** psychoticusrex@hotmail.com (Psychoticus Rex)
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T06:16:54-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1341c7e4.0108270516.797add4d@posting.google.com>`

```
I've recently been told by my illustrious employer that CoBOL
programmers are going without work, are continuously unemployed, and
that generally no one wants them.

Is this true of the current market?

Yes I understand the demand has saciated since the Y2K fiasco, but
surely the code that was arround then is still arround now and still
needs to be maintained?

Certainly Third Generation languages haven't eliminated all need for
CoBOL just yet have they?  SQL/PL might be toted as the last word in
database languages but is there no room for CoBOL.

I don't have a personal stake in this other than setting my mind at
ease that the world hasn't gone tipsy tervy and to get a shot back at
my so-well-informed employer who would propose to preach to me....ok
so I'm spitefull. :)

Someone?
```

#### ↳ Re: The demand for CoBOL programmers...

- **From:** ookpik1@yahoo.co.uk (Dean Powell)
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T11:04:34-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f7686d4.0108271004.73a4e47c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1341c7e4.0108270516.797add4d@posting.google.com>`

```
I can tell you that here in Edmonton, Canada, the CoBOL market is very
much alive and well, thank you very much.  Perhaps mainframe CoBOL use
has declined in recent years, simply becuase the use of mainframes
themselves has gone down, but the need for PC CoBOL programmers
(Merant and Fujitsu come to mind as popular packages) is still pretty
strong.  I personally don't see CoBOL ever going away.

As for Y2K, I wouldn't refer to it as a fiasco.  I spent 18 months on
our Y2K project...the changes weren't high profile, but everyone at
our company knew full well they had to be made.  I've seen some
wingnuts complain in print of a high-level conspiracy by consultants
to make ourselves a lot of extra money, but any level-headed person
I've ever talked to seems to agree that Y2K was a very real potential
problem.

Regards,
Dean Powell
Clockwork Technologies (http://www.clockwork-technologies.ab.ca)
Edmonton, Canada

psychoticusrex@hotmail.com (Psychoticus Rex) wrote in message news:<1341c7e4.0108270516.797add4d@posting.google.com>...
> I've recently been told by my illustrious employer that CoBOL
> programmers are going without work, are continuously unemployed, and
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> Someone?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: The demand for CoBOL programmers...

- **From:** "Timothy" <timhillock@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T21:48:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Qezi7.39799$n75.9386705@news4.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<1341c7e4.0108270516.797add4d@posting.google.com> <1f7686d4.0108271004.73a4e47c@posting.google.com>`

```
It was a real problem.  I've never been a consulated.

I first ran into the Y2K problem back in 1982.   At that time our dates for
the year were 2 digits.  I built a case to get them increased to 4.  A
compremise was reached.   Only for new files and databases that the years
were to have the full year.  At that time it was considered to be too much
work to change existing files and programs.   Some one must have had a
crystal ball back in 82.   In 1998 the entire department was downsized to
the parent company.  All information was moved over to their system.  They
(P.C.) hadn't even started work on their system.  Other than in 1982 I
didn't have too much of a Y2K problem in changing programs.  At the new
place where I started to work, files were already in 4 year digits.


Tim
