## Thread: Creator? — 15 msgs

### Creator?

- **From:** "Jeremy Deadmond" <jdeadmond@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T17:09:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M8vi7.19481$sa.10009360@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`

```
Anyone know who created COBOL?  I was told she was an admiral or something
like that.
```

#### ↳ Re: Creator?

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T12:25:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8A826D.D87BDF00@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<M8vi7.19481$sa.10009360@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`

```
Jeremy Deadmond wrote:

> Anyone know who created COBOL?  I was told she was an admiral or something
> like that.

Commander Grace Hopper was one of the people who was involved with the
definition and early development of the language.   I heard her speak here
(Winnipeg) a long time ago: she was a charming person and very entertaining.
Also very sharp indeed.  (I use her rank of Commander because that's what she
was at the time.  I think she did retire as an Admiral).

If you look at the acknowledgment page of your COBOL manual, it ought to have
the usual potted description of the origin and status of the language.

PL
```

#### ↳ Re: Creator?

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@paralynx.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T12:03:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8A997B.725E@paralynx.com>`
- **References:** `<M8vi7.19481$sa.10009360@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`

```
Jeremy Deadmond wrote:
> 
> Anyone know who created COBOL?  I was told she was an admiral or something
> like that.

Grace Hopper was the oldest ever serving Admiral in the US Navy (she even beat 
Rickover).

Grace is generally credited with "inventing" COBOL, but a lot of the stuff was simply 
consolidated from elsewhere.  For example, the PICTURE clause was actually invented by a 
bunch of tea shop accountants when they set up the LEO system for Joe Lyons Teashops in 
the UK.

(I worked with somebody from the original LEO crew in the mid 60s).
```

#### ↳ Re: Creator?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T21:10:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8Hyi7.184290$ai2.13131104@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<M8vi7.19481$sa.10009360@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com>`

```
COBOL was the first high-level language, and "invented" by a committee
(think camel/horse) under the guidance of the Department of Defense.

Amazing, isn't it?

something
> like that.

>
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Creator?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
I think the name Jeremy was looking for is Rear Admiral Grace Murray Hopper
(USNR).    I found a shortish biography at
http://www.cs.yale.edu/homes.tap/files/hopper-story.html that talks about
her role in the development of COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Volker Bandke wrote:

> On Mon, 27 Aug 2001 21:10:28 GMT, "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would have thought that FORTRAN preceded COBOL too. The following is an hierachy chart from a book on OO languages - I can't
agree/disagree the structure, but it's interesting. Bear with me this wretched Netsacape doesn't let me type a decent tree. (What you
type is NOT what you get !). You'll have to 'Maximize' to see the tree clearly :-
.
............................ FORTRAN (kind of the GrandDaddy/GrandMommy)

.......................2.. ALGOL60(1960).....................................  .................................................3.
LISP
..............................................................................................................................................(1958-1961)

2A.COBOL .. 2B.ALGOL68 .......2H.CPL.....................2N.SIMULA
.....................  2C.PASCAL........  2I..BCPL................................
......................................................2J.B(1971)............................
......................................................2K.C(1972)........2O..SMALLTALK.....2P. EIFFEL..............
......................................................2L. C++.........
..................................... ...........(C++ links FROM..
..................................................Simula and TO Modula3)
......... from Pascal
2D..ADA .....2F.Modula2............2M..JAVA

2E.ADA 95...2G.Modula 3

The LISP branch is difficult to show, but there's PROLOG directly from FORTRAN then the group includes SCHEME, ML, GUIDE, Common LISP
and CLOS.

Needless to say OO COBOL is not included <G>. If it was, in my view, it should show a link to SMALLTALK for OO syntax.. OO COBOL owes
nothing to development of JAVA. It was 'in the works' before JAVA took off.

Jimmy

>
```

```
Ed Guy wrote:

> > I would have thought that FORTRAN preceded COBOL too.
>
> It did.  Fortran was 1954. Algol was 1958 (Algol60 was an upgrade).
>

BTW - you recently made a reference to Joe Lyons. Two days ago speaking to a
thirtyish-Canadian working in London, he wasn't aware of the Joe Lyons tea
houses. Perhaps they are defunct. Is Joe Lyons still around ?

That Lector OMR reader of theirs - the concept followed  through my career
until I left UK. ((a) Unigate - although order/delivery documents were printed
and keypunched, it paralleled Joe Lyons turnaround documents for van salesmen.
(b) Debenhams - similarly as you may have noted I got involved with the ICL
OMR  - UDT (Universal Document Transport) .- for Payroll, Hire Purchase, Food
Accounts Payable. For our planned Order System, which never got off the ground
before I left, the OMR was an absolute MUST because of volumes.)

Interesting note - knowing what we were planning our hardware merchandisers
paralleled our draft forms and produced pre-printed forms for stores to order
(manually) Pyrex from James A. Jobling up in Newcastle. Typically, nobody told
their order desk girls what was coming. When they received our orders with
THEIR product codes - they grabbed all the Debenhams forms first. First time
ever, this put them out of stock of major sellers - but we got all that we
wanted !

Just kept coming back to that early concept from the Lector machine <G>

Jimmy
```

```
in the mid 60s.
 
Well ahead of its time, but Canadians wouldn't buy a Canadian machine so it sort of 
"reverse brain drained" to the UK.  There were some VERY bright people working on that 
box. A brilliant chap called Roger Moore (no, not the "saint") who looked like "Flying 
Officer Kite" created "Business Extensions" for Algol so that Sask Power could do its 
commercial work as well as its engineering work in Algol.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creator?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T01:39:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gJXi7.2069$KV3.155210@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<M8vi7.19481$sa.10009360@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com> <8Hyi7.184290$ai2.13131104@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <d7emot8hb8ffiraoc68ni6m1c8cf3qfuu3@4ax.com> <3B8BDC3F.7924E61B@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

If you find a summary of Grace's accomplishments, I believe you will find
that she was the creator of the first "algebraic" compiler known as A-1.
From there other things she did included B-1, and B-2 (became known as
Flowmatic).

I have no doubt, however, that someone somewhere can point to some other
development that fits into the chain and muddels the picture.

For others following this subject, Flowmatic was introduced to commercial
and military customers prior to COBOL.  Work at the AFLC in Ohio extended
B-2 by creating a cross  compiler on a Univac I to generate programs for
other makes of computers in the Univac barn at the time.

Grace's office (including her of course) did a PR job on potential customers
for Flowmatic.  Some of them joined in her search for the next business
compiler, and a number of large companies in various lines of business
(users) created CODASYL, and set up three committees.  Short range, Mediam
and Long Range Committees to start the ball rolling.  Among those who were
on the Executive Committee was Charles Phillips of the DOD.  The short range
committee was given three months to use FLOWMATIC, AMICO (
AFLC's version of FLOWMATIC, AND two works in progress, one by Honeywell,
the other by IBM.  It took the short range group a year to reach agreement
on the first spec. That group was disolved, and the COBOL Committee set up.
At first it operated like congress (a vendors group, and a users group).
This became unworkable (you see it in Washington all of the time), so those
two groups were merged, and rules adopted as to membership between groups,
and other elements.  Bob Beamer, Grace Hopper, and someone from Honeywell
were named to the long range group, and the game plan to obtain vendor
compliance began.  I was one of the people who introduced Flowmatic into our
company, worked with AFLC to create a larger language to reflect the IBM705
line, became a member of the Users Group of the COBOL committee, and was
otherwise involved over a period of 20 years.  Summaries of these events
reflect the view of others who where involved, and these views may not
coinside. While other details might clarify the case of who invented COBOL,
I believe that we should say Grace did, and lots of folks agreed with her.
As far as FORTRAN is concerned, I believe it was an IBM development to
support their scientific systems.  In any case, to the end, I believe Grace
claimed the first algebraic compiler, A-1.

Warren Simmons
warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Creator?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T08:18:38+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8C974E.14BACB1A@Azonic.co.nz>`
- **References:** `<M8vi7.19481$sa.10009360@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com> <8Hyi7.184290$ai2.13131104@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
> 
> COBOL was the first high-level language, 

Not even close.  Not only was it preceeded by Fortran but Cobol was an
amalgam of ideas from existing proprietry high-level languages of the
time, this is why there are references to 'IBM Commercial Translator',
'Flow-Matic' and 'FACT' in the copyright notice.

The DoD wanted a single language that ran on all their machines and not
several proprietry languages and so forced the companies to merge their
own languages into one that they would all implement.

The incentive for doing this was that otherwise the DoD would choose one
language and only use that machine range excluding the other two.
```

```
Tim
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creator?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T02:07:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u7Yi7.2100$KV3.157681@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<M8vi7.19481$sa.10009360@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com> <8Hyi7.184290$ai2.13131104@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3B8C974E.14BACB1A@Azonic.co.nz> <LsUi7.47494$n75.11119336@news4.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
IBM's COBOL Committee member at the time of PL/1 was also a member of a
SHARE
group trying to replace COBOL. In that effort, they included some of the
COBOL features, and the chair of that group in SHARE, called a meeting in
Cinci, OH to convince several large IBM user organizations that they should
drop the support of COBOL, and pick up on PL/1.  I attended.  On the night
of my arrival, I was told about the draft PL/1, given a copy, and expected
to attend the meeting the next day.

This must be like playing chess.

After a quick review, registry of some complaints, and out departure on the
evening flight out of there, the saying "would you believe PL/2" came alive.
I might say that GUIDE AND SHARE were not together on this.

As in all things, it pays to look for the path of the money. What is going
on now with the adoption of open standards, and smooth use of most of the
tools desired today looks like another chess move.

Warren Simmons
warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net

Timothy <timhillock@home.com> wrote in message
news:LsUi7.47494$n75.11119336@news4.rdc1.on.home.com...

mind.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Creator?

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T01:39:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gJXi7.2070$KV3.155088@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<M8vi7.19481$sa.10009360@news1.rdc1.sfba.home.com> <8Hyi7.184290$ai2.13131104@bin2.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com> <3B8C974E.14BACB1A@Azonic.co.nz>`

```
Here is an example of the difference of view surrounding COBOL. While
Richard's view is correct in the minds of many, 'IBM Commercial Translator'
was the name of the contribution of the IBM at that time, Grace, and Bob
Beamer worked closely to take care of each other's needs. The Honeywell
language was under development by Computer Science, and specs for it were
not available to us.

DOD's membership of the CODASYL group was in a position to require the
yearly declaration of each vendor doing business with them to sign and
return a contract each year. Charlie arranged three things for COBOL.

1. He arranged with the DOJ that users and vendors could in these
circomstances meet together legally.

2. In that first year following the publication of COBOL, vendors selling
computers to the Government's Military branches at least had to answer one
question before the renewal would occur.  That was something to the effect
of :
Will you or will you not supply a COBOL Complier for your hardware?
Those who failed to answer had to wait for their funds until they did.

3. Made it easy for various branches of the service to become members of the
Users Group of the COBOL committee.

Warren Simmons
warren.simmons@worldnet.att.net

news:3B8C974E.14BACB1A@Azonic.co.nz...
```

<a id="t-2e3bc34d5a"></a>
## Thread: STD - correction to previous information that I have posted — 1 msgs

### STD - correction to previous information that I have posted

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T21:29:42-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mevqp$qdm$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Previously, I had indicated that ONE of the differences between the
J4/NCITS/ANSI "group" versus the WG4/SC22/ISO "group"- was that "ANSI"
things were (by definition) "open" while ISO things were not.

Today (while dealing with something else), I discovered that it *is* true
that all J4 meetings are "open" and that all of their documents are
"available to the public" - while WG4 meetings are closed and their
documents are *not* generally available.

HOWEVER, it is *not* true that NCITS or ANSI meetings and documents are "by
definition" open to anyone who requests them.  Past (and current) J4 (COBOL)
"project proposals" that were approved for doing various revisions ('85,
Intrinsic Functions, Corrections Amendment, Full Revision, etc) have
included the following wording:

"4.4 Legal Considerations ...
 Public Interest: Public interest is served by opening all meetings to the
public. Committee membership is opened to all engaged in some aspect of
COBOL use or implementation.  All committee documents are available for
public review through the X3 secretariat."

This *does* confirm (as far as I understand it) that the requirement for
"open meetings" and ability to review J4 documents *did* exist at one time.
HOWEVER, there is no such statement (that I am currently aware of) for NCITS
or ANSI documentation or meetings.  Furthermore, I was informed today that
the "project proposal" that includes this statement is an "historical"
document and does not (necessarily) still apply. (Actually, as I recall the
requirement for membership that one must be  "involved with COBOL" was
removed a few years ago.)  This does *not* mean that J4 has "closed"
meetings or hidden documents.

If you are interested in the document (semi-poor scan quality) that this
quote is from, see
 http://www.ncits.org/temp/pp23b.pdf

available from
 http://www.ncits.org/tc_home/j4.htm

Again, I am not in ANY WAY claiming that there is or has been a change in
"normal ways of working" at J4, NCITS, or ANSI (much less WG4, SC22, or
ISO).  I simply wanted to publicly "correct" incorrect information that I
had previously communicated.
```

---

<a id="t-347dadd78f"></a>
## Thread: Been there, Done that, STILL wearing the T-shirt <G> — 3 msgs

### Been there, Done that, STILL wearing the T-shirt <G>

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-27T21:43:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mf0jl$sk$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I just had to share the following quote with this group,

"Revision of elements currently in the standard.
    The maximum of five years as stipulated by ANSI procedures has been
shown to be somewhat long for COBOL, given the current rate of advancement
in computer technology.  ... Something closer to three years would appear to
be more effective. It would have the double effect of minimizing
revolutionary changes (resulting from excessive delays between revisions)
and keeping the standard closer to defacto practices."

Ok, ready to LAUGH ???

    S
    C
    R
    O
    L
    L

     |
    V






That quote is from March 1974 - when J4 (then X3J4) was just getting
approval for what was (eventually) to become the '85 Standard !!!

For the complete document (well worth its weight in laughs and smiles), see
  http://www.ncits.org/temp/pp23.pdf
```

## Thread: browsing a transient data queue — 8 msgs

### browsing a transient data queue

- **From:** alex <aort527@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T06:37:01-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8B825D.7D76AC53@hotmail.com>`

```
I run a CICS transaction that runs a Natural Program (Software AG).
The display output from this transaction had to be routed to a
TRANSIENT DATA QUEUE to keep CICS from abending.

Does anybody know how to browse this TRANSIENT DATA QUEUE???
```

#### ↳ Re: browsing a transient data queue

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T16:20:27+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6h6notsiti3nme2o5hqglme91pb8l91u4s@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8B825D.7D76AC53@hotmail.com>`

```
On Tue, 28 Aug 2001 06:37:01 -0500 alex <aort527@hotmail.com> wrote:

:>I run a CICS transaction that runs a Natural Program (Software AG).
:>The display output from this transaction had to be routed to a
:>TRANSIENT DATA QUEUE to keep CICS from abending.

:>Does anybody know how to browse this TRANSIENT DATA QUEUE???

   EXEC CICS READQ TD

You can do it thru CECI and CEDF as well.
```

#### ↳ Re: browsing a transient data queue

- **From:** Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T21:44:50+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<josephk-8F4E0F.21445028082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3B8B825D.7D76AC53@hotmail.com>`

```
In article <3B8B825D.7D76AC53@hotmail.com>, alex <aort527@hotmail.com> 
wrote:

> I run a CICS transaction that runs a Natural Program (Software AG).
> The display output from this transaction had to be routed to a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

You cannot "BROWSE" a Transient Data Queue.
You can read the data off - which destroys the queue - and then put it 
back.

Check out transaction CEBR.

You run CEBR without parameters. This assigns you a Temporary Storage 
Queue. Then issue the GET command to read the TD q into the TS q and 
display it. If you want to keep the data in the TD q, do a PUT command 
to write the TS q back to the TD q.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: browsing a transient data queue

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T21:20:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108282020.578c6a6c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8B825D.7D76AC53@hotmail.com> <josephk-8F4E0F.21445028082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
Joseph J Katnic <josephk@iinet.net.au> wrote in message news:<josephk-8F4E0F.21445028082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au>...
> In article <3B8B825D.7D76AC53@hotmail.com>, alex <aort527@hotmail.com> 
> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> to write the TS q back to the TD q.

The concept of "destructive reads" of Transient Data is a popular
misconception.  Transient Data is a catch-all for a number of data
structures/devices, including Sequential Data Sets (QSAM).  The CICS
control program reads the file, and just as with any other program,
cannot re-read a record once it has been passed.  Therefore, the
Current Record Pointer is kept by the CICS control program, and
multiple CICS application programs reading the same file ("queue")
start where the last one left off.  It IS possible, using commands
usually kept secret by Systems Programmers <G>, to close and re-open
the file, and read it again.

In alex's case, you can't read it WHILE it is still open for the
task(s) which is (are) writing to it, since it was opened in CICS as
either OUTPUT or EXTEND.
 
The file can be read in batch programs, or by an assortment of on-line
utilities such as File-Aid or SPF.

Stephen J Spiro
President
Wizard Systems
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: browsing a transient data queue

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T08:53:46+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dl2pot0mk4gh7g9gf5eufnmhelg780fdfh@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8B825D.7D76AC53@hotmail.com> <josephk-8F4E0F.21445028082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <4145699b.0108282020.578c6a6c@posting.google.com>`

```
On 28 Aug 2001 21:20:04 -0700, stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J
Spiro) wrote:

>The concept of "destructive reads" of Transient Data is a popular
>misconception.  Transient Data is a catch-all for a number of data
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>the file, and read it again.

Well, this is partially true, as far as QSAM files are concerned.  In
CICS we call the Extrapartition Destinations.  They can either be
opened for Input, or for output, and not both at the same time, once
you read a record, you cannot read it again until you close and reopen
the queue.  So far, so good

A lot of CICS TQ queues are Intrapartition Destinations, though.
These can be read from one task, and written by another task _at the
same time_.  The reads are truely destructive, as there is no way to
read the same record again later.  Space of read record (better CIs)
can be reused for records of completely different queues.  The rules
change a little bit when recovery comes into the picture - Records are
retained until the task completes successfully
```

```
> Spiro) wrote:

Volker,

As you well know, I know zilch about mainframe programming. But as I read you and Stephen, it set me wondering. Of
course Queues as such are nothing new to programming, covered by introductory courses on Data Structures. I have a
'91 COBOL '85 textbook where the authors devoted a chapter to Data Structures describing Queues, Stacks, Chained
Lists (OO languages call them Linked Lists).

Just musing through my  head - as and when OO collections become generally available, I wonder if they will help
solve those problems above, read once, but you can't read again until you've gone through your close and open
process. The simplest form of collection, is an Ordered Collection, where records can be added to the 'Tail' as
'intrinsics' (pic 9s or pic x's). They can be retrieved using indexing techniques (pointers if you like). The Net
Express on-line documentation suggests using Ordered Collections for Queues or Stacks, without even having to
'dream up' a new collection template to handle these..

Linked Lists (in the Collection hierarchy of OO utilities) could be a possibility in OO COBOL when J4 discuss this
topic in depth. Curious - can anybody illustrate with a current example where they effectively use Linked/Chained
Lists currently, either non-OO COBOL, or OO COBOL and other OO languages ? (What's behind my question is that
Linked Lists techniques might be possible using other collection class types, without the necessity of a specific
class for Linked Lists).

PS: Evaluates - enjoyed the following <G>

>   DISPLAY "Oops, we have a Hermaphrodite"

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: browsing a transient data queue

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T09:40:55+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aiauotcqqsi84l9o7gpp759059cl7v4o8p@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8B825D.7D76AC53@hotmail.com> <josephk-8F4E0F.21445028082001@echo-01.iinet.net.au> <4145699b.0108282020.578c6a6c@posting.google.com> <dl2pot0mk4gh7g9gf5eufnmhelg780fdfh@4ax.com> <3B8D27D3.6A01833D@home.com>`

```
On Wed, 29 Aug 2001 17:31:36 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:
>As you well know, I know zilch about mainframe programming. 

don't worry about that, there are quite a few people around with that
condition  <g>


>Of course Queues as such are nothing new to programming, covered by introductory courses on Data Structures.

Correct.  That is one of the problems there is with CICS Transient
Data Queues.  They come in several flavours, the QSAM variety (the one
Stephen spoke about), which is a QUEUE (in the sense of FIFO
processing).  Closing and Reopening for reading makes the contents
available again

The second flavour, though, is a special type of QUEUE which, in a
UN*X context, is called a PIPE

The use of such a PIPE in CICS is traditionally like this:

Many applications/end users (like people ordering some article)  drop
some information into one end of the pipe (via WRITEQ).  Those could
be termed the PIPE clients. on the other end of the PIPE there is a
single task (the PIPE server, then) which
a) get's automatically started by CICS as soon as data comes its way
b) Removes the data from the PIPE (via READQ) and does is processing
(like printing shipping papers, invoices, whatever).  This part is
repeated over and over until there are no more data, then the server
process terminates

From the application point of view, only one record is available at
any one time:  You can only write to the end of the PIPE, and you can
only read from the beginning of the pipe

Linked Lists are a different solution to a different problem.  They
allow to access a specific element out of a set without having to know
its explicit position (no index, no direct pointer) by following the
set elements via the link pointers

Technically, a CICS INTRAPARTITION TRANSIENT DATA DESTINATION (that is
the full term for the thing I called PIPE above) is maintained as a
LINKED LIST on disk (in a VSAM ESDS)

I only use LINKED LIST when following control block chains, not
usually for end user application data storage

      
      
      
      
                                                            
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_        
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,     
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'   
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)        
                                                            
      What does CICS mean? - Can't Implement Consistent Software
      
        (Another Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)
```

## Thread: Acu: Right click in an entry-field — 4 msgs

### Acu: Right click in an entry-field

- **From:** "Erlend Moen" <erlend.moen@disystemer.no>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T16:52:30+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mgb6r$jq1$1@stockholm.tele1europe.no>`

```
I have an entry-field that is initially read-only. However I would like to
detect if the user right-clicks in the field and thereby activate a
function.

I've tried to play with the mouse-flags without any luck. I've tried to play
with the KEYSTROKE-environment-variable. No matter what I do, the
copy/cut/paste/undo/select all-menu pops up when I right click. There must
be a way to solve this? Anyone?

Regards,

Erlend
```

#### ↳ Re: Acu: Right click in an entry-field

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T23:13:52+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<UX$KVIAgeBj7IwGg@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<9mgb6r$jq1$1@stockholm.tele1europe.no>`

```
Hi Erlend,

The only thing you can do is declare your own right-click ('popup') menu 
for that entry field (use the POP-UP MENU phrase). You can then detect a 
menu selection in the normal way. Hope this helps!

Best regards

Nigel
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Acu: Right click in an entry-field

- **From:** Nigel Eaton <nigele@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T10:27:27+01:00

- **Message-ID:** `<YG4ZhtA$bgj7Iw2R@rcav8r.demon.co.uk>`

```
Actually, on reflection, there may be another approach. You could use 
(instead of an entry-field) a one row, one column grid control.

This will look and feel much like an entry-field but has the advantage 
of generating both MSG-GRID-RBUTTON-DOWN and MSG-GRID-RBUTTON-UP events.

It may introduce different "challenges" (I haven't actually tried this 
lately) but may give a more seamless appearance if you need it.

Best regards

Nigel
```

## Thread: EVALUATE problem — 52 this month / 108 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### EVALUATE problem

- **From:** Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T08:57:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
OK-I must be missing something. I'm evaluating a status variable & want
to take the same action for 2 different values but I haven't found any
way to do so except to code it twice. I just can't believe that that's
necessary, so what am I missing?

EVALUATE STATUS-FIELD
    WHEN 'A'
        PERFORM A-OR-B-ACTION
    WHEN 'B'
        PERFORM A-OR-B-ACTION
END-EVALUATE.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "Jeff Farkas" <JeffFarkas@noland.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T12:54:45-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mgieq$201mi$1@ID-101435.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
Try this out..

EVALUATE TRUE
   WHEN STATUS-FIELD = 'A'
      PERFORM A-OR-B-ACTION
   WHEN STATUS-FIELD = 'B' OR 'C'
     PERFORM A-OR-B-ACTION
END-EVALUATE

Hope it helps,
Jeff
```

```
On 28-Aug-2001, Calvin Crumrine <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us> wrote:

> OK-I must be missing something. I'm evaluating a status variable & want
> to take the same action for 2 different values but I haven't found any
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-EVALUATE.

This is working code:
 EVALUATE C-TRA97-FLAG
     WHEN 'G'
     WHEN 'L'
     WHEN 'N'
     WHEN 'O'
     WHEN 'P'
     WHEN 'R'
     WHEN 'S'
     WHEN 'T'
       CONTINUE
     WHEN OTHER
        SET SW-INPUT-ERROR TO TRUE
        DISPLAY 'CONVERT-DATA TABLE ERROR, RECORD :'
                 CONVERT-DATA
        DISPLAY '             C-TRA97-FLAG="'
                                    C-TRA97-FLAG          '"'
 END-EVALUATE.
```

```
>OK-I must be missing something. I'm evaluating a status variable & want
>to take the same action for 2 different values but I haven't found any
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>END-EVALUATE.


You can just have the WHENs with the last one in the list specifiying the
action, such as:

EVALUATE STATUS-FIELD
    WHEN 'A'
    WHEN 'B'
        PERFORM A-OR-B-ACTION
END-EVALUATE.


WHEN 'A' OR 'B', as suggested by another poster will not work, at least not on
the compilers I've used.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T15:12:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mgu73$lt3$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <3B8BDE01.65976EB9@home.com> <9mgn7v$21go2$1@ID-101435.news.dfncis.de>`

```
Parts of this thread seem to be "out of order" - but to test a single data
item for two values, there are two "valid" (conforming" ways of doing this,

Evaluate Data-Item
  When "Value1"
  When "value2"
      Perform Either-1-or-2
 End-Evaluate

   *OR*

Evaluate True
  When Data-Item = "Value1" or "Value2"
    Perform either-1-or-2
End-Evaluate

the one thing you may NOT do  is

Evaluate Data-Item
  When "Value1" or "Value2"
    Perform either-1-or-2
End-Evaluate

   ***

Actually, there is also a 3rd technique which is using 88-levels, eg.

01 Data-Item Pic X(10)
    88  Good-Value  "Value1" "Value2".

Evaluate True
   When Good-Value
      Perform either-1-or-2
End-Evaluate

With only "two values" to be tested, which you use is pretty much a matter
of choice.  With more complex situations, you really can find one "clearer"
than the other.
```

```
Wondered when you would jump in <G>. Remembered that one after my second
message; could have written it :-

01 char                        pic x.
    88 AField                value "A".
    88 BField                value "B".
    88 ValidChar           value "A" thru "B".

move "A" to char
Evaluate true
    when ValidChar etc.....
    when other etc....

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T20:12:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8BFC08.CC74FDF3@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <3B8BDE01.65976EB9@home.com> <9mgn7v$21go2$1@ID-101435.news.dfncis.de>`

```


Jeff Farkas wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
> news:3B8BDE01.65976EB9@home.com...
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> tried it with MF 4.3 and it doesn't complie.

Sorry Jeff, that was written in shorthand from memory <G>. Net Express 3.1.
Trouble is there are just SO MANY options with the EVALUATE. The following
works but CHECK-IT-4 fails in this particular piece of code - you would hardly
want to put two tests on the same field !.

Generally speaking, I think the code for CHECK-IT-2  and 3 is the
easiest(clearest)  to follow.
------------------------------------------------------------
PROGRAM-ID. Evaluate1.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 char                         pic x.
   88 Afield                    value "A".
   88 BField                    value "B".
   PROCEDURE DIVISION.
move "A" to char
perform CHECK-IT-1
perform CHECK-IT-2
perform CHECK-IT-3
perform CHECK-IT-4
move "B" to char
perform CHECK-IT-1
perform CHECK-IT-2
perform CHECK-IT-3
perform CHECK-IT-4
STOP RUN.

CHECK-IT-1.
Evaluate true
    when char = "A" or char = "B"
        display "Check1 - I've got A or B"
    when other
        display "Check1 - Test failed"
End-evaluate
.
CHECK-IT-2.
Evaluate true
    when char = "A"
    when char = "B"
        display "Check2 - I've got A or B"
    when other
        display "Check2 - Test failed"
End-evaluate
.
CHECK-IT-3.
Evaluate true
  when Afield
  when BField
        display "Check3 - I've got A or B"
    when other
        display "Check3 - Test failed"
End-evaluate
.
CHECK-IT-4.
Evaluate true also true
    when char = "A" also char = "B"
        display "Check4 - I've got A or B"
    when other
        display "Check4 - Test failed"
End-evaluate
------------------------------------------------------.
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

Jimmy, I don't think so,  The first WHEN can never become true!  Look
at it like this

EVALUATE TRUE
   WHEN gender="male" ALSO gender="female"
      DISPLAY "Oops, we have a Hermaphrodite"
      DISPLAY "Do they really exist"
   WHEN OTHER
      display "This is probably the case that will always be displayed
END-EVALUATE
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Evaluate true
>     when char = "A" or char = "B"

You could say here:
      WHEN Char = "A" OR "B"

>         display "Check1 - I've got A or B"
>     when other
>         display "Check1 - Test failed"
> End-evaluate



> Evaluate true also true
>     when char = "A" also char = "B"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> End-evaluate

While that will compile I don't think that you checked or tested it. 
The ALSO is an AND and so the  display should be changed to: DISPLAY
"Check4 - I've got an A _and_ a B in the same character"
```

```
In Realia WB 3.1, the WHEN 'A' OR 'B' only works with an EVALUATE TRUE 
as was suggested before.  However, you can use WHEN 'A' THRU 'B' and it 
will work.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T20:51:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108281951.298964fd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <3B8BDE01.65976EB9@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote 

> 
> EVALUATE STATUS-FIELD
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


As some have pointed out, this doesn't work (SOME compilers may allow
it).
" 'A' or 'B' " is a PARTIAL CONDITIONAL EXPRESSION, inexplicably left
out of the syntax in the 1985 standard.  It WILL be in the next
standard.  It was already in the draft when I joined the committee in
1995, and has been one of the most stable features in the proposal. 
I'm surprised more compiler makers have not implemented it yet.
(HINT!)

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
check it out at 
http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/

stephenjspiro at hotmail.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T00:52:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mi02f$eov$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <3B8BDE01.65976EB9@home.com> <4145699b.0108281951.298964fd@posting.google.com>`

```
"Stephen J Spiro" <stephenjspiro@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:4145699b.0108281951.298964fd@posting.google.com...
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote
>
 <snip>
>
> As some have pointed out, this doesn't work (SOME compilers may allow
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 1995,
and has been one of the most stable features in the proposal.
> I'm surprised more compiler makers have not implemented it yet.
> (HINT!)
>

Actually - this isn't (quite) a valid "partial expression" in the draft
Standard.  To be valid it would need to have an "=" added to it, e.g.

Evaluate Field-1
   When = "A" or "B"
     Perform  A-or-B-routine
End-Evaluate

With the next Standard, this type of coding can actually be expanded to
something like:

Evaluate Field-1
   When = "A" or "B"
     Perform  A-or-B-routine
    When < "L"
      Peform Another-Routine
    When Not >=  "R"
      Perform Something-else
 End-Evaluate
End-Evaluate
```

```
Micro Focus Net Express and Server Express compilers.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T03:10:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
In my never ending quest to understand the COBOL standards (which may or may
not apply to my COBOL maintenance work of the last 30+ years), I really
would like an answer to my question.

I'm probably showing my complete ignorance to the current COBOL standards,
but  in the COBOL environment that I'm in, the code would be:

IF STATUS-FIELD = "A" OR "B"
   PERFORM A-OR-B-ACTION
        ELSE
   PERFORM IT-AINT-A-OR-B
END-IF.

And to be quite honest, my actual code would be minus the else and end-if,
since all the compilers I work with default to the now forbidden "next
sentence", which would be any code that doesn't meet the "if" condition.

I'm really not trying to cause a fuss here, but am I so out of date that I
now should be using "Evaluate" instead of "If" in a two variable
environment, and more than that, should I have a dozen different replies as
to how the "Evaluate" should really be coded?

As I move into my twilight years of COBOL maintenance, should I just give it
up and become a PC technician, 'cause I've observed that whatever is
standard today on a PC sure isn't standard tomorrow!  And I've become quite
good at dealing with that <G>!

Denny
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T04:42:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8C7310.DAA74723@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```


Denny Brouse wrote:

> In my never ending quest to understand the COBOL standards (which may or may
> not apply to my COBOL maintenance work of the last 30+ years), I really
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The short answer Denny is that the above is succinct and fits your particular
need, plus as you say you can code it, quite cleanly without the End-if. That
works for you - stick with it ! I've got into the habit of using ENDs and it
comes naturally and with OO code/ call it parallel to a PERFORM PARA - I find it
only necessary to use that final 'Period/Full Stop'. Actually in OO I don't even
do that because the following gives me the 'Period/Full Stop' :-

        some code.....
        some more code    (no period/full stop)

End method. "doSomething".


> And to be quite honest, my actual code would be minus the else and end-if,
> since all the compilers I work with default to the now forbidden "next
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As you get into you twilight years only use those EVALUATES if you have a need.
The sort of simple rules I use :-

    (a) Simple test - if xxxx else xx end-if
    (b) Get beyond that where you need to nest IFs and I opt for EVALUATES
         visually it looks cleaner (i.e. indentation)
    (c) But to 'kick-off' an OO program with my "begin" Method, I'll opt for a
        'simple' set of IFs :-

    set ContinueProgram  to true
    invoke self "openFiles"

    if    ContinueProgram
          invoke self "createDialogs"

        if ContinueProgram
            invoke self "createCollections"

            if ContinueProgram
                invoke self "printReport"

                if ContinueProgram
                    invoke self "closeFiles"

        .            <<<----- note just one period/full stop

Both a blessing and a curse - COBOL gives us so many options to do the same
thing.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T10:41:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TF3j7.19797$0W6.2619964578@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <3B8C7310.DAA74723@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

Thanks very much for clarifying that.  I was hoping I wasn't inserting
maintenance code that would cause the next guy after me to curse me.  I have
seen the Evaluate statement in some of the programs I'm maintaining and it
certainly beats nested ifs.  It's use has also caused a bit more maintenance
for me if I have to insert new routines into a complex Evaluate, since I'm
really not used to using it, and based on this thread, I'm not the only guy
who has a little trouble with it.  But overall, I understand how it works,
and use it where needed.

As yet, I have not moved into OO, although the Fujitsu PowerCobol product
I'm working with now is event driven and from what you described is
certainly similar to OO.  Hopefully, I'll continue to learn as much as I
can.  I just don't want to leave too big of mess for the next guy.

Thanks,

Denny
```

```
> > Or where you expect a likelihood of future WHENs being added to the
> > logic.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> checked everything ?"

In pilot training, I always wondered whether the maintenance crew were told
to leave a screw loose on T37s occasionally - and then note whether we
caught it.  That was the first training jet and they seemed to have loose
screws fairly often in their skin.   Maybe they just got loose a lot
naturally - that was a tough old bird anyway, and catching it probably
didn't matter.

But certainly a trainee didn't want his trainer to find something overlooked
by the trainee.

Pilots have to be anal about such things.
```

```
I have apre-compiler (IDMS) that puts in "NEXT SENTENCE", but my compiler
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T11:43:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PFpj7.13869$qh7.1679457324@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <9mirl9$4ph$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard,

We compile on a Unisys NX-5600 (COBOL85).  I didn't mean to imply that the
compiler actually put in the words "next sentence".  Compiled Unisys COBOL
code tends use the assembler code of BNE (branch not equal) when the IF
statement is false and the "branch not equal" always goes to the "next
sentence".  So most of the code I see (I didn't write most of
it-maintenance, you know <g>), is coded:

IF REPORT-CODE = "A"
   PERFORM REPORT-A-CODE.
IF REPORT-CODE = "B"
   PERFORM REPORT-B-CODE.
IF REPORT-CODE = "C"
   PERFORM REPORT-C-CODE.

Or I still have a bunch of programs that use GO TO DEPENDING ON.

Maybe not the most efficient code, but quite easy to add:

IF REPORT-CODE = "Z"
   PERFORM REPORT-Z-CODE.

or modify the GO TO DEPENDING ON code.

It's funny, but this compiler also has a "WAIT" verb, which I noticed caused
quite a discussion elsewhere in this group.  It can be used to WAIT xx
seconds, wait on an input event, wait on a queued event, etc.

Even though most of the world is IBM, and I did start my programming career
on IBM gear, it would be hard for me to go back to that environment now.

Denny

"Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu> wrote in message
news:9mirl9$4ph$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 28-Aug-2001, "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> > And to be quite honest, my actual code would be minus the else and
end-if,
> > since all the compilers I work with default to the now forbidden "next
> > sentence", which would be any code that doesn't meet the "if" condition.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> SENTENCE", and how does that work?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T20:15:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8E9FB7.15E3CED7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <9mirl9$4ph$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <PFpj7.13869$qh7.1679457324@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com>`

```


Denny Brouse wrote:

> Howard,
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well Denny on this one I think you are wrong if the code is EXACTLY as you
illustrate above <G>. It is inefficient. Should there be a PERIOD after every IF
or is it as you've written, a nested set of IFs. with only a final Period..

(1) Assuming each IF is separate with a PERIOD. If = "A" you are gong to perform
"A" - BUT - then you are gong to unnecessarily test the rest, after having
printed Report "A"..

(2) Assuming it is nested, (which I don't think is what you intended - because
you haven't used any indentation), then if it is anything but "A" it is not
going to fall through and check the rest.!

This is where EVALUATE pays off in spades, including your maintenance adding the
"Z" :-

Evaluate true
   when  REPORT-CODE = "A"
             PERFORM REPORT-A-CODE.
    when  REPORT-CODE = "B"
              PERFORM REPORT-B-CODE.
    when   REPORT-CODE = "C"
              PERFORM REPORT-C-CODE.
    when other (eXtremeProgramming) - "Oops I've got an error" OR "Who cares,
              ignore it !"
End-evaluate

Now you want to add your "Z" report - just stick a line in above the "when
other".

As an afterthought you establish you *actually* produce more "C" reports than
any other - rearrange and put the "C" at the top, so it gets the first hit.

You could also use "Evaluate false" - but I hate that - testing if not negative.
It's like the sloppy English saying, "I aint got none" and you know what that
really translates to. Mind you, don't confine criticism to the Brits. Oxford
Dictionary got so tired of the continual N. American abuse of the language that
they eventually , reluctantly, included a new word "irregardless". ( I read that
in the newspapers but can't check it out because they are no longer on-line due
to copyright problems. It's about the same time, on pronunciation,  that they
a-OK'd the Chairman of the Board singing 'Nooh Yorrrk, Nooh Yorrrk").

PS: Netscape spellchecker kicks out 'irregardless' as invalid for both US and UK
options.

Jimmy
```

```

On 30-Aug-2001, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

> > IF REPORT-CODE = "A"
> >    PERFORM REPORT-A-CODE.
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> going to fall through and check the rest.!

That is assuming the test IS unneccessary.   It is quite possible that
REPORT-A-CODE resets REPORT-CODE.   But if you want to do something like
that - make sure to document it in the code!!   And if you don't - then that
is bad code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T15:08:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mmdgj$egi$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <9mirl9$4ph$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <PFpj7.13869$qh7.1679457324@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <3B8E9FB7.15E3CED7@home.com>`

```
Just to clarify, the COBOL85 compiler on the Unisys NX5600 generates, for
    EVALUATE REPORT-CODE
    WHEN "A" PERFORM REPORT-A-CODE
    WHEN "B" PERFORM REPORT-B-CODE ...
the same object code as for
    EVALUATE TRUE
    WHEN REPORT-CODE = "A" PERFORM REPORT-A-CODE
    WHEN REPORT-CODE = "B" PERFORM REPORT-B-CODE ...
which for this simple case is the same code as for
    IF REPORT-CODE = "A" PERFORM REPORT-A-CODE
    ELSE IF REPORT-CODE = "B" PERFORM REPORT-B-CODE ...

All three of these avoid the "retest" that
    IF REPORT-CODE = "A" PERFORM REPORT-A-CODE.
    IF REPORT-CODE = "B" PERFORM REPORT-B-CODE.
entails.

And, by the way, there is no assembler on the Unisys NX-5600, or any of its
ancestors, nor is there a BNE operator among the mnemonics for the hardware
instructions.  The mnemonics for the code generated for testing the result
of these single-character comparisons are EQUL, BRFL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Denny Brouse" <denden@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T04:00:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hZDj7.21257$5Q5.2874309655@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <9mirl9$4ph$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <PFpj7.13869$qh7.1679457324@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <3B8E9FB7.15E3CED7@home.com> <9mmdgj$egi$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck,

Thanks for clarifying that.  I knew I'd probably open up a can of worms
here, but I'm just trying to provide point of view from a non-mainstream
(i.e. non IBM) platform, which I absolutely love to work on by the way.

And I do try to use END-IFs nowadays, but keep in mind I'm the only Unisys
programmer in our shop.  I handle maintenance on about 230 COBOL programs
(most of which thankfully don't need much maintenance per se), the PC
programming, the MCP upgrades,  COMS and network TCP/IP support, and support
for over 50 clients at our disaster recovery center.  So if I have to add a
"Z" report, even though I'd really like to rewrite the code to become more
standards friendly and efficient using the Evaluate, I simply do not have
the time to do so.  As I see new releases coming from Unisys, it seems I'm
not the only one who doesn't have the time to do this <G>.

Remember that this code was ported over from a V-series, i.e. IPS835 versus
A-IPS 030, so some of the code has become by default "non-efficient".  The
BNE was from the V-Series days.  Kind of like the V-SERIES "COPY A= FROM
HERE TO THERE" which you can no longer do on the A/NX Platform.  Geez...wild
cards even existed in DOS 2.1.  I can provide specific code examples of what
I mentioned in my posts if you wish, but what really is the point?  As I
said before "I didn't write this code, I just have to work with it".  My new
programs have a more "compliant" look to them, and I do use Evaluate and
END-IF if I need to.  I just don't get to write that many new programs (gee,
there's a typical maintenance statement if I ever heard one).

Thanks to all for humoring me.  I've had a bad week with complaints on Check
Image Quality and I seem to be venting here.  Let's see....three check
sorters running 2000 checks per minute all the while they are microfilming,
photo imaging, endorsing, pocketing to different pockets and updating a
database to the tune of 400,000-600,000 checks per day.  And the images are
a little fuzzy at times?  Give me a break. It simply boggles my mind that
all this stuff can happen this quickly, and 98% of the code is in COBOL.
Also, the check sorter programs are written in Motorola 68000 Assembler, so
Assembler in fact does exist on the NX-5600.  COBOL just doesn't generate it
<BG>.  Oh, did I mention that I change those programs once in a while too?
<VBG>.

I'm done now.

Denny
```

```
Getting back to:

Evaluate true also true
when char = "A" also char = "B"
display "Check4 - I've got A or B"
when other
display "Check4 - Test failed"
End-evaluate

"Check4 - I've got A or B" will never display. This is the kind of error that
the compiler should catch. A warning msg, at minimum, is called for. How about
it, committee guys?

Jack
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T20:03:20-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mpc7n$ie4$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <9mirl9$4ph$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <PFpj7.13869$qh7.1679457324@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <3B8E9FB7.15E3CED7@home.com> <9mmdgj$egi$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <pvTj7.76$4z.1018@www.newsranger.com>`

```
The draft standard *does* allow (but not require) compilers to "flag"
(reject? other?) when a program violates a "semantic" rule (not a syntax
rule), e.g.

01  Field-0  Pic 9 Value 0.
  ..
Compute SomeNumeric = 10 / (field-0 - field-0)

(i.e. divide by zero)

However, there is not (and I hope there never is) a REQUIREMENT that
compilers have to do "full logic" checking.  Taking this example, would you
want it to flag

01  Field-A  Pic X Value "a'.
01  Another-A Pic X Value "a".

Evaluate True Also True
  When somefield = Field-A also somefield < Another-A or > another-A

Must it check to see if any of these field are ever modified?  How about if
a CALL statement *might* give addressability to where these fields are
defined?

Bottom-Line:
  Compilers check SYNTAX (and *may* give you "helpful hints" when something
is EASY for them to do - given their particular compiler design);
  Compilers do NOT check that you know what you are coding or that you
understand run-time logic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Irregardless

- **From:** Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com>

```
On Thu, 30 Aug 2001 20:15:21 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:
 Oxford
>Dictionary got so tired of the continual N. American abuse of the language that
>they eventually , reluctantly, included a new word "irregardless". 

From my American Heritage Dictionary (1994)

 irï¿½reï¿½gardï¿½less (ï¿½rï¿½ï¿½-gï¿½rdï¿½lï¿½s) adv. Non-Standard. Regardless.
[Perhaps from ir(respective) + regardless.]
ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ï¿½ 
USAGE NOTE: The label Non-Standard does only approximate justice to
the status of irregardless. More precisely, it is a form that many
people mistakenly believe to be a correct usage in formal style but
that in fact has no legitimate antecedents in either standard or
nonstandard varieties. (The word was likely coined from a blend of
irrespective and regardless.) Perhaps this is why critics have
sometimes insisted that there is ï¿½no such wordï¿½ as irregardless, a
charge they would not think of leveling at a bona fide nonstandard
word such as ain't, which has an ancient genealogy.

        (Another Wisdom from my fortune cookie jar)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Irregardless

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T17:01:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8FC3C4.46690A4@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <9mirl9$4ph$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <PFpj7.13869$qh7.1679457324@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <3B8E9FB7.15E3CED7@home.com> <30juotklopq2fhs2aulk83k18268939i76@4ax.com>`

```


Volker Bandke wrote:

> On Thu, 30 Aug 2001 20:15:21 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
> wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> word such as ain't, which has an ancient genealogy.

Aint that the truth <G>. I'll use the word for 'fun' when writing, but 'die' before
I'd use it verbally. Years ago BBC TV did a series 'The Pallisers" basically about
politics, roughly 1840-1860 time span. The word "ain't" kept cropping up as
legitimate usage by the genteel society portrayed in the series. I haven't checked,
but it's a fair bet Charles Dickens put the word into the mouths of some of his
characters. Then again getting into the 20th century, Gershwin's Porgy and Bess, "It
aint necessarily so......".

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Irregardless

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard.brazee@cusys.edu>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T18:13:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mok1v$qhr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <f2Zi7.19572$te2.2568523016@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <9mirl9$4ph$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <PFpj7.13869$qh7.1679457324@newssvr17.news.prodigy.com> <3B8E9FB7.15E3CED7@home.com> <30juotklopq2fhs2aulk83k18268939i76@4ax.com> <3B8FC3C4.46690A4@home.com>`

```

On 31-Aug-2001, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:

> Aint that the truth <G>. I'll use the word for 'fun' when writing, but 
> 'die' before  I'd use it verbally.

At one time, "verbally" didn't mean "spoken rather than written".  Only the
other definitions applied.  I like to think of people who use "verbally" and
"orally" as synonyms as having "verbal sex" as well.

But as this thread shows - language changes.  (I don't use "loan" as a verb,
nor say "media is", nor "Data is" (to keep on topic)).

An acceptable plural of schema is schemata - so I suppose is subschemata.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T12:12:06+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b8d86f3_6@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us>`

```
The problem here is not with EVALUATE, it's with the choice of construct.

Use IF.

if  status-field = 'A'
            OR
    status-field = 'B'
         perform A-or-B-process
end-if

The main objection will be "future maintenance". What happens when we add
other statuses that "map" to the same or different processes? (EVALUATE is
ideally suited to such a scenario...)

Loadsa options...

1. Change the "end-if" to an "else" and add further "if"s...

2. Let the next poor sod who maintains it deal with it...

3. Convert each status-field value into a binary number then use GO
TO...DEPENDING ON...

4. Convert each status-field  value into a memory address of the procedure
you want it to be processed by, then set a pointer with the status field and
branch on it. (Very elegant...really like this one...)

5. Wait for the next COBOL Standard to be produced at which point partial
conditionals in EVALUATE will be supported, and you can say WHEN 'A' OR 'B'
(Good option...by the time this happens you won't be working there...in fact
there may not even still be a Company)

HTH

Pete.

"Calvin Crumrine" <Calvin_Crumrine@dced.state.ak.us> wrote in message
news:3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us...
> OK-I must be missing something. I'm evaluating a status variable & want
> to take the same action for 2 different values but I haven't found any
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>



-----=  Posted via Newsfeeds.Com, Uncensored Usenet News  =-----
http://www.newsfeeds.com - The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
 Check out our new Unlimited Server. No Download or Time Limits!
-----==  Over 80,000 Newsgroups - 19 Different Servers!  ==-----
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "Robert Sales" <Robert.Sales@merant.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T09:55:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mkvjn$2sn$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <3b8d86f3_6@goliath.newsgroups.com>`

```
But there's no need for the language to support WHEN 'A' OR 'B' and the
draft standard does NOT allow this.  You can already simply say:
evaluate status-field
when 'A'
when 'B'
    perform A-or-B-process
end-evaluate

An example of partial conditions as defined in the draft standard:
evaluate numeric-field
when < 5
    display "less than 5"
when > 8
    display "greater than 8"
end-evaluate
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE problem

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T14:07:35+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b8ef0b6_7@goliath.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8BCD75.8D9B460F@dced.state.ak.us> <3b8d86f3_6@goliath.newsgroups.com> <9mkvjn$2sn$1@hyperion.eu.merant.com>`

```
You are quite correct, Robert.

Several people have pointed out that the correct form would be:

WHEN = 'A' OR 'B'

Not that it matters... my post was never intended to be taken seriously.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-1d33bffbc8"></a>
## Thread: Job Opening in Charleston, SC — 2 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs)_

### Job Opening in Charleston, SC

- **From:** "Mike Lucich" <lucichm@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T20:54:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QxTi7.126368$k7.31819501@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
An IT services firm that supplies programmers to my firm has an immediate
opening for a COBOL programmer/analyst.  The position is full-time, in
Charleston, South Carolina.  The work involves maintenance programming on a
financial application programmed in Micro Focus COBOL and IBM Object REXX,
and running under Windows NT.  Salary is $36,000 to $50,000, commensurate
with experience.  If you're interested, please send me a resume and I'll
forward it on.
```

#### ↳ Re: Job Opening in Charleston, SC

- **From:** "Lynn Randall" <LERandall@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T19:27:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b904a5e_2@news1.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<QxTi7.126368$k7.31819501@news1.rdc1.tn.home.com>`

```
Is this really typical FTE salaries?  It seems pretty low to me.   I would
be embarrassed to offer $36k to even a trainee, unless they were absolute
beginners.  And $50k for a top developer?  Maybe they're not willing to pay
for a top developer; they just want an 'adequate' developer.  Maybe
Charleston has a bit lower cost of living than Portland, but I have a hard
time believing it's *that* much of a difference.

Perhaps it's the difference between mainframe developers and MF Cobol
developers(?).  If MF Cobol developers are getting paid that badly, I'll
stick with MVS.

So all you MF / Fujitsu / Realia / whatever-non-mainframe-environment people
out there... is this how the market is for you nowadays?

just trying to figure this out...
```
