## Thread: jsab rtn added to cobol ptr pgm (swareq) — 1 msgs

### jsab rtn added to cobol ptr pgm (swareq)

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2001-08-28T22:47:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c13013$5c124e60$a2de2a3f@chottel>`

```
To All:

Here is the revised program.  When running in batch the component id was
'jes2'.

       ID DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.   COBPTR.
       AUTHOR.       USCS.
       DATE-WRITTEN. 08/22/01.
       DATE-COMPILED.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    VS COBOL II POINTER FEATURE EXAMPLE                        *
      *    USING MVS CONTROL BLOCKS                                   *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT TEST-FILE ASSIGN TO DD1.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  TEST-FILE
           LABEL RECORDS STANDARD
           RECORDING MODE IS F
           BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
           DATA RECORD IS TEST-REC.
       01  TEST-REC PIC X(80).
      /
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

TEST***01  WS-TEST                            PIC S9(9) COMP VALUE ZERO.
       01  WS-WORK                             PIC S9(9) COMP.
       01  WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT                  PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-LEN                              PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-LENGTH REDEFINES WS-LEN          PIC X(02).
       01  WS-FLAG                             PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  WS-FLAG1 REDEFINES WS-FLAG          PIC X(02).

       01  WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-TIOT-SEG-POINT.
           05  WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT REDEFINES WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.

       01  WS-JFCB-POINT.
           05  WS-JFCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-JFCB-PTR REDEFINES WS-JFCB-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-JFCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES  WS-JFCB-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-JFCB-LOW-3               PIC X(3).

       01  WS-POINT.
           05  WS-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-PTR REDEFINES WS-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-LOW-3                    PIC X(3).

       01  WS-SWA-POINT.
           05  WS-SWA-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-SWA-PTR REDEFINES WS-SWA-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
           05  WS-SWA-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-SWA-PTR.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-SWA-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-QMAT-POINT.
           05  WS-QMAT-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-QMAT-PTR REDEFINES WS-QMAT-POINTER
                                               PIC S9(9) COMP.
      /
       01  WS-UCB-POINT.
           05  WS-UCB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-UCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-UCB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-UCB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-DEB-POINT.
           05  WS-DEB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-DEB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-DEB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-DEB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-DCB-POINT.
           05  WS-DCB-POINTER                  USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  WS-DCB-POINT-RED REDEFINES WS-DCB-POINTER.
               07  FILLER                      PIC X.
               07  WS-DCB-LOW-3                PIC X(3).

       01  WS-CVT-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-CVT-ADDR-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-ASCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-ASCB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-ASSB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-ASSB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  WS-JSAB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           05  WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER            USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * PUT THE STATEMENTS BELOW IN AN ASSEMBLER PROGRAM TO LOCATE    *
      * THE DISPLACEMENTS OF THE SYMBOLS.  THESE LINES WERE SHIFTED   *
      * TO THE RIGHT TO MAKE COBOL COMMENTS.  SHIFT THEM BACK TO THE  *
      * LEFT SO THAT THE WORD 'TITLE' IS IN COLUMN 10.                *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *              TITLE 'PREFIX STORAGE AREA'                      *
      *              IHAPSA LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'TASK CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *              IKJTCB LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'SECONDARY TASK CONTROL BLOCK'             *
      *              IHASTCB LIST=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'DATA EXTENT BLOCK'                        *
      *              IEZDEB LIST=YES                                  *
      *              TITLE 'DATA CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *     DCBDS    DSECT                                            *
      *              DCBD  DSORG=PS                                   *
      *              TITLE 'UNIT CONTROL BLOCK'                       *
      *     UCBDS    DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFUCBOB                                         *
      *              TITLE 'TASK I/O TABLE'                           *
      *     TIOTDS   DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFTIOT1                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JOB FILE CONTROL BLOCK'                   *
      *     JFCBDS   DSECT                                            *
      *              IEFJFCBN LIST=YES                                *
      *              TITLE 'COMMUNICATION VECTOR TABLE'               *
      *              CVT DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                           *
      *              TITLE 'SUBSYSTEM COMMUNICATION VECTOR TABLE'     *
      *              IEFJSCVT                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JOB STEP CONTROL BLOCK'                   *
      *              IEZJSCB                                          *
      *              TITLE 'SWA QUEUE AREA'                           *
      *              IEFQMNGR                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JES COMMUNICATION TABLE'                  *
      *              IEFJESCT                                         *
      *              TITLE 'JSAB'                                     *
      *              IAZJSAB DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                       *
      *              TITLE 'ASCB'                                     *
      *              IHAASCB DSECT=YES,LIST=YES                       *
      *              TITLE 'ASSB'                                     *
      *              IHAASSB LIST=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'VSAM ACB'                                 *
      *              IFGACB DSECT=YES                                 *
      *              TITLE 'REMOTE AREAS'                             *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      /
       LINKAGE SECTION.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAPSA)  X'21C' = DECIMAL 540                  *
      *                         X'224' = DECIMAL 548                  *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  PSA.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(540).
           05  TCB-PTR                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(04).
           05  ASCB-PTR                     USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  PSA-ASM REDEFINES PSA.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(540).
           05  PSATOLD                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(04).
           05  PSAAOLD                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IKJTCB)  HAS A 32 BYTE PREFIX AREA             *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  TCB-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  TCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DEB-ADDR                     USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  TIOT-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(164).
           05  JSCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(128).
           05  STCB-POINTER                 USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  TCB-ASM REDEFINES TCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  TCBDEB                       USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  TCBTIO                       USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(164).
           05  TCBJSCB                      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(128).
           05  TCBSTCB                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(CVT)                                           *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  CVT-POINTER                      USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  CVT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(296).
           05  JESCT-POINTER                USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  CVT-ASM REDEFINES CVT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(296).
           05  CVTJESCT                     USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJESCT)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JESCT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  JESSSCVT-POINTER             USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  JESCT-ASM REDEFINES JESCT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  JESSSCT                      USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJSCVT)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  SSCVT.
           05  SSCVT-EYE-CATCHER  PIC X(04).
           05  NEXT-SSCVT                   USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  SUBSYSTEM-NAME     PIC X(04).
       01  SSCVT-ASM  REDEFINES SSCVT.
           05  SSCTID             PIC X(04).
           05  SSCTSCTA                     USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  SSCTSNAM           PIC X(04).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFTIOT1)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  TIOT.
           05  JOB-NAME           PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-PROC           PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-STEP           PIC X(08).
       01  TIOT-ASM REDEFINES TIOT.
           05  TIOCSTPN           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOCPSTN           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOCSJSTN          PIC X(08).

       01  TIOT-SEG.
           05  TIO-LEN            PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(03).
           05  DD-NAME            PIC X(08).
           05  SWA-V-ADDR         PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  UCB-ADDR           PIC X(03).
       01  TIOENTRY REDEFINES TIOT-SEG.
           05  TIOELNGH           PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(03).
           05  TIOEDDNM           PIC X(08).
           05  TIOEJFCB           PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  TIOEFSRT           PIC X(03).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFJFCBN)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JFCB.
           05  DS-NAME            PIC X(44).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(74).
           05  VOL-SER            PIC X(06).
       01  JFCB-ASM REDEFINES JFCB.
           05  JFCBDSNM           PIC X(44).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(74).
           05  JFCBVOLS           PIC X(06).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEZDEB)  DEB HAS A 36 BYTE PREFIX AREA         *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  DEB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  NEXT-DEB-ADDR      PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DCB-ADDR           PIC X(03).
       01  DEB-ASM REDEFINES DEB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  DEBDEBB            PIC X(03).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DEBDCB             PIC X(03).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(DCBD)                                          *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  DCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DEVICE-TYPE        PIC X.
               88  DISK-3380-X2E  VALUE X'2E'.
               88  DISK-3390-X2F  VALUE X'2F'.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DSORG              PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  RECFM              PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  DDNAME             PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(14).
           05  BLKSIZE            PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(18).
           05  LRECL              PIC S9(4) COMP.
       01  DCB-ASM REDEFINES DCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(17).
           05  DCBDEVT            PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DCBDSORG           PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  DCBRECFM           PIC X(02).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  DCBDDNAM           PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(14).
           05  DCBBLKSI           PIC S9(4) COMP.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(18).
           05  DCBLRECL           PIC S9(4) COMP.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEZJSCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JSCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(244).
           05  QMPL-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  JSCB-ASM REDEFINES JSCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(244).
           05  JSCBQMPI           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IEFQMNGR)                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  QMPL.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  QMAT-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  QMPL-ASM REDEFINES QMPL.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(24).
           05  QMADD              USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  QMAT.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(12).
           05  QMAT-NEXT-POINTER  USAGE IS POINTER.

       01  SWA.
           05  JFCB-ADDR          USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MODGEN(IHASTCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  STCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(188).
           05  JSAB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  STCB-ASM REDEFINES STCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(188).
           05  STCBJSAB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IAZJSAB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  JSAB.
           05  JSAB-EYE-CATCHER   PIC X(04).
           05  JSAB-NEXT-PTR      USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  JSAB-FLAG1         PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  COMPONENT          PIC X(04).
           05  JOB-ID             PIC X(08).
           05  JOB-NBR            PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  USERID             PIC X(08).
       01  JSAB-ASM REDEFINES JSAB.
           05  JSABID             PIC X(04).
           05  JSABNEXT           USAGE IS POINTER.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(05).
           05  JSABFLG1           PIC X.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(02).
           05  JSABSCID           PIC X(04).
           05  JSABJBID           PIC X(08).
           05  JSABJBNM           PIC X(08).
           05  FILLER             PIC X(08).
           05  JSABUSID           PIC X(08).
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAASCB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ASCB-POINTER                     USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(336).
           05  ASSB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASCB-ASM REDEFINES ASCB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(336).
           05  ASCBASSB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    SYS1.MACLIB(IHAASSB)                                       *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       01  ASSB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(168).
           05  JSAB-POINTER       USAGE IS POINTER.
       01  ASSB-ASM REDEFINES ASSB.
           05  FILLER             PIC X(168).
           05  ASSBJSAB           USAGE IS POINTER.
      /
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
           PERFORM JOB-STEP-NAME.
           PERFORM SUBSYSTEM-NAMES.
           PERFORM JFCB-INFO.
           PERFORM DCB-INFO.
           PERFORM JSAB-INFO.

           GOBACK.
      /
       JOB-STEP-NAME.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    JOB NAME AND STEP NAME                                     *
      *      PSA + X'21C' -> TCB -> TIOT                              *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT TO TIOT-POINTER.
           DISPLAY 'JOB NAME=' JOB-NAME
                   '  JOB PROC=' JOB-PROC
                   '  JOB STEP=' JOB-STEP.
           DISPLAY '         '.
      /
       SUBSYSTEM-NAMES.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY SUBSYSTEM NAMES FROM SSCVT CHAIN                   *
      *    CVT -> JSECT -> SSCVT                                      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'00000010' TO WS-CVT-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF CVT-POINTER TO WS-CVT-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF CVT TO CVT-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF JESCT TO JESCT-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF SSCVT TO JESSSCVT-POINTER.
           DISPLAY 'SUBSYSTEM NAME=' SUBSYSTEM-NAME.
           PERFORM UNTIL NEXT-SSCVT IS = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF SSCVT TO NEXT-SSCVT
               DISPLAY 'SUBSYSTEM NAME=' SUBSYSTEM-NAME
           END-PERFORM.
           DISPLAY '         '.
      /
       JFCB-INFO.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------
      *    FIND DDNAMES AND ASSOCIATED DSNAMES
      *    PSA+X'21C' -> TCB -> TIOT -> TIOT SEG -> SWAREQ(SVA) -> JFCB
      *---------------------------------------------------------------
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT TO TIOT-POINTER.
           SET WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER TO TIOT-POINTER.
           ADD 24 TO WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT.
           SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER.
           PERFORM UNTIL TIO-LEN = LOW-VALUES
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-POINT
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-JFCB-POINT
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-SWA-POINT
               MOVE SWA-V-ADDR TO WS-SWA-LOW-3
               PERFORM SWAREQ
               SET ADDRESS OF JFCB TO  WS-POINTER
               DISPLAY 'DDNAME=' DD-NAME
               DISPLAY 'DSNAME=' DS-NAME
               DISPLAY 'VOL=SER=' VOL-SER
               DISPLAY '********************************************'
               MOVE ZERO TO WS-LEN
               MOVE TIO-LEN TO WS-LENGTH(2:1)
               ADD WS-LEN TO WS-TIOT-SEG-PNT
               SET ADDRESS OF TIOT-SEG TO WS-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
           END-PERFORM.
      /
       SWAREQ.
           DIVIDE WS-SWA-PTR BY 16
               GIVING WS-WORK
               REMAINDER WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT.

           IF WS-RIGHT-HEX-DIGIT NOT = 15
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR + 16
           ELSE
               MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF JSCB TO JSCB-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF QMPL TO QMPL-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF QMAT TO QMAT-POINTER
               SET WS-QMAT-POINTER TO QMAT-POINTER
               PERFORM UNTIL WS-SWA-PTR <= 65536
                   SET WS-QMAT-POINTER TO QMAT-NEXT-POINTER
                   SET ADDRESS OF QMAT TO QMAT-NEXT-POINTER
                   COMPUTE WS-SWA-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR - 65536
               END-PERFORM
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-SWA-PTR + WS-QMAT-PTR + 1
               SET ADDRESS OF SWA TO WS-POINTER
               SET WS-POINTER TO JFCB-ADDR
               COMPUTE WS-PTR = WS-PTR + 16
            END-IF.
      /
       DCB-INFO.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY DCB INFORMATION                                    *
      *    PSA+X'21C' - > TCB -> DEB -> DCB                           *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           OPEN INPUT TEST-FILE.
           MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-DEB-POINT.
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET WS-DEB-POINTER TO DEB-ADDR.
           PERFORM UNTIL WS-DEB-POINTER IS = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF DEB TO WS-DEB-POINTER
               MOVE ALL LOW-VALUES TO WS-DCB-POINT
               MOVE DCB-ADDR TO WS-DCB-LOW-3
               SET ADDRESS OF DCB TO WS-DCB-POINTER
TEST***********DISPLAY 'DCB-PTR=' WS-DCB-POINTER
               DISPLAY ' DDNAME=' DDNAME
               DISPLAY '  DSORG=' DSORG
               DISPLAY '  RECFM=' RECFM
               DISPLAY 'BLKSIZE=' BLKSIZE
               DISPLAY '  LRECL=' LRECL
               IF DISK-3380-X2E
                   DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=3380'
                   DISPLAY '**************************************'
               ELSE IF DISK-3390-X2F
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=3390'
                       DISPLAY '**************************************'
                    ELSE
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=????'
                       DISPLAY 'DEVICE-TYPE=' DEVICE-TYPE
                       DISPLAY '**************************************'
                    END-IF
               END-IF
TEST***********DIVIDE WS-LEN BY WS-TEST GIVING WS-WORK
               MOVE NEXT-DEB-ADDR TO WS-DEB-LOW-3
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE TEST-FILE.
      /
       JSAB-INFO.
           SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO NULL.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    DISPLAY JSAB INFORMATION                                   *
      *    PSA+X'21C' - > TCB -> STCB -> JSAB                         *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           MOVE X'0000021C' TO WS-TCB-ADDRESS-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB-POINTER TO WS-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF TCB TO TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF STCB TO STCB-POINTER.
           IF JSAB-POINTER OF STCB IS NOT = NULL
               SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO JSAB-POINTER OF STCB
               SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-POINTER OF STCB
           END-IF.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    PSA+X'224' - > ASCB -> ASSB -> JSAB                        *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL
               MOVE X'00000224' TO WS-ASCB-ADDRESS-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF ASCB-POINTER TO WS-ASCB-ADDR-POINTER
               SET ADDRESS OF ASCB TO ASCB-POINTER
               IF ASSB-POINTER IS NOT = NULL
                   SET ADDRESS OF ASSB TO ASSB-POINTER
                   SET WS-ASSB-ADDR-POINTER TO ASSB-POINTER
                   IF JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB IS NOT = NULL
                       SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB
                       SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-POINTER OF ASSB
                   END-IF
               END-IF
           END-IF.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *    IF JSAB-FLAG1 > 127 THE JSAB IS INVALID                    *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL
               CONTINUE
           ELSE
               MOVE ZERO TO WS-FLAG
               MOVE JSAB-FLAG1 TO WS-FLAG1(2:1)
               PERFORM UNTIL WS-FLAG <= 127 OR
                             WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS = NULL OR
                             JSAB-EYE-CATCHER NOT = 'JSAB'
                   SET WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER TO JSAB-NEXT-PTR
                   SET ADDRESS OF JSAB TO WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER
                   MOVE ZERO TO WS-FLAG
                   MOVE JSAB-FLAG1 TO WS-FLAG1(2:1)
               END-PERFORM
           END-IF.

           IF WS-JSAB-ADDR-POINTER IS NOT = NULL
               DISPLAY 'EYE-CATCHER=' JSAB-EYE-CATCHER
               DISPLAY '  COMPONENT=' COMPONENT
               DISPLAY '     JOB ID=' JOB-ID
               DISPLAY ' JOB NUMBER=' JOB-ID
               DISPLAY '     USERID=' USERID
           ELSE
               DISPLAY 'JSAB INFORMATION NOT FOUND'
           END-IF.

```

<a id="t-b18158b5b8"></a>
## Thread: NetExpress and OLE — 3 msgs

### NetExpress and OLE

- **From:** pa@schaubroeck.be (Peter Alluyn)
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T14:30:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3b8cf994.5719755@news.eunet.be>`

```
Hello,

I am using NetExpress 3.0 and I want to use OLE-automation to do an
integration with MS-Word.  I managed to open Word (with invoke ....)
and open a document.  How can I start a macro ???

Thanks Peter
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress and OLE

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T19:39:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8D45CF.2CE5E1CD@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b8cf994.5719755@news.eunet.be>`

```


Peter Alluyn wrote:

> Hello,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks Peter

Peter,

Be curious to see what reply you get on this. It might shut some people
off, because they will consider the question as being specifically
directed at Net Express. I've got as far as you but I'm puzzled how I
can get to "Page 3 paragraph 3"  in a particular document,
automatically.

That was one of the nice features in DOS. You could buy inexpensive
utilities which captured keystrokes and turned them into a macro. Does
anybody know if such keystroke macro utilities are available for Windows
which could be either 'called' or 'invoked'.

Three who are definitely doing the same as you in Fujitsu - Denny, Peter
and Thane - have you a routine that you use to capture keystrokes when
OLE-ing ? Richard, I know you are OLE-ing - any particular technique to
to do this ?

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: NetExpress and OLE

- **From:** "Mike Kinasz" <mkinasz@cbspayroll.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T01:56:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F3hj7.139156$oh1.55520164@news2.rdc2.tx.home.com>`
- **References:** `<3b8cf994.5719755@news.eunet.be>`

```
Hi..... I created a word doc called C:\NETEST.DOC with a macro in it called
MACRO1 which displays a "hello world" message box.  Stepping through the
following code in your animator should work..........   I am using NE 3.1
but hopefully this will work for you.   It took a while to figure out when I
needed it way back when.......

Later,
Mike Kinasz


       $set ooctrl(+P) mfoo

       PROGRAM-ID. WRD000.
       OBJECT SECTION.
       CLASS-CONTROL.
       COM-OBJECT IS CLASS "$OLE$Word.Application".

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01 WRD-APP-OBJECT                        OBJECT REFERENCE.
       01 WRD-DOCS-OBJECT                       OBJECT REFERENCE.
       01 WRD-DOC-OBJECT                        OBJECT REFERENCE.
       01 WRD-SOURCE-FILE                       PIC X(128).
       01 WRD-MACRO-NAME                        PIC X(128).
       01 OLE-TRUE                              PIC 9(1) VALUE 1.
       01 OLE-FALSE                             PIC 9(1) VALUE 0.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        100000-MAINLINE.
           MOVE "C:\NETEST.DOC" TO WRD-SOURCE-FILE.

           INVOKE COM-OBJECT "new" RETURNING WRD-APP-OBJECT.

           INVOKE WRD-APP-OBJECT "setVisible"
                USING OLE-TRUE.

           INVOKE WRD-APP-OBJECT "getDocuments"
                RETURNING WRD-DOCS-OBJECT.

           INVOKE WRD-DOCS-OBJECT "Open"
                USING BY REFERENCE WRD-SOURCE-FILE.

           INVOKE WRD-APP-OBJECT "getActiveDocument"
                RETURNING WRD-DOC-OBJECT.

           INVOKE WRD-APP-OBJECT "Run"
                USING "MACRO1".

           INVOKE WRD-DOC-OBJECT "Close"
                USING OLE-FALSE.

           INVOKE WRD-APP-OBJECT "Quit".

           STOP RUN.
        100099-MAINLINE-EXIT.




"Peter Alluyn" <pa@schaubroeck.be> wrote in message
news:3b8cf994.5719755@news.eunet.be...
> Hello,
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks Peter
```

<a id="t-421b2dc9f3"></a>
## Thread: How do I convert a hex character to its decimal value? — 8 this month / 9 total

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How do I convert a hex character to its decimal value?

- **From:** brvinc@hotmail.com (Brad Vincent)
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T08:20:10-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com>`

```
We are receiving a file which contains a field that is 1 byte long and
it contains a hex value. This value is not a packed field. It's simply
a hex character to represent a 3-digit insurance plan number (which in
our case can be any number from 0 - 199).

We need to convert this hex character to its decimal equivalent. For
example, the single byte may contain X'A8'. I have to convert it
(somehow) to the decimal value 168. How would I go about doing this?

Thanks!

Sincerely,
Brad Vincent
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I convert a hex character to its decimal value?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T16:20:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uD8j7.134605$VV1.9877136@bin1.nnrp.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com>`

```
01  SINGLE-HEX-BYTE    PIC X.
01  DOUBLE-BYTE.
    02  DOUBLE-BYTE-COMP  PIC S9(4) COMP-5 VALUE 0.
01  NEW-VALUE      PIC 999.

MOVE SINGLE-HEX-BYTE TO DOUBLE-BYTE(1:1).
MOVE DOUBLE-BYTE-COMP TO NEW-VALUE.

Some compilers permit the definition of a single-byte binary (COMP)
value. DON'T GO THERE as this will introduce mind-bending bugs.



"Brad Vincent" <brvinc@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com...
> We are receiving a file which contains a field that is 1 byte long
and
> it contains a hex value. This value is not a packed field. It's
simply
> a hex character to represent a 3-digit insurance plan number (which
in
> our case can be any number from 0 - 199).
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I convert a hex character to its decimal value?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T18:53:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010829145343.29724.00006108@nso-fd.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com>,
brvinc@hotmail.com (Brad Vincent) writes:

>
>We are receiving a file which contains a field that is 1 byte long and
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You give no mention of Operating System or Compiler level.

My first thought would be to use FUNCTION ORD(hex-value-char-field).

Short of that, you could go the BFH route and break it down to the digit 
components and then arithmetically derive the decimal number.

Have you tried a Table search with all the values defined?

All the above methods will work, it just depends on the compiler level
available and the coding style that would be easiest for future maintenance.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I convert a hex character to its decimal value?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T14:38:27-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mjgen$7b$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com>`

```
Assuming that your compiler supports ANSI/ISO Intrinsic Functions, then use
the following code (which is independent of ASCII/EBCDIC or any other
"table" implications)

01  Input-1-byte  Pic X.
01  Output-Num  Pic 999.
   ...
Compute Output-Num = (Function Ord (Input-1-Byte)) - 1

FYI - the "minus 1" is because Function Ord goes 1 thru 256 (or whatever)
while you probably want 0 thru 255
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I convert a hex character to its decimal value?

- **From:** "Brad in MI" <bNOrSPAMv4inMEc@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T20:53:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RCcj7.4623$Fv3.404725@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com>`

```
Thanks, gang. Good advice.

My analyst informs me that the COMP-X (or COMP-5) is an invalid option
in our version of COBOL. I think we may be on version 4 of VS COBOL
II. It's been a while.

We'll give the Function Ord a try in the morning and see what that
does. Otherwise, would we not have to go the unfortunate Binary route
and break it down? (Yuk!)

Thanks.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How do I convert a hex character to its decimal value?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T17:08:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mjp7v$7ok$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com> <RCcj7.4623$Fv3.404725@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
If you are using VS COBOL II - Release 4, then

A) remember that IBM has already dropped support for that compiler

B) you will NOT have access to any intrinsic functions

C) To use intrinsic functions (and to run on an IBM supported compiler)
upgrade to:
   IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM
   (assuming you aren't on VSE - there is an upgrade their too)
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I convert a hex character to its decimal value?

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T17:25:12-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mjm8g$k6b$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com>`

```
Assuming this is an IBM Mainframe environment :

03  WS-PACKED-X PIC X(02).
03  WS-PACKED-V9 REDEFINES WS-PACKED-X
                                    PIC 9(01)V9 PACKED-DECIMAL.
03  WS-DISPLAY-X PIC X(03).
03  WS-DISPLAY-V9 REDEFINES WS-DISPLAY-X
                                    PIC 9(02)V9.
03  WS-DISPLAY REDEFINES WS-DISPLAY-X
                                     PIC 9(03).
03  WS-INS-PLAN   PIC X(01) VALUE X'A8'.
03  WS-HWORD-X PIC X(02).
03  WS-HWORD REDEFINES WS-HWORD-X
                                    PIC S9(04) BINARY.

MOVE WS-INS-PLAN TO WS-PACKED-X (1:1).
MOVE X'0F' TO WS-PACKED-X (2:1).
MOVE WS-PACKED-V9 TO WS-DISPLAY-V9.
INSPECT WS-DISPLAY-X CONVERTING X'FAFBFCFDFEFF'
                                             TO 'ABCDEF.

At this point, WS-DISPLAY-X (1:2) is equal to 'A8'.

MOVE LOW-VALUE TO WS-HWORD-X (1:1).
MOVE WS-INS-PLAN TO WS-HWORD-X (2:1).
MOVE WS-HWORD TO WS-DISPLAY.

At this point, WS-DISPLAY is equal to 168.

HTH....

Bill

"Brad Vincent" <brvinc@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com...
> We are receiving a file which contains a field that is 1 byte long and
> it contains a hex value. This value is not a packed field. It's simply
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Brad Vincent
```

#### ↳ Re: How do I convert a hex character to its decimal value?

- **From:** stephenjspiro@hotmail.com (Stephen J Spiro)
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T19:46:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4145699b.0108311846.3e5bc6a5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com>`

```
brvinc@hotmail.com (Brad Vincent) wrote in message news:<cd2e77cf.0108290720.131c98ad@posting.google.com>...
> We are receiving a file which contains a field that is 1 byte long and
> it contains a hex value. This value is not a packed field. It's simply
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Brad Vincent

The compiler converts one numeric format to another automatically.  If
the source field is two bytes (or more), make sure it is defined COMP
or BINARY, and just move it to a PIC 999 DISPLAY field.  If it is one
byte, define it as alphanumeric and move it to DIELD-X in the example,
then move HALFWORD to your PIC 999 DISPLAY field.

01  HALFWORD       PIC 9(4) BINARY
    VALUE ZERO.
01  FILLER REDEFINES HALFWORD.
    05   FILLER    PIC X.
    05   DIELD-X   PIC X.

Ummmm, if your computer is Little-endian You may have to reverse the
positions of FILLER and DIELD-X.

There will be one-byte true BINARY in the next standard; if your
compiler supports it now, you can avoid the HALFWORD stuff.

Stephen J Spiro
Member, J4 COBOL Standards Committee
check it out at 
http://people.ne.mediaone.net/doncobol/

stephenjspiro at hotmail.com
```

### Using Microsoft C DLL's -- How To

- **From:** "Fred Stevens" <f.stevens3@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T21:52:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qudj7.292463$v5.29322873@news1.rdc1.ct.home.com>`

```
A client of mine has Java code running under a web server that they want to
access from COBOL programs. They are a MicroFocus user but don't want to
have to force their clients to upgrade to NetExpress. I suggested that they
could get to the Java code using socket I/O. There is a neat set of
open-source DLL's made available by Catalyst that take care of most of the
pain and frustration of dealing with sockets.

But, in order to use these DLL's I have to be able to call them. I know it
can be done but I can't, for the life of me, get a handle on the details so
I'm coming up blank. Is there someone out there that can point me in the
right direction?
```

- **Message-ID:** `<Xns910D7ACE3162Dgeirknaplundhotmailc@194.19.1.61>`

"Fred Stevens" <f.stevens3@home.com> wrote in
news:qudj7.292463$v5.29322873@news1.rdc1.ct.home.com: 

> A client of mine has Java code running under a web server that they
> want to access from COBOL programs. They are a MicroFocus user but
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Use "Quickview" on the DLL to se the entry points.
Then use the "trick":

Set <var-defined-as-PROCEDURE POINTER) to "ENTRY "<TheNameOFJavaDLL.dll>".

Then just call (dynamic or static) <some-exported-Java-entry> USING 
<whatever>.
Since I guess we are talking about Java methods, the actual parameters to 
the method may require some research (much more easy with old "dirty" C-
funtions within a ordinary dll).
```

## Thread: Defining Variable Length Record — 7 this month / 15 total

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### Defining Variable Length Record

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T02:40:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010829224034.01906.00002729@mb-fu.aol.com>`

```
I'm in the process of converting a program from COBOL II to COBOL OS/390. 
We're trying to get everything into AMODE (31) - still - stuff left over from
Y2K and the change to LE.  I'm having problems with the FD, I've gone brain
dead on how to define the variable length record in the file.

The original code was:

FD  INPUT-FILE
      RECORDING MODE IS S
      LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
      BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
      RECORD CONTAINS 0 TO 281 CHARACTERS.
01  INPUT-REC.
      05  FILLER              PIC XX.
          etc etc until 281 is defined.


The compiler is complaining about the RECORD CONTAINS or the RECORDING MODE.  I
can't win.  What am I missing?  I can remove the RECORD CONTAINS and change to
RECORDING MODE IS V but then it says RECORDING MODE is comments.  I've read
thru the manual and nothing is making sense (which for me isn't unusual at
times).

These are SMF records I'm working with. 

Eileen
```

#### ↳ Re: Defining Variable Length Record

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-29T22:13:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mkb3f$cen$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010829224034.01906.00002729@mb-fu.aol.com>`

```
Are these really spanned records?  If so, then you should have a RECORD size
that is *larger* than the Block size that you have specified.  I don't think
that BLOCK CONTAINS 0 is valid with RECORDING MODE IS S.  However, I haven't
found this (yet) in the dox.

My suggestion would be to TRY removing the RECORDING MODE IS S (if this
ISN'T a spanned record).  If it is a spanned record, then change the BLOCK
CONTAINS to the actual blocksize.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Defining Variable Length Record

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T04:21:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20010831002113.01669.00004985@mb-ml.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<9mkb3f$cen$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Answer to Bil Kl:  I don't know if the records are spaned or not.  The original
program had RECORDING MODE IS S and I'm just recompiling after changing those
things no longer supported.

Answer to Bill (WOB):  There is no DEPENDING ON CLAUSE.  That's what started
the whole mess in the first place :).

Answer to Colin:  Well - I'm not sure exactly which records are being used in
this particular program. It appears it is calculating run times.  Not being the
least bit fluent in assembler I wouldn't be able to add a subroutine to do the
I/O.

Today I hit another program with the same problem.  This time it had RECORDING
MODE IS V.  No DENDING ON clause.  The error I'm getting is below (I remember
to cut/paste and send it to myself).  This came from the first program, but the
2nd is the same message - just change the file name and record length.


IGYGR1201-E   The first integer of the "RECORD CONTAINS" clause for file 
"SMF-IN" was different from the minimum calculated record size "281".  "281"
was assumed as the first integer.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Defining Variable Length Record

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T11:54:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9moffn$2me$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<9mkb3f$cen$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <20010831002113.01669.00004985@mb-ml.aol.com>`

```
This message help understand the problem.  I looked in the migration guide
but couldn't find it documented there, BUT the solution sounds pretty
simple,  change the 1st integer in your
   Contains N to M
to the smallest record size ACTUALLY DEFINED in the program (and which
NICELY appears in the compiler message).  Don't change anything else.

My GUESS is this is just another of those "undocumented extensions" in OS/VS
COBOL that no longer works.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Defining Variable Length Record

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T17:59:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8FD172.B16D4976@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9mkb3f$cen$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <20010831002113.01669.00004985@mb-ml.aol.com> <9moffn$2me$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill and  Eileen,

Don't want to confuse the issue, but in case it helps Eileen, and if it is
possible for her to do the equivalent on a mainframe. I got the following to
work with Net Express for Line Sequentials; I haven't tried it on Sequentials,
where there could be several different Record Types with different sizes..

FD Myfile
       record varying from 0 to 1000 depending on MyFileRecordSize.
01 Myrecord            pic x(1000).

READS return each individual record size into MyFileRecordSize.

Jimmy

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> This message help understand the problem.  I looked in the migration guide
> but couldn't find it documented there, BUT the solution sounds pretty
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

#### ↳ Re: Defining Variable Length Record

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T07:07:25-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9ml6cb$n1a$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<20010829224034.01906.00002729@mb-fu.aol.com>`

```
RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE FROM 0 TO 281 CHARACTERS DEPENDING ON
WS-RECORD-SIZE.

03  WS-RECORD-SIZE PIC  9(04) BINARY.

The WS field must be unsigned. This is due to the underlying Assembler code
within the Macro, which in so many words, can't deal with a signed-field
(could be negative in the Add Logical instruction).

HTH....

Bill

#### ↳ Re: Defining Variable Length Record

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T15:22:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8EBCB4.55F94A02@ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<20010829224034.01906.00002729@mb-fu.aol.com>`

```
Eileen,
I have written COBOL programs that process SMF data, but only with the aim of
extracting "facts" from the records, and using (much!) simpler output record
formats.

In order to simplify the COBOL program, I called a simple Assembler program to read
the records and return them to the caller, including the ever so useful RDW (Record
Descriptor Word) which contains this record's true length.

No doubt, you could handle the I/O directly in COBOL, as I've written programs to
read Load Modules (in order to create a report using the Program Signature Bytes),
and they are RECFM=U.

Here's a COBOL description of an SMF Type 30 record:
      *    THE FOLLOWING DATA ITEMS ARE REFERENCED IN THE COPY MEMBER,
      *    BUT CANNOT BE CODED INSIDE IT:
       01  SMF30ACL-LONG.
        05                PIC X(01).
        05 SMF30ACL-LO    PIC X(01).
       01  SMF30ACL-N     REDEFINES
           SMF30ACL-LONG  PIC S9(4) BINARY.
      *    THIS IS A DESCRIPTION OF SMF RECORD TYPE 30 WHICH IS
      *    SUITABLE FOR USE IN A PROGRAM'S WORKING-STORAGE.
      *    IT INCLUDES THE DATA THAT CAN BE ADDRESSED IN THE "FD" AND
      *    THE RECORD HEADER THAT COBOL NORMALLY HIDES FROM THE
      *    PROGRAM.
       01  SMF-TYPE-30.
      *    THE "VARIABLE LENGTH RECORD HEADER" IS INCLUDED IN THIS
      *    DESCRIPTION BECAUSE THERE ARE NUMEROUS OFFSETS IN THE
      *    RECORD THAT ARE RELATIVE TO THE TRUE START OF THE RECORD,
      *    AND IT WILL BE EASIER TO ADDRESS THE DIFFERENT SEGMENTS
      *    IF THE OFFSET VALUES DON'T HAVE TO BE MANIPULATED.
        05 SMF-RECORD-HEADER BINARY.
        10 SMF30LEN                   PIC S9(4).
        10 SMF30SEG                   PIC S9(4).
        05 SMF-30-RECORD.
      *    NESTED COPY STATEMENT:
           COPY SMF30.

Note the nested COPY, which contains:
      *    HEADER / SELF-DEFINING SECTION:
       06  SMF30HDR.
       10  SMF30FLG                   PIC X(01).
       10  SMF30RTY                   PIC X(01).
        88 SMF-REC-TYPE-30                      VALUE X'1E'.
       10  SMF30TME                   PIC S9(8) BINARY.
       10  SMF30DTE                   PIC S9(7) PACKED-DECIMAL.
       10  SMF30SID                   PIC X(04).
        88 SMF-TYPE-30-S1                       VALUE 'S190'.
        88 SMF-TYPE-30-S6                       VALUE 'S690'.
       10  SMF30WID                   PIC X(04).
       10  SMF30STP                   PIC S9(4) BINARY.
        88 SMF-TYPE-30-JOB-START                VALUE +1.
        88 SMF-TYPE-30-INTERVAL-ACT             VALUE +2.
        88 SMF-TYPE-30-INTERVAL-ACT-L           VALUE +3.
        88 SMF-TYPE-30-STEP-TOTAL               VALUE +4.
        88 SMF-TYPE-30-JOB-TERM                 VALUE +5.
        88 SMF-TYPE-30-SYS-ADDR-SPACE           VALUE +6.
       10  SEGMENT-TRIPLES                      BINARY.
      *    THE SEVERAL SEGMENTS WHICH MAY BE PRESENT AFTER THE FIXED
      *    PART OF THE RECORD EACH HAVE A "TRIPLE" HERE.
      *    THE FIRST FULLWORD IS AN OFFSET FROM THE START OF
      *    THE RECORD (INCLUDING THE RECORD CONTROL WORD).
      *    THE SECOND HALFWORD IS THE LENGTH OF THE SEGMENT.
      *    THE THIRD HALFWORD IS THE NUMBER OF SUCH SEGMENTS.
      *    MISSING SEGMENTS WILL HAVE ZERO IN THE "OFFSET", BUT MAY
      *    HAVE NON-ZERO VALUES IN THE OTHER FIELDS.
       15  SMF30SOF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30SLN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30SON                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30IOF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30ILN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30ION                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30UOF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30ULN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30UON                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30TOF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30TLN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30TON                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30COF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30CLN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30CON                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30AOF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30ALN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30AON                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30ROF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30RLN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30RON                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30POF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30PLN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30PON                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30OOF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30OLN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30OON                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30EOF                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30ELN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30EON                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30EOR                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30RVD                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30EOS                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30DRO                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30DRL                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30DRN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30ARO                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30ARL                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30ARN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30OPO                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30OPL                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30OPN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30OPM                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30UDO                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30UDL                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30UDN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30UDS                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30RMO                   PIC S9(9).
       15  SMF30RML                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30RMN                   PIC S9(4).
       15  SMF30RMS                   PIC S9(9).
       06  VARIABLE-SEGMENTS.
       08  SUBSYSTEM-SECTION.
           COPY SMF30S.
       08  IDENTIFICATION-SECTION.
           COPY SMF30I.
       08  I-O-ACTIVITY-SECTION     OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30UON.
           COPY SMF30U.
       08  COMPLETION-SECTION       OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30TON.
           COPY SMF30T.
       08  PROCESSOR-SECTION        OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30CON.
        10 PIC X(76).
       08  ACCOUNTING-SECTION       OCCURS 0 TO 255  DEPENDING SMF30AON.
           COPY SMF30A.
       08  STORAGE-SECTION          OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30RON.
           COPY SMF30R.
       08  PERFORMANCE-SECTION      OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30PON.
           COPY SMF30P.
       08  OPERATOR-SECTION         OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30OON.
           COPY SMF30O.
       08  EXCP-AREA-IN-RECORD      OCCURS 0 TO 255  DEPENDING SMF30EON.
        10 PIC X(22).
       08  APPC-CUMULATIVE-SECTION  OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30ARN.
           COPY SMF30AR.
       08  APPC-SECTION             OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30DRN.
           COPY SMF30D.
       08  OPEN-MVS-SECTION         OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30OPN.
           COPY SMF30OP.
       08  USAGE-DATA-SECTION       OCCURS 0 TO 255  DEPENDING SMF30UDN.
           COPY SMF30UD.
       08  AUTO-RESTART-SECTION     OCCURS 0 TO 1    DEPENDING SMF30RMN.
           COPY SMF30RM.

Here is (the further) nested SMF30U:
      *    I-O-ACTIVITY SECTION:
       10  SMF30INP PIC S9(9) BINARY.
       10  SMF30TEP PIC S9(9) BINARY.
       10  SMF30TPT PIC S9(9) BINARY.
       10  SMF30TGT PIC S9(9) BINARY.
       10  SMF30RDR PIC X(01).
       10  SMF30RDT PIC X(01).
       10  SMF30TCN PIC S9(9) BINARY.
       10  SMF30DCF PIC S9(9) BINARY.
       10  SMF30RSB PIC X(02).
       10  SMF30TRR PIC S9(9) BINARY.

I won't include all of the rest of the description of the Type 30 record; you can
get it from the IBM documentation, or you can ask me if you want the rest.

Here's a sample of the COBOL CALL:
 READ-SMF.
     CALL      SMF98GET  USING SMF-TYPE-30
                               SMFINPUT-STATUS
     IF       '00'          =  SMFINPUT-STATUS
              ADD      1    TO RECORD-COUNT
     ELSE
       MOVE   '10'          TO SMFINPUT-STATUS
     .

Here's a sample of the Assembler:
GET      TITLE 'SMF READER ROUTINE CALLED BY "SMF98" COBOL PROGRAM'
SMF98GET CSECT
         PRINT NOGEN
SMF98GET AMODE 31
SMF98GET RMODE 24
GET      CSAVE R12,RETCODE
         L     R6,0(R1)        RECORD AREA ADDRESS
         L     R3,4(R1)        FILE STATUS ADDRESS
         USING FILESTAT,R3
         MVC   FILEST,=C'00'   ASSUME FILE WILL PROCESS WITHOUT ERRORS
         SPACE
         LA    R9,SMFINPUT     DCB ADDRESS
         USING IHADCB,R9
         TM    DCBOFLGS,DCBOFOPN FIRST TIME?
         BO    READREC
         OPEN  SMFINPUT
         TM    DCBOFLGS,DCBOFOPN OPEN SUCCESSFUL?
         BO    READREC
         MVC   FILEST,=C'35'   PASS BACK AN ERROR FILE STATUS
         LA    R15,12
         ST    R15,RETCODE     SET RETURN CODE = ERROR
         B     EXIT
         SPACE
READREC  BAL   R11,READRECS
         SR    R15,R15
         ST    R15,RETCODE     SET RETURN CODE = NORMAL
EXIT     EXIT  GET
         SPACE
READRECS GET   SMFINPUT        LOCATE THE NEXT LOGICAL RECORD
         LR    R8,R1
         LH    R9,0(R8)
         LR    R7,R9
         MVCL  R6,R8           RETURN RECORD TO CALLER'S AREA
         BR    R11
EOF      MVC   FILEST,=C'10'   RETURN END OF FILE STATUS,
         CLOSE SMFINPUT        CLOSE SMF FILE
         FREEPOOL SMFINPUT
         BR    R11
         DS    0D
RETCODE  DC    F'0'
         SPACE
         DS    0D
SMFINPUT DCB   DDNAME=SMFINPUT,DSORG=PS,MACRF=GL,RECFM=VB,EODAD=EOF
         DCBD  DSORG=PS,DEVD=DA
FILESTAT DSECT
FILEST   DS    CL2
         END

…[22 more quoted lines elided]…

## Thread: OT - Internet Explorer V6.0 — 23 this month / 77 total

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - Internet Explorer V6.0

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T00:14:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mki96$bit$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I don't know how many of "you" use which browser for Internet access, but
this is a warning (in case you might fall into a problem I ran into today).

On Microsoft's "update" page, they moved Internet Explorer V6.0 from their
"beta" software to their production software (some time this week).  Wanting
to be current (and trying to solve another problem with my firewall), I
installed it.

Everything installed fine

  UNFORTUNATELY,
    about half the internet sites that I usually use stopped working.
Talking to one of their support people, I found out that MS IE 6.0 does
*not* support current Sun Java.  Hence, many sites stop working "as
designed".

Bottom-line:
  My recommendation would NOT to install this upgrade (at least until you
hear of some solution to this problem)
```

This came up in the media a month ago. It was also discussed on
COMP.LANG.JAVA

Ultimately, M$ were licensed to use JAVA, and included the JVM in
their software. M$ customized it, in contravention to their license. They
got taken to court. Sun won, M$ dropped JAVA. It's my understanding
that there should be a prompt to download the  JAVA JVM when needed,
similar to other products needed to browse web pages.

However, when I first downloaded IE6 months ago, it didn't hook up with
my existing JVM and it didn't ask if I wanted to download a new JVM.

Now I hear the M$ .NET may be some form of competition for JAVA,
why am I not surprised when IE6 STILL can't cope with JAVA?

```
On Thu, 30 Aug 2001 05:42:53 GMT, "Rufio" <davecawdell@home.com>
wrote:

>This came up in the media a month ago. It was also discussed on
>COMP.LANG.JAVA
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>why am I not surprised when IE6 STILL can't cope with JAVA?

.NET (pronounced dot-net) will probably present strong competition to
Java.

 .Net languages will all compile to the same MS Intermediate Language
(MSIL, or IL for short)
that will be compiled to native code at run-time
 by a Just-In Time Compiler (a JITter); 

once-compiled, instructions will be cached to avoid re-compilation,
inside loops, for example.

The Common Language Runtime (CLR) runs on top of Windows and will
perform the compilation. 

The designers of .Net have impressive credentials - Anders Hejlsberg
created Turbo Pascal and was team leader for the design or Delphi.

Some people are already working on a Linux version of the .Net
Development Framework URL http://www.go-mono.com ),
which would give us what Java has not  - portability.

There are apparently 18 .Net languages in the works.
The main .Net language will be C# (pronouced c-sharp) 
and it looks a lot like Java and C++,
but much clearer, and without the irritants.

Irritants. They have gotten rid of a lot of the bug-producing features
of C++ : pointer arithmetic, header files, preprocessors, indiscrimate
type-casting, and so on. A garbage collector handles memory management
- i.e. no more memory leaks.

Fujitsu is developing the COBOL.NET compiler. 
A preview is available for download at this URL : 
http://www.adtools.com/dotnet/index.html

By the way, there is an example of an ASP.Net page written in
COBOL.NET at the following URL :
http://www.aspalliance.com/aldotnet/examples/coboldotnet.aspx

The author of that example admits that he doesn't know enough about
COBOL to explain what is going in in the example, and nicely invites
anyone competent enough to do so; could one the OO-COBOL gurus here
help him out?

Robaire ( R. Miller )
```

First, in a separate message, Volker has pointed us to the same article.

Unfortunately there are no OO COBOL gurus using this site. And if anybody
claims they are, either you or I can tell them, "You are full of s....!". Me -
still got my driver's learner plates up.

Can't be sure - but that site example you quote, I think it is 'theoretical'.
Fujitsu is close because it uses the Repository syntax, which Net Express as
yet doesn't, because they were 'early starters' before J4 discussed
Repository. Without delving into the J4 FCD , I think the example includes
'future' OO syntax which *may* not even be currently available in Fujitsu
(????). Recall an F/J article by Basmim Khadim, (their Chief Architect), where
F/J would be introducing, as an extension., to allow scripting syntax in COBOL
programs.

Nevertheless it is a good example to illustrate the linkage to .Net. However,
I would be reluctant to comment on something I can't actually test, even
though I can understand what it is doing.

Calin in Toronto recently pointed us to a Micro Focus white paper showing
their approach to using the .Net, plus there was to be a later paper. Any
follow-up on that Calin ? Or can you glean something from your jungle drums
Bill ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB

I obtained the information at the following URLs:

The MSDN subscription costs US$ 2499 for one year.
( ... waiting for you to get over the shock ...)
Renewal or upgrade from previous year is generously priced at US$2000

That's for the Universal level, which gives you access to Visual
Studio.Net Beta 2, which will allow you to compile and run your
COBOL.Net programs with the compiler which you buy from Fujitsu.
There's an apocryphal adage : "Never buy a house on VISA." Now we can
add " or a software development environment". 

BTW - according to the Webmaster, in a priveate email,
the example at URL
http://www.aspalliance.com/aldotnet/examples/coboldotnet.aspx
was developed with the Fujitsu compiler, 

A last note on Visual Studio - version 6 ( not .Net)
retails for US$1 619 ( US$1 079 for an upgrade );
 it includes Visual Basic, Visual C++, Visual Interdev, 
Visual J++, Visual FoxPro, and other Microsoft stuff. Very pricey.

>Considering there are many of us already pissed off at COBOL runtime fees, I don't
>see too many COBOLers getting excited about signing up for .Net and paying for the
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Jimmy

That which never ceases to amaze is that systems (political, weather,
social, animal,...) always adjust themselves, no matter what we do to
them or within them. The other thing that amazes me is the hubris of
the human beings trying to control these systems. For example, that of
Central Bank directors who set interest rates in an attempt to control
(they will say influence) markets and economists who explain them.

The software industry is also a system and I think that Microsoft have
influenced it to an extent that it (the system) is now ready to lash
back - MS are introducing .Net, an extraordinary feat in my
estimation, that will (yes, if it works - I'm assuming that it will)
really allow programs to be transported across systems with *no*
modifications.

How is the system going to lash back?
I think that three elements will do this  :

You identified the first - 
people are fed up with paying enormous fees to MS.

Second,  a .Net Development Framework is being developed for Linux
(URL http://www.go-mono.com ) under the open source licence.
Development languages will certainly follow.

Third, several governments (French, Brasilian, Florence-Italy) are
considering enacting laws forcing their public service departments to
use open source software ( Linux instead of Windows, StarOffice
instead of MS Office, ...) when such software fulfills their
requirements.

According to feedback from a reader of the article, the movement
exists in the USA -- he cites a local governement in Florida as having
adopted open source. I haven't verified this, but wouldn't be at all
surprised.

Here is a reference to the article : Nations uniting for open source.
http://www.zdnet.com/zdnn/stories/news/0,4586,2809001,00.html?chkpt=zdnnh082901

Part of their (governements) expressed concerns is what data that MS
may be returning concerning governement users; what they like about
open source software is that you get the source code, can compile it
yourself, and verify what it is doing.

According to Paul Festa, the author the ZD-Net article, MS have
qualified these proposed laws as "un-American".
Now that's gotta hurt <G>

I'm going to read the slew of feedback the author got for this
article; the feedback comes after the links and other stuff following
the article and can easily be missed.

Robaire
```

The symbol for the Canadian dollar was right in front of you -
I prefixed with the letters "US" to make sure you would understand
that I was not talking about Canadian dollars. I don't have the symbol
for US dollars in my character set (maybe I can get a discount if I
order Euro and US dollar charactres at the same time).
Canadian dollar symbol = $ ( S with a single vertical bar )
 American dollar symbol would be similar but have *two* vertical bars
due to the fact that it comes from superimposing the letter U over the
letter S.

>But first things first....
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>MCM

I'll let Mr. Gavan field that one, if he cares to do so.
I'm Quï¿½bï¿½cois, so anything I would have to say about Canadians would
have to be discounted by readers, which discounting would require a
knowledge of Canadian-Quï¿½bec politics, to which, I suspect, most
Americans are quite oblivious.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Internet Explorer V6.0

- **From:** "Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T10:15:53-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mlhu0$126n$1@allgood.news.cais.net>`
- **References:** `<9mki96$bit$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <xnkj7.4831$Xv3.806902@news1.rdc1.az.home.com>`

```
Its not that Microsoft "Can't" support JVM, its that they won't..
And this, like it or not, is yet another nail in the coffin of Java as a
client side language.  It may continue to be used on the server side but web
sites will run from it like the plague on the browser side.
Why?
Simple - at www.aboutlegacycoding.com 87% of our viewers use Internet
Explorer.  As editor, do you think I'm gonna risk loosing viewers out of
that 87%?  Nope - I just won't use Java on the site.

But why not just tell people to simply download the JVM?

Two reasons - first, if you look at the Study that Georgia Institute of
Technology does each year on the internet, you will find that the great
majority of users out there are cattle.  They buy a new machine - it comes
with an OS (and a browser).  They NEVER update or change that browser.  Tech
studies show that, on the majority of machines, the machine life and the
browser life are almost identical.  It takes almost two years for a new
browser to reach mass adoption.  This is hard for us Geeks to understand -
because anyone who knows how to get into this news group - probably
downloads the newest of everything as soon as its avail.  But we are NOT the
norm.

Second Reason - Even if a user downloads JVM, Microsoft is going to scare
them off.  Not only has MS stopped including the JVM in IE6, if you download
it, they set anything with a java extention to their "High Risk" security
settings - which means that the user gets a warning any time they hit a site
with a Java Applette.  Spencer Katt of E-week wrote about this over a month
ago.  Of course the user can go into the security settings and change this -
but how many of the cattle know how?  And how many will be scared off -
because Microsoft says this is a security risk?

No, Microsoft is a steam roller.  If Bill has decided to kill Java, then its
time to start digging the grave.  It may take him a few years to finish it
off, but he will.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Internet Explorer V6.0

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T20:34:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** bit.listserv.ibm-main,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8EA454.7A4E0D67@home.com>`
- **References:** `<9mki96$bit$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <xnkj7.4831$Xv3.806902@news1.rdc1.az.home.com> <9mlhu0$126n$1@allgood.news.cais.net>`

```


Editor wrote:

Agree with what you are saying, and while we can 'admire' Bill's business savvy,
it really does leave me like a Nervous Nellie that one guy is controlling it
all. Probably wont help, but the only 'saviour' for Java could be IBM.

What Bill experienced as a result of downloading the latest - sheer arrogance
and a complete disregard for their consumers. Just imagine if the Golden Arches
tried to shove only veggie-burgers down our throats.

> ITwo reasons - first, if you look at the Study that Georgia Institute of
> Technology does each year on the internet, you will find that the great
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> norm.

Disagree with the last bit - not all us Geeks download the latest. Can't
remember whether or not it was VISOC or Net Express, but I complained bitterly,
"Am I the only one in Canada using this product ?." Back came a reply for a
Vancouver software house, "We'll use it when it gets beyond the 'magic' number
Version 1.0". Wise words.

Same goes for Netscape - they keep plastering me with messages 'Upgrade to
Version 6.1" Might be OK now but my local direct cable folks said about a year
ago, "They have problems. Give it a miss until it settles down". Maybe V 6.1 has
solved any quirks in V 6.0.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT - Internet Explorer V6.0

- **From:** "Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@nospam.enternet.co.nz>

```
An interesting viewpoint, Buddy...

Comments below...

Pete.
"Editor" <Editor@aboutlegacycoding.com> wrote in message
news:9mlhu0$126n$1@allgood.news.cais.net...
> Its not that Microsoft "Can't" support JVM, its that they won't..
> And this, like it or not, is yet another nail in the coffin of Java as a
> client side language.  It may continue to be used on the server side but
web
> sites will run from it like the plague on the browser side.
> Why?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, the fact that the Browser doesn't support it can be solved by a simple
download. How long do you think it will be before fully automatic
downloadable "plug in"s for the JVM start to appear?

I recall when the big Browser Wars betwen Netscape and IE got started, many
Web Sites offered a link to download whichever Browser you wanted. I'm sure
you remember seeing things like "Best viewed with IE4..." and a MicroSoft
Logo with a link attached.

> But why not just tell people to simply download the JVM?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> with an OS (and a browser).  They NEVER update or change that browser.
Tech
> studies show that, on the majority of machines, the machine life and the
> browser life are almost identical.  It takes almost two years for a new
> browser to reach mass adoption.  This is hard for us Geeks to understand -
> because anyone who knows how to get into this news group - probably
> downloads the newest of everything as soon as its avail.  But we are NOT
the
> norm.
>
Yes, there is an element of this.

Personally, I don't download the latest version of everything as soon as
it's available (I was dragged kicking and screaming into IE5...<G>). But the
"cattle" can have a plug-in download automatically in the background while
they view a Web Site... Once permission has been obtained, the rest can be
transparent.



> Second Reason - Even if a user downloads JVM, Microsoft is going to scare
> them off.  Not only has MS stopped including the JVM in IE6, if you
download
> it, they set anything with a java extention to their "High Risk" security
> settings - which means that the user gets a warning any time they hit a
site
> with a Java Applette.  Spencer Katt of E-week wrote about this over a
month
> ago.  Of course the user can go into the security settings and change
this -
> but how many of the cattle know how?  And how many will be scared off -
> because Microsoft says this is a security risk?
>
Well, I envision an Applet which changes this setting as soon as the JVM is
installed... <G>

> No, Microsoft is a steam roller.  If Bill has decided to kill Java, then
its
> time to start digging the grave.  It may take him a few years to finish it
> off, but he will.
>
I really hope you're wrong about this. I certainly wouldn't give up the ship
too early...

I seem to recall he was going to take over the Internet too, a couple of
years ago... that hasn't happened... yet.

It is very possible that user resistance will have an effect. We have seen
Bill (Klein) posting here saying that many of his favourite sites no longer
"work" under IE6. People who frequent certain sites will continue to do so.
If the new Browser doesn't meet their needs, they'll either get it fixed so
it does, or change it. It is by no means certain that they'll abandon their
favourite Web Sites because the new Browser doesn't support them.

I understand your position with aboutlegacycoding, but I think (hope...) you
are being premature in abandoning Java on the site.

Why not continue to use it , but offer a free download if people can't see
Java enabled stuff?

Agree this one is too close to call at this point... I just HOPE you're
wrong...<G>

Pete.

PS. I also HOPE there will be a solution to this "problem"....

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T09:55:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers,alt.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_999165253@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2000/07/02
Version: 3.01
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Sunday, July 2, 2000

The COBOL FAQ has moved.  To view the most recent version, please check out the
web version at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send private
email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

The following is Appendix B.5 - Changes to create Version 3.01

 o Recorded new location of the FAQ
 o Updated some old (bad) web links
 o Added some new COBOL books (particularly those written or contributed to - by
frequent comp.lang.cobol submitters).
 o Added information about LegacyJ (PERCobol) and changed the company name from
"Synkronix" to "LegacyJ".
 o Updated information for changes in product and company names and addresses
where available
 o Updated the URLs for downloading the draft of the next COBOL Standard and
CobCy
 o Started the Samples/Example Section
 o Tried to get rid of most specific references to Windows/NT, Windows/95,
Windows/98, and Windows/2000.
 o Added links to Xinotech
 o Added information on Norcom and GUI ScreenIO
 o Added (back) information on Wang
 o Added (back) information on the Tiny-COBOL ('74 Standard) project
```

I have been teaching COBOL for the past three years using Fujitsu COBOL
v3.  During that time I have had trouble turning on the color coding
within the Fujitsu editor.  I always thought it was problem related to
the use of Version 3 of the compiler.  I am finding, now, that I have
the same problem with Version 6.  I expect that I am missing some key
step.

I have the Display Format window set so that "Color Keywords and
Comments" is checked.  I have also turned off "Matched Case of
Keyword".  This is a new install of version 6 and I am editing a program
from scratch.

The ruler line at the top of the editor and the line numbers are in
red.  All other text is black.

Is there some trick that I have missed?

Thanks for any help.

Floyd Johnson
```

## Thread: Exec cics return vs Goback — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Exec cics return vs Goback

- **From:** jason <cz.systems@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T01:33:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8EEBA0.5F2FC5AF@home.com>`

```
Hi all,

Here's the scenario

Pgm a exec cics links to program b.  Program b sets some commarea variables
and "goback"s to pgm a.

Can anyone tell me how "goback" can work in the above situation?  

We've got the above situation, and, for the most part, it works fine.  We
are having some unpredictable results in production that we can't reproduce
in test, so I'm replacing the gobacks with returns (but leaving one
unreachable goback for compiler purposes).

Thanks in advance,

Jason Thorpe
```
