#### ↳ Re: Exec cics return vs Goback

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-30T22:58:28-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9mmu3n$bsu$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3B8EEBA0.5F2FC5AF@home.com>`

```
Jason,

**** Begin Dissertation ****

        Could you substitute a CICS Dynamic CALL, with Program "A" calling
(instead of linking-to) program "B'? This would only make sense if "A" calls
"B" more than once during its invocation.

        What is the size of program "B"? Does it have a large WS allocation?
Do you rely upon the VALUE clauses to be in affect when you currently issue
the LINK-API ? Is this a required DPL (Distributed Program Link) ?

        When "A" Dynamically Calls "B", program "B" must return to program
"A" via a GOBACK and WS variables are left in their last-used state. If "A"
Links to "B", program "B"  must return to program "A" via a "CICS RETURN".
When a "GOBACK" is issued in a "CALL" scenario, the WS of program "B" is NOT
freed until task termination, whereas, a "CICS RETURN" (issued in a LINK-API
scenario) frees the storage acquired by "B". Please keep this non-freeing of
residual WS in mind as it could save you from the dreaded "SOS" situation.

        Note that if you issue a "CICS RETURN" from program "B" after being
Called by program "A", this will terminate the run-unit and possibly
terminate the entire task.

        If program "A" were CICS/Assembler, you could NOT Dynamically Call
Cobol program "B". In this case, you must use continue to use the LINK-API.
If everything is COBOL, then this is a moot point.

**** End Dissertation ****

        Sorry for the long-winded assessment but, Dynamically Called
programs have their place, but require special consideration before
implementing. In regards to your dilemma, I have NEVER issued a "GOBACK"
from a program that had been accessed via a LINK-API and never would
(especially if Linked-To from CICS/Assembler). The old "UNPREDICTABLE
RESULTS" warning comes to mind and this is what may be occurring in your
situation as it seems to be unpredictable.

        BTW, coding a GOBACK directly after the CICS RETURN is common
practice and can be left as such. In spite of what the IBM manuals indicate,
I think you can't go wrong in changing the GOBACK's to CICS RETURN's. I've
been coding CICS/Command level (Macro prior to that) since Version/Release
1.4 (1975?) and this is the way it has always worked. Grandpa says "If it
works, don't fix it", but in this case, you must fix it before going forward
;o)

Carry On,

Bill

"jason" <cz.systems@home.com> wrote in message
news:3B8EEBA0.5F2FC5AF@home.com...
> Hi all,
>
> Here's the scenario
>
> Pgm a exec cics links to program b.  Program b sets some commarea
variables
> and "goback"s to pgm a.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> are having some unpredictable results in production that we can't
reproduce
> in test, so I'm replacing the gobacks with returns (but leaving one
> unreachable goback for compiler purposes).
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hard Sci-Fi
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Exec cics return vs Goback

- **From:** jason <cz.systems@home.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T11:31:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3B8F77D2.ECC4DB4E@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8EEBA0.5F2FC5AF@home.com> <9mmu3n$bsu$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks for the reply.  

This is a messy, multi-program transaction, all working with cics links. 
It's not in my mandate to change things to "calls".  Also, b links to
program c, so it would involve significant restructuring.  Yeah, there's
significant WS, and program b is not a monster, but rather 2k lines.  Don't
know the size of the load offhand.  No DPL's

Looks like the whole shebang was copied from batch skeletons, as there are
gobacks sprinkled around in about 7 programs under the transaction.  I'm
leaving a dummy goback in each, but replacing the rest by cics returns.  

I'm feeling that this is the cause of my unpredictable results, especially
as some of the gobacks are handling for db2 not founds and there's no file
handling for most of the vsam in program b.

Nobody can give me a definitive answer on what a goback does under cics, I
guess, because it JUST ISN'T DONE. 

Thanks again.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T11:41:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9moeoa$eru$1@slb7.atl.mindspring.net>`

#### ↳ Re: Exec cics return vs Goback

- **From:** Daniel Jacot<daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T12:10:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v8Lj7.263$F4.331@www.newsranger.com>`
- **References:** `<3B8EEBA0.5F2FC5AF@home.com>`

```
GOBACK works in such situations as well as EXEC CICS RETURN does, at least on
OS/390. Other environments may have other rules. Refer to the CICS Programming
Guide which says about returning from subprograms:
'The linked subprogram must return using either RETURN or the COBOL statement
GOBACK.'

I'd guess another bug in your application.

You can reach the most current CICS Programming Guide at:
http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/DFHJAP36/CCONTENTS
or directly to the reffering chapter at:
http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com:80/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/DFHJAP36/1.2.3

On Fri, 31 Aug 2001 01:33:31 GMT, in article <3B8EEBA0.5F2FC5AF@home.com>, jason
stated...
>
>Hi all,
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hard Sci-Fi

-------------------------------------------------
With kind regards
Daniel Jacot
-------------------------------------------------
visit us at: http://www.winterthur.com
```

```
I am not a programmer of cobol, however the product we use is written in
Acucobol.  And subsequently, whenever a new field is introduced into the
database the new field is sometimes populated with the 20202020.02.

Could someone be kind enough to explain what this means, and what impact it
has on data?  And what sort of remedy should be done to ensure it doesnt
happen at all?

Thanks in advance


Steven
```

- **References:** `<EeKj7.53$La7.324@news1.sunsh1.vic.optushome.com.au>`

```


Steven Sinfield

the field was not  initialized,
move zeros to field  first

this  20202020  etc  is HEXAdECIMAL  OF  "SPACES"

lages


> I am not a programmer of cobol, however the product we use is written in
> Acucobol.  And subsequently, whenever a new field is introduced into the
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Steven
```

#### ↳ Re: 20202020.02

- **From:** "Jerry P" <jerryp@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2001-08-31T22:24:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<t8Uj7.115013$Lw3.7211567@news2.aus1.giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<EeKj7.53$La7.324@news1.sunsh1.vic.optushome.com.au>`

```
As another response pointed out, the field was initialized as blanks.

The obvious impact is that the numeric field starts off with 20
million dollars in it.

Assuming this is not what you want, put the appropriate value in the
field - don't ADD a new value, PLACE (MOVE, INITIALIZE, PRIME) a value
in the field.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>

```

you to read COBOL source files, set parameters for searches using
keywords and extract specific items to an output file; e.g., ability to
pick up references to using and returning parameters for Linkage,

possible.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
